volume 66

The International Humanitarian Law Series is a series of monograhs and edited volumes which aims to promote scholarly analysis and discussion of both the theory and practice of the inter-national legal regulation of armed conflict.

The series explores substantive issues of International Humanitarian Law, including:

– Protection for victims of armed conflict and regulation of the means and method of warfare – Questions of application of the various legal regimes for the conduct of armed conflict – Issues relating to the implementation of International Humanitarian Law obligations – National and international approaches to the enforcement of the law and – The interactions between International Humanitarian Law and other related areas of inter-

national law such as Human Rights, Refugee Law, Arms Control and Disarmament Law, and

Intenational Criminal Law.



The titles published in this series are listed at brill.com/ ihul





Prolonged Occupation


and International Law

Israel and Palestine



Edited by

Nada Kiswanson and Susan Power

Foreword



I am honoured to write the foreword for this book which aims to focus inter-national attention on the extraordinary length of the belligerent occupation of the Palestinian territory by Israel. Inherently only a temporary status, this occupation has now lasted more than half a century.

Over thirty years ago, in December 1987, Al- Haq organised its first interna-

tional conference on “International Law and the Administration of Occupied Territories”. Our aim was to seek international expertise on the question of how an occupier should administer occupied territory under a prolonged occupation, a question which had been largely unexplored since after World War ii. Working with Al- Haq at the time, I helped to organise that conference

and subsequently edited the collection of papers.1

Humanitarian law was a backwater at that time, so we were asking experts

to draw on precedents from decades earlier to consider how Israel should be administering the Palestinian territory it occupied. At that time the prolonged nature of the occupation, already in place for twenty years, preoccupied many of our contributors, but who then could have imagined that it would still be continuing for over 55 years, until today?

Yet soon after publication of that book, humanitarian law became all too

topical in a number of ways: the Gulf War, the occupation of Kuwait by Iraq, the establishment and developing jurisprudence of the UN International Criminal Tribunals for the former Yugoslavia and Rwanda, the International Criminal Court, and the International Court of Justice’s advisory opinion of 2004 on the Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory.

Al- Haq’s book, published under my name in 1992, provided persuasive evi-

dence of the violations of international law in Israel’s administration of the territories it occupied. We had hoped that by making the international com-munity aware of the maladministration and exploitation by Israel of the ter-ritories it occupied and of its violation of international humanitarian and human rights law, we could contribute to the pressure on Israel to change. Regrettably this did not happen and the opportunities for such change now seem ever more remote. Rather the occupation deepened and as explored in the current volume, came closer in many ways to de facto annexation, with



1 Emma Playfair (ed), International law and the Administration of Occupied Territories

(oup 1992).

x Foreword

expansion of settlements, the building of the Wall, restrictions on movement, and seizure of Palestinian land and resources, as well as the continued system-atic repression of the Palestinian civilian population.

I was therefore glad to know that Al- Haq had again taken the initiative, in

partnership with the Palestinian Centre for Human Rights, Birzeit University’s Institute of Law, and the International Federation for Human Rights to bring together international legal and practitioner expertise in an international con-ference in 2018 on “The Threshold from Occupation to Annexation”. That con-ference and the passing of half a century of occupation gave momentum to the present volume, edited by Nada Kiswanson and Dr Susan Power focusing on the critical issue of prolonged occupation and international law.

I was privileged to work with Al- Haq in its early formative years and to

have known the Palestinian Centre for Human Rights since its establishment in Gaza. I observed both organisations over the years as they faced seemingly insurmountable challenges and admired their steadfastness in overcoming these and in sustaining the extraordinary quality of their work in document-ing, defending, and promoting human rights in Palestine. At the time of writ-ing, there is a concerted effort to stifle Palestinian human rights organisations and silence Palestinian civil society at large, including by criminalising, arrest-

ing, and threatening human rights defenders.2 The vibrant international law discourse and human rights movement is, however, resilient. Since the con-ference held by Al- Haq in 1987, not only has international humanitarian law developed rapidly, but documents have come into the public domain which have enabled researchers to review key aspects of the events surrounding the establishment of the State of Palestine and the occupation of Palestine. While many of these remain “classified”, enough has been made accessible for Palestinian, Israeli and international historians to add considerably to earlier understandings of what really happened in 1948 and 1967 to the Palestinian people. Some of these findings are drawn on by the distinguished contributors to the present volume, including some who also contributed to our conference in 1987.

Chapters in this book cover an array of topics and aspects of the prolonged

occupation and Israel’s control, ranging from colonialism and apartheid to the



2 See for example ‘UN Special Rapporteurs Condemn Israel’s Designation of Palestinian

Human Rights Defenders as Terrorist Organisations – Press Release’, UN News, 25 October

2021; ‘Israel Must Safeguard Human Rights Defenders in Occupied Palestinian Territory

and Within Its Borders – UN Expert’, UN News, 11 August 2021; unhrc, Report of the Special

Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the Palestinian Territories Occupied Since 1967,

Michael Lynk, a/ hrc/ 34/ 70, 13 April 2017, pages 8– 17.

Foreword xi

illegality of occupation. Nada Kiswanson, in her excellent introduction, sum-marises and groups the chapters. Importantly, many of the chapters in this book propose specific actions which can and should be taken by States, col-lectively and individually. Such actions include asking the United Nations to organise the necessary steps towards a fair solution for Palestinian refugees, including financial resources to cover compensation. They also include calls for the direct involvement of the international community in bringing an end to settlement activities in the West Bank and the lifting of the blockade of the Gaza Strip. In agreeing that Israel’s exercise of control over Palestinians is unac-ceptable, chapters examine Third States’ responsibilities under international law and underline their duties not to recognise as lawful a situation created by serious breaches arising under a peremptory norm of general international law, nor to render aid or assistance in maintaining that situation. Notably, the European Commission should be pressed to propose a law to stop trade with illegal settlements, but if the Commission fails to do so, then Member States should act unilaterally in compliance with its legal obligations. Considering the potential role of the International Criminal Court, contributors stress the importance of realistic expectations of what can be achieved through the icc while anticipating that it can expose some crimes committed by Israeli offi-cials and in turn bring much needed justice to some victims.

I applaud Al- Haq and their co- convenors of the conference and Nada

Kiswanson and Dr Susan Power for their efforts in bringing new analyses and, importantly, recommendations for urgent action to the international commu-nity. I sincerely hope that drawing on the recommendations put forward by the contributors to this book, action will be taken to bring justice to Palestinians and Israelis so badly affected by the long years of conflict.

Emma Playfair



Acknowledgements



This book began a fledgling idea, caught in a moment in The Hague in 2017, as the editors working together and grappling with the extent and implications of Israel’s de facto annexation of the occupied Palestinian territory, discussed the need to create space for advanced academic discourse and reflection on the broader subject of prolonged occupation. At the time, twenty- five years had followed since the publication of Emma Playfair’s seminal edited book International Law and the Administration of Occupied Territories. As the military administration hurtled past the half century mark, it was time to look deeper, past the administration of the territory and at the very nature of the occupa-tion itself, its prolonged and permanent character, and resulting implications.

The editors would like to extend their gratitude to all the contributors to

this book for their thought- provoking, stimulating chapters, written during an exceptional time- period.

The editors are also grateful to Al- Haq, Birzeit University – Institute of Law,

the Palestinian Centre for Human Rights, and the International Federation for Human Rights for organising the international conference on “The Threshold from Occupation to Annexation”, which took place on 3– 4 October 2018 in Palestine, that encouraged us to pursue our longer- term aspiration to develop this book.

In particular, the editors wish to thank Rania Muhareb for her drive and

commitment in spearheading the organisation of the joint academic confer-ence hosted in Birzeit and Professors Jamil Salem and Mustafa Mari for con-vening and leading the initial selection committee and setting the seeds for the book which followed. Also, many thanks to the team at Al- Haq, who worked tirelessly behind the scenes including Lillian Zanairi, Laura Thomas, Samer Khalifah, and Tahseen Ellayyan.

The editors are also grateful to Emma Playfair, Shawan Jabarin, John Dugard,

and John Quigley for their invaluable and kind support. Emma’s book served as a model of excellence throughout the process.

The editors are especially indebted to the wonderful team at brill, who

have provided their guidance and expertise, in particular Lindy Melman and Thalien Colenbrander, and the Editors of the International Humanitarian Law Series.

Susan wishes to thank the inspirational and dynamic Legal Research and

Advocacy Team at Al- Haq, Seamus Cox, Teresa Power, and family for their con-tinuous encouragement.

Acknowledgements xiii

Last, but certainly not least, Nada wishes to thank her mother, husband, and

children, without whom this book would not have been possible.

Nada Kiswanson

Dr Susan Power

Notes on Contributors



Maha Abdallah

is a legal researcher and human rights advocate. She holds an ll.m. in interna-tional human rights law from the Irish Centre for Human Rights.

Rimona Afana

is Assistant Professor of Peace Studies at Kennesaw State University and Visiting Scholar at the Emory University School of Law, Vulnerability Initiative.

Shane Darcy

is Professor and Deputy Director, Irish Centre for Human Rights, School of Law, National University of Ireland Galway.

Manuel Devers

is a lawyer specialised in international law and EU law. He holds a Master of Laws in EU law and international law from the Riga Graduate School of Law and a Master of Laws in international law from University Paris i Panthéon- Sorbonne.

John Dugard

is Professor of Law and ad hoc Judge at the International Court of Justice. He served as the Special Rapporteur on the Human Rights Situation in the Occupied Palestinian Territory between 2001 and 2008.

Marya Farah

is a lawyer focusing on international human rights law and advocacy. She holds a Juris Doctor, Master of Public Policy, and Bachelor of Arts in Political Science and Organisational Studies.

Anita Ferrara

is a postdoctoral lecturer at the Irish Centre for Human Rights, National University of Ireland, Galway.

Sahar Francis

is General Director of Ramallah- based Addameer Prisoner Support and Human Rights Association, a Palestinian NGO providing legal and advocacy support to Notes on Contributors xix

Palestinian political prisoners in Israeli and Palestinian prisons. An attorney by training, she joined the Association in 1998, first as a human rights lawyer, then as Head of the Legal Unit.

Aeyal Gross

is Professor of International and Constitutional Law at the Tel- Aviv University. He is also a Visiting Professor of Law at soas, University of London.

Thomas Hammarberg

is a Swedish diplomat and human rights expert. He has been a member of the Swedish Parliament. He represented Sweden in the Refugee Working Group established through the Madrid Peace Process in 1991. He was also Sweden’s Ambassador on Humanitarian Issues and Secretary- General of the Olof Palme International Center. He previously served as Secretary- General of Amnesty International and Secretary- General of Save the Children (Sweden). He was Council of Europe Commissioner for Human Rights from 2006 to 2012.

Suha Jarrar

was a Senior Legal Researcher with Al- Haq. She specialised in environmental law under occupation.

Asem Khalil

is Dean of the Faculty of Graduate Studies and Professor at the Birzeit University. He obtained a PhD from the University of Fribourg.

Nada Kiswanson

is Legal Counsel practicing international criminal and human rights law. She has an ll.b and ll.m from Uppsala University and an ll.m from Leiden University. She has appeared before the International Criminal Court on behalf of hundreds of individual Palestinian victims. She has also worked with and represented Palestinian human rights organisations, primarily Al- Haq, before inter- governmental organisations and judicial institutions.

Tom Moerenhout

is a legal scholar specialised in international economic and humanitarian law. He has attained two Master degrees and a PhD in international relations and international law at the Graduate Institute of International and Development Studies in Geneva.

xx Notes on Contributors

Ray Murphy

is Professor at the Irish Centre for Human Rights, National University of Ireland Galway.

Emma Playfair

is a British lawyer, former Al- Haq volunteer and Representative of the Ford Foundation’s Middle East and North Africa Office.

Susan Power

is Head of Legal Research and Advocacy at Al- Haq, a Palestinian NGO, based in Ramallah, Palestine. She has a PhD from Trinity College, Dublin and she lectured for seven years at Griffith College Cork and Dublin.

John Quigley

is Professor Emeritus at the Moritz College of Law at the Ohio State University.

John Reynolds

is Associate Professor at the School of Law and Criminology, Maynooth University, National University of Ireland.

Halla Shoaibi

is Assistant Professor at Birzeit University and Master Program Director of the Birzeit Master Program in Israeli Studies. She obtained a PhD from the American University.

Vito Todeschini

is a legal expert in the areas of human rights, international humanitarian law and accountability, with a particular focus on Palestine/Israel and the wider MENA region. He has a PhD from Aarhus University.



Introduction

Nada Kiswanson



“[N] o matter how long the road has been, ‘never- ending’ cannot be an accept-able description for any situation in which human rights are violated and abused”, said United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights, Michelle Bachelet, in her address to the UN Committee on the Exercise of the Inalienable

Rights of the Palestinian People in 2021.1 Although the situation in Palestine is untenable, “never- ending” accurately describes the current state of affairs. There is today no prospect of Israel either retreating from Palestine or allowing Palestinians to return to their homes. In a deepening environment of political inertia, Israel is instead tightening its grip over Palestine.

Palestine and Israel did not arrive at this point coincidentally. Historical

accounts and analyses of the events that resulted in the establishment of the State of Israel and the 1967 invasion and occupation of Palestinian territory support the reading that Israel intends to take control over at least the entirety

of mandate Palestine.2 Moreover, Israel’s policies and practices in the occupied Palestinian territory, its conduct towards Palestinians, and statements issued by its elected officials disclose a design to subjugate or expel Palestinians for

the benefit of a Jewish homeland in Palestine.3 For example, in 2018, the Israeli Parliament enacted the Jewish Nation- State Basic Law declaring the “Land of Israel” the “historic national home of the Jewish people” and the “State of

Israel” the “national state of the Jewish people”.4 The quasi- constitutional law proclaims that self- determination in the State of Israel is “unique to the Jewish people” and enshrines the “development of Jewish settlement as a national



1 ‘Bachelet Describes “Disastrous” Human Rights Situation Across Occupied Palestinian

Territory’, UN News, 7 December 2021.

2 See for example Chapters 1, Quigley, 5, Reynolds, and 16, Hammarberg.

3 unhrc, Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the Palestinian

Territories Occupied Since 1967, a/ hrc/ 49/ 87, 21 March 2022, paras 12– 22.

4 Israel, Basic Law: Israel – The Nation State of the Jewish People, 25 July 2018. See for translation

and commentary Adalah, Israel’s Jewish Nation- State Law, 20 December 2020. On 8 July 2021,

Israel’s Supreme Court rejected fifteen petitions about the validity of the law as part of the future

constitution due to its content. The Supreme Court opined that the content of the law does not

detract from the value and principle of equality and the “difficulties” pointed to by the petition-

ers could be addressed by way of interpretation. According to its interpretation, the provision

on self- determination being unique to the Jewish people does not deny self- determination

by other groups at the “sub- national” level. hcj 5555/ 18, Hassoun v The Knesset (8 July 2021).

2 Kiswanson

value”.5 It also commits the State to “act to encourage [Jewish settlement] and

to promote and to consolidate its establishment”.6 According to astute inter-pretations of these provisions, the law’s scope transcends the 1967 borders and subordinates both Palestinian citizens of Israel, occupied Palestinians,

and Palestinian refugees, to Jewish supremacy.7 Another indication of Israel’s intentions towards the opt is the Parliamentary Subcommittee for Judea and

Samaria8 proposal of November 2020 to renew the settlement of titles in the

West Bank.9 The UN Secretary- General described the proposed renewal as “an irreversible act of sovereignty by a permanent regime, and so subverts the

principle that occupation is inherently temporary”.10

The international community has implicitly and explicitly acknowledged

Israel’s intention to exercise perpetual control over Palestine. As early as 1979, the UN Security Council called on Israel to “desist from taking any action which would result in changing the legal status and geographical nature and materi-ally affecting the demographic composition of the Arab territories occupied

since 1967, including Jerusalem”.11 In 1980, the UN Security Council deplored the “persistence of Israel in changing the physical character, demographic

composition, institutional structure and status of the Holy City of Jerusalem”.12 And in 2016, the UN Security Council condemned “all measures aimed at alter-ing the demographic composition, character and status of the Palestinian Territory occupied since 1967, including East Jerusalem, including, inter alia,

Introduction 3

the construction and expansion of settlements, transfer of Israeli settlers, con-fiscation of land, demolition of homes and displacement of Palestinian civil-

ians, in violation of international humanitarian law and relevant resolutions”.13

US Secretary of State, John Kerry, spoke to the same effect in his speech on

the US vote on the 2016 resolution. In what he described as an “honest, clear- eyed conversation about the uncomfortable truths and difficult choices”, he warned that “[t] he status quo is leaning towards one state and perpetual occu-

pation” and that “Israel can be either Jewish or Democratic, it cannot be both”.14

What Kerry brushed over with his “uncomfortable truths” remark are the

magnitude and impact of Israel’s control over Palestine and Palestinians. Since the beginning of the occupation 55 years ago and the Nakba 74 years ago, gen-erations of Palestinians are born into, live, and die under Israeli racialised sub-jugation. Israel dictates who Palestinians in the opt may reside with and visit, the education and medical treatment they can access, whether their property rights will be respected and restored, the roads they are permitted to travel on, and who passes judgement on them. Israel’s hold over the Palestinian people and territory is invasive and coercive, and it affects Palestinians everywhere. Israeli laws and policies exclude Palestinian refugees from the scope of persons allowed to settle in what is now the State of Israel and deny them entry to and

residency in the opt.15 These measures of control are products of a system engineered to fracture Palestinians into demographically controllable sub- groups, with each group allowed to exist only within assigned disconnected

and delineated pockets in Palestine, or outside.16 The effects hereof are pro-found: not only does Israel risk fraying the Palestinian social and national fab-ric, but it has turned “home”– a basic human need and an intimately personal, ancestral, and innate experience and place of belonging – into the central focus of Palestinian yearning and struggle.

Israel’s occupation constitutes a deprivation of Palestinian choice and lib-

erty at a time when humanity insists on individual and collective exercise of

the right to self- determination – the bedrock of human rights.17 Despite this



13 unsc Resolution 2334 (23 December 2016).

14 Sarah Begley, ‘Read John Kerry’s Full Speech on Israeli Settlements and a Two- State

Solution’, Time, 28 December 2016.

15 See Chapter 5, Reynolds.

16 See for example Human Rights Watch, A Threshold Crossed: Israeli Authorities and the

Crimes of Apartheid and Persecution, 2021, pages 170– 201.

17 Attesting to this, the right to self- determination is enshrined in Articles 1 of the UN

International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights. See also UN Human

Rights Committee, General Comment 12: The Right to Self- determination of Peoples (Article 4 Kiswanson

insistence and the UN General Assembly affirmation in 1960 “that all peoples have an inalienable right to complete freedom, the exercise of their sover-

eignty and the integrity of their national territory”,18 Israel continues to sup-press Palestinian self- determination without a global shrug of shoulders.

Israel’s criminalisation of the symbolic representation of Palestine strikes at

the core of Palestinian self- determination. The public display of the Palestinian flag and dissemination of material of “political significance” without permis-sion from the Israeli military commander is prohibited by Israeli military order

and has resulted in the mass- incarceration and killing of Palestinians.19 On 13 May 2022, Israeli militarised police attacked the expression of Palestinian self- determination during the funeral procession of Shireen Abu Aqleh when they ripped Palestinian flags from mourners and assaulted Palestinians singing the national anthem and chanting slogans. They severely beat the pallbearers,

causing many of them to lose hold of the casket.20 Shireen Abu Aqleh, an iconic

Al- Jazeera journalist, was shot and killed by Israeli forces.21 Her killing ignited Palestinians who came together in solidarity in one of the largest Palestinian funeral processions in East Jerusalem. This incident is reminiscent of the many Palestinians killed and injured by Israeli soldiers while wrapped in or waving

the Palestinian flag during the Great March of Return in the Gaza Strip.22

Confronted with Israel’s unrelenting hold over the occupied Palestinian

territory, the editors invited legal practitioners and academics to reflect on the ways in which Israel’s control is conceptualised, framed, and manifested. The contributors were encouraged to consider the legal consequences that flow thereof and to offer alternative perspectives and characterisations of

tion [of the right to self- determination] is an essential condition for the effective guaran-

tee and observance of individual human rights and for the promotion and strengthening

of those rights”.

18 UN Declaration on the Granting of Independence to Colonial Countries and Peoples,

adopted by unga Resolution 1514 (14 December 1960).

Introduction 5

Israel’s control and conduct.23 Contributors to this book agree that rights and responsibilities are not diminished by the passing of time or by virtue of the nationality, race, or religion of the wrongful State or individual perpetrator. If anything, the duration of the occupation must give pause to the international community’s perceptions and portrayals of Israel’s actions and cause them to re- evaluate what they have acquiesced to. It should also trigger a genuine conversation about the impact of States’ dealings with Israel on the integrity, efficacy, and implementation of international law.

This book confirms that an occupation is a temporary situation, that the

occupied population retains sovereignty while under the administration of the Occupying Power, and that their dignity and human rights shall be respected. Under no circumstance does international law tolerate the colonisation of the occupied territory, hence the prohibition against the transfer of the nation-als of the Occupying Power into occupied territory. Indefinite occupation and colonialism collide with the foundational requirement under international humanitarian law that the occupied territory be returned to the occupied population.

International law has flourished since the beginning of Israel’s occupation

in 1967: we have since seen the adoption of the major international human rights treaties and the Additional Protocols to the four Geneva Conventions, the establishment of international and special courts and tribunals, and the exemplary use of multilateral cooperation in situations of conflict and colo-

nialism around the world.24 Regrettably, the abundance of legal norms, princi-ples, structures, and mechanisms has not affected the degree of Israel’s control, let alone ensured that it is firmly placed in the hands of the occupied, indig-enous, Palestinian population. Israel’s position and duties vis- à- vis Palestine and Palestinians and the framing of its control are put under the magnifying glass in the first part of this book. Inevitable and indispensable conversations around the value and limits of international law, especially the law of occupa-tion, are either addressed in or underpin chapters in this book.

John Quigley begins the discussion in Part 1. He revisits in Chapter 1 the

historical events that resulted in the occupation of the Palestinian terri-tory and posits that the occupation is illegal since its inception. His chapter uncovers Israel’s targeted strategy to take control over Palestine before the 1967 war erupted and Israel’s orchestrated use of pre- emptive self- defence.



23 The editors encouraged a broad range of views and sought to facilitate active discussion.

The views adopted and argued in the chapters are those of the authors of the chapters

alone. They are not to be attributed to the editors, their employers, or clients.

24 See Chapter 9, Dugard.

6 Kiswanson

Vito Todeschini, however, argues in Chapter 2 that the occupation becomes illegal with the passing of time. He differentiates between the jus ad bellum and jus in bello frameworks and concludes that “a belligerent occupation that lasts for a period of time exceeding the necessities of self- defence becomes unlawful because it breaches the principles of necessity and proportionality and, by implication, the prohibition on the use of force enshrined in the UN Charter and customary international law”.





Chapter 3, written by Ray Murphy, Anita Ferrara, and Susan Power, ques-


tions the continued relevance of the international law of occupation to the situation in the opt and applies the Third World Approaches to International Law lens. “By examining how the interplay of international law and practice are manipulated to serve neo- colonial interests in Palestine”, the authors sug-gest that international law was not intended to apply to an occupation that has lasted for more than half a century.

Aeyal Gross contributes Chapter 4 to the book. It focuses on Israel’s con-

trol over the Gaza Strip and describes how Israel’s so- called disengagement from the area is an attempt by Israel to evade its legal responsibilities. Aeyal Gross proposes a functional approach to occupation, one that recognises its transformed form and emphasises the need to protect a people under foreign control and values the raison d’être of international humanitarian law.





Chapter 5 by John Reynolds looks beyond the international humanitarian


law of occupation. His chapter views Israel’s “apartheid system as a core feature of settler- colonial rule in Palestine since the Nakba and – more recently – as an increasingly central focus of international legal analysis of the Palestinian reality”. John Reynolds underscores Palestinians’ framing of their experience as that of apartheid and ties together the frameworks of colonialism and

apartheid. Chapter 6 also discusses the colonialism framework. Rimona Afana explains in that chapter, that Israel’s “occupation functions as a tool of and smokescreen for colonialism”. She argues that in treating Israel’s control as colonialism, new ways of viewing the transitional justice and conflict resolu-tion will emerge.

Chapters 7 and 8 give attention to particularly pervasive aspects of Israel’s

control that have far- reaching consequences for all Palestinians living in the

opt. Sahar Francis elaborates in Chapter 7 on Israel’s use of military orders and military courts to mass- incarcerate, punish, and silence Palestinians as a means to cement its control over the occupied territory. She views the Israeli judicial military system as an annexationist tool. Shane Darcy takes a closer look at the prison system from the standpoint of international criminal law and the involvement of private companies in Part 2.

Introduction 7

The last chapter of Part 1 is a poignant contribution. It is written in memory

of Suha Jarrar, a friend and colleague who passed away too soon, at the young age of 30. Suha Jarrar was an avid member of the Palestinian human rights community and pioneer in the field of environmental law under occupation.





Chapter 8 is based on her writings for Al- Haq. Taking Masafer Yatta as a pri-mary case study, her chapter evaluates Palestinians’ ability to adapt to environ-mental changes and illustrates the adverse impacts of Israel’s occupation on Palestinian environment and natural resources.


Those involved in what is casually referred to as the “Palestinian/ Israeli con-

flict” are military and civilian persons, States, corporations, and organisations. They breathe air into Israel’s control and reinvigorate its efforts to colonise Palestine. Irrespective of how Israel’s control is understood, these actors have legal responsibilities. Part 2 of this book assesses the legal responsibilities and involvement of individuals, corporations, and Third States.

John Dugard contributes the first chapter of Part 2. Focusing on peremptory

norms of international law and States’ responsibilities, Chapter 9 compares the international community’s responses to apartheid South Africa and Palestine. John Dugard asks imperative questions around States’ selective approaches to breaches of peremptory norms and highlights the powers that States can harness to end unlawful behaviour. He demonstrates how States, unilaterally and collectively, have failed to challenge Israel and to hold it accountable for its violation of a host of peremptory norms. He concludes that this failure has “weakened respect for human rights and humanitarian law”, as well as “brought international law into disrepute and given rise to cynicism about the respect States have for international law”.





Chapter 10 by Manuel Devers and Tom Moerenhout addresses Third States’


responsibilities in the European Union setting. They provide that measures aimed at ending trade with illegal settlements, like those in the opt and Western Sahara, do not constitute sanctions. Their chapter further under-scores that non- recognition and non- assistance are trade measures under EU law and makes a compelling case for why the EU must act where peremptory norms are breached, or else its Member States will violate their international law obligations.

States are not the only actors relevant to Israel’s occupation. Policies and

plans adopted by Israeli governments are conceived and executed by indi-

viduals. Chapters 11 through 14, examine different dimensions of individual criminal responsibility and the International Criminal Court. With the icc

complementarity framework as its point of departure, Chapter 11 explains why the court presents an unparalleled opportunity to secure victims’ rights to jus-tice, truth, and reparation. I analyse in that chapter the potential for justice 8 Kiswanson

through the Israeli military justice system and Supreme Court and aver that Israel is neither willing nor able to hold its own citizens criminally responsible for their role in the commission of war crimes and crimes against humanity in the territory of the State of Palestine.

With the involvement of corporations in the mistreatment of Palestinian

detainees and prisoners in mind, Chapter 12 by Shane Darcy explores the individual criminal legal responsibility of private sector individuals and enter-prises. Shane Darcy draws attention to various modes of liability and entry-

points for prosecutions considering icc law and policies. Chapter 13 by Susan Power views the systematic economic exploitation of the Palestinian territory within the meaning of the war crime of pillaging. She contemplates on the prosecution of pillage at international trials related to World War ii and scruti-nises the potential for modern- day prosecutions under the Rome Statute.





Chapter 14 by Halla Shoaibi and Asem Khalil relates to the issue of juris-


diction and sovereignty at the icc. It provides that the occupation does not restrict the sovereignty of an occupied State, and that it has the authority to allow the icc to exercise criminal jurisdiction over those committing Rome Statute crimes over its territory. Relatedly, their chapter argues that the Oslo Accords provisions do not bar the icc from exercising jurisdiction over the State of Palestine because these provisions conflict with the Palestinian right to self- determination and are inconsistent with Palestine’s duties under the Geneva Conventions.

There is a growing world- wide movement to hold businesses that fuel armed

conflicts for their own profit to account for their wrongdoing.25 In 2020, the United Nations Office of the High Commissioner issued a report on business enterprises involved in activities related to Israeli settlements in the opt. That report lists 112 business entities domiciled in Israel and in other States that are

engaged in settlement activities.26 Marya Farah and Maha Abdallah’s chapter on Israel’s annexation of the occupied Palestinian territory through utilities

addresses corporate responsibility. They posit in Chapter 15 that businesses’ distribution of and control over water resources, telecommunications, and electricity networks at the behest of Israel has “facilitated the physical, eco-nomic, and demographic transformation of the opt in a manner that wholly defies international law”.

25 See for example unhrc Resolution 26/ 9 (26 June 2014).

26 ohchr, Database of All Business Enterprises Involved in Activities Detailed in Paragraph

96 of the Independent Fact- Finding Mission to Investigate Implications of the Israeli

Settlements on the Civil, Political, Economic, Social, and Cultural Rights of the Palestinian

People Throughout the Occupied Palestinian Territory, including East Jerusalem, a/ hrc/ 43/

71, 12 February 2020.

Introduction 9

The last chapter of the book closes the circle. Chapter 16 by Thomas

Hammarberg recalls Jewish settlers’ ethnic cleansing of mandate Palestine

for the purpose of establishing a Jewish State. Chapter 16 further expounds on the current situation of Palestinian refugees and substantiates their continued right to return. His chapter also discusses the roles and responsibilities of the UN, in particular the United Nations Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees.

The last chapter reminds us that we must unfold the past if we are to under-

stand the root- causes that set Israel on its current trajectory of apartheid, colonialism, and annexation and resolve the intergenerational trauma and injustice caused.





Part 1


Legal Frameworks and Characterisations



∵





chapter 1


Israel’s Unlawful 1967 Invasion of Palestine

John Quigley



The 1967 occupation of Palestine territory by Israel has evoked a stream of con-demnation at the United Nations. The UN General Assembly and UN Security Council have castigated Israel for annexing the eastern part of Jerusalem and

for inserting its own citizens into residential settlements,1 for devastating the

Arab economy,2 and, most to the point, for continuing the occupation.3 At the same time, the UN has given only scant attention to how the occupation began and whether it began in a manner that would make Israel’s even temporary control lawful. The UN has never examined the initiation of the war that led to the occupation. An effort was made to do so in the days immediately following the end of hostilities, but that effort was thwarted by the United States, which

argued that the issue should not be addressed.4 The UN undertook no inquiry into the facts, even though they were hotly contested, and even though a deter-mination that Israel acted unlawfully would trigger an obligation on the part of the UN Security Council to act to reverse its consequences. This dereliction on the part of the UN was not inevitable. When Israel invaded and occupied Arab territory in 1956, the UN demanded the immediate removal of the invading

force.5 As we shall see in this chapter, Israel complied.

This chapter will examine the events of the 1967 war that resulted in Israel’s

occupation of Palestine territory – the Gaza Strip and the West Bank, including East Jerusalem – and the subsequent responses of the international commu-nity. This chapter will also analyse Israel’s use of force in light of applicable jus ad bellum norms, to assess whether Israel’s occupation of Palestine territory was initially lawful.



1 unsc Resolution 465 (1 March 1980); unsc Resolution 446 (22 March 1979); unsc Resolution

2334 (23 December 2016); unga Resolution 76/ 12 (1 December 2021).

2 unga Resolution 70/ 225 (22 December 2015).

3 unga Resolution 75/ 22 (2 December 2020).

4 unsc Official Records (14 June 1967) UN Doc. s/ pv.1360, page 20.

5 The resolution called for prompt withdrawal to 1949 armistice lines. unga Resolution

997(es- i) (2 November 1956).



© John Quigley, 2023 | DOI:10.1163/9789004503939

14 Quigley

1 Inaction at the UN Security Council

The UN Charter requires the organisation to deal with aggression when aggres-

sion occurs.6 A basic reason for the existence of the UN coming out of World War ii was to stop acts of aggression. Yet in 1967, when Israeli military forces invaded and occupied Egypt’s Sinai Peninsula and two parts of Palestine (the Gaza Strip and the West Bank of the Jordan River, including East Jerusalem), the UN Security Council confined itself to calling for a ceasefire. Israel was the party on the offensive and it defied these resolutions. Therefore, on 8 June 1967, the Soviet Union tabled a resolution to demand a withdrawal to pre- war lines and to point a finger at Israel for aggression (“Soviet draft”). The Soviet draft resolution minced no words. It read:

The Security Council,

Noting that Israel has disregarded the Security Council decisions calling

for the cessation of military activities (s/ res/ 233 of 6 June 1967 and s/

res/ 234 of 7 June 1967),

Considering that Israel not only has not halted military activities but has

made use of the time elapsed since the adoption of the aforementioned

Council resolutions in order to seize additional territory of the United

Arab Republic and Jordan,

Noting that even now Israel is continuing military activities instead of

halting its aggression, thus defying the United Nations and all peace-

loving States,

1. Vigorously condemns Israel’s aggressive activities and its violations

of the aforementioned Security Council resolutions, of the United Nations Charter and of United Nations principles;

2. Demands that Israel should immediately halt its military activities

against neighbouring Arab States and should remove all its troops from the territory of those States and withdraw them behind the armistice lines and respect the status of the demilitarized zones, as

prescribed in the General Armistice Agreements.7



6 UN Charter, Article 39.

7 unsc Official Records (8 June 1967) UN Doc. s/ pv.1351, page 5.

Israel’s Unlawful 1967 Invasion of Palestine 15

This last clause was a reference to the armistice agreements that had ended hostilities in 1949. The draft was aimed in the first instance at Israel’s incur-sion into Egypt, which had merged with Syria in 1958 to form what was called

the United Arab Republic.8 When Israeli forces moved southward, they took the Gaza Strip along with Egypt’s Sinai Peninsula. The reference to Jordan in the draft resolution was to the fact that by 8 June 1967, the date of the draft resolution, Israeli forces were in the process of occupying the West Bank of the Jordan River. As the draft resolution was being debated, the US announced that it would abstain. It would take no stand on the issue on the rationale that it was more productive to work towards an overall reconciliation between Israel and

the neighbouring Arab States.9

The Soviet draft was put to a vote on 14 June 1967, separately on each of

the two paragraphs. On the paragraph condemning Israel for aggression, no State voted in the negative, but only Bulgaria, India, Mali, and the Soviet Union voted in favour. The other eleven States abstained. Under the UN Charter, nine affirmative votes out of the UN Security Council membership of fifteen were needed for a resolution to pass, so even though more States voted in favour than against, the first paragraph did not pass. The second paragraph suffered the same fate. Ethiopia and Nigeria joined Bulgaria, India, Mali, and the Soviet

Union in voting in favour, but the other nine States abstained.10 This was a remarkable outcome. No State voted against the Soviet draft, which meant that no State took the position that Israel was not the aggressor.

The UN Security Council’s failure to take a more definitive stance was

strange, not only because one State was occupying the territory of others, but because armistice agreements were in force that required all the States involved to stay within the territory they held when fighting ended in 1949. It was undisputed that Israeli troops were going past the armistice lines and were holding territory on the other side. The UN Charter required the UN Security Council to counter aggression and resolve breaches of the peace. Even if the members were not prepared to state a position on responsibility for starting the hostilities, a demand to withdraw to previously held positions would have been an obvious, if modest, approach.



8 Egypt from 1948 considered itself a belligerent occupant of the Gaza Strip. The merger of

Egypt with Syria did not affect the status of the Gaza Strip.

9 unsc Official Records (13 June 1967) UN Doc. s/ pv.1358, pages 9– 12.

10 unsc Official Records (14 June 1967) UN Doc. s/ pv.1360, pages 18– 19.

16 Quigley

2 No Support for Israel’s Justificatory Claims

A number of the States that abstained from voting on the Soviet draft gave explanations. Ethiopia said it agreed with both paragraphs of the Soviet draft but, like the United States, wanted language that would promote Middle East

peace more broadly.11 Hence, Ethiopia thought that Israel was the aggressor and that it should withdraw but it wanted the UN Security Council to do more than what was called for in the Soviet draft. Several UN Security Council mem-bers indicated that they thought the hostilities needed to be dealt with in the context of the Cold War. They did not want a split in the UN Security Council between the Soviet Union, which was in general hostile to Israel, and the US,

which was broadly protective of Israel.12 Japan said that the Soviet approach

of seeking a condemnation of Israel was divisive.13 Britain said that the UN

Security Council should not “intensify animosities” by condemning Israel.14 The British delegate took this approach despite the fact that British Foreign Secretary George Brown had just stated in Parliament that the right was on Egypt’s side. Asked by a Member of Parliament about the hostilities, Brown said that the Arab argument that Israel was the aggressor had “not only plau-

sibility but legality and force”.15 Denmark, in an obvious reference to the Cold War split, said that the UN Security Council had to “approach this problem in

the light of the political realities”.16 Canada did not “see what useful purpose

will be served by an attempt to force a vote” on the Soviet draft.17

The US said that a condemnation of Israel plus a call for troop withdrawal,

per the Soviet draft, would only “let everything go back to exactly where it was before the fighting began on 5 June”, leaving the opposing forces once again

“poised for combat”.18 It said:

Once again, opposing forces would stand in direct confrontation poised

for combat. Once again, there would be no international machinery to

keep them apart; once again, Aqaba would be blockaded against the free

and innocent passage of all maritime nations; once again nothing would

Israel’s Unlawful 1967 Invasion of Palestine 17

be done to resolve the deep- lying grievances on both sides that have fed

the fires of conflict in the Near East for twenty years.19

The US statement overlooked the obvious point that an occupation by Israel of more Arab territory would make it more, not less, difficult to secure a broader settlement. The statement was also in direct contradiction of the position the US took in 1956 when Israel invaded Egypt and was occupying Egyptian terri-tory. Then, the US led the charge in the UN Security Council to force Israel out. Under pressure from the US, Israel withdrew from Egyptian territory and from

the Gaza Strip in 1957.20

China was “opposed to the use of force, as is enjoined by the Charter of the

United Nations”.21 But China was impressed that Israeli representatives were promising that Israel did not plan to hold the territories permanently. China clearly saw Israel as the aggressor but was abstaining because Israel would not stay long- term. China referred to:

[T] he declarations that have been repeatedly made by Israel that it has no

territorial designs against its neighbours. Those declarations, we believe,

are solemn commitments, for history has clearly shown that no secure

peace can be built upon the basis of territorial changes that are brought

about by the use of force.22

France was more direct in pointing to Israel as the aggressor. It declared in the UN Security Council that “[w] e understand the concern which prompted the Soviet delegation to submit its draft resolution. We believe that conquest by force of arms cannot confer the right to occupy a territory. As a question of

principles, we cannot but support the Soviet proposal”.23 France explained that its decision to abstain on the vote stemmed from practical considerations. It said that “[e]ither the draft resolution will not be adopted, or, if it is, its chances

of being implemented are questionable”.24



19 Ibid., page 20.

20 Laurence Burd, ‘Israel Gets New Ike Plea; Cabinet in Jerusalem Delays Pull- out Order’,

Chicago Daily Tribune, 3 March 1957; ‘U.N. in Gaza; Israelis Out; Complete Pull- out, Set up

New Regime Today’, Chicago Daily Tribune, 7 March 1957.

18 Quigley

Egypt chastised the UN Security Council. “When an overt act of aggression is

committed”, it declared, “it is the primary responsibility of the Security Council to condemn that act and to take all necessary measures to restore international

peace”.25 Egypt said that the Council was failing to discharge its “primary duties in the maintenance of peace and security” because the fact of aggression was

clear.26 Mali feared that the UN Security Council’s inaction would set a prec-edent. It said that “[t] he impunity enjoyed by Israel after its aggression is an encouragement and a guarantee that the Council would apparently be ready to

grant to any future aggressor”.27 The tenor of the UN Security Council’s discus-sion was thus that Israel was the aggressor. Despite Israel’s claim that it acted lawfully, no Member State proposed a resolution to condemn the Arab States.



3 Israel’s Obfuscation

One other aspect of the debate on the Soviet draft resolution was that there was some uncertainty over the facts. Brazil said that “the evidence before us – that is, the reports of the Secretary- General – offer no grounds for a clear- cut

identification of the Power which struck first”.28 Argentina said that the factual information supplied by the UN Secretary- General and by the parties “provide us with a vague picture of the situation, some aspects of which are controver-

sial”.29 Nigeria said it was happy to vote for an Israeli withdrawal, as it did, but

wanted more time before taking a position on aggression.30

The reason for confusion over the facts was that the US, the only UN

Security Council member that had inside information on how the war started, was refusing to say what it knew. The US avoided the question of the initiation of the hostilities, even though it knew Israel to have struck first in a fashion

that constituted aggression.31 It was the only Member State that possessed that information. So, the entire debate presented a curious spectacle. The issue before the UN Security Council was how to address the hostilities, and the only

member that really knew was not talking.32 The US delegate kept silent when

25 unsc Official Records (13 June 1967) UN Doc. s/ pv.1358, page 31.

26 unsc Official Records (14 June 1967) UN Doc. s/ pv.1360, page 21.

27 unsc Official Records (13 June 1967) UN Doc. s/ pv.1359, page 6.

28 unsc Official Records (14 June 1967) UN Doc. s/ pv.1360, page 16.

29 Ibid., page 6.

31 Dean Rusk, As I Saw It (W.W. Norton 1990) 386– 387.

19, 118– 122.

Israel’s Unlawful 1967 Invasion of Palestine 19

other delegates said they could not figure out who was responsible for the

hostilities.33

The US was privy to the facts because it had been in intense confidential dis-

cussion with Israel in the weeks leading up to Israel’s invasion.34 That discus-sion was led by Israel’s Foreign Minister Abba Eban, who went to Washington to gain the support of the Administration of President Lyndon Johnson for an invasion. Israel was planning to invade Egypt, having assessed, quite correctly, that the Egyptian forces that had been drawn up near the frontier with Israel in the Sinai Peninsula were in a vulnerable posture, and could readily be over-run and annihilated. That situation opened an opportunity for Israel to deci-mate the army of its strongest Arab opponent. Israel’s success would prove the assessment to have been accurate.

Eban tried to get the Johnson Administration to believe that Egypt would

soon attack, therefore that the US should support Israel in attacking Egypt, or at least turn a blind eye. In Israel’s previous aggression against Egypt, in 1956,

the US had come down hard on Israel and had forced a withdrawal.35 Now Israel was poised to try again, and it did not want a reprise of the role that the US had assumed in 1956.

The US was on Israel’s side in its conflict with the Arab world, so Eban

was seeking help from a sympathetic source. The shift from a Kennedy Administration to a Johnson Administration had moved the US firmly into Israel’s corner. At the same time, the US did not favour an Israeli invasion of Egypt. It feared that the Soviet Union might intervene militarily on the Arab side, putting the US in the awkward position of having to save Israel, and risk starting a superpower military confrontation.

The Johnson Administration was unreceptive to Eban’s entreaties. It did

not think Egypt would invade Israel. The more insistent Eban became that Israel was in imminent danger, the more insistent Johnson and the US State Department became in their response that Egypt was not going to invade. They confronted Eban with assessments by the Central Intelligence Agency, which was monitoring the situation intensively. The cia said that Israel enjoyed supe-riority over Egypt in its air war capacity, which would be crucial in the event of hostilities. Israel’s ground forces, the cia assessed, were able to “maintain



33 unsc Official Records (14 June 1967) UN Doc. s/ pv.1360, pages 6 and 16 (Brazil and

Argentina).

34 Memorandum of Conversation, Washington, 26 May 1967, 10:30 a.m., frus 1964– 1968, vol

35 ‘Eisenhower Sends New Appeal to Ben- Gurion: Urges Israel Premier to Order Troops to

internal security, defend successfully against simultaneous Arab attacks on all fronts, launch limited attacks simultaneously on all fronts, or hold on any three

fronts while mounting successfully a major offensive on the fourth”.36 The cia said that Egypt was not poised to attack Israel. The aim of the Egyptian troop build- up in the Sinai Peninsula near the frontier with Israel, the cia said, was that the Egyptians were “embarrassed because they had not helped the Syrians in April, then made a big show of marching into Sinai, partly to show good

faith, partly in hopes of deterring the Israelis”.37

Here the cia was referencing an armed confrontation in April 1967, only

one month earlier, in which Israel and Syria had exchanged fire. The cia’s point was that the Egyptian troop build- up was a reaction to that episode. Egypt, as the strongest among the Arab States, was providing a show of force to deter Israel from attacking Syria. Syria and Egypt, as previously noted above, were united politically as the United Arab Republic.

At a 26 May meeting in Washington, Eban told US Secretary of Defence

Robert McNamara that “the Israeli Cabinet had met just before his trip and the decision was made to fight” but that Israel “had delayed thus far in striking

because of President Johnson’s urgings”.38 McNamara replied “that an Israeli attack under present circumstances would have most serious consequences” and that the US “cannot undertake to support Israel if Israel launches an

attack”.39 Eban insisted that the Government of Israel knew “that a uar- Syrian

attack was imminent”.40 McNamara replied that according to US intelligence, “the Egyptian deployments were defensive in character and anticipatory of a

possible Israeli attack”.41 General Earle Wheeler, Chair of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, asked Eban if he meant “that Israel knew with certainty the Egyptians’ intent, for example through an agent”, or on the basis of the Egyptian army’s “troop disposition”. According to the meeting minutes, Eban added no

information.42

President Johnson told Eban that “our best judgment is that no military

attack on Israel is imminent”.43 He told Eban that “Israel must not make itself responsible for initiating hostilities”. According to the meeting minutes “[w] ith emphasis and solemnity, the President repeated twice, Israel will not be alone

unless it decides to go it alone”.44 When Eban insisted that Egypt was about to attack, McNamara “explained to Eban that three separate intelligence groups had looked into the matter in the last twenty- four hours” and that the Egyptian

troop deployment was defensive.45 President Johnson repeated, “that an attack

is not imminent”.46

President Johnson’s insistence that Israel should not initiate hostilities in a

manner that would make it responsible led Israel to try another tack. It asked

for US acquiescence in a scheme to draw Egypt into initiating hostilities.47 The Israel Defence Forces was already well advanced in a plan to send a ship into the Gulf of Aqaba, hopefully drawing fire from Egyptian shore batteries at the Straits of Tiran, whereupon Israel would launch its invasion of Egypt. The ship was fully outfitted in a port on the East African coast, awaiting an order to sail into the Straits of Tiran. Ephraim Evron, Israel’s Minister at its embassy in Washington, approached Walt W. Rostow, a presidential advisor, and asked if this scenario would satisfy Washington’s injunction that Israel not fire the

first shot.48 Evron explained it as a “probe at the gulf of Aqaba” that “would

be made by an Israeli ship. The first shot would be fired by the uar”.49 When that occurred, the idf “would attack the installations at Sharm al- Sheikh cov-

ering the straits of Aqaba. The next move would be Nasser’s”.50 Walt W. Rostow explained the remainder of the anticipated scenario: “[t] he Israelis believe he [Nasser] would attack Israel on a wide front and probably other Arab nations

would join the attack”.51 At that point, Israel would launch a major attack of its own.



43 Memorandum of Conversation, Washington, 26 May 1967, 7:15 p.m., frus 1964– 1968, vol

19, page 142.

44 Ibid., page 143.

45 Ibid., page 145.

46 Ibid.

47 Michael Bar- Zohar, Histoire Secrète de la Guerre d’Israël (Fayard 1968) 182.

49 Ibid.

50 Ibid.

51 Memorandum from the President’s Special Assistant (Rostow) to President Johnson, 2

22 Quigley

4 Invasion with Embellishments

The Government of Israel did not wait for an answer. It sent another emis-sary to Washington, one who made no effort to comply with the US demands. Meir Amit, who headed Israel’s espionage agency Mossad, put the matter more bluntly to Secretary of Defence McNamara. McNamara explained the next day to British officials what Amit had told him. The Israelis “would try to destroy the Egyptian airforce first and thus gain ability for a tank strike to take Sinai

and the Straits”.52 He said that “the Israelis think they can win in 3– 4 days”.53

Amit’s task had been to find out what the US would do once Israel invaded

Egypt. Amit sought an assurance that Israel would not face a reprise of President Dwight Eisenhower’s reaction to Israel’s 1956 invasion into Sinai. The Israeli leadership made its decision to invade in a 3 June meeting. Amit, attending the meeting, reported that his contact with US officials in Washington left him con-fident that the US would not expose Israel as the aggressor. Amit related some years later that at this 3 June meeting, the Johnson Administration, despite its

protestations, would not be unhappy if Israel invaded Egypt.54

At a meeting of Israel’s Ministerial Committee on Security on 4 June, held

to make final plans, Prime Minister Levi Eshkol expressed concern that Israel

might be denounced for aggression.55 The Cabinet then met and nonetheless

authorised the invasion.56 The authorisation document, whose text was made public in 1972, recited that “the Government ascertained that the armies of Egypt, Syria and Jordan are deployed for immediate multi- front aggression,

threatening the very existence of the state”.57 However, since Cabinet members knew that not to be the case, some of them conferred to devise a story to give

to the UN Security Council.58

When the invasion was launched the next morning, Prime Minister Eshkol

sent a letter to President Johnson claiming that Israel had acted in response to shelling by Egypt of three Israeli settlements near the Gaza Strip in the



52 Memorandum of Conversation, 2 June 1967, frus 1964– 1968, vol 19, page 237.

53 Ibid.

54 See statement of Meir Amit. Richard B. Parker, The Six Day War: A Retrospective (University

Press of Florida 1996) 144.

55 Ami Gluska, The Israeli Military and the Origins of the 1967 War: Government, Armed Forces

and Defence Policy 1963– 1967 (Routledge 2007) 253.

56 Ibid., page 255.

57 Asher Wallfish, ‘Meir Reveals Text of Cabinet Decision’, Jerusalem Post, 5 June 1972.

58 Tom Segev, 1967: Israel, the War, and the Year That Transformed the Middle East (Henry

Holt 2007) 336.

Israel’s Unlawful 1967 Invasion of Palestine 23

early morning hours of 5 June.59 At the White House, Eshkol’s message landed on the desk of President Johnson’s Special Assistant Walt W. Rostow. Rostow added his own commentary when he passed the communication on to Johnson. Rostow explained that Eshkol “builds his case mainly on the gen-eral environment, but refers to bombardment of three Israel towns as the trig-

ger”.60 As for the bombardment of three towns, Rostow added that “[a] t least

that’s his story”.61 The White House foreign policy team quickly concluded that

Eshkol’s claim was fabricated and that Israel had simply attacked on its own.62 Once it became clear, as it quickly did, that the Soviet Union was not inter-vening on the Arab side, the Johnson advisors breathed a collective sigh of

relief and slipped into a mode of supporting Israel at the diplomatic level.63

At the UN Security Council, Eban responded to Egypt’s charge of aggression.

He related a series of actions by Egypt that he depicted as hostile and as presag-ing further Egyptian action. Egypt had asked the UN to remove a peacekeeping force that was stationed on Egypt’s side of the border with Israel. Egypt had announced restrictions against Israeli shipping through the Straits of Tiran into the Gulf of Aqaba. Egypt had drawn a military force up to the frontier with Israel. But all this was context. Understanding that none of this would suffice to protect Israel from a charge of aggression, Eban repeated Eshkol’s claim of early morning shelling on 5 June by Egypt of three Israeli settlements. Eban added another detail: in the early morning hours of 5 June “approaching

Egyptian aircraft appeared on our radar screens”.64 In fact, Egypt’s fighter air-

craft were all on the ground near Cairo.65 The claim about a shelling of three settlements, though difficult to put forward credibly in light of the massive nature of Israel’s assault that same morning, could not immediately be dis-proved, and Israel’s main concern was that the UN might try to stop it before it had degraded the Egyptian army and gained control of the Arab territory into which it was moving.



59 Walworth Barbour, Telegram from the Embassy in Israel to the Department of State, 5 June

1967, frus 1964– 1968, vol 19, page 302 (transmitting letter of Ben- Gurion to Johnson).

60 Ibid.

24 Quigley

5 UN General Assembly Refuses to Back Israel

In light of the UN Security Council’s inaction, an emergency special session of the UN General Assembly was called. Such a session had been called in 1956 after a similar failure of UN Security Council action. This time, a call for withdrawal was not achieved. Nonetheless, the UN General Assembly session allowed the broader membership to hear Israel’s claims and to state their views on legal responsibility for the hostilities.

Eban reminded the UN General Assembly of what he had told the UN

Security Council about Egyptian aircraft and the shelling of settlements as pre-cipitating events to Israel’s invasion. “On the fateful morning of 5 June”, Eban said, “when Egyptian forces moved by air and land against Israel’s western coast and southern territory, our country’s choice was plain. The choice was to

live or perish, to defend the national existence or to forfeit it for all time”.66 As in the UN Security Council, no State indicated that it accepted Eban’s factual claims. Cyprus expressly rejected them, saying that it found “no evidence of

Arab armed attack or invasion of the territory of Israel”.67 Spain said that the timing belied Eban’s claims. “We have only to consider the deployment and movement of the combat troops of the attacking army”, it said, “and the mas-sive surprise bombing technique, to arrive at a strong moral conviction as to

who unleashed the hostilities”.68

Several States addressed anticipatory self- defence as a potential Israeli jus-

tification but rejected it as a matter of law. Malaysia said that a “[p] re- emptive strike is not recognized by the Charter; for otherwise, the excuses for resort-ing to war would soon multiply and the very purpose of the Charter in the

maintenance of peace would be nullified”.69 “Even if we accept, for the sake of argument”, said Zambia, “that there was a threat to the State of Israel the right course would have been for Israel to bring its complaint to the United

Nations”.70 A threat, Zambia said, does not constitute an armed attack under

Article 51 of the UN Charter.71



66 unga Official Records (19 June 1967) UN Doc. a/ pv.1526, page 12.

67 unga Official Records (29 June 1967) UN Doc. a/ pv.1541, page 7– 8.

68 unga Official Records (28 June 1967) UN Doc. a/ pv.1539, page 9.

69 unga Official Records (29 June 1967) UN Doc. a/ pv.1541, page 7.

70 unga Official Records (27 June 1967) UN Doc. a/ pv.1538, page 9.

71 Ibid.

Israel’s Unlawful 1967 Invasion of Palestine 25

6 Occupation of the Gaza Strip and the West Bank

Cyprus said at the UN General Assembly session that Israel’s occupation of Arab territory was the result of aggression. “The picture that unfolded before the eyes of the world”, it said, “clearly presented both the acts and the effects of aggression: co- ordinated armed attack by air and land, invasion and occupa-

tion of the territories of three Arab States”.72

The illegal occupation of the parts of Palestine that had escaped Israel’s

reach in 1948 would be the longest- lasting effect of the 1967 invasion. Israel’s aggression against Egypt carried over to the Palestine territory it took and occupied. As of 1967, the Gaza Strip was Palestine territory under control by Egypt. The West Bank area was under the control of Jordan, which came to Egypt’s defence. As a result, Jordan was acting in what is called collective self- defence. A State that assists a State that is victim of aggression is a partici-pant in the defence of the victim State. That right exists under the UN Charter, which provides in its provision on self- defence that “[n] othing in the present Charter shall impair the inherent right of individual or collective self- defence if an armed attack occurs against a Member of the United Nations until the Security Council has taken measures necessary to maintain international

peace and security”.73

Jordan and Egypt, as it happened, had just concluded a mutual defence

treaty that referenced that right, reciting:

The two contracting powers consider any armed attack on either state

or its forces an attack on both. Consequently, acting on the legitimate

right of individual and collective self- defense, they commit themselves to

hasten each to the assistance of the attacked state and immediately take

all measures and employ all means at their disposal, including the use of

armed forces, to repulse the attack.74

The existence of such a treaty is not, however, a prerequisite to acting in defence of a State that has been attacked. Israel claimed that it advised Jordan that if Jordan refrained from participating in the hostilities, Israel would not



72 unga Official Records (29 June 1967) UN Doc. a/ pv.1541, pages 7– 8.

73 UN Charter, Article 51.

74 See Article 1 of the agreement. ‘Text of the Egyptian- Jordanian Mutual Defense Treaty

Signed in Cairo’, New York Times, 31 May 1967.

26 Quigley

move against it.75 Whether such a communication was issued was never clar-ified, but it would not in any event alter the legal situation between Israel and Jordan. The rules on use of force, what is called jus ad bellum, cannot be avoided by advising a State to refrain. Moreover, even if Israel was justified in responding to Jordan’s use of force, its own use of force would be limited to countering Jordan’s use of force. Jordan quickly stopped military action against Israel.



7 Israel’s Motivation for Aggression

Israel had its own reasons for taking the Gaza Strip and West Bank. The Zionist conception of a Jewish State was for one that would extend eastward at least to the Jordan River. The statehood declaration issued in 1948 spoke ambigu-

ously of Eretz- Israel (Land of Israel).76 As that declaration was being drafted, the question arose of whether borders should be declared. David Ben- Gurion opposed the idea, not wanting to set limits. But the drafters also wanted to rely on the UN General Assembly resolution of 29 November 1947, which had

proposed an Arab and a Jewish State and had drawn borders.77 To do so might confine Israel to those borders. As of May 1948, Zionist militias were already taking territory beyond those borders. The solution was to state that the dec-laration was issued “on the strength of” the UN General Assembly resolution, but without mentioning the resolution’s projected borders. The drafters also wanted President Harry Truman’s blessing for their declaration, and Truman made clear to the Jewish Agency, represented in Washington by Eliahu Epstein, that he wanted to know just what it was he was being asked to recognise in

terms of territory.78 So Epstein told Truman, who had not seen the text of the statehood declaration, that Israel was being proclaimed “within frontiers approved by the General Assembly of the United Nations in its Resolution of

November 29, 1947”.79



75 UN Secretary- General, Measures Taken by Israel to Change the Status of the City of

Jerusalem, A/ 6753, S/ 8052, 10 July 1967 (reproducing letter of 10 July 1967 from Abba Eban,

Foreign Minister of Israel).

76 The Declaration of the Establishment of the State of Israel, 14 May 1948.

77 Ibid.

78 Memorandum of Conversations by the Under Secretary of State (Lovett), Washington, 17

May 1948, frus 1948, vol 5, page 1005.

79 The Agent of the Provisional Government of Israel (Epstein) to President Truman,

Washington, 14 May 1948, frus 1948, vol 5, page 989.

Israel’s Unlawful 1967 Invasion of Palestine 27

Later in 1948, the Israel Defence Forces went well into territory designated in

the UN General Assembly resolution for an Arab State, in Galilee and in south-ern Palestine. The UN Security Council saw what the idf was doing. The UN Security Council adopted resolution 56 in August 1948, which by its terms was aimed at all parties, but by its intent was aimed at the Zionist militias, since they were the ones that were advancing. That resolution stipulated that “[n] o party is

entitled to gain military or political advantage through violation of the truce”.80

In a November 1948 resolution, the UN Security Council directed all parties

“to withdraw those of their forces which have advanced beyond the positions

held on 14 October”.81 In December 1948, Britain charged that the “southward movement” of the militias was “flagrantly contrary” to the November resolu-

tion.82 Pressure from Britain and the US kept the militias from taking the rest of Palestine. In 1956, when the Israeli Defence Forces occupied the Gaza Strip,

Foreign Minister Golda Meir claimed it as an “integral part” of Israel.83 The idf

departed, however, again under pressure from the US.84

Israel’s seizure of the Gaza Strip and the West Bank in 1967 was thus part of a

long- term effort to hold all of Palestine. Earlier in the 1960s, the idf command began serious planning for an occupation of the Gaza Strip and the West Bank. Another war was regarded as only a matter of time. Regardless of where a war might start, the idf would take hostilities into Arab- held territory. In 1963, the idf General Staff Branch, headed by Yitzhak Rabin, drew up a directive titled “Organization Order: Military Government in State of Emergency”, providing detail on an administrative structure for an anticipated belligerent occupation of territory that included the West Bank and Gaza Strip. Belligerent occupation was correctly understood by the idf planners as a temporary situation. The Organization Order went one step further, however, to suggest that an effort would be made to annex the occupied territory. “A convenient political situ-ation might develop”, it read, “which will make it possible to retain occupied

territory indefinitely”.85 Annexation of territory under belligerent occupation

is an act that constitutes the crime of aggression.86



80 unsc Resolution 56 (19 August 1948).

81 unsc Resolution 1961 (1 November 1948).

82 unsc Official Records (15 December 1948) UN Doc. s/ pv.384, 16.

83 Homer Bigart, ‘Israel Terms Gaza Strip an Integral Part of Nation’, New York Times, 11

November 1956.

84 ‘U.N. in Gaza; Israelis Out; Complete Pull- out, Set up New Regime Today’, Chicago Daily

Tribune, 7 March 1957.

85 Adam Raz, ‘Israel Claimed its 1967 Land Conquests Weren’t Planned. Declassified

Documents Reveal Otherwise’, Haaretz, 3 June 2021.

86 Rome Statute, Article 8bis(2)(a).

28 Quigley

The idf’s planning was quite specific. It was based on the brief period of

military rule in the Gaza Strip between 1956 and 1957 and the more extensive period of military rule over Arab- populated parts of Israel between 1948 and 1966. Three command posts were envisaged, one for Gaza Strip/ Sinai, one

for the West Bank, and one for the Syrian Golan.87 Named individuals were

“appointed” to the major administrative posts.88 Training courses were run for

legal staff.89 An instruction book was produced that explained how to manage schools in the occupied territories, by “weeding out” teachers or administrators who were hostile, and as well how to encourage and reward teachers or admin-

istrators who “collaborate”.90



8 A Tale of Changing Stories

The afterlife of Israel’s Foreign Minister Abba Eban’s false story is curious. Once Israel securely held the parts of Palestine that had eluded it in 1948, and once it was clear that Israel had no proof of the supposed Egyptian shelling, or an approach by Egyptian aircraft, the story could not be sustained. Prime Minister Eshkol, when asked about the hostilities one month later, did not claim an attack by Egypt but premised Israel’s initiation of the hostilities on Egypt’s

restrictions on Israeli shipping into the Gulf of Aqaba.91

The story on which Israeli officialdom ultimately settled was the one that

Eban had unsuccessfully pressed on the White House, namely, that Egypt was on the verge of invading Israel. This story had the advantage that it was impossible to disprove one hundred percent. The downside of the story was the infirmity of anticipatory self- defence as a matter of law and the fact that so many Israeli offi-cials knew the story to be false. Within a few years, one official after another said that Egypt had not been on the verge of attacking. Yitzhak Rabin, Chief of Staff at the time of the invasion, told a reporter that the entire Israeli leadership knew

that Egypt was not going to attack.92 Menachem Begin, who was a member of



87 Shlomo Gazit, The Carrot and the Stick: Israel’s Policy in Judaea and Samaria, 1967– 68

(B’nai B’rith Books 1995) 17.

88 Ibid., page 8.

89 Ibid., page 11.

90 Ibid., page 14; Ilan Pappé, The Biggest Prison on Earth: A History of the Occupied Territories

(OneWorld 2017) xvi.

91 Ariyeh Tzimuki, ‘We will let the Vatican Have Some Control Over the Holy Places in

Jerusalem’, Yediot Aharonot, 7 July 1967.

92 Eric Rouleau, ‘Le Général Rabin Ne Pense Pas Que Nasser Voulait La Guerre’, Le Monde, 29

February 1968.

Israel’s Unlawful 1967 Invasion of Palestine 29

the Israeli Cabinet in 1967, stated openly that Israel had not been under threat

of an invasion by Egypt.93 Mordecai Bentov, another Cabinet member, said that

the story about an anticipated Egyptian attack was “invented”.94

In the aftermath of the 1967 war, the UN Security Council deferred to the

US. It avoided directly addressing the jus ad bellum issue, thereby avoiding the question of who was at fault. UN Security Council resolution 242, adopted in November 1967, was its only statement on the hostilities. That resolution did refer to “the inadmissibility of the acquisition of territory by war”, but that ref-

erence was to annexation.95 “Acquisition” in regard to territory refers to sover-

eignty, not to occupation in warfare.96

Resolution 242 did call on Israel to withdraw but without addressing respon-

sibility for the initiation of the 1967 hostilities. Even that call to withdraw was less than clear because of its inclusion in a paragraph that also issued a call to Arab States to make peace with Israel. Resolution 242 stated that the UN Security Council:

1. Affirms that the fulfilment of Charter principles requires the estab-

lishment of a just and lasting peace in the Middle East which should include the application of both the following principles:

(i) Withdrawal of Israel armed forces from territories occupied in

the recent conflict;

(ii) Termination of all claims or states of belligerency and respect

for and acknowledgment of the sovereignty, territorial integrity and political independence of every State in the area and their right to live in peace within secure and recognized boundaries free from threats or acts of force.

Like the UN Security Council, the UN General Assembly failed to condemn Israel as the aggressor. At the UN General Assembly’s emergency special ses-sion on the 1967 war, held in late June 1967, the Soviet Union again proposed

a condemnation of Israel and again it failed to carry.97 In neither body was

93 Israel Ministry of Foreign Affairs, ‘Address by Prime Minister Begin at the National

Defense College’, 8 August 1982; ‘Excerpts from Begin Speech at National Defense College’,

New York Times, 21 August 1982.

94 Mordecai Bentov, ‘For Whom and Why Are the Settlements Necessary?’, Al- Hamishmar, 14

Press 1963) 2. Explaining “acquisition” as a term that relates to sovereignty over territory.
