30 Quigley

any resolution proposed to condemn Egypt. While many Member States found Israel at fault for initiating the hostilities, none – apart from Israel – suggested

that Egypt or Jordan was at fault.98 Nonetheless, the failure of either body to condemn Israel left the door open to Israel to portray itself as having acted defensively. That false narrative about the 1967 war is used by Israel to justify its continued control of Palestine. After the Israel- Palestine Camp David nego-tiations in 2000, Israeli Prime Minister Ehud Barak attributed Israel’s success in avoiding territorial concessions, at least in part, to a public perception that Israel acted defensively in 1967. He said, that, “although we were the ones to fire the first shot, the world saw us as trying to free ourselves of strangulation

by our neighbors”.99 In a recapitulation of the Camp David talks, Barak wrote, “Israel occupied the West Bank and Gaza after it was attacked … [a] s I told Clinton and Arafat many times, both before and at Camp David, we will never

apologize for our victory in 1967”.100



9 Conclusion

The magnitude of Israel’s depredations as belligerent occupant over the dura-tion of its occupation of the Palestine territory that it captured in 1967 yields a compelling argument that the occupation must end. When depredations attain a certain level, it may be that the only measure to remedy them is to terminate the occupation entirely. However, the illegality of Israel’s occupation rests in the first instance on the jus ad bellum violation that brought it into occupation. As a result of that violation, the occupation became unlawful the day it began. Despite its reticence at that time, the United Nations should re- visit the jus ad bellum issue. In the immediate aftermath of the 1967 war, Israel, with the complicity of the United States, was able to deflect attention from its aggression. Today, the facts showing Israel’s aggression would be difficult to contest. A reasoned confirmation of Israel’s 1967 aggression by the UN would serve the cause of peace in the Middle East.



98 John Quigley, The Six- Day War and Israeli Self- Defense: Questioning the Legal Basis for

Preventive War (cup 2013) 104– 108.

99 Ehud Barak, ‘The Myths Spread About Camp David Are Baseless’ in Shimon Shamir and

Bruce Maddy- Weitzman (eds), The Camp David Summit – What Went Wrong?: Americans,

Palestinian- Israeli Conflict (Academic Press 2005) 117, 130.

chapter 2


Out of Time: On the (Il)legality of Israel’s Prolonged

Occupation of the West Bank

Vito Todeschini



Israel’s occupation of the Palestinian territory has lasted for 55 years at the time of writing. While its duration has earned it the label of “prolonged” occu-pation, the question the present chapter will explore is whether its length ren-ders the occupation itself illegal. Under the legal frameworks that specifically regulate a situation of occupation – international humanitarian law and the law on the use of force – an occupation must be temporary. Each framework, however, attaches different value to the question of duration. International humanitarian law does not set an end date on belligerent occupation. It is rather concerned with ensuring that the protections it affords, which aim to safeguard the rights and well- being of the local population, remain applicable until the Occupying Power stops exercising effective control over the occu-

pied territory.1 On the other hand, the law on the use of force, also referred to as jus ad bellum, requires an occupation to end as soon as the circumstances justifying its establishment cease to exist. With specific reference to the law of self- defence, this will occur when an armed attack is repelled or otherwise

terminated.2

Against this background, the present chapter will consider to what extent

time is a factor that impacts the legal status of an occupation. Accordingly, it will look at time through the prisms of international humanitarian law, partic-ularly the law of occupation, and jus ad bellum. In so doing, it will posit that the infringement of the principle of temporariness makes an occupation illegal under both bodies of law.

Viewing Israel’s prolonged occupation of the West Bank as illegal, in

turn, may encourage the international community to adjust and shift their approaches and policies vis- à- vis the Palestinian territory. In this sense, fram-ing the occupation in terms of illegality allows to abstract from the single – albeit systematic – violations of specific international norms committed by



1 See Section 1.

2 See Section 2.



© Vito Todeschini, 2023 | DOI:10.1163/9789004503

32 Todeschini

Israel, and to consider the overall picture. This may then allow for a stronger case in calling on Third States to fulfil their obligations under international law to endeavour to bring Israel’s occupation of the Palestinian territory to an end.

This chapter will only address Israel’s occupation of the West Bank, includ-

ing East Jerusalem. The delimitation of the analysis’ scope3 is related to the specific focus the present chapter adopts when looking at the illegality of bel-ligerent occupation. In other words, the Occupying Power’s actual adminis-tration of the occupied territory. Regarding the Gaza Strip, Israel continues to exercise effective control over it, to the effect that it remains an occupied terri-

tory under international humanitarian law.4



1 Duration of Occupation under International Humanitarian Law

Article 42 of the Hague Regulations provides that “[t] erritory is considered occupied when it is actually placed under the authority of the hostile army. The occupation extends only to the territory where such authority has been estab-

lished and can be exercised”.5 A belligerent occupation has been further under-

stood to be established when a territory is placed under the effective control6



3 The results of the present analysis may be applied to the Golan Heights as well, given that

Israel has formally annexed this territory. However, the Golan Heights belongs to Syria

and is not part of the Palestinian territory, which is the focus of this chapter.

4 Since the 2005 “disengagement plan” and the withdrawal of its military and civilian pres-

ence from the Gaza Strip, Israel no longer administers this territory in the same terms it

does in the West Bank. While the Gaza Strip will accordingly not be part of the focus of

the present analysis, this should in no way lead to the conclusion that Israel’s belligerent

occupation of the Gaza Strip cannot be deemed illegal; such an assessment simply falls

outside the scope of this examination. Also, this should not be read as a conceptual com-

partmentalisation of the various areas of the Palestinian territory, which is comprised

of the West Bank, including East Jerusalem, and the Gaza Strip, and the unity of which

must be preserved. See for example unhrc Resolution 40/ 23 (22 March 2019); unhrc,

Report of the Detailed Findings of the Independent International Commission of Inquiry on

the Protests in the Occupied Palestinian Territory, a/ hrc/ 40/ crp.2, 18 March 2019, para 67;

Shane Darcy and John Reynolds, ‘An Enduring Occupation: The Status of the Gaza Strip

from the Perspective of International Humanitarian Law’, 15(2) Journal of Conflict and

Security Law (2010) 211.

Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory (Advisory

6 It has been pointed out that certain States prefer to employ the term “authority”. See for

example Marco Longobardo, The Use of Armed Force in Occupied Territory (cup 2018) 35. On the (Il)legality of Israel’s Occupation of the West Bank 33

of the armed forces of a foreign State.7 Whether or not effective control is established over a given territory determines the beginning and end of an occupation, and consequently the applicability of international humanitarian

law, in particular the law of occupation – the Hague Regulations,8 the Fourth

Geneva Convention,9 Additional Protocol i,10 and related customary interna-

tional law.11 Under international humanitarian law, situations of occupation formally classify as international armed conflicts, in other words armed con-

frontation between two or more States.12 In addition to international humani-

tarian law, human rights law remains applicable in situations of occupation.13 Accordingly, an Occupying Power must ensure to the members of the local

population the range of human rights it is bound by under international law.14

The International Court of Justice,15 the UN Security Council,16 and the

Israeli Supreme Court17 have all characterised the West Bank as occupied ter-ritory in which the Hague Regulations and the Fourth Geneva Convention

34 Todeschini

apply.18 However, Israel defines the West Bank as “contested territory” due to the purported lack of a former sovereign power before 1967, when it took control thereof. Accordingly, Israel rejects the de jure applicability of the law of occupation and claims to apply de facto the “humanitarian provisions” of

the Fourth Geneva Convention.19 This position has been widely rebutted.20

Additionally, notwithstanding Israel’s continued opposition,21 the icj and var-ious UN human rights bodies have acknowledged that the full range of Israel’s obligations under human rights treaties continue to apply to its conduct in the

West Bank.22

In legal terms, belligerent occupation must always be temporary.23 This

means that an occupation cannot become permanent, result in annexation of the occupied territory, or cause unilateral changes of its legal and political

status by the Occupying Power.24 Consequently, an Occupying Power has the obligation to preserve, as far as possible, the status quo in the occupied terri-

tory.25 The temporary character of belligerent occupation is reflected in the

terms and spirit of the applicable law. Article 43 of the Hague Regulations26

On the (Il)legality of Israel’s Occupation of the West Bank 35

and Article 64 of the Fourth Geneva Convention27 strictly limit the legislative competence of an Occupying Power to those acts that are necessary to restore

and maintain public order and civil life,28 ensure the well- being of the local

population,29 and guarantee its own security. In this sense, the Occupying Power must act as a de facto temporary “administrator and usufructuary” of the

occupied territory,30 and it must exercise its authority in the best interest of

the local population in a way that resembles a trust or fiduciary relationship.31

While prescribing that belligerent occupation must be temporary, interna-

tional humanitarian law does not set any specific time limit on its duration. The Fourth Geneva Convention establishes that “[i] n the case of occupied territory, the application of the present Convention shall cease one year after the gen-eral close of military operations”, save for a number of provisions that continue

to apply “for the duration of the occupation”.32 In turn, Additional Protocol i provides that its application ceases “in the case of occupied territories, on the

termination of the occupation”.33 The missing end date of the applicability of

27 Article 64 of the Fourth Geneva Convention provides that local laws can be repealed

only when they pose a threat to the Occupying Power’s security or if they constitute an

obstacle to the application of the Convention itself. The same provision prescribes that

new legislation may be enacted only when “essential to enable the Occupying Power to

fulfil its obligations under the present Convention, to maintain the orderly government

of the territory, and to ensure the security of the Occupying Power”. While the text refers

to penal laws only, it is acknowledged that the provision covers other laws as well. See

for example Iain Scobbie, ‘International Law and the Prolonged Occupation of Palestine’,

United Nations Roundtable on Legal Aspects of the Question of Palestine (2015) page 13.

28 While the English version of Article 43 of the Hague Regulations refers to “public order

and safety”, the French text refers to “l’ordre et la vie publique” (public order and civil

life). This second formulation is considered to be more accurate. See Orna Ben- Naftali,

Aeyal Gross and Keren Michaeli, ‘Illegal Occupation: Framing the Occupied Palestinian

Territory’, 23(3) Berkeley Journal of International Law (2005) page 563, footnote 54.

29 Among other things, the Occupying Power must ensure the provision of food and medi-

cal supplies to the civilian population, as well as guarantee public health. Moreover, pri-

unless military operations render it absolutely necessary. See Hague Regulations, Article

33 Additional Protocol i, Article 3(b). While Additional Protocol i has formally superseded

non- States Parties to Additional Protocol i “have expressed their intention of seeing all

ihl rules applied until the end of an occupation”. See Vaios Koutroulis, ‘The Application 36 Todeschini

the law of occupation relates to the very nature of international humanitar-ian law as a legal framework designed to govern the conduct of the parties to a conflict without imposing conditions on when a conflict should begin and

end. Those dates, it is argued, are governed by the jus ad bellum.34

As long as an occupation lasts, the goal of international humanitarian law

is to regulate and restrain the authority of the Occupying Power, safeguard the occupied territory, and protect its population. Hence, in principle, interna-tional humanitarian law, while prohibiting annexation of occupied territory, does not expressly forbid an occupation to be prolonged. While it is not pos-sible to set a specific time lapse to define such a situation, one possibility is to consider as prolonged any belligerent occupation that “extends into a period when hostilities are sharply reduced – i.e., a period at least approximating

peacetime”.35 As Vaios Koutroulis noted, however, prolonged occupation is neither a distinct legal category nor a situation governed by a separate legal regime. It is a descriptive term used to characterise belligerent occupations lasting for a protracted period of time, which remain fully subject to the law of

occupation.36

Israel’s High Court of Justice has employed the indeterminacy of the end

date of belligerent occupation, and the related concept of prolonged occupa-tion, to expand Israel’s legislative and governmental authority over the West

Bank.37 The hcj has several times recognised that, although belligerent occu-pation is temporary in nature, it may be “long term” and, in absence of limits thereto established by international humanitarian law, it may last as long as



of International Humanitarian Law and International Human Rights Law in Situation of

Prolonged Occupation: Only a Matter of Time?’, 94(885) International Review of the Red

Cross (2012) 165, pages 173‒174.

34 See below section 2.

35 Adam Roberts, ‘Prolonged Military Occupation: The Israeli- Occupied Territories Since

1967’, 84(1) American Journal of International Law (1990) 44, page 47, who defines a

prolonged occupation as “an occupation that lasts more than 5 years”. See also unsc

Resolution 471 (5 June 1980); unsc Resolution 476 (30 June 1980). Both resolutions

referred to Israel’s occupation as “prolonged” 13 years after its beginning.

36 Vaios Koutroulis, ‘The Application of International Humanitarian Law and International

Human Rights Law in Situation of Prolonged Occupation: Only a Matter of Time?’, 94(885)

‘International Law and the Prolonged Occupation of Palestine’, United Nations Roundtable

on Legal Aspects of the Question of Palestine (2015) pages 19‒32; David Kretzmer and Yaël

Ronen, The Occupation of Justice: The Supreme Court of Israel and the Occupied Territories

(oup 2021).

On the (Il)legality of Israel’s Occupation of the West Bank 37

the Occupying Power holds the occupied territory under its effective control.38 In this perspective, in the Abu ‘Aita case the hcj has affirmed that:

The needs of any area, whether under military government or otherwise,

will naturally change over the course of time, along with attendant eco-

nomic developments. […] The length of time that a military government

continues may affect the nature of the needs involved, and the urgency

to effect adjustment and reorganization may increase as more and more

time elapses. […] In the legal interpretation of Article 43, the relation-

ship between the time element, and the form taken by the provisions of

Article 43 is stressed more than once. It follows that the time element is

a factor affecting the scope of the powers, whether we regard military

needs, or whether we regard the needs of the territory, or maintain equi-

librium between them.39

According to the hcj, the time factor allows to expand, rather than limit, the authority of the Occupying Power under Article 43 of the Hague Regulations. In the Yesh Din case, the hcj has further stated that:

As has been held in many occasions under our rulings, the belligerent

occupation of Israel in the Area [the West Bank] has some unique char-

acteristics, primarily the duration of the occupation period that requires

the adjustment of the law to the reality on the ground, which imposes a

duty upon Israel to ensure normal life for a period, which even if deemed

temporary from a legal perspective, is certainly long- term. Therefore, the

traditional occupation laws require adjustment to the prolonged dura-

tion of the occupation, to the continuity of normal life in the Area and

to the sustainability of economic relations between the two authorities –

the occupier and the occupied.40

The hcj has built on this argument to rule that Israel’s exploitation of quarries in the West Bank is in line with Articles 43 and 55 of the Hague Regulations,



38 hcj 69/ 81, Abu ‘Aita et al. v Regional Commander of the Judea and Samaria Area et al. (5

April 1983); hcj 393/ 82, Jam’iat Iscan Al- Ma’almoun v idf Commander in the Judea and

Samaria Area (28 December 1983).

39 hcj 69/ 81, Abu ‘Aita et al. v Regional Commander of the Judea and Samaria Area et al. (5

38 Todeschini

namely, with the Occupying Power’s role as de facto temporary “administrator and usufructuary” of the occupied territory. In the hcj’s view, it is Israel’s duty under international law to take care of the continuity of “normal life” in the

occupied territory, including its economic development.41

The above-mentioned rulings show how the hcj has employed the pro-

longed nature of the occupation, which is entirely imposed by Israel, to accord Israel expanded powers. What the hcj has done, therefore, is to invert the terms of the equation; instead of subjecting the facts to the law, for example by requiring Israel to end the occupation in accordance with the principle of temporariness, it has subjected the law to the facts, adjusting the terms of the Hague Regulations to the (intentional) protracted duration of the occupation. The prolonged nature of the occupation, thus, has effectively been used to exceed the authority accorded by the Hague Regulations. As Iain Scobbie put it, the hcj “has employed the doctrine of prolonged occupation to buttress an interpretation of the Hague Regulations which obliterated the restraints

placed upon the occupant by [ihl]”.42 This way, the hcj has employed inter-national humanitarian law to justify Israel’s expansion and appropriation pol-icies in the West Bank.

According to some views, however, the protracted character of an occupa-

tion, and the undue expansion of the Occupying Power’s authority, have the effect of rendering an occupation illegal. Orna Ben- Naftali, Aeyal Gross, and Keren Michaeli argued that the legality of an occupation is to be measured against three fundamental principles of the law of occupation: (i) belligerent occupation does not allow the Occupying Power to acquire sovereignty over the occupied territory; (ii) the Occupying Power’s prerogatives are limited to the management of public order and civil life in the occupied territory; and (iii) occupation must be temporary. In these authors’ views, “[t] he violation of any one of these principles, [...] unlike the violation of a specific norm that

reflects them, renders an occupation illegal per se”.43



41 Ibid., paras 10– 13. For further caselaw on this point, see Iain Scobbie, ‘International Law

and the Prolonged Occupation of Palestine’, United Nations Roundtable on Legal Aspects

of the Question of Palestine (2015).

42 Ibid., page 22. See also Al- Haq, Legitimising the Illegitimate? The Israeli High Court of

Justice and the Occupied Palestinian Territory, November 2010.

43 Orna Ben- Naftali, Aeyal Gross and Keren Michaeli, ‘Illegal Occupation: Framing the

Occupied Palestinian Territory’, 23(3) Berkeley Journal of International Law (2005)

551, pages 554‒555. For a critical analysis of this perspective, see Yaël Ronen, ‘Illegal

Occupation and its Consequences’, 41(1) Israel Law Review (2008) 201, pages 207– 211;

David Hughes, ‘Moving from Management to Termination: A Case Study of Prolonged

Occupation’, 44(1) Brooklyn Journal of International Law (2018) 109, pages 153– 164. On the (Il)legality of Israel’s Occupation of the West Bank 39

Building on these arguments, the former UN Special Rapporteur on the

Situation of Human Rights in the Occupied Palestinian Territory, Michael Lynk, elaborated a four- part test, according to which belligerent occupation turns illegal under international humanitarian law when one of the following principles is violated:

(1) The belligerent occupier cannot annex any of the occupied terri-tory; (2) The belligerent occupation must be temporary and cannot be either permanent or indefinite; and the occupant must seek to end the occupation and return the territory to the sovereign as soon as reasonably possible; (3) During the occupation, the belligerent occupier is to act in the best interests of the people under occu-pation; (4) The belligerent occupier must administer the occupied territory in good faith, including acting in full compliance with its duties and obligations under international law and as a member of

the United Nations.44

Such a test has the merit of providing a purposive interpretation that advances one of the main goals of the law of occupation: preventing an occupation from becoming permanent, which would also potentially amount to a breach of the jus ad bellum prohibition against the acquisition of territory through the use

of force – regardless of whether the use of force was lawful or not.45 This way, temporariness acquires a specific normative character to the effect that bellig-erent occupation becomes illegal whenever its protracted nature is instrumen-tal to the adoption by the Occupying Power of measures aimed to appropriate the occupied territory or portions thereof, or that are otherwise incompatible

with the duty to return such territory as soon as feasible.46

Israel’s intention to make its occupation permanent is testified by the sys-

tem of residential, industrial and agricultural settlements, and related infra-structures such as roads and water systems, which have been established and

developed in the West Bank, including East Jerusalem, since 1967.47 The Wall

and its associated regime have further pursued the incorporation of major settlements, especially those located around East Jerusalem, into Israel’s terri-

In light of this, it is submitted that Israel’s prolonged occupation of the West Bank may be considered illegal under inter-

national humanitarian law.51

applied Israeli law to the West Bank on a territorial basis, hence giving effect

In that respect, de jure annexation is an act that formalises both the per-

manent nature of Israel’s occupation and the de facto annexation, effectuated through the settlements, of the concerned portions of the West Bank. As such, it plainly contravenes the principle of temporariness, reinforcing the claim that the occupation itself can be deemed illegal under international humani-tarian law.



2 Duration of Occupation under the Jus Ad Bellum

The occupation of a State’s territory by foreign forces constitutes a typical

instance of unlawful use of force,59 and violates the prohibition on the use of force enshrined in Article 2(4) of the UN Charter, which has attained customary

as well as jus cogens status.60 In particular, occupation resulting from unlaw-ful use of force amounts to an act of aggression. This is set out in UN General Assembly resolution 3314 on the Definition of Aggression, according to which “any military occupation, however temporary, resulting from [...] invasion or

attack” constitutes aggression.61 The Rome Statute,62 which incorporates the

54 Yesh Din, The Occupation of the West Bank and the Crime of Apartheid: Legal Opinion, June

55 Yesh Din, Annexation Legislation Database.

57 Lubna Masarwa and Mustafa Abu Sneineh, ‘Israel’s Defence Minister Launches

Proposal as Unity Deal Nears’, Haaretz, 6 April 2020.

60 icj, Military and Paramilitary Activities in and Against Nicaragua (Judgement) icj Reports

14 (1986) 190; Yoram Dinstein, War, Aggression and Self- Defence (cup 2011) 105 and 117.

61 unga Resolution 3314 (14 December 1974).

62 Aggression has previously been categorized as a crime against peace. See London Charter

of the International Military Tribunal, 8 August 1945, Article 6(a); Nazi Conspiracy and

Aggression: Opinion and Judgement (US Government Printing Office 1947) page 48; ilc,

‘Principles of International Law Recognized in the Charter of the Nuremberg Tribunal 42 Todeschini

definition of aggression agreed upon by the General Assembly, establishes that occupation resulting from unlawful use of force also amounts to the crime of

aggression, for which those responsible may be held criminally liable.63 The same applies when occupation turns into annexation, which breaches the pro-

hibition of territorial acquisition through the use of force64 and constitutes an

act of aggression as well.65

As is well known, the UN Charter provides for two exceptions to the prohibi-

tion on the use of force: military action under its Chapter vii (Articles 39– 42) and self- defence under Article 51. When the UN Security Council determines that a threat to international peace and security exists, it may authorise mil-

itary action.66 Occupation authorised by the Security Council to maintain or restore international peace and security would not, in this perspective, amount

to a breach of the prohibition on the use of force.67 The objective of tackling a threat to international peace and security also implies that a Security Council- authorised occupation may last for the time necessary to address such a threat, meaning it could in principle be prolonged. However, even a Security Council- authorised occupation would be limited. Security Council action must always conform with the purposes and principles set out in Articles 1‒2 of the UN

Charter,68 among which are respect for human rights, self- determination,

Commentaries’, a/ cn.4/ ser.a/ 1996/ Add.l, 6 May– 26 July 1996, Article 16.

63 Rome Statute, Article 8bis(2)(a). It is worth noting that, while Israel’s occupation of the

Palestinian territory in principle falls under the definition of aggression provided for in

the Rome Statute, Article 15bis(5) bars the International Criminal Court’s exercise of juris-

diction over the crime of aggression vis- à- vis non- States Parties and States Parties which

have not expressly accepted such jurisdiction. Given that Israel has not acceded to the

Rome Statute, at present Israeli officials cannot be prosecuted for the crime of aggression

before the icc (whereas they can for all other crimes under the Rome Statute). While

Article 15ter provides that the icc may exercise jurisdiction over the crime of aggression

against non- States Parties pursuant to a referral of the UN Security Council, it is highly

unlikely that States holding the veto power, and which are supportive of Israel, would

allow such a referral to pass.

sovereignty and the territorial integrity of States. In addition, the Security

Council must respect the peremptory norms of international law,69 otherwise

known as jus cogens.70 While its resolutions, assuming they are compliant with

the UN Charter, may generally prevail over conflicting international law,71 they

cannot go against jus cogens lest becoming devoid of legal effect.72

The right to self- determination – as both a UN Charter purpose73 and a

norm jus cogens74 – is especially relevant as a limit to the duration of a UN Security Council- authorised occupation. In fact, the legal effect of belliger-

ent occupation is to suspend temporarily the exercise of sovereignty,75 which

44 Todeschini

in turn is vested in the people entitled to the right to self- determination – in

occupied territories, the people under occupation.76 From a legal standpoint, any occupation needs to comply with the principle of self- determination, which imposes an obligation to refrain from forcible action that deprives a people of their right to self- determination and to respect the exercise of this

right.77 The duration of an occupation must be temporary in order to reconcile with the right to self- determination: an indefinite occupation would be tanta-

mount to a nullification of the right to self- determination.78 Given that the UN Security Council has itself an obligation to respect and promote the exercise of the right to self- determination, it follows that also Security Council- authorised occupations must comply with the principle of temporariness. While the maintenance of international peace and security may justify that an occupa-tion lasts for a protracted period of time, such overarching objective cannot entail an undue limitation of the right to self- determination. Hence, it can be argued that a requirement of temporariness exists also in relation to Security Council- authorised occupations. While the latter are in principle permitted, they cannot be indefinite or permanent, in violation of the UN Charter and jus cogens – particularly, the right to self- determination.

Self- defence is the second exception to the prohibition on the use of force.79

Under international law, a State may use force in self- defence only when it

Accordance with the Charter of the United Nations, Principle 4(6); iccpr, Article 1; ice-

scr, Article 1.

78 Orna Ben- Naftali, Aeyal Gross and Keren Michaeli, ‘Illegal Occupation: Framing the

Occupied Palestinian Territory’, 23(3) Berkeley Journal of International Law (2005) 551,

page 599, which reads “[i] f occupation ‘could, from a legal point of view, continue indef-

initely’, the interests it is designed to protect – the interest of the occupied people to

reach the point in time when they regain control over their lives and exercise their right

to self- determination […] – would be rendered meaningless. Thus, the core assumptions

of the normative regime of occupation would be defied if an occupation can be stymied

indefinitely. The temporary, as distinct from the indefinite, nature of occupation is thus

the most necessary element of the normative regime of occupation, as it gives meaning

and effect – both factual and legal – to the concepts of liberty, freedom, and the right to

self- determination”. See also unhrc, Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Situation of

Human Rights in the Palestinian Territories Occupied Since 1967, Francesca Albanese, A/ 77/

356, 21 September 2022.

79 UN Charter, Article 51. The right of self- defence is part of customary international law;

see icj, Military and Paramilitary Activities in and Against Nicaragua (Judgement) icj

Reports 14 (1986) para 176.

On the (Il)legality of Israel’s Occupation of the West Bank 45

is the victim of an armed attack, in other words a use of armed force of a certain scale and gravity. If the offensive force does not reach such a thresh-

old, a State is not entitled to act in self- defence.80 Moreover, defensive force

must respect the requirements of immediacy, necessity and proportionality.81 Immediacy prescribes that no undue time lapse exist between the occurrence

of the armed attack and the exercise of self- defence.82 Necessity requires that

a State resort to armed force solely when alternative options are unavailable.83 Proportionality allows the State to use only the degree of armed force that is

essential to repel and neutralise an armed attack.84 Use of armed force in self- defence that fails to meet such cumulative requirements is in breach of inter-

national law.85 It is also worth highlighting that contemporary international

law does not envisage self- defence as a modality for acquiring territory.86

In principle, the occupation of the aggressor’s territory may be justified

under the right of self- defence whenever necessary to repel an armed attack,

and provided it complies with the above- mentioned requirements.87 Pursuant to the principle of proportionality, the State acting in self- defence can occupy only the amount of the aggressor State’s territory that is required to neutralise the armed attack. In accordance with the principle of necessity, furthermore, the State acting in self- defence must withdraw from the occupied territory as soon as the armed attack has ended. In the very moment an occupation ceases to serve the purpose of repelling an armed attack, its continuation gives rise to



80 Ibid., paras 195 and 211. It remains an open question whether self- defence can only be

used in relation to an armed attack that is ongoing or has actually occurred, or also in

respect of an armed attack that is imminent, in other words, about to occur; see Marko

Milanovic, ‘The Soleimani Strike and Self- Defence Against an Imminent Armed Attack’,

ejil Talk!, 7 January 2020.

81 icj, Military and Paramilitary Activities in and Aagainst Nicaragua (Judgement) icj

Reports 14 (1986) paras 194, 237; icj, Legality of the Threat or Use of Nuclear Weapons

(Advisory Opinion) icj Reports 226 (1996) para 41; icj, Oil Platforms (Judgement) icj

Reports 161 (2003) para 76.

82 Yoram Dinstein, War, Aggression and Self- Defence (cup 2011) 233.

83 Ibid., pages 231‒232.

84 Ibid., pages 232‒233, 244, 248; Christopher Greenwood, ‘Self- Defence’ in Max Planck

Encyclopedia of Public International Law (oup 2011) para 28.

85 Ibid., para 4. Article 51 of the UN Charter further requires States to report to the Security

Council on the measures adopted in self- defence. See also icj, Legality of the Threat or Use

of Nuclear Weapons (Advisory Opinion) icj Reports 226 (1996) para 44.

86 Rainer Hofmann, ‘Annexation’ in Max Planck Encyclopedia of Public International Law

(oup 2013) paras 23‒24.

87 Antonio Cassese, Self- Determination of Peoples: A Legal Reappraisal (cup 1995) 99; Matteo

Fornari ‘I Cinquant’anni Della Guerra Dei Sei Giorni: Alcune Questioni (Irrisolte) Poste

Dalla Risoluzione 242’, 2 La Comunità Internazionale (2018) 185, pages 192‒195. 46 Todeschini

a breach of the prohibition on the use of force, possibly amounting to an act

of aggression in its own turn.88 This means that, under the law of self- defence, an occupation must be temporary and, by implication, that prolonged occupa-tions will rarely meet the criteria of necessity and proportionality.

As Christine Gray noted, while States like Israel and South Africa invoked

self- defence in order to justify their presence in South Lebanon (1978‒2000) and Angola (1981‒1988), respectively, such prolonged occupations have been

condemned as unnecessary and disproportionate in terms of self- defence.89 Indeed, the longer an occupation lasts, the harder it will be for the Occupying

Power to justify its presence on foreign territory.90 Even when some sort of threat to the victim State persists, the protracted military presence on foreign territory becomes an unnecessary and disproportionate measure as long as such a threat does not concretise into an armed attack.

This reasoning may be applied to Israel’s prolonged occupation of the

Palestinian territory.91 Israel justifies its presence by claiming that it took con-

trol of the Palestinian territory following a self- defence war,92 a legal argument which is fundamentally flawed. Even if it was accepted that the initial occu-pation of the Palestinian territory and the Golan Heights during the Six- Day

War had been undertaken in legitimate self- defence,93 which John Quigley

refutes in Chapter 1, its protracted duration is unjustified under the law of self-

defence.94 The victory of Israel in that conflict proves that any existing armed attack by Egypt, Jordan and Syria was successfully repelled. Moreover, Israel first signed a disengagement agreement with Syria (1974), reinforced by the

presence of the UN Disengagement Observer Force in the Golan Heights,95 and



88 Eyal Benvenisti, The International Law of Occupation (oup 2012) 16– 17.

89 Christine Gray, International Law and the Use of Force (oup 2008) 154‒155.

90 Vaios Koutroulis, ‘The Application of International Humanitarian Law and International

Human Rights Law in Situation of Prolonged Occupation: Only a Matter of Time?’, 94(885)

International Review of the Red Cross (2012) 165, page 168.

91 Ralph Wilde, ‘Using the Master’s Tools to Dismantle the Master’s House: International

2020) 3, pages 21– 35.

92 Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Israel, ‘Israeli Settlements and International Law’, 30

November 2015.

93 This should not be read as an argument supporting the view that during the Six- Day War

Israel acted in self- defence. For analysis, see John Quigley, The Six- Day War and Israeli

Self- Defense: Questioning the Legal Basis for Preventive War (cup 2013).

94 Matteo Fornari ‘I Cinquant’anni della Guerra dei Sei Giorni: Alcune Questioni (Irrisolte)

Poste dalla Risoluzione 242’, 2 La Comunità Internazionale (2018) 185, page 195.

95 Agreement on Disengagement between Israeli and Syrian Forces, 31 May 1974; unsc

Resolution 350 (31 May 1974).

On the (Il)legality of Israel’s Occupation of the West Bank 47

subsequently reached a peace treaty with Egypt (1979) and Jordan (1994).96 The absence of an ongoing or immediate armed attack by the States against which Israel fought the Six- Day War means that the law of self- defence cannot justify the maintenance of belligerent occupation in any of the territories occupied in 1967, including the West Bank.

Israel’s self- defence claims should also be considered in light of the annex-

ation of portions of the West Bank.97 The de jure annexation of East Jerusalem since 1967 and the creation of at least 300 settlements where more than 700,000 settlers live, which has resulted in the de facto annexation of the portions of the West Bank where these are located, evidence Israel’s intention to appropriate,

in whole or in part, the territories it occupied in 1967.98 Establishing such a per-manent presence on the Palestinian territory, in turn, excludes the possibility of framing Israel’s occupation of the West Bank in self- defence terms, as such conduct inherently violates the principles of necessity and proportionality and the underlying requirement that occupation under self- defence be temporary. Moreover, annexation is itself an act of aggression, and self- defence can never

justify the acquisition of territory by force.99

Against this background, it can be concluded that Israel’s prolonged occu-

pation of the Palestinian territory breaches the requirement of temporariness of occupations inherent in the principles governing self- defence under the jus ad bellum. By failing to be justified under the law of self- defence, Israel’s pro-longed occupation of the Palestinian territory constitutes an unlawful use of force amounting to an act of aggression, in breach of the UN Charter and cus-tomary international law.



3 Responses to Breaches of the Principle of Temporariness

An infringement of the principle of temporariness determines the illegal-ity of an occupation under both international humanitarian law and the jus ad bellum. As the analysis sought to demonstrate, this is the case for Israel’s



96 Treaty of Peace between the Arab Republic of Egypt and the State of Israel, 26 March

1979; Treaty of Peace between the State of Israel and the Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan,

26 October 1994. Jordan already renounced all claims over the West Bank in 1988. See King

Hussein of Jordan, Address to the Nation, 31 July 1988.

97 See above Section 1.

98 Israel’s formal annexation of the Golan Heights in 1981 further supports such a claim.

99 Rainer Hofmann, ‘Annexation’ in Max Planck Encyclopedia of Public International Law

(oup 2013) paras 23‒24.

48 Todeschini

prolonged occupation of the West Bank. In light of this, the law of international responsibility prescribes on Israel an obligation to cease its unlawful actions,

withdraw its military and civilian presence from the occupied territory,100 and make adequate reparation in the form of restitution, compensation and satis-

faction both to the State of Palestine and individual Palestinians.101

Given Israel’s military occupation and partial de jure annexation of the

Palestinian territory amount to acts of aggression, the UN Security Council may in principle adopt all available measures under Chapter vii of the UN Charter, including economic sanctions and military action, to compel Israel to withdraw from the Palestinian territory. Despite having repeatedly affirmed the inadmissibility of territorial acquisition by force – an act of aggression in

and of itself – in its relevant resolutions,102 the Security Council has never determined that Israel’s occupation of the Palestinian territory constitutes a

“threat to international peace and security”,103 which is the necessary prelim-inary step required to adopt Chapter vii measures. This is explained by the persistent use on the part of the US of the veto power, which has historically

shielded Israel from being the target of any forcible action.104

The illegality of the prolonged occupation of the West Bank triggers legal

consequences for Third States as well. Under Common Article 1 of the Geneva Conventions, Third States must “ensure respect” for international humani-tarian law which, as held by the icj, translates into an obligation “to ensure

compliance by Israel with international humanitarian law […]”.105 Given that Israel’s prolonged occupation of the West Bank violates one of the pillars of



100 unsc Resolution 242 (22 November 1967) already requested Israel to withdraw its armed

forces from the territories it had occupied in June 1967, and to respect the sovereignty,

territorial integrity and political independence of all States in the area.

2(2) Yearbook of the International Law Commission (2001), Articles 30, 31, 34‒37; icj, Legal

the First Geneva Convention: Convention (I) for the Amelioration of the Condition of the

the law of occupation – the principle of temporariness – Third States should adopt, in order to fulfil their Common Article 1 obligation, any lawful measures at their disposal to compel Israel to put an end to it.

Under the jus ad bellum, a prolonged occupation infringes upon the pro-

hibition on the use of force, which is a jus cogens norm. In this respect, the law of international responsibility prescribes Third States neither to recog-nise nor to render aid or assistance in perpetuating the prolonged occupation

of the West Bank, and to cooperate to bring it to an end.106 Third States also have the faculty to invoke Israel’s responsibility in the interest of the State of Palestine in order to request the cessation of the occupation, guarantees of

non- repetition, and reparation.107 Third States may even adopt lawful counter-measures, including of an economic or diplomatic character, to compel Israel

to conform to international law.108

Finally, it has been suggested that the UN General Assembly and the icj

may be invested of the matter. Efforts could be made to revive the General Assembly’s position on the illegality of Israel’s occupation, while a second icj advisory opinion could be sought to declare the occupation per se contrary to

international law.109 Although not definitive in themselves, these declarations would be a significant addition to Palestine’s legal and political arsenal, which may then be employed as part of the engagement with the international com-munity in the efforts to end Israel’s occupation of the Palestinian territory.



4 Conclusion

A belligerent occupation is an exceptional situation within an international legal order based on formal sovereign equality between States and the exercise



106 ilc, ‘Report of the International Law Commission on the Work of its Fifty- Third Session’,

2(2) Yearbook of the International Law Commission (2001), Articles 40– 41; icj, Legal

Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory (Advisory

Opinion) icj Reports 136 (2004) para 159.

107 ilc, ‘Report of the International Law Commission on the Work of its Fifty- Third Session’,

2(2) Yearbook of the International Law Commission (2001), Article 48.

108 Ibid., Article 54.

109 Ardi Imseis, ‘Negotiating the Illegal: On the United Nations and the Illegal Occupation

of Palestine, 1967– 2020’, 31(3) European Journal of International Law (2020) 1055, pages

1082– 1084; unhrc Report of the Independent International Commission of Inquiry on the

Occupied Palestinian Territory, including East Jerusalem, and Israel, A/ 77/ 328, 14 September

2022, paras 84 and 92(a).

50 Todeschini

by peoples of their right to self- determination.110 Accordingly, international law regulates belligerent occupation on the assumption that it will terminate as soon as circumstances allow. This is the reason why, under both international humanitarian law and the jus ad bellum, a belligerent occupation is meant to be temporary. And yet, while both bodies of law incorporate the principle of temporariness, they conceptualise it differently.

While international humanitarian law refrains from setting an end date to

a belligerent occupation, it treats the Occupying Power as a de facto tempo-rary “administrator and usufructuary” of the occupied territory, which should strive to maintain, as far as possible, the status quo within it. Thus, interna-tional humanitarian law conceives the exercise of authority by the Occupying Power over foreign territory as inherently temporary, even though it does not prescribe its termination. The assumption is indeed that belligerent occupa-tion serves the conduct and purposes of war, meaning that it is supposed to end when such necessities cease to exist. If the Occupying Power wilfully and purposely prolongs a belligerent occupation because it intends to annex the occupied territory, de jure or de facto, then it contravenes the very principle of temporariness upon which the law of occupation is founded. It then follows that belligerent occupation becomes illegal under international humanitarian law the moment that the Occupying Power implements measures designed to appropriate the occupied territory on a permanent basis. In relation to Israel’s occupation of the West Bank, such measures are implemented through the establishment and expansion of settlements and the transfer of settlers, and through laws that purport to formally annex the totality or portions of the West Bank. This situation has engendered what Michael Lynk has called “an occupation in perpetuity”, which constitutes “the antithesis of international

humanitarian law”.111

Differently from international humanitarian law, the jus ad bellum regu-

lates the legality of the use of armed force between States and, accordingly, determines the unlawfulness of military occupations that contravene the UN Charter and customary international law. When military occupations are part of a use of armed force that is lawful, especially with respect to the right of self- defence, the jus ad bellum prescribes an end date on them. In particular,



110 Orna Ben- Naftali, Aeyal Gross and Keren Michaeli, ‘Illegal Occupation: Framing the

Occupied Palestinian Territory’, 23(3) Berkeley Journal of International Law (2005) 551,

pages 553– 556.

111 unhrc, Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the

Palestinian Territories Occupied Since 1967, Michael Lynk, a/ hrc/ 49/ 87, 21 March 2022,

paras 15 and 54.

On the (Il)legality of Israel’s Occupation of the West Bank 51

the principles of necessity and proportionality require a State acting in self- defence, which has occupied the aggressor State’s territory to repel an armed attack, to withdraw its forces as soon as the latter is terminated. Consequently, a belligerent occupation that lasts for a period of time exceeding the neces-sities of self- defence becomes unlawful because it breaches the principles of necessity and proportionality and, by implication, the prohibition on the use of force enshrined in the UN Charter and customary international law. As the present analysis has shown, Israel’s attempts to justify its military presence in the West Bank on the basis of self- defence are unfounded, which means that its occupation of the West Bank is illegal under the jus ad bellum.

Legally speaking, time is not on the side of the Occupying Power, and Israel’s

prolonged occupation of the West Bank does not escape this basic rule. If it were otherwise, the fundamental premises on which the international legal order rests would be negated.

chapter 3


The Occupation of Palestine from a twail Lens

Ray Murphy, Anita Ferrara and Susan Power



This chapter will examine the international legal framework governing the situation in Palestine from a Third World Approaches to International Law lens, set out what the twail movement is, and assess its relevance today. In so doing, it will address how the interplay of international law and practice are manipulated to serve neo- colonial interests in Palestine. This chapter will establish how Israel has instrumentalised international law arguments to evade its responsibilities under the Geneva Conventions. It will further explore how regime bias underpins the deference of the Israeli High Court of Justice to the military necessity arguments of the military commander, which in turn leads to a distortion of international law in the jurisprudence of the court. This distortion is then replicated in textbooks and other influential secondary inter-national law materials. In this way, this chapter posits that international law has enabled Israel’s fragmentation, structural domination and exploitation of the Palestinian people. Given the failure of the international community to intervene, this chapter questions what the consequences are if international humanitarian law principles are continually breached during unending occu-pations intended to facilitate full colonisation.



1 Third World Approaches to International Law

Third World Approaches to International Law analyses the manner in which international law, throughout the ages, has operated as “a system of exclusion

or oppression”.1 In this way the twail approach considers international law’s role in enabling the structural domination and exploitation of States and peoples. This approach is especially useful and pertinent in the Middle East where international law has historically been exploited in order to maintain



1 Fréderic Mégret, ‘International Law as Law’ in James Crawford and Martti Koskenniemi (eds),

The Cambridge Companion to International Law (cup 2012) 80– 84. See also Antonio Cassese,

International Law (oup 2005) 11; Gerry Simpson, ‘International Law in Diplomatic History’ in

James Crawford and Martti Koskenniemi (eds), The Cambridge Companion to International

Law (cup 2012) 34.



© Ray Murphy et al., 2023 | DOI:10.1163/978900450

The Occupation of Palestine from a twail Lens 53

the imperial ambitions of other States. twail scholars consider that in order to respond to contemporary challenges to international order, international law must ensure greater participation and representation of disenfranchised peoples and promote a more equitable distribution of the world’s resources.

The source of much contemporary international law is commonly traced to

the 1648 Peace of Westphalia.2 It is not surprising that the international legal

system that evolved subsequently was a decidedly European project.3 Only so- called “civilised” European States were regarded as full subjects of interna-

tional law and thus members of international society.4 By restricting recogni-tion of statehood to “civilised” nations, European States were able to control the evolution of customary international law to suit their purposes. They also afforded themselves the role of preparing the “un- civilised” nations for subjec-

tion to international law.5

The exact treatment of non- European regions under the Euro- centric

framework of international law depended on their supposed level of civil-

isation.6 James Lorimer divided humanity into “civilised” European States, “savage” extra- European peoples and an intermediate category of “barbarous”

semi- civilised States.7 States falling in the intermediate category, including the Ottoman Empire, were subjected to capitulation treaties, which afforded

enforcement of treaties, and the initiation and conduct of armed conflict originated outside

of Europe, most notably, in the ancient Near East. See David J Bederman, ‘International law

in the Ancient World’ in David Armstrong (ed), Routledge Handbook of International Law

(Routledge 2009). See also Malcolm N. Shaw, International Law (cup 2014) 10– 13. Before

1648, medieval legal theory and the jus gentium helped enable Western European imperi-

alism, Antony Anghie, Imperialism, Sovereignty and the Making of International Law (cup

2004) Chapter 1. Anne Orford and Florian Hoffmann (eds), The Oxford Handbook of the

Theory of International Law (oup 2016) 273– 75.

International Law 1870– 1960 (cup 2001).

(cup 2005) 144– 145. See also Antony Anghie, Imperialism, Sovereignty and the Making of

International Law (cup 2004) 35.

(cup 2005) 147– 148.

6 Antonio Cassese, International Law (oup 2005) 26.

54 Murphy, Ferrara and Power

non- reciprocal privileges to European (and subsequently American) resi-

dents.8 “Savages” were subjected to outright colonisation through treaties of cession, annexation and conquest. The origins of many of the contemporary problems in the Middle East region can be traced to the exploitation of inter-national law for often short- sighted national gain.

In this way, international legal doctrines were devised to enable the colo-

nial project.9 Nowhere is this more evident than in the terrae nullius prin-ciple. Under this principle no sovereign title existed over territory outside of the Euro- centric society of States and consequently that territory was open to colonial conquest. The principle of recognition stipulated that recognition of statehood by the other members of the international society, namely Western

States, was sufficient to secure external sovereignty.10 The principle of effective-ness posited that de facto control of territory was sufficient to secure internal

sovereignty.11 Indigenous populations could attempt to forcefully defend their territory against colonial powers. Such efforts were more often than not subject to failure owing to the disparity in military capacity of such populations when compared to that of European powers. Furthermore, the situation was wors-ened by the fact that armed conflicts between colonial powers and indigenous populations were not considered subject to the laws of war between so- called civilised States and “barbarians” and “savages” who were deemed less vulnera-

ble to injury.12 This left indigenous populations disadvantaged with little alter-

native but to conclude unequal treaties with the colonial powers.13

In the course of 20th century, the United States and the Soviet Union over-

took the European States and became international superpowers. Despite support for the right to self- determination, colonialism continued after World War i. Indeed, the League of Nations imposed an obligation on the mandate

effective can produce legal consequences”. Antonio Cassese, International Law (oup

2005) 12– 13.

12 Antonio Cassese, International Law (oup 2005) 28, 32.

13 Ibid., page 28.

The Occupation of Palestine from a twail Lens 55

powers to “civilise” the subjects of the mandate system.14 World War ii expe-dited the decolonisation process and the principle of self- determination was

enshrined in the UN Charter.15 However, the retention of a reference to the “general principles of law recognized by civilized nations” in the Statute of the International Court of Justice remains an implicit recognition of international

law’s colonial legacy.16 Furthermore, the World War ii victorious States were afforded a permanent position on the UN Security Council and the power of veto. The UN General Assembly was a representative and egalitarian organ, more reflective of the concept of sovereign equality which former colonies were able to use to codify international legal principles favourable to their

interests.17 However, global economic disparities often rendered the former

colonies dependent upon industrialised nations.18

Although many twail scholars consider that international law still repli-

cates the “civilising mission” through mechanisms such as the International Criminal Court and the principle of Responsibility to Protect, they acknowledge that international law is no longer exclusively an instrument of the strong. The principle of the sovereign equality of States means that the interests of weaker States must be accommodated to some extent, even if this is limited. The fact that even powerful States feel the need to legally justify their actions suggests

that international law can constrain the exercise of power to some degree.19 Furthermore, former colonies value international law’s respect for pluralism as

a means of defending their independence.20 At the same time, authoritarian rulers in post- colonial States have exploited the legal principles of sovereignty and non- interference in the domestic affairs of a State to legitimise their neo- imperial practices. twail scholars agree that if international law is to respond

to contemporary global challenges, it will need to address demands for greater

participation, distributive justice, and non- selective enforcement.21

In this way international law also presents a potential tool for those that have

been oppressed and disadvantaged by its utilisation in the past. International human rights principles in particular provide a benchmark by which to mea-sure the behaviour of States and to use in order to vindicate rights, especially within the international human rights framework. Likewise, more recent developments in international law such as the International Criminal Court provide important mechanisms for individual accountability.



2 How International Law Operates a ‘System of Exclusion or

Oppression’ in Palestine

This section draws from twail theory to explain how the application of international law is subverted in Palestine and how hegemonic States and the powerful Zionist lobby have operated to ensure the continuation of Israel’s colonisation of Palestine. In this manner, international law has operated as a tool to fragment, exclude and oppress the Palestinian people, as well as deny their collective right of self- determination for decades.

2.1 Using unga Resolution 181 to Deny Palestinian Self- determination

How international law operates to the exclusion of the oppressed is evidenced in the systematic fragmentation of the Palestinian people, with 12 million refugees in the diaspora denied their right of return, while the collective Palestinian people have been denied their right of self- determination since 1948. Early patterns of exclusion were evidenced in the administration of Palestine as a Class A mandate (which meant that Palestine was provision-ally recognised as an independent nation), the administration of which was

supposed to revert back to the Palestinian people post- mandate.22 However, instead of relinquishing control back to the Palestinian people, as required of Class A mandates such as transpired in the Class A mandates of Iraq, Syria and Lebanon, the Zionist lobby mobilised the United Nations to enact UN General Assembly resolution 181. In the summer of 1947, future Israeli Prime



21 Fréderic Mégret, ‘International Law as Law’ in James Crawford and Martti Koskenniemi

(eds), The Cambridge Companion to International Law (cup 2012) 88. See also BS Chimni,

‘Legitimating the International Rule of Law’ in Crawford and Koskenniemi (eds), The

Cambridge Companion to International Law (cup 2012) 294– 295, 317.

22 The Covenant of the League of Nations, 28 June 1919, Part 1, Article 22. The Occupation of Palestine from a twail Lens 57

Minister Menachem Begin warned the UN that the “peace of the world” would be threatened if “‘the Hebrew [Biblical] homeland’ is not given in full to the

Zionists. ‘Whatever might be signed or pledged’”.23 The subsequent UN General Assembly resolution 181, voted on by the international community of nations, infringed upon the internationally recognised right of self- determination of the Palestinian people and awarded Palestinian lands to the recently arrived colonialist settler minority in Palestine. The Palestinian people were at this time the majority population in Mandate Palestine and they had permanent sovereignty over their land and national resources.

2.2 A Permanent Occupation of the Palestinian Territory In the occupied Palestinian territory, composed of the West Bank, including East Jerusalem and the Gaza Strip, Israel is flouting the laws of occupation to progress and expand its settler colonisation. Many twail scholars have high-lighted that the international rules- based system premised on the equality of States and offered as a “neutral discipline” is in fact an “instrument of naked power, skillfully dressed up so as to hide its objective of controlling the col-

onized world for the benefit of the colonial powers”.24 Similarly, the narrow de facto framework of military occupation which was originally intended to protect the property and assets of smaller weaker States against the aggran-disement of the Occupying Power, is today employed by Israel as a State sanc-tioned tool of colonisation. Nobel Laureate Desmond Tutu’s statement in 2014 that, “Israel has created an apartheid reality within its borders and through its occupation”, speaks to the manipulation of occupation law to advance settler

colonial and apartheid practices.25

While the legality of occupation de jure is governed by international law

and the UN Charter, whether a situation amounts to an occupation is deter-mined by a factual assessment on the ground. As such, the lawfulness of the occupation is irrelevant to the de facto application of international humanitar-ian law. The Hague Regulations and the Fourth Geneva Convention contain a number of important provisions relating to occupation in this regard. Article 42 of the Hague Regulations states that “territory is considered occupied when



23 Thomas Suárez, ‘Israel has Never had any Intention of Honouring Either the 1947 Partition

Plan or 1967 Borders’, Middle East Monitor, 10 October 2019.

24 Muthucumaraswamy Sornarajah, ‘The Asian Perspective to International Law in the Age

of Globalization’, 5 Singapore Journal of International and Comparative Law (2001) 284,

page 285.

25 Robert Naiman, ‘Anti- Apartheid Archbishop Tutu Calls Presbyterians to Back Divestment

from Israeli Occupation’, Truth Out, 21 June 2014.

58 Murphy, Ferrara and Power

it is actually placed under the authority of the hostile army. The occupation extends only to the territory where such authority has been established and

can be exercised”.26

According to common Article 2 of the four Geneva Conventions, the pro-

visions of the Conventions apply to any territory occupied during interna-tional hostilities. This includes situations where the occupation meets with no armed resistance. Meanwhile, Article 43 of the Hague Regulations prescribes

the fundamental administrative obligations of an Occupying Power.27 It has

been described as the “mini- constitution” of an occupation regime.28 Article 43 places a conservationist role on the Occupying Power whereby it is regarded

as trustee but there is no transfer of property.29 It also imposes a fundamental duty of governance over the occupied territory and this entails a range of duties from respect for fundamental human rights to provision of health care (Fourth

Geneva Convention, Articles 55 to 57).30 However, the situation is regarded as temporary and the occupant cannot behave as though the population of the

occupied territory are its own nationals.31

Critically, the permanent nature of Israel’s now 55- year occupation, as well

as the violation of specific peremptory norms of international law raises serious concerns as to the illegality of the occupation. One of the fundamental prin-ciples of occupation is its temporary nature as well as the prohibition against

altering the sovereignty of the occupied territory.32 It is noteworthy that the principle of temporariness enshrined in the Hague Regulations is reinforced

December 2011) para 8.

29 Hague Regulations, Article 43. “The authority of the legitimate power having in fact

passed into the hands of the occupant, the latter shall take all the measures in his power

to restore, and ensure, as far as possible, public order and safety, while respecting, unless

absolutely prevented, the laws in force in the country”.

by Article 47 of the Fourth Geneva Convention.33 It deprives of legal effect any measures which otherwise could bring about a change in status of the occu-pied territory. Therefore, the law of occupation recognises that occupation is

an exceptional deviation from the normal order of sovereignty.34 This also acts as a restraint on the occupant in how it deals with the local population who are

protected persons under international humanitarian law.35

In the Palestinian territory, Israel, through its five decade long occupation,

“has extended its authority far beyond what is permitted by international law and it is clear that the prolonged and entrenched nature of the Israeli occu-pation has resulted in the enduring denial of the most basic rights of the

Palestinian people”.36 This led UN Special Rapporteur, John Dugard, to con-clude in his 2007 report to the UN Human Rights Council that “elements of the [Israel’s] occupation [of the Palestinian territory] constitute forms of colo-

nialism and of apartheid, which are contrary to international law”.37 However the failure of the High Contracting Parties of the Fourth Geneva Convention to intervene and assess the legality of the decades long occupation of the Palestinian territory, exemplifies how dominant powers can manipulate inter-national law for the purposes of maintaining domination and oppression of the occupied population, and to service settler- colonial aims.

2.3 Israel’s Manufactured Reversioner Rule As Antony Anghie observes, international law rules such as territorial acqui-sition, State succession, State responsibility and recognition, have been con-

structed by States.38 Nowhere is this more evident than in Israel’s ad hoc instrumentalisation of international law arguments on State recognition or non- recognition to evade its responsibilities under the Geneva Conventions.

Under international law, Israel is bound by the Hague Regulations as cus-

tomary international law and is a party to the Geneva Conventions.39 Although Israel is not a party to Additional Protocol i, much of the Protocol applies as customary international law. Further, international human rights treaties con-tinue to apply in the context of an armed conflict and Israel has similar extra-territorial obligations under human rights treaties to which it is a party. That these laws are binding on Israel’s occupation of the Palestinian territory has

been authoritatively confirmed by the International Court of Justice,40 the UN

Security Council,41 and the UN General Assembly.42

Nevertheless, the Government of Israel has adopted an ambiguous position

declaring that the status of the West Bank and Gaza is unclear, arguing that

there was no prior legitimate sovereign43 and that it is therefore questionable whether the Fourth Geneva Convention applies to its administration of the

territory.44 According to Israel’s Ministry for Foreign Affairs:

Occupied territories are territories captured in war from an established

and recognized sovereign. As the West Bank and Gaza Strip were not

under the legitimate and recognized sovereignty of any state prior to the

Six Day War, they should not be considered occupied territories.45

This is what Israel coins the “missing reversioner” theory – that is, that the laws of occupation are inapplicable in a situation where the displaced power was not the lawful sovereign, or where such power was simply missing in the occu-

pied territory.46 In July 2002, the Israeli government established a controversial

3 How the Israeli High Court of Justice Maintains Oppression over

Palestinians to Facilitate Settler Colonisation

According to scholars, one important thread examined in twail is regime bias which presents itself in how international law is interpreted and applied

in internal processes to affect the Third World.55 According to twail expert Gathii:

Regime bias therefore refers to examining the choices made between

alternative ways of crafting legal rules, the meanings ascribed to a par-

ticular rule whether in its application by an administrative agency, or at

the adjudication stage by a domestic judicial body, or an international

tribunal.56

Others assert that such “different interpretations of similar or identical rules of international law can undermine the general integrity of international law, and

the overall consistency of international law”.57

One of the central legal arguments proffered by the Israeli military com-

mander, and legal representatives is a broad stroke argument that acts have

been carried out by the military in consideration of military necessity. The “mil-itary necessity”, “military operations”, or “military need” defence is intended to mitigate against the armies’ actions in relation to the appropriation of land, destruction of Palestinian properties, the killing of Palestinians, and a myriad of other criminal acts in violation of the laws of armed conflict carried out to facilitate the ongoing settler colonial enterprise. In Schnitzer v Chief Military Censor, the Israeli Supreme Court, sitting as the High Court of Justice, outlined that the “Court is able and obliged to review the reasonableness of professional discretion in every area, so must it do so in the realm of security. In other words, there are no special limits on the power of judicial review in matters of State

security”.58 That being said, in practice, the security defence of the military commander acting in military necessity, provides a blanket justification for the military commander, who is essentially treated as a separate organ of the State.

Regime bias plays out in the continual deference of the hcj to the mili-

tary commander’s arguments of military necessity. This in turn, provides for a distortion of the legal principles. The overly broad interpretation of military necessity is invoked to justify almost any decision. The hcj has even added considerations of threats from outside the occupied territory into rationales deferring to the military commander’s military necessity arguments, consider-ations which exceed the competence of the Occupying Power’s administrative

authority.59 Article 52 “military needs” have traditionally been very narrowly applied to the direct needs of the army, such as food, quartering of horses, and shelter. The Military Tribunal at Nuremberg emphasised the direct needs per-mitting limited requisitions under Article 52 in Krupp:

All authorities are again in agreement that the requisitions in kind and

services referred to in Article 52, concern such matters as billets for the

occupying troops and the occupation authorities, garages for their vehi-

cles, stables for their horses, urgently needed equipment and supplies for

the proper functioning of the occupation authorities, food for the Army

of Occupation, and the like.60

occupied territory.61 In the Beit Sourik case, the hcj linked considerations of Article 23(g) of the Hague Regulations, which specifically addresses active con-flict and enshrines a broader “military necessity” exception, together with its considerations of Article 52 of the Hague Regulations, which provides for lim-ited requisition of private property for “military needs” in occupied territory – considering both alongside Article 53 of the Fourth Geneva Convention as cre-

ating “a single tapestry of norms”.62 As noted by Alexander Orakhelashvili, “[m] ilitary operations in the West Bank ceased a long time ago and the Wall itself is hardly meant to serve the needs of the Israeli army. Whatever the situ-

ation in the West Bank, it cannot currently be denoted as a state of war”.63 By distorting the test, and drawing from the deck of the general laws of armed con-flict, the hcj justified the appropriation of vast swathes of private Palestinian land for the construction of the Annexation Wall – “an obstacle” – the court emphasised which is “intended to take the place of combat military opera-

tions”.64 Additionally, large tracts of Palestinian land in Area C have been been

declared protected areas or closed military zones for “military needs”65 and a systematic attempt is being made to depopulate these areas of Palestinians, in

particular the Bedouin.66

illegal occupation policies’”.73 While the jurisprudence of the hcj, the judicial arm of the colonial Occupying Power, should be regarded as sui generis, as the regime bias of an unconstrained rogue State, the findings themselves have become replicated as part of the body of cited jurisprudence in the textbooks

of international law students.74 The hcj caselaw features on the authoritative

4 How International Law Enables the Structural Domination and

Exploitation of the Palestinian People

Although international humanitarian law was intended to curb some of the excesses of authority of the malevolent Occupying Power and preserve the economic substance of the territory for the returning sovereign, Third States have contributed to the perpetuation of the colonisation of Palestine, both through their failure to hold Israel accountable and the denial of Palestinian statehood. The deliberate suppression of the oppressed by dominant powers is described in Paolo Freire’s seminal thesis, the “Pedagogy of the Oppressed” wherein he states:

The more human world to which they [the oppressed] justly aspire,

however, is the antithesis of the ‘human world’ of the oppressors – a

world which is the exclusive possession of the oppressors, who preach

an impossible harmony between themselves (who dehumanize) and the

oppressed (who are dehumanized). Since oppressors and oppressed are

antithetical, what serves the interests of one group disserves the interests

of the others.76

That Israel profits from the perpetuation of the colonisation is evident; Israel benefits from 306 million US dollars per year in fiscal leakages, in addition to the direct revenues from natural resources exploitation, and approximately 600 million US dollars in profits from the sale of settlement produce to inter-

national markets.77 While Palestinian losses from not being able to develop resources in Area C under full Israeli civil and security control are estimated

A recent investigate report from a coalition of 25 Palestinian, regional, and international civil soci-ety organisations found that “as of May 2021, European investors also held US$

141 billion in shares and bonds of these [settlement] companies”.79

Many of the countries still advocating for a two state solution under the Oslo

Accords are the same countries that refuse to recognise Palestinian statehood, while financial institutions domiciled in their jurisdictions profit from Israel’s

pillage of Palestinian resources.80 For example, the US, who was central to the Oslo Accords, has not only failed to recognise the State of Palestine, but has closed the offices of the Palestine Liberation Organization in Washington, in addition to relocating the US embassy to Israel to Jerusalem – territory which

Palestine remains sovereign over.81 Meanwhile as the State of Palestine moved to hold individual Israeli actors accountable for war crimes and crimes against humanity at the International Criminal Court, seven States (Austria, Australia, Brazil, Czech Republic, Germany, Hungary and Uganda) submitted amicus curiae voicing their non- recognition of Palestinian statehood to the Pre- Trial

Chamber, in an attempt to reinforce Israel’s decades- long impunity.82

twail academics therefore quite rightly conclude that “international law

that was shaped in the colonial era was not a neutral discipline but an instru-ment of naked power, skillfully dressed up so as to hide its objective of con-

trolling the colonized world for the benefit of the colonial powers”.83 Nowhere is this more keenly evident than in the power dynamics that underpin the

sanctions regime, which give rise to “distributive inequalities”.84 In the case
