of Palestine, the abject failure of the international community to circumvent the veto block in the UN Security Council and place regional and individual economic sanctions and countermeasures on Israel is clearly political. As the international community mobilised to sanction Russia, one month into its aggressive war on the Ukraine, the US, EU, and UK placed economic individ-ual sanctions on over 1,000 businesses and individual oligarchs linked to the

Kremlin.85 For Palestine, even Israel’s announced de jure annexation planned

for June 2020, did not garner the sanctioning response of Third States.86



5 Conclusion

A serious weakness in any negotiated solution between Israel and Palestine is the imbalance of power and the consequent asymmetrical bargaining posi-tion of Palestinians. This too is where international law can play an important role in ensuring a fair outcome consistent with international norms. Although the political outlook looks bleak right now, it will not always remain so. International law is not an a la carte menu that powerful States may pick and choose from depending on what is politically expedient or the whim of a pop-ulist political leader. At the same time, it must be acknowledged that interna-tional humanitarian law was never intended to apply to a 55- year occupation and it is right to question its use in providing a veil of legality for something never envisioned in the first instance. In so doing it makes all States complicit in maintaining the current situation. The problem is what will replace it? Human rights are not relegated to second place in situations of occupation. It remains imperative that international law remain at the forefront of efforts to achieve a just and lasting peace and that international humanitarian law is central to this strategy.



85 ‘What Sanctions Are Being Imposed on Russia Over Ukraine Invasion?’, bbc News, 12

April 2022.

86 Susan Power, ‘Annexation and Human Rights’ in State of Palestine (ed), Looming

Annexation: Israel’s Denial of Palestine’s Right to Exist (2020).





chapter 4


Reducing the Friction: A Functional Analysis of the

Transformed Occupation of the Gaza Strip

Aeyal Gross



In 2004, Israel announced its plan to withdraw all its troops and citizens from the Gaza Strip, claiming that it had “come to the conclusion that there is cur-rently no reliable Palestinian partner with which it can make progress in a two-

sided peace process”.1 The plan stated that “relocation from the Gaza Strip and from an area in Northern Samaria should reduce friction with the Palestinian

population”,2 and that “the completion of the plan will serve to dispel the claims

regarding Israel’s responsibility for the Palestinians in the Gaza Strip”.3 The plan called for Israel to evacuate all existing towns and villages that Israelis inhab-ited and end the permanent presence of Israeli security forces in the Gaza

Strip.4 At the same time, the plan stated that “Israel will guard and monitor the external land perimeter of the Gaza Strip, will continue to maintain exclusive authority in Gaza air space, and will continue to exercise security activity in

the sea off the coast of the Gaza Strip”,5 reserving “its fundamental right of self- defence, both preventive and reactive, including where necessary the use of

force, in respect of threats emanating from the Gaza Strip”.6 On 12 September 2005, on the day that the last Israeli soldiers left, the Israeli army’s Chief of Southern Command, Major General Dan Harel, issued an official decree pro-

claiming the end of military rule in the Gaza Strip.7

Importantly, neither the disengagement plan nor the official decree issued

by Major General Dan Harel explicitly announced the end of the occupation. Furthermore, rather than referring to reducing control over the Gaza Strip, the

70 Gross

disengagement plan referred to reducing friction, alongside a general state-ment on the purpose of the plan as meant “to lead to a better security, political,

economic and demographic situation”.8 The final version of the disengage-

ment plan (unlike the first version that had been made public9) stopped short

of expressly negating the status of the Gaza Strip as occupied territory.10 This has, however, not deterred Israel from arguing that it does not occupy the Gaza Strip in proceedings before the Israeli High Court of Justice, when it seeks to limit its role and responsibilities towards Palestinians living there.

Israel’s carefully crafted language in the disengagement plan and related

decree, its position before its own court, and the de facto evacuation of set-tlements and withdrawal of troops, have triggered an academic debate about the legal status of the Gaza Strip. This chapter will contribute to that debate. It will, on the one hand, point to the problematic nature of the argument that the occupation of the Gaza Strip has ended and, on the other hand, illustrate the limits of a position that the disengagement plan did not have any bearing whatsoever on the legal status of the territory. This chapter will adopt a func-tional, as opposed to conceptual approach to the question of whether the Gaza Strip is still occupied. It will argue that the occupation of the Gaza Strip did not end but was transformed into a form of remotely controlled occupation. The functional approach argued in this chapter understands this transformed form of occupation and is founded in the applicable law of occupation and its

raison d’être.11

This chapter will first describe Israel’s continued exercise of control over

the Gaza Strip after 2005. Second, it will discuss the inflexible binary academic debate around the occupation of the Gaza Strip and develop an alternative functional approach. Third, it will analyse the Israeli Supreme Court’s view of and approach to the occupation of the Gaza Strip. Finally, this chapter will suggest that the debate over whether the Gaza Strip is occupied is reflective of Israel’s indeterminacy towards the status of the Palestinian territory occupied by it in 1967 and explain the role of the functional approach as one that, facing this indeterminacy, aims to create accountability where power is exercised.

occupied territory”.

1 Israel’s Continued Control of the Gaza Strip

International organisations and non- governmental groups, including Palesti-nian human rights groups and the Israeli non- governmental organisation Gisha, have all shed light on Israel’s complex methods of controlling the Gaza Strip after its disengagement in a series of reports. These reports detail how the disengagement did not end Israel’s control but instead changed it in form and

expression.12 The reports point to Israel’s continued substantial control over the Gaza Strip’s land crossings and complete control over the Gaza Strip air-space and territorial waters. Israel’s physical hold over the Gaza Strip enables it to restrict Palestinians from fishing off the coast of the Gaza Strip and prevents Palestinians from using their rich land that is located within the so- called buf-fer zone. Furthermore, Israel controls most of the supply of water, electricity, and fuel to the Palestinian population in the Gaza Strip, as well as cellular and

electronic communication lines.13

The reports also describe sporadic yet not uncommon incursions of Israeli

troops into the Gaza Strip and Israel’s control of the Palestinian Population Registry, as well as its tax policy and tax revenue transfers. Not least, they depict how Israel prevents the passage of people and goods between the West Bank and the Gaza Strip, and the Gaza Strip and the wider world, thereby severely

Disengagement Plan, February 2005; Al- Haq, Al- Haq’s Rejoinder to Gisha’s “Scale of Control”

Position Paper: Israel’s Continued Responsibility as the Occupying Power in the Gaza Strip,

12 December 2011; Al- Haq, One Year after the “Disengagement”: Gaza still Occupied and

under Attack, 17 July 2011; Al- Haq, Shifting Paradigms – Israel’s Enforcement of the Buffer

Zone in the Gaza Strip, 23 August 2011; Gisha, Disengaged Occupiers: The Legal Status of

Gaza, January 2007; Gisha, Scales of Control, November 2011; Gisha, Ten Years Later, 8

September 2015; Gisha, Hand on the Switch: Who’s Responsible for Gaza’s Infrastructure

Crisis?, January 2017; Gisha, 50 Shades of Control, 7 June 2017. See also Sari Bashi and

Kenneth Mann, ‘Control and Responsibility: The Legal Status of the Gaza Strip After the

“Disengagement”’, 14 Hamishpat [College of Management Law Journal] (2010) 35.

Studies (2012) 27.

restricting Palestinians’ freedom of movement and interaction with the out-side world.

The epitome of Israel’s continued control over the Gaza Strip after its disen-

gagement is its imposition of the closure on the Gaza Strip, considered by the UN Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the Palestinian

Territories Occupied since 1967 to be a form of collective punishment.14 Because of the closure imposed on the Gaza Strip airspace and its control over land crossings, any Palestinian in the Gaza Strip wanting to travel from an Israeli or Jordanian airport (there is no airport in the Gaza Strip) needs to go through Israel. The irregularly managed and mostly closed Rafah crossing into Egypt does not offer a sustainable alternative. Palestinians that neverthe-less manage to leave through the Rafah crossing face an arduous, insecure, and long- distance journey through the Sinai desert to Cairo.

1.1 The Imposition of the Closure on the Gaza Strip In the 2006 elections to the Palestinian Legislative Council, Hamas gained a majority of votes and ended the Fatah dominance that had prevailed since the Palestinian Authority’s establishment. In March 2007, a Hamas- led national unity government was formed together with Fatah, the largest group in the Palestine Liberation Organization. This alliance broke down and following its success in an armed struggle with Fatah in June 2007, Hamas assumed overall control of the Gaza Strip. As a result, pa President Abbas, himself a member of Fatah, dismissed the Hamas- led government. Ever since, the West Bank has

been under Fatah control and the Gaza Strip under Hamas.15

In the wake of the internal Palestinian developments in 2007, the Israeli

Security Cabinet authorised restrictions on the movement of goods and people to the “humanitarian minimum” as a means of pressuring the Hamas regime in the Gaza Strip. Israel’s declared intention was to block all goods beyond what it considered “essential for the survival of the civilian population”, halting exports and production, and preventing the passage of items deemed to be “luxu-

ries”.16 The policy was framed by Israel as “sanctions” and later as “economic



14 unhrc, Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the Palestinian

Territories Occupied Since 1967, Michael Lynk, a/ hrc/ 44/ 60, 15 July 2020.

15 See Iain Scobbie, ‘Gaza’ in Elizabeth Wilmshurst (ed), International Law and the

the Territories, idf, to Gisha, Legal Center for Freedom of Movement, 13 January 2010 (on

file with authors).

warfare”.17 In essence, it was designed, according to the Israeli government, to compel Palestinians to pressure Hamas to stop firing rockets, and to release

Gilad Shalit, the Israeli soldier captured by Hamas in June 2006.18 Outgoing goods were banned and incoming goods were allowed only when considered

essential and only in the required minimum.19 Concerning fuel and electricity, the minimum was recognised as a legitimate standard in the Bassiouni judge-

ment of the Israeli Supreme Court that will be discussed in Part 3 below.20 Restrictions were arbitrary, as apparent in the kind of foodstuffs that were pro-

hibited and allowed.21 Ground coriander, for example, was not allowed into the

Gaza Strip, whereas hyssop was permitted.22 Pasta was banned until the direct intervention of John Kerry, then US Senator, who discovered it was prohibited

(though rice was allowed).23 Israel’s policy was formalised on 19 September 2007 in a decision by Israel’s Security Cabinet declaring the Gaza Strip hostile

territory and imposing a closure on it.24

Israel cancelled the ban on most consumer products and raw materials only

in 2010 following Israel’s military operation against the Gaza Freedom Flotilla. However, it has continued to ban the entry of building materials and the export of finished products to markets in Israel and the West Bank, with exceptions made for building materials for internationally sponsored projects, and limited

1.2 Israel’s Control over Land Crossings There are two land crossings for Palestinians to enter and leave the Gaza Strip: the Rafah crossing into Egypt and the Erez crossing into Israel. Through the 2005 Agreed Document on Movement and Access, Israel controlled the movement of people not only from Gaza into Israel, but also between Gaza and Egypt via the Rafah crossing, despite the disengagement and until 2007. During that period, Israel monitored Palestinians’ movement via cameras and intervened in decisions about entry into the Gaza Strip and whether the cross-

ing would stay open.26 After Palestinians attacked an Israeli military outpost at Kerem Shalom and captured in June 2006 an Israeli soldier, Gilad Shalit, Israel

kept the crossing mostly closed.27 After June 2007, when Hamas formed the de facto government in the Gaza Strip, Israel froze the implementation of the

Agreed Document on Movement and Access.28 Ever since, and given changes

of government in Egypt, the crossing has often been closed.29 From mid- 2012 to mid- 2013, the crossing operated regularly and some 40,000 crossings in both directions were recorded each month. From July 2013 to May 2018, it remained mostly closed. Pressure on Egypt to open it, generated by the Great March of Return protests and deteriorating humanitarian conditions in Gaza, led to the crossing being open five days per week, with a monthly average of 11,000

entries and exits recorded in the first half of 2019.30

In an effort to circumvent the siege, Palestinians have attempted to

breach the border31 and dig tunnels under it.32 They have also entered ad hoc

Egypt- Hamas arrangements that led to unpredictable and irregular openings

for brief periods in response to pressures from both sides of the border.33

The question of who can and who has the authority to open and close the

Rafah land crossing changes over time.34 Clearly, under the Agreed Document on Movement and Access, Israel had exercised powers over the Rafah crossing alongside other parties, but the scope and type of control that it had changed after June 2007. After that, Egypt and Hamas appear to have had the great-est control of the Rafah crossing. The form of exercise of power by Israel also changed after the flotilla incident in 2010 when Israel partly eased the siege,

especially regarding the import of goods.35

1.3 Israel’s “Naval Blockade” on the Gaza Strip Israel has exercised full control over the territorial waters off the Gaza Strip coast since the beginning of the occupation. With only a few isolated excep-

tions, Israel has never allowed any vessel to enter or leave these waters,36 and it has imposed significant restrictions on fishing activities along the

Gaza Strip coast.37 Notwithstanding, Israel declared in January 2009, during the broad- scale military operation called “Cast Lead”, that it was imposing a “naval blockade” on the Gaza Strip. The naval blockade declaration remains

in force.38

The Turkel Commission, appointed by the Israeli government after the Gaza

Freedom Flotilla operation to examine the terms of the naval blockade, held that the idf’s actions were no longer subject to the law of belligerent occupa-tion after the disengagement. Instead, given the “armed conflict” with armed Palestinian groups inside the Gaza Strip, the law of naval warfare now sup-

posedly applied.39 The alleged recent legal form of Israel’s control – a naval blockade as part of an armed conflict – fails, however, to camouflage the fact

2 The Academic Debate over the Status of the Gaza Strip: Diverging

Positions and the Need for a Functional Approach

Part 1 above has shown that while Israel’s withdrawal of settlers and military from the Gaza Strip changed the nature and degree of Israel’s control, it did not end it. This section will present academic views on Israel’s status as occupier in the Gaza Strip after the “disengagement” and suggest a functional approach, according to which Israel is bound by the duties of an Occupying Power to the extent that its actions affect the lives of Palestinians in the Gaza Strip.

2.1 The Existing Academic Debate Yuval Shany has suggested a three- pronged test to examine whether a territory in general, and the Gaza Strip in particular, is occupied. The test places greater emphasis on the potential for effective control than on actual control by con-sidering (1) the physical presence of hostile troops in the area, (2) the ability of troops to exercise effective powers of government, and (3) the incapability of

the legitimate government to exercise effective powers of government.40 These three conditions are cumulative and therefore, according to Shany, an occupa-

tion is terminated when one factor is negated.41

In Shany’s view, the test for determining the end of occupation represents a

“mirror image” of the test for determining its beginning. Shany argues that the first condition of occupation, being the physical presence of hostile forces, has been reversed and the territory is thus not occupied when hostile forces are not present. He rejects the idea that effective control can be exercised with-out the actual and continuing presence of troops on the ground and argues that without them an occupier could not be expected to execute its obligations under jus in bello – maintain law and order, provide basic services, and so forth. Shany therefore concludes that despite Israel’s power to influence events in the Gaza Strip through its control of the airspace, maritime zones, and most border entries, its ability to enforce day- to- day law and order in the Gaza Strip

is minimal to non- existent.42 As for the second condition, Shany acknowledges



40 Yuval Shany, ‘Faraway, So Close: The Legal Status of Gaza after Israel’s Disengagement’, 8

Yearbook of International Humanitarian Law (2005) 369, pages 376– 777.

41 Ibid., pages 378– 379.

42 Ibid., pages 381– 382.

A Functional Analysis of the Transformed Occupation 77

that Israel retains some powerful points of leverage over Palestinians’ lives in the Gaza Strip, but that it can hardly govern the Gaza Strip effectively and con-

trol day- to- day life.43 Regarding the third condition, Shany notes that the pa (at the time of writing Hamas is more significant as it exercises de facto govern-ment control) operates tens of thousands of governments officials and security personnel who assume the daily functions of government in the Gaza Strip. Even if its actual exercise of control is unsatisfactory or insufficient, the pa is

in a much better position than Israel to exercise authority.44 It is important to note that Shany’s application of his test to Israel’s control over the Gaza Strip was done in 2005 and pre- dates Israel’s imposition of the closure. In any case, Shany concludes that Israel may still have international obligations vis- à- vis the Gaza Strip derived from the laws of armed conflict applicable to Israeli military operations in and around the Gaza Strip, from the general law of obli-gations, and from human rights law, but that it cannot execute its obligations

to maintain law and order and provide basic services.45 Shany’s view can be criticized based on the interpretation of the law of occupation which in accor-dance with Article 42 of the Hague Regulations puts emphasis on the ability to

exercise control.46 A discussion of the international caselaw on the existence

of occupation47 and analysis of his view according to it, exceeds the scope of this chapter. That said, Shany’s reliance on existing caselaw is too restrictive.

Iain Scobbie holds a different view than Shany’s. One significant differ-

ence with Shany is that Scobbie separates the question of whether an existing occupation has been terminated from the question of whether an occupa-tion has been established. Scobbie seemingly concedes that it is necessary for ground forces to create and maintain control at the beginning of an occupa-tion. However, its termination, Scobbie suggests, may well involve different considerations, and the conditions required to end an occupation are not as clearly delineated in the governing instruments, as those determining when

an occupation has been established.48 While, according to the traditional test,

43 Ibid.

44 Ibid., page 383.

45 Ibid. See also Yuval Shany, ‘The Law Applicable to Non- Occupied Gaza: A Comment

on Bassiouni v. Prime Minister of Israel’, 42 Israel Law Review (2009) 101.

46 United States Military Tribunal, Trial of Wilhelm List and Others, 1947– 1948, 8 Law Reports

of Trials of War Criminals (1949) page 17.

47 See Aeyal Gross, The Writing on the Wall, Rethinking the International Law of Occupation

(cup 2017) 61– 113.

48 Iain Scobbie, ‘An Intimate Disengagement: Israel’s Withdrawal from Gaza, the Law of

Occupation and of Self- Determination’ in Eugene Cotran and Martin Lau (eds), Yearbook

of Islamic and Middle Eastern Law 2004– 2005, Volume 11, 2005– 2006 (Brill Publishers

2006) 6– 7.

78 Gross

occupation ends when authority passes back into the hands of the displaced sovereign, Scobbie suggests, that in order to claim that this condition has been met, the transfer must be shown to be genuine, and the post- occupation gov-ernment must be able to exercise sovereign authority. To determine whether a post- occupation entity can exercise actual independence over the territory in question, Scobbie requires that not only factual but also normative factors

be taken into account.49 In Scobbie’s view, to consider Israel’s withdrawal of its ground troops in isolation from its continued control of the airspace is to

ignore the context.50 He argues that the pa does not have the exclusive gov-ernment powers characteristic of a sovereign and should rather be seen as an

administration to which the occupant devolved competence.51 To be legally effective a termination of occupation needs to conform to the requirements

of self- determination.52 Israel’s unilateral withdrawal shows no respect for self- determination, either as a process (through the free expression of the Palestinians’ will) or in its substance (the occupant’s duty to maintain territo-

rial integrity).53 Scobbie notes that the Gaza Strip is neither formally nor actu-ally independent. Indeed, according to the disengagement plan, Israel retains control over the Gaza Strip’s maritime zones and airspace, and authorities in the Gaza Strip are unable to exercise control “to the exclusion of any other

State, the function of a State”.54 Scobbie understands the disengagement as an attempt on Israel’s part to retain powers over the territory while divesting itself of its responsibility towards the Palestinian population in that territory. In his view, a State cannot unilaterally exempt itself from the performance of

its duties under international law, as is the case here.55

Shany and Scobbie, do agree that a decision must be made on whether the

Gaza Strip remains occupied after the disengagement, and both consider the relevant powers of Israel and the Palestinians. Unlike Shany, who adheres to a factual approach, Scobbie suggests a normative approach, that places the dis-engagement’s respect for the principle of self- determination at the centre. The

52 Ibid., page 21.

A Functional Analysis of the Transformed Occupation 79

contrast between these two positions may be viewed as a reincarnation of the Oslo debate, when scholars were divided on Israel’s continued occupation of

areas controlled by the pa.56 In fact, the debate on the occupation of the entire Palestinian territory, or parts thereof, is as old as the occupation; after 1967, Israel consistently argued that the Fourth Geneva Convention did not apply in the territories it occupied during the war as they did not belong to a sovereign State. The Israeli Supreme Court held that the Hague Regulations apply but left open the question of the applicability of the Fourth Geneva Convention

based on the notion that it was not incorporated into Israeli law.57 This Israeli engineered indeterminacy about the status of the Palestinian territory could be viewed as an attempt to evade some of its responsibilities towards the Palestinian population while retaining power over the territory. In this sense, Israel’s disengagement from the Gaza Strip adds to the indeterminacy of the occupation.

In light of Israel’s rejection of the norms that place on it the responsibility

to provide for the local population and embracing of the norms that allow it to exercise powers in accordance with its perceived military needs, Scobbie’s position emerges as more persuasive. Nevertheless, like Shany, he anchors his view in the occupation/ non- occupation antithesis and rests it on the

sovereignty- occupation dichotomy58 by insisting that only the establishment of sovereignty can signal the end of occupation. Scobbie rightfully rejects the notion that the end of occupation is a mirror image of its beginning, but the claim that only the establishment of sovereignty can signal the end of occu-pation implies a binary structure that cannot fully capture situations such as that of the post- disengagement Gaza Strip. A third option, one that recognises the existence of non- binary situations but precludes a pick and choose mecha-

nism that allows for the evasion of responsibilities is suggested here.59

(cup 2017) 181– 204.

59 For a more nuanced approach, that considers that occupation continues to exist, see also

Yoram Dinstein, The International Law of Belligerent Occupation (cup 2019) 297– 303. For

a discussion of Dinstein’s position and where it stands in relationship to my own position,

see Aeyal Gross, The Writing on the Wall, Rethinking the International Law of Occupation

(cup 2017) 210– 211.

2.2 A Functional Approach to the Legal Status of the Gaza Strip The polarised debate on the legal status of the Gaza Strip illustrates the limits of the framework of the discussion. Occupation seems in this debate to be one

of the “concepts of jurisprudence” that Felix Cohen60 describes as “existing in their absolute purity, freed from all entangling alliances with human life”

and creating a “heaven of legal concepts”.61 Cohen describes the approach in

which legal concepts are “thingified” as “transcendental nonsense”.62 In this approach, legal analysis ignores practical questions of value or positive fact and takes refuge in “legal problems” that can always be answered by manipu-lating legal concepts in certain approved ways that Cohen argues bar the way

to “intelligent investigation of social facts and social policy”.63 Occupation, which as a legal term is a creation of law, can thus be considered one more of

the “magic solving words”, as Cohen called them.64

“Magic solving words” and debates around whether a situation falls or does

not fall within a certain definition of occupation inevitably result in a circu-lar exercise that is detached from the reality of lived lives. In Cohen’s legal realist suggestion, norms should not follow from abstract concepts but rather the opposite. For instance, rather than saying that a labour union can be sued because it is “a person” or “a quasi- corporation”, one ought to take the approach that a labour union is “a person” or “quasi- corporation” because it can be

sued.65 Whereas the former determination is coined in “transcendental” terms by asserting what sounds like a proposition but cannot be confirmed or refuted by positive evidence or ethical argument, the latter avoids an argument formu-

lated as a vicious circle.66 In the current context, such analysis has resulted in contradictory “factual” and “notional” concepts around the end of occupation,

or between a “de facto and a de jure” end of occupation.67

The alternative to the world of “heavenly legal concepts” and “transcen-

dental nonsense” is in Cohen’s terms the “functional approach” that “rep-resents an assault upon all dogmas and devices that cannot be translated into terms of actual experience” and from which “all sorts of empirical decisions



60 Felix S Cohen, ‘Transcendental Nonsense and the Functional Approach’, 35 Columbia Law

Review (1935) 809.

61 Ibid., page 809.

62 Ibid., page 811.

63 Ibid., page 820.

64 Ibid., pages 820– 821.

65 Ibid., page 813.

66 Ibid., page 814.

67 icrc, Expert Meeting: Occupation and Other Forms of Administration of Foreign Territory,

2012, page 27.

A Functional Analysis of the Transformed Occupation 81

are supposed to flow”.68 “If the functionalists are correct”, argues Cohen, “the

meaning of a definition is found in its consequences”.69 To paraphrase Cohen’s approach to contract, we should consider whether or not liability should be attached to certain acts when considering if an occupation exists. This is an ethical question that cannot be answered in purely legal terms since that

would make it circular.70

A functional approach takes into account the major purpose of the law

of occupation to protect people living under the rule of a foreign entity and determines that norms from the law of occupation apply as long as the foreign power exercises control. At the same time, a functional approach is necessary in order to avoid an abstract determination that the Gaza Strip is occupied, while ignoring the elements on which Israel does not exercise control.

To illustrate how the discussion on whether occupation exists or not has

been taking place in conceptual terms, consider the landmark Bassiouni case (addressed in detail in Part 3), in which the Israeli Supreme Court, sitting as

High Court of Justice, held that Israel no longer occupies the Gaza Strip.71 In that case, the court held that Israel is not bound by the duties of an Occupying Power concerning the supply of electricity and fuel and may consequently limit deliveries. The court’s finding was supposedly derived from the (heavenly) legal concept of “occupation”, or rather a perceived lack thereof. While the hcj’s dic-tum in this case left room for moving beyond the conceptual approach toward a more functional one, its ratio decidendi remained within the bounds of the conceptual approach in ways that does not make Israel accountable for the power it exercises over electricity in Gaza. Other judicial cases point more

explicitly in a similar direction.72 I address them in detail in my book73 and will

only mention here the Aerial Bombardment decision74 by the Ethiopia Eritrea Claim Commission. The Ethiopia Eritrea Claim Commission held that it was

68 Felix S Cohen, ‘Transcendental Nonsense and the Functional Approach’, 35 Columbia Law

Review (1935) 809, pages 822– 823.

69 Ibid., page 838.

70 Ibid., pages 840– 842.

71 hcj 9132/ 07, Jaber Al- Bassiouni Ahmed et al. v Prime Minister and Minister of Defence (30

January 2008) para 2.

72 This in contrast to other cases which emphasise physical presence of the occupying mil-

itary and make the requirements for the determination of the existence of occupation

more rigid. See ECtHR, Sargsyan v Azerbaijan, Judgement, 16 June 2015.

73 Aeyal Gross, The Writing on the Wall, Rethinking the International Law of Occupation (cup

2017) 61– 113.

74 Eritrea- Ethiopia Claims Commission, Partial Award, Western Front, Aerial Bombardment

and Related Claims, Eritrea’s Claims 1, 3, 5, 9– 13, 14. 21, 25, 26 Between The State of Eritrea and

The Federal Democatic Republic of Ethiopia, 19 December 2005, para 27. 82 Gross

not necessary to determine abstractly whether an occupation exists to deter-mine the occupier’s duties. Instead, it was enough to hold that where Ethiopia, even if it ruled the territory in question for a short time, exercised power as

an occupier, occupation existed and duties followed.75 In this logic, Ethiopia’s duties derived from its actual exercise of power, not from locating the situation into an abstract concept of “occupation” or “sovereignty”.

Epistemologically, the question of whether occupation exists is often inde-

terminate, and this indeterminacy often becomes a form of control. The func-tional approach proposed, and a version of which the icrc eventually adopted, may be used to ensure rather than circumvent accountability, while recognis-ing that some actions are beyond the Occupying Power’s effective control and

others are undesirable.76

Arguably, the functional approach may encourage occupiers to exercise

fewer powers to avoid responsibility. This is not a problem per se if this means that, in the Israeli/ Palestinian context, Palestinians have more control over their own lives. Clearly, however, the functional approach must not mean that



75 Aeyal Gross, The Writing on the Wall, Rethinking the International Law of Occupation (cup

2017) 75– 78.

76 I first introduced the “functional approach” to occupation in a conference in 2007 and

then in writing in an opening post for an online symposium that addressed my proposal.

See Aeyal Gross, ‘Rethinking Occupation: The Functional Approach’, Opinio Juris, 23 April

2012. For responses and the full symposium, see Kevin Jon Heller, ‘Symposium on the

Functional Approach to the Law of Occupation’, Opinio Juris, 23 April 2012. In 2015, the

icrc adopted this position stating that, while in principle the law on the end of occu-

pation should mirror its beginning, “in some specific and rather exceptional cases […]

the law of occupation may continue to apply within the territorial and functional limits

of such competences” and effective control may continue – for the purpose of the law

of occupation – in spite of the lack of physical presence of foreign forces in the territory

concerned. In the same document, the icrc also recognised that geographical contiguity

between belligerents could facilitate the remote exercise of effective control, and the con-

tinued application of relevant provisions of the law of occupation is all the more import-

ant in such scenario. See icrc, International Humanitarian Law and the Challenges of

Contemporary Armed Conflict – Report of the 32 International Conference of the Red Cross

and Red Crescent, October 2015, page 12. This view was adapted in the Commentary of

2016 to the First Geneva Convention. icrc, Commentary on the First Geneva Convention

of 12 August 1949: Convention I for the Amelioration of the Condition of the Wounded and

Sick in Armed Forces in the Field (cup 2016) pages 304– 313. While recent reality has led

some scholars and the icrc to recognise the need for a more “functional” approach to the

beginning or the end of occupation, my view on the “functional” approach is not limited

to exceptional cases of beginning or end of occupation. Indeterminacy about the exis-

tence of occupation and the need for an anti- conceptual approach is typical of many, if

not most, post- wwii occupations. See Gisha, (Re)- introducing the Functional Approach to

Occupation, 15 December 2015.

A Functional Analysis of the Transformed Occupation 83

an Occupying Power is relieved of its duties when no one else is exercising them, or when it prevents a legitimate sovereign from exercising them. Article 43 of the Hague Regulations remains instructive here. Similarly, the functional approach does not deny that several different entities may be considered responsible in some cases. Importantly, it should not be interpreted in a way that strengthens the “pick and choose” approach. The functional approach aims to ensure that powers that exercise control, even in scenarios that do not look like “classic” occupation, are prevented from avoiding responsibility for their actions by denying, transforming, or relinquishing part of their control.

The functional approach exposes a number of characteristics that, as noted,

were already inherent in the disengagement plan: (1) the continuation of the pre- disengagement ambiguity concerning the situation in general and the cur-rent status of this specific area in particular with the indeterminacy of the sit-uation, in itself a form of control, (2) the circumstances allowing Israel to go on exercising some of an occupant’s powers while negating the applicability of norms protecting the local population, and (3) the continuing shift to fric-tionless control – recall the disengagement’s purposes as including “reducing friction” between the Israeli army and the local Palestinian population. In this regard, the disengagement must be understood in tandem with other attempts at reducing friction, especially the Oslo Accords, the building of the Wall, and the handing over of the management of some of the Israeli checkpoints in the

occupied Palestinian territory to private security companies.77

Some of the arguments raised as part of the binary debate show the recur-

rence of these characteristics. Shany’s position, requiring the continued ground presence of a hostile force for an occupier to execute its obligations, led him to conclude that Israel’s ability to enforce day- to- day law and order in the Gaza Strip was minimal to non- existing. The duties that Shany notes Israel cannot fulfill, seem to be the ones that Scobbie would argue Israel must fulfill because it could not unilaterally exempt itself from its duties as an Occupying Power. This polarity leaves no room for an Occupying Power that exercises a transformed form of control.

If one was to endorse a binary approach and hold that the Gaza Strip is occu-

pied, then Article 43 of the Hague Regulations would stipulate that Israel not only has the power, but also the duty to act in the Gaza Strip to protect civilians from the local government. According to Article 43, the Israeli army should on this basis have intervened to restore public order and safety in the Gaza Strip



77 For a detailed discussion see Aeyal Gross, The Writing on the Wall, Rethinking the

International Law of Occupation (cup 2017) 253– 256. 84 Gross

on various occasions, in particular during the 2007 clash between Fatah and

Hamas.78 In one of the most often cited decisions on the existence of occu-pation, the Hostages case from the post- World War ii International Military Tribunal, the tribunal held that an occupiers’ power “is as great as his respon-

sibility”.79 In this context, this would mean that given the occupier’s responsi-bility under Article 43, its power includes the right – and in fact the duty – to act in the Gaza Strip in case of internal hostilities. We can discern from the

Ethiopia Eritrea Claim Commission’s Aerial Bombardment decision80 an oppo-site proposition. In other words, that an occupier’s responsibility should be as great as its power, or in fact as great as the power it exercises over the territory and its residents. The functional approach suggests that Israel may have no duty to intervene in incidents and developments that are outside its purview (movement of goods and people are undoubtedly within its purview). But this example also shows that a finding that Israel continues to occupy the Gaza Strip is a double- edged sword in that greater responsibility may as a result be justified in contexts where Israel’s interventions are unwanted, such as in the struggle between Fatah- Hamas or even in mundane matters (consider the run-ning schools).

The binary approach is thus a dubious tool for anyone seeking to guarantee

protection to a civilian population living under occupation, not only because deciding that the law of occupation does not apply while power is still exercised entails far- reaching consequences, but also because of the equally far- reaching consequences of deciding that it does. Those who advocate greater protection for Palestinians through the application of the law of occupation may thus, paradoxically, undermine the exercise of Palestinian self- governance, however limited, and reinforce Israel’s power. Given the lack of full and substantive self- determination (which in the Palestinian case must include effective self- government in both the West Bank and Gaza Strip), it may appear at first sight preferable to characterise the situation as one of occupation and assert the full scope of duties associated with occupation. But is reinforcing the occupant’s right and duty to intervene always desirable? A functional approach may help to avoid this double bind and maintain the framework of the law of occupation.



78 On the dispute, see Nathan J Brown, ‘The Hamas- Fatah Conflict: Shallow but Wide’, 34(2)

Fletcher Forum of World Affairs (2010) 35.

79 United States Military Tribunal, Trial of Wilhelm List and Others, 1947– 1948, 8 Law Reports

of Trials of War Criminals (1949), page 57.

80 Eritrea- Ethiopia Claims Commission, Partial Award, Western Front, Aerial Bombardment

and Related Claims, Eritrea’s Claims 1, 3, 5, 9– 13, 14. 21, 25, 26 Between The State of Eritrea and

The Federal Democratic Republic of Ethiopia, 19 December 2005, para 27.

A Functional Analysis of the Transformed Occupation 85

3 Israel’s Supreme Court on the Status of the Gaza Strip

Sitting as the Israeli High Court of Justice, Israel’s Supreme Court has addressed the status of the Gaza Strip in several rulings. The most important ruling, in the Bassiouni case, dealt with Israel’s decision to reduce or limit the supply of fuel and electricity to the Gaza Strip as part of the Israeli government’s sanctions against Hamas’s rule. Israel declared Hamas a terrorist organisation that had

taken control of the Gaza Strip and turned it into hostile territory.81

Both parties to the proceedings agreed that Israel supplied sixty percent

of the electricity needed for the Gaza Strip82 but disagreed on the acceptable consequence of restricting electricity. The petitioners argued that the restric-tions would cause electricity cuts in hospitals, stop the pumping of clean water to the civilian population, and inflict serious and irreversible damage to essen-

tial humanitarian needs of the Gaza Strip.83 They also pointed to the situation at the time of the litigation. The Israeli Air Force had bombed the local power plant and local authorities had already been compelled to cut power every day for several hours. The power cuts affected the functioning of essential services. Palestinians in the Gaza Strip had also been prevented from receiving clean drinking water in their homes and the restrictions had interrupted the func-

tioning of water and sewage pumps.84

The respondents claimed that reducing the electricity supply would not

affect the basic humanitarian needs of Gaza Strip residents.85 They also argued that Palestinians had conceded that, should restrictions be imposed, they could regulate loads by reducing consumption in the distribution area of a cer-

tain line without affecting the supply for humanitarian needs.86

The hcj addressed the extant situation and noted that “since September

2005, Israel no longer has effective control over what happens in the Gaza

Strip”.87 “Military rule that had applied in the past in this territory”, noted the hcj, “came to an end by a decision of the government, and Israeli soldiers are no longer stationed in the territory on a permanent basis, nor are they in

charge of what happens there”.88 Given these circumstances, the hcj held

81 hcj 9132/ 07, Jaber Al- Bassiouni Ahmed et al. v Prime Minister and Minister of Defence (30

January 2008) paras 2– 6.

82 Ibid., para 6.

83 Ibid., para 5.

84 Ibid., para 7.

85 Ibid., para 6.

86 Ibid., para 10.

87 Ibid., para 12.

88 Ibid.

86 Gross

that “the State of Israel does not have a general duty to ensure the welfare of the residents of the Gaza Strip or to maintain public order in the Gaza Strip according to the entirety of the law of belligerent occupation in international

law”.89 Furthermore, the hcj held that Israel has no effective ability, in its pres-

ent position, to enforce order and manage civilian life in the Gaza Strip.90 The hcj made this determination without citing any of the relevant international caselaw on the existence of occupation. It referred to what it saw as both the lack of effective control and the lack of capability to exercise such control. But the hcj stopped short of determining that Israel does not owe Palestinians in the Gaza Strip any duties. Instead, it determined that:

In the prevailing circumstances, the main obligations of the State of Israel

relating to the residents of the Gaza Strip derive from the state of armed

conflict that exists between it and the Hamas organization that controls

the Gaza Strip. These obligations also derive from the degree of control

exercised by the State of Israel over the border crossings between it and

the Gaza Strip, as well as from the relationship that was created between

Israel and the territory of the Gaza Strip after the years of Israeli military

rule in the territory, as a result of which the Gaza Strip is currently almost

completely dependent upon the supply of electricity from Israel.91

Regarding the first set of these duties, the one deriving from the state of armed conflict, the hcj cited international humanitarian law provisions that the respondents had stated that they applied in this case, such as Article 23 of the Fourth Geneva Convention determining that a party to a conflict must allow the free passage of consignments intended for civilians of the other party. The hcj noted, however, that the respondents had argued this is a very limited obligation relating to medical equipment and foodstuffs, clothing and med-icine intended for children under the age of fifteen, and expectant mothers. The respondents also cited Article 70 of Additional Protocol i, claiming that it constitutes customary international law, which imposes a general and broader obligation requiring parties to allow the rapid and unimpeded passage of essen-tial goods for the civilian population. They also cited Article 54 of Additional Protocol i, prohibiting the starvation of civilians as a method of warfare as well



89 Ibid.

90 Ibid.

91 Ibid.

A Functional Analysis of the Transformed Occupation 87

as any attack of installations required by the civilian population, including

foodstuffs, agricultural areas, and drinking water installations.92

Israel’s High Court of Justice thus accepted the State’s position that the

applicable humanitarian duties are those between parties to an armed con-flict, but not the broader duties of an occupying army. In the State’s interpre-tation, these humanitarian obligations did not require it to allow passage of “non- essential goods” or goods in amounts that exceeded “what is required for

basic humanitarian needs”.93 The State argued that the amount of electricity entering the Gaza Strip is indeed sufficient for the proper functioning of all the

humanitarian services in the territory.94 The hcj did not elaborate on the two other sources of obligations it cited and determined that the planned reduc-tion of the supply of electricity does not breach Israel’s humanitarian obliga-tions within the context of the armed conflict taking place between it and the

Hamas organisation that controls the Gaza Strip.95 It rejected the petition, holding that the amount of electricity supplied by Israel is capable of satisfying

the humanitarian needs of the Gaza Strip.96

This decision can be considered from several perspectives. On the factual

side, the hcj accepted the State’s arguments that the supply of electricity could be reduced without causing power stoppages in hospitals, affecting water sup-plies and essential services, and disrupting life for the entire civilian popula-tion. Other than citing to the fact that the State had submitted that Palestinian officials had informed it, that they could manage the load reductions without harm to humanitarian needs and purposes, and relying on a submission by the Israeli army, the ruling does not explain why the hcj found the army’s position more convincing than that of the petitioners.

More generally, the hcj accepted that the law of occupation in its entirety

does not apply to the case, because military rule in the area had come to an end by a decision of the government and Israeli soldiers were no longer stationed in the territory on a permanent basis, or in charge of what happened there. The hcj determined that Israel has no effective control of the Gaza Strip and there-fore it has no obligation towards the Palestinian population nor is it capable of maintaining public order in the Gaza Strip and ensuring the welfare of its resi-dents. The hcj did not explain what theory or facts led it to this conclusion and it did not engage any of the existing caselaw on the issue. By holding that Israel



92 Ibid., para 13.

93 Ibid., paras 15– 16.

94 Ibid., para 16.

95 Ibid., para 18.

96 Ibid., para 22.

88 Gross

has no effective control, the hcj is ignoring other forms of control discussed throughout this chapter, whereby the occupying force disavows its responsibil-ity (dismantles the military regime) and shifts to a form of control that entails minimal friction (through the absence of a permanent military presence). As for the hcj’s determination that, in its present position, the Israeli army has no capability to enforce order and manage civilian life in the Gaza Strip, the question is whether these issues are relevant to the matter at hand. Endorsing a functional rather than a binary view of occupation would have clarified that Israel has duties deriving from its control that may not encompass the mainte-nance of public order, a question that, in any event, was not before the court.

Given the location of the Israeli army and its proven ability to enter the

Gaza Strip at will, one might question whether it lacks the power needed to restore order in the Gaza Strip. What is unquestionable is that Israel does con-trol much of life in Gaza, as the case before the hcj actually shows. The hcj ruling, that Israel has no effective control while it reviews Israeli actions that illustrate extensive control of civilian life in the Gaza Strip, is somewhat para-doxical. By implicitly allowing such actions, the hcj itself, becomes part of the control mechanism.

Notably, the hcj did not hold that Israel has no duties towards the Gaza

Strip. It held that Israel is not to be bound by the broader duties entailed in the law of occupation. This caveat significantly narrows the duties of the con-trolling State toward the population affected by its acts, as illustrated below. The two additional sources of duties, on which the hcj did not elaborate, are duties deriving from Israel’s control over its border crossings with the Gaza Strip, and duties following from the relationship with the Gaza Strip after the many years of Israeli military rule, which have resulted in the area’s almost complete dependence on Israel for its electricity supply. In its ruling, the hcj limits the second source of duties to those following from Israel’s control of the border crossings. Yet, the statement that the laws of occupation do not apply “in their entirety” could suggest that these duties apply to any form of control exercised by Israel – crossings, the airspace, the waterways etcetera – beyond the basic humanitarian duties applying between combatant parties. Taken to its logical conclusion then, this determination could end up under-mining the rationale of the hcj’s ruling itself, which dealt with an issue that Israel does control. As such, the Bassiouni decision can also be seen as poten-tially supporting a functional rather than a conceptual approach to occupa-tion. A deeper analysis would have viewed control of the border crossing as part of Israel’s extant powers in Gaza, approaching its control of the fuel and electricity supply as one instance of a broader form of control, implying duties A Functional Analysis of the Transformed Occupation 89

more akin to those of an occupier than to those of a combatant party. The hcj, however, did not do this.

The third source deals with duties derived from the relationship created

after the many years of Israeli military rule that resulted in Gaza’s almost com-plete dependence on Israel for the supply of electricity. These obligations, which may at times be incumbent on the occupier even after occupation ends, have been described by some scholars as post- occupation duties. A notion per-

haps worth developing.97 The failure of the hcj’s decision is starkly evident here; although its discussion of how much fuel and electricity the Gaza Strip will receive demonstrates Israel’s continued control, the hcj’s finding suggests that the opposite is true. In this case, then, control (dis)appears – present and absent at the same time.

The effect of the Bassiouni decision was felt in a subsequent case concern-

ing Israel’s Law on the Incarceration of Unlawful Combatants from 2002.98 This law allows for the internment of people who take part in hostile activities against Israel or are members of a force that conducts hostile activities against Israel and who do not have Prisoner of War status, according to the Third

Geneva Convention.99 Given the law’s referral to the Third Geneva Convention and its declared statement of purpose – to facilitate the internment of unlawful combatants in a way that fits Israel’s obligations under international humani-tarian law – the hcj held that the law is intended to apply only to members of a terrorist organisation acting against the security of the State outside Israel, but not to citizens and residents of Israel who threaten its security. The hcj

held in regard to the latter that other legal means are available.100 In an obiter dictum, the court noted that, given that the West Bank is under Israel’s effective control, residents of the West Bank should probably be subject to adminis-trative detention as regulated by military legislation in the West Bank, rather

than to the Incarceration of Unlawful Combatants Law.101 The case involved the detention of two Palestinians from the Gaza Strip who had previously been

90 Gross

detained under orders issued by the Israeli Military Commander of the Gaza Strip as an occupied territory, which allowed for administrative detention. On 12 September 2005, however, on the day the Southern Command issued a proc-lamation about the end of the Israeli military government in the Gaza Strip, the appellants were issued detention warrants signed by the idf Chief of Staff,

under the Incarceration of Unlawful Combatants Law.102 Holding that the law in question is only relevant to external agents, the hcj noted that “external agents” may include residents of an enemy State but also residents of the Gaza Strip, which is no longer under belligerent occupation. Following the end of the Israeli military government in Gaza in 2005, Israel has no permanent pres-ence in the Gaza Strip and no possibility of fulfilling the duties of an occu-pier under international law. Any attempt by Israel to fulfil these duties, held the hcj, including the duty to maintain public order and security, will require complex and lengthy military operations. According to the hcj, Gaza should not be considered an area under belligerent occupation in terms of interna-tional law in the absence of real potential for effective control, even though Israel has some duties toward Gaza residents given the sui generis nature of

the circumstances.103

In making this determination, the Supreme Court relied, inter alia, on the

International Court of Justice ruling in the Armed Activities case.104 It held that the icj had emphasised the importance of physical presence for the existence of occupation. Since this is no longer the case in regard to the Gaza Strip, it considered Palestinians living there as foreign agents to whom this law may

apply.105 This shift in status implies that Palestinians from the Gaza Strip that are detained by Israel enjoy less protection after the disengagement. This downgrading of protection is significant because, compared to other Israeli laws, the Incarceration of Unlawful Combatants Law ensures appreciably less

guarantees of due process.106 The courts’ distinction between the West Bank and the Gaza Strip for the purpose of this law clearly reveals that the deci-sion that the Gaza Strip is no longer under Israeli control allows for harsher



102 Ibid., para 1.

103 Ibid., para 11.

104 icj, Armed Activities in the Territory of the Congo (Democratic Republic of Congo v Uganda)

(Judgement) icj Reports 116 (2005). See the discussion and critique of this case in Aeyal

Gross, The Writing on the Wall, Rethinking the International Law of Occupation (cup

2017) 75– 78.

105 CrimA 6659/ 06, Riad Saadai Abed Al Hamid Iyad v The State of Israel (2008) para 11.

(cup 2017) 221– 222.

A Functional Analysis of the Transformed Occupation 91

measures to be taken against its residents, who can still be subject to detention

in ways less respectful of due process.107

In the same case, the petitioners argued that Israel should have released

them at the end of the military regime in the Gaza Strip. The court disagreed and found that Israel had no such duty as long as they represent a security risk, especially since the armed conflict continued. Israel’s ability to detain Palestinians from the Gaza Strip thus mutated into a harsher form, which was justified by the new situation being akin to hostilities between Israel and a “foreign” power.

The hcj put emphasis in this case on the army’s physical presence. By con-

trast, a dynamic, functional, and normative approach to control all its man-ifestations are not necessarily based on permanent ground troop presence. Although Hamas controls the daily running of government services, it is Israel that controls who and what enters and leaves the area. Israel significantly

shapes life in Gaza108 and its responsibilities should correspond to that abil-ity. Consider further the field of education. Israel may not control the selec-tion and supervision of schoolteachers in the Gaza Strip or the curriculum. It does affect, however, the right to education through its restrictions on the movement of people and goods. The hcj has received petitions regarding Palestinian students who want to study elsewhere, teachers who want to teach in the Gaza Strip, schoolbooks, materials needed to build schools, and so forth. The functional approach would view Israel’s responsibility in this sphere as that of an occupier, but commensurate to its power.

An exhaustive discussion of the growing body of caselaw is beyond the

scope of this chapter.109 Just to illustrate the discussion on education above, it is worth mentioning that the hcj rejected twice the petition of a group of students who had received scholarships to study occupational therapy in the

West Bank, a course not available in the Gaza Strip.110 Israel classified indi-viduals aged 16– 35 as a risk group and banned their entry into the West Bank

the law.

even though, individually, they did not pose a security risk. The State argued that, even if these students entered the West Bank without any intention of engaging in terrorist activities, they might be influenced by the surroundings. In its submission, the State distinguished between the Gaza Strip as no lon-ger being under Israeli belligerent occupation, and the West Bank as an area that still was, with Israel holding overall security responsibility. Israel refused to treat this as a humanitarian case and allow the students entry, even for the two months of clinical training needed to complete their degree, for which

they had studied online.111 This is but one example of how Israel continues to control many aspects of the life of Gaza’s residents post- disengagement, when its control of crossings, airways and waterways effectively prevents these stu-dents from completing their studies. Other cases have dealt with restrictions

on people who needed medical care outside of the Gaza Strip,112 or needed

to move for other purposes such as family visits,113 and sports competition,114 amongst others.

In these situations, the hcj has often accepted the position of the Israeli

government that Palestinians from the Gaza Strip are “residents” of an “Enemy

Territory” with no right to enter Israel.115 It has treated Palestinians who wish to enter Israel or the West Bank as “strangers” rather than as people to whom Israel owes a special duty as an Occupying Power.

But Palestinians are not “strangers”. In one case that illustrates Israel’s selec-

tive approach to the Gaza Strip, the hcj upheld a prohibition imposed by the Israeli authorities on a resident of the Gaza Strip to return to home from Israel, using regulations that allow the State to prevent Israelis from leaving Israel for alleged security reasons. It suggested that Palestinians from the Gaza Strip are strangers of a unique nature and, unlike tourists, Israel can restrict their free-

dom of movement with ease.116 There are also instances where Israel permits

travel beyond the humanitarian minimum.117 As Michal Luft argues, this and other cases illustrate the existence of a legal gap in Israel’s position towards Palestinians from the Gaza Strip. On one hand, Israel formally relates to them as “strangers” and considers that it does not owe them special duties, despite

its vast control over their lives. On the other hand, its policy towards them is vague and arbitrarily applied to obscure Israel’s international duties without

jeopardising its actual control over the Gaza Strip and its interests therein.118

In some cases, the hcj has chosen not to clarify what the status of the

Gaza Strip is. For example, the hcj refrained from issuing a comprehensive judicial decision on the status of the Gaza Strip in the case concerning the occupational therapy students. The hcj held that such a determination would be mistaken given the liquidity of the situation and that there is no need to decide on complex international law questions regarding the matter given the sui generis nature of the situation. It said it preferred a humanitarian and prag-matic solution. The hcj’s “pragmatist” approach did not help the petitioners

in this case.119



4 Closure, Siege, or Occupation: From the Policing to the

Belligerency Model

In December 2008, following the continued firing of rockets from the Gaza Strip, Israel launched a large- scale military operation against the Gaza Strip that lasted three weeks. If Israel still maintains responsibility as an Occupying Power after the disengagement, the question is whether the full range of the laws on the use of force, including the jus ad bellum principle of self- defence and the jus in bello regulation of the conduct of hostilities, apply. As we shall see, the answer to that question depends on the determination of the status of the Gaza Strip.

An Occupying Power is unable to invoke the right to self- defence vis- à- vis

the territory that it occupies. Its security powers in the occupied territory are restricted to the maintenance of public order and safety in the interest of the occupied population under Article 43 of the Hague Regulations, and admin-istration of the occupation pursuant to the Fourth Geneva Convention. Self- defence is invoked against an external threat – not against a territory that a State controls and where it enjoys policing powers. If an armed conflict erupts (or rather re- erupts) within the occupied territory, regardless of the initiating party, the jus in bello provisions relevant to the conduct of hostilities will pre-sumably apply between the Occupying Power and the occupied population. To the contrary, one may argue if an occupation does not exist, that Israel may

118 Ibid.

119 hcj 11120/ 05, Osama Mahmud Hamdan et al. v The Southern Military Commander (7

August 2007).

94 Gross

resort to force under the jus ad bellum principle of self- defence under Article 51 of the UN Charter. By disengaging from the Gaza Strip, Israel may be under the impression that it has greater freedom to conduct military activities in the

Gaza Strip on the basis of the right to self- defence.120 Indeed, the supposed end of occupation may be considered to permit Israel to operate more force-fully in the Gaza Strip on the basis of the jus ad bellum right to self- defence.

Critics of the position that Israel’s occupation of Gaza Strip ended correctly

note that the disengagement enabled Israel to have its cake and eat it too; in other words, it placed Israel in a position of a (remote) occupier and that of an external belligerent. But to conclude on the basis of this valid critique that Israel cannot invoke a right to self- defence concerning the Gaza Strip – given that it is still occupied territory and only law enforcement operations are there-

fore allowed121 – alludes to the limits of the binary approach that attempts to argue that the occupation is unchanged. This argument fails to consider the absence of both a permanent Israeli military presence and an occupation regime engaged in policing and law enforcement.

The hcj’s decision on the Israeli army’s raid of the six- ship Mavi Marmara

flotilla that tried to break Israel’s naval blockade of the Gaza Strip and ended

in the death of ten passengers should be considered in this context.122 The hcj found that the naval closure had been imposed to prevent the entry of weap-ons into the Gaza Strip after Hamas gained control over government func-

tions.123 This approach raises questions about the restrictions on movement discussed in the previous section and the shift from an occupation to a bellig-erency model discussed in this one, and points out their interrelations. Israel claimed a right to maintain its naval blockade, declared during “Operation Cast Lead”. But the blockade, as noted above, has been a continuous feature of Israel’s control over the Gaza Strip. As an Occupying Power (1967– 1993), Israel prevented use of the Gaza Strip’s territorial waters for the movement of

not implemented after Israel bombed the construction site in 2001.125 After the disengagement (2005), Israel prevented access to the Gaza Strip from the

change Israel’s control of Palestinian waters,127 which it had blocked contin-

uously since 1967.128 Barring isolated exceptions Israel has never permitted

vessels to enter or leave the territorial waters of the Gaza Strip,129 and has also

enforced significant restrictions on fishing activities along the Gaza coast.130

Furthermore, if this declaration was based on the existence of hostilities,

why was it still valid after the end of the Israeli operation in January 2009? A discussion of the flotilla raid, the legality of using force, and the law of the

sea, cannot be addressed in this chapter,131 but the incident points to the vio-lence that Israel exercises when maintaining the naval blockade and retaining control after the disengagement.

Scholars have argued that the naval blockade was illegal since it served

no military purpose and was only meant to put political pressure on Hamas.

Moreover, even if it were legal, it violated the principle of proportionality,132

was tantamount to prohibited collective punishment,133 and violated Israel’s

of which the blockade is but one manifestation.135

Under the functional approach proposed here, the blockade can and

should be seen as a situation violating the duties that Israel, as occupier, owes the Palestinian population. The argument that the suitable paradigm is that of closure and naval blockade rather than occupation should thus be viewed as another attempt to shift from an occupation model, involving duties toward protected persons, to a belligerent model. In Israel’s belliger-ent model, Palestinians are citizens of an enemy State that it does not recog-nise as an independent State but it is also Israel that maintains an extensive measure of control over its population, thus preventing them from exercising self- determination.

Food insecurity in the Gaza Strip, caused by the closure, provides further

illustration.136 The Turkel Commission was appointed by the Israeli govern-ment to investigate the May 2010 events of the flotilla and to examine the legal-ity of the naval blockade. The Commission determined that the blockade had never been intended to starve the civilian population, and that given the mon-itoring and protection mechanisms adopted by Israel to prevent a humanitar-ian crisis in the Gaza Strip, the closure could not be said to be unlawful under the laws regulating naval siege. The Commission thereby largely downplayed the data submitted by human rights organisations attesting to extremely high

As argued in this chapter, an analysis of the closure and the naval blockade’s

legality cannot be based solely on the laws on the conduct of hostilities. The analysis must take into account obligations stemming from the law of occu-pation (as well as international human rights law). In this regard, Article 59 of the Fourth Geneva Convention requires the Occupying Power to allow and facilitate consignments “[i] f the whole or part of the population of an occu-

pied territory is inadequately supplied”.140 Additionally, Article 55 of the Fourth Geneva Convention determines that:

To the fullest extent of the means available to it, the Occupying Power has

the duty of ensuring the food and medical supplies of the population; it

should, in particular, bring in the necessary foodstuffs, medical stores and

other articles if the resources of the occupied territory are inadequate.

The obligation imposed on the Occupying Power to allow relief consignments to reach the civilian population is unconditional, meaning that “consignments

to occupied territories must be permitted to cross even a blockade line”.141 As

ditions.143 The shift from occupation to belligerency, “naval blockade”, and “siege”, exempts Israel from an occupier’s responsibilities. Its obligations are minimised even if its control, in a different guise, continues to fundamentally

affect Palestinians living in the Gaza Strip.144 Even though the implementation

of human rights norms may lead to similar results,145 complex questions arise about their extra- territorial applicability – specifically that of socio- economic

rights.146 Although in this case, human rights norms would probably have led

to the same results given the degree and length of Israel’s control,147 applying the relevant norms of occupation law on a functional basis is significant. The example of food insecurity demonstrates the risks entailed in accepting the declaration that occupation is over in the Gaza Strip, but also the limits of the conceptual approach to law generally, and to occupation specifically.

A Functional Analysis of the Transformed Occupation 99

all, did Israel formally recognise it as falling under the rubric of “occupation”. Shifting from a conceptual to a functional notion of control requires consider-ing the different guises of control. The indeterminacy of the status of the Gaza Strip after 2005 and the departure of Israeli troops and settlements should not blind us to the persistence of control. The disengagement rather underscored its implications: Palestinians in the Gaza Strip are not considered citizens of Israel, protected persons under the law of occupation, nor citizens of a sov-ereign independent State. They cannot be considered citizens of a sovereign independent State because of Israel’s continued occupation of the West Bank, the restrictions that Israel imposes on Gaza’s ability to exercise sovereignty, and the American backed opposition to a unilateral Palestinian declaration of independence. Thus, even if Palestine did begin to gain international rec-

ognition as a State after 2012,148 it remains occupied and unable to exercise sovereignty.

As noted, Israel rejected in 1967 the de jure application of the Fourth Geneva

Convention to the opt. The disengagement enabled it to seek exemption even

from responsibilities it had recognised or assumed before.149 As Eyal Weizman notes, tightening the siege of Gaza is the culmination of a process of transfor-

mation from physical “occupation” to “humanitarian management”.150 In Adi Ophir’s poignant formulation, Israel has been keeping the occupied Palestinian territory, in particular the Gaza Strip, on “‘the verge’ of a humanitarian catastro-

phe”,151 a term first used by the UN Special Rapporteur on the Right to Food

in the context of the opt.152 At the same time, it has asserted that it will do

its utmost, if required, to avoid crossing the threshold of such catastrophe.153

“The idea is to put the Palestinians on a diet, but not to make them die of hun-

ger”, Dov Weisglass, Advisor to the Israeli Prime Minister, reportedly stated.154

Going back to the strong effects of Gaza’s closure on food security,155 we should recall that crises often attract more attention than widespread and persistent

vulnerabilities.156 Whereas hungry people “look the part”, as Carolan points out, malnourished people whose diets are lacking in certain essential micro-nutrients, do not. But the results of malnutrition, which Israel does not con-sider falls within its responsibility anymore, are no less detrimental: reduced

well- being and a shortened life span.157 The words of the Director of unrwa Gaza Operations are most telling in this context:

Here you don’t see kids with blown up bellies … [H] ere they are not starv-

ing. But if you do a little research on the medical side you will find that

there is malnutrition and a very high prevalence of anemia which has to do

with the diet … [T]hose who are keeping a very good household economy

are eating, perhaps, one meal a day. Many families are eating every two

days. But nobody is starving. Everybody is hungry, nobody is starving.158

When a population is on the verge of humanitarian disaster, the spectators, be they the courts of law or the courts of public opinion, are not as alert to the harms as they would be if the sufferers were “looking the part”. Deliberations on whether “hunger” exists in the Gaza Strip in, for example, the Turkel

Commission missed this “hidden hunger”.159 This point is crucial for under-standing the conditions in the opt in general but, more significantly, in “post-

disengagement Gaza”.160

The ones affected by food insecurity join those who died by violence, which

only exacerbated in post- disengagement Gaza. The situation in the Gaza Strip replicates a process that began with the Oslo Accords, where Israel supposedly relinquished control in the opt, only to shift its military activity from that of an occupier exercising security duties to that of a belligerent. This shift has been particularly evident in the three Israeli military operations in the Gaza Strip

between 2009– 2014 in which thousands of civilians have died,161 and Israel’s repression of the Great March of Return of 2018 in which 214 Palestinians, 46

of them minors, were killed.162 The Israeli Supreme Court’s decision to uphold the rules of engagement used by the Israeli army during the Great March of

Return also underscores this shift.163

Under a law enforcement model of occupation, lethal force can only be used

under very strict circumstances to protect life. Under a belligerency model, however, occupying forces are permitted to attack enemy combatants as well as civilians directly participating in the hostilities. Civilian losses are not prohib-ited either, unless they are the result of indiscriminate, disproportionate, and

targeted attacks.164 The model of occupation with reduced friction ironically

causes more lethal friction.165 If international law is to be relevant rather than merely a cover for a transformation of control that involves less responsibil-ity but more lethal power, it must recognise through a functional approach, new forms of control. These detrimental effects of the purported transforma-tion of occupation highlight the urgency of increasing accountability, rather than denying or diminishing it when we cannot see occupiers with “boots on the ground”. The European Court of Human Rights’s insistence of the “boots on the ground” requirement for occupation in the Nagorno- Karabakh cases it issued in 2015, as well as a similar insistence on the part of some authors, offers an inadequate solution to the changing nature of control described in

this chapter.166



5 Conclusion

As shown throughout this chapter, Israel’s indeterminacy concerning the status of the Gaza Strip is but another facet of its indeterminacy concerning the existence of an occupation of the entirety of the opt. Similarly, the hcj’s finding that the Gaza Strip is a sui generis case, with the Gaza Strip being nei-ther a State nor an occupied territory (which is also the official position of the

Israeli government),167 is another expression of Israel’s attempted exception-alism of its occupation of the entirety of the opt. The argument that the opt represents, as a whole, a sui generis situation was voiced by Israel’s Attorney General (and later President of the Supreme Court) Meir Shamgar shortly after

1967, when Israel did not fully recognise the status of the opt as occupied.168 Israel’s indeterminacy enables it to “pick and choose” the legal regimes and duties that best suit it and evade its legal responsibilities.

This chapter has proposed a functional approach to Israel’s control of the

Gaza Strip. In essence, the functional approach binds Israel to the duties of an Occupying Power where its actions affect the lives of Palestinians in the Gaza Strip. This is so because the functional approach acknowledges that there



165 Compare with James Ron, Frontiers and Ghettos: State Violence in Serbia and Israel

(University of California Press 2003) 14.

166 ECtHR, Chiragov et al. v Armenia, Grand Chamber Judgement, 16 June 2015; ECtHR,

Muradyan v Armenia, Judgement, 24 November 2016.

167 Israeli Ministry of Foreign Affairs, ‘The Operation in Gaza: Factual and Legal Aspects’, 29

July 2009, para 30.

168 Meir Shamgar, ‘The Observance of International Law in the Administered Territories’, 1

Israel Yearbook on Human Rights (1971) 262, page 263.

A Functional Analysis of the Transformed Occupation 103

are significant undesirable consequences to the binary, inflexible approaches proposed by other academics. Guided by the raison d’être of the law of occu-pation, and mindful that Israel’s control has transformed but not ended, the functional approach seeks to hold Israel accountable for its continued hold over the Palestinian population of the Gaza Strip.
