The author is grateful to Idan Reiter and Shani Tsur for their research assis-tance and to Sari Bashi, Tania Hary and Michal Luft for their helpful sugges-tions. Further thanks to the Tami Steinmetz Center for Peace Research at Tel Aviv University for its support of this research.





chapter 5


Apartheid and International Law in Palestine

John Reynolds



The Israeli occupation of the West Bank including East Jerusalem and Gaza Strip, now “prolonged” to the point of absurdity, began in 1967. Israeli apartheid was already effectively in place for some decades prior: the legal inscription of difference and domination by the settler State, from its foundation in 1948. This chapter will conceptualise and contextualise this apartheid system as a core feature of settler- colonial rule in Palestine since the Nakba and – more recently – as an increasingly central focus of international legal analysis of the Palestinian reality. The chapter begins in the first section by situating Israeli apartheid within Zionism’s colonial project in Palestine, and by highlighting the longstanding Palestinian intellectual and activist traditions which have provided the foundations for now- widespread understandings of the apart-heid nature of Israel’s regimes of oppression and exclusion of the Palestinians. The second section will trace the vital role that Palestinian lawyers and rights organisations played from the 1980s in developing extensive legal analyses of Israeli apartheid, which western liberal human rights organisations have lately endorsed and elaborated. The third section will flesh out some of the key legal and material characteristics of Israel’s apartheid regime as not just a racially discriminatory regime but a colonial- apartheid regime. The fourth section will consider the tactical possibilities and pitfalls for movements seeking to mobil-ise international law to end apartheid, with reference to the definitions and prohibitions of apartheid that exist across a number of branches of interna-tional law. How central or peripheral international law will ultimately be to the dismantling of Israeli apartheid remains an open question, but it is certain that it must be dismantled.



1 Framing Israeli Apartheid

The Israeli apartheid regime is a centrepiece of the juridical and constitutional expression of Zionism’s colonial project in Palestine. Settler- colonialism is the core ideological pillar from which apartheid flows as a derivative and imple-menting structure, defined by the logics of conquest and colonisation, racial-isation and replacement. A settler- colonial State is, typically, a racial State.



© John Reynolds, 2023 | DOI:10.1163/978900450393

Apartheid and International Law in Palestine 105

Nadia Abu El- Haj emphasises the “racist character of the Israeli state – the organization of the state around the distinction between Jew and non- Jew, military and civilian legal systems, enclosure and movement, and, since the

1967 war, the additional distinction between citizen and subject”.1 Apartheid was most visibly enacted from the outset through the Israeli State’s citizenship, nationality and immigration laws, its land and property regimes, and its inter-nal military- emergency regime governing the minority of Palestinians who had not been ethnically cleansed during the Nakba and had acquired Israeli citizenship. Israeli apartheid was subsequently channelled into the West Bank including East Jerusalem and Gaza Strip after 1967. Many of its most overt manifestations are indeed visible in the occupied territory, but it is not lim-ited to the Israeli occupation and certainly cannot be understood in isolation from Israel’s own basic laws, character and constitution. The prolonged post- 1967 occupation itself, similarly, cannot be understood in isolation from the underlying structures of Israeli settler- colonialism and apartheid. Apartheid in this sense provides a valuable and vital conceptual link between settler- colonialism in historic Palestine and the prolonged occupation of partitioned Palestinian territory.

This understanding of apartheid having its roots in settler- colonialism and

being instituted prior to and beyond the occupied territory is not a novel lens, nor one that has only been superimposed retroactively. It was indeed already asserted by Palestinian scholars, thinkers and activists before the occupation had begun. In 1965, Fayez Sayegh wryly noted that “the Zionist settler- state” had learned everything it could from the white settler- States in Europe and Asia and applied their templates in a tailored and less explicit fashion: “whereas the Afrikaner apostles of apartheid in South Africa, for example, brazenly pro-claim their sin, the Zionist practitioners of apartheid in Palestine beguilingly

protest their innocence!”.2 Through the 1960s and 70s, intellectuals, scholars and activists associated with the plo’s Palestine Research Center were intent on internationalising the Palestinian struggle “by theorizing the particular and

connected forms of racism animating Zionist settler colonialism”.3 They placed an emphasis on Israeli State racism and apartheid, and critiqued Zionism as a

form of racism.4 As such, while articulations and analysis of Israeli apartheid

1 Nadia Abu El- Haj, ‘Racial Palestinianization and the Janus- faced Nature of the Israeli State’,

44 Patterns of Prejudice (2010) 27, page 30.

2 Fayez Sayegh, Zionist Colonialism in Palestine (plo Research Center 1965) 27.

3 Keith Feldman, A Shadow over Palestine: The Imperial Life of Race in America (University of

Minnesota Press 2015) 229.

4 This culminated in the adoption of UN General Assembly Resolution 3379 declaring Zionism

as “a form of racism and racial discrimination”.

106 Reynolds

have further proliferated – and have in some cases been focused primarily on the occupied territory, especially following the fragmentation and consolida-tion of Israel’s occupation under the Oslo Accords – it is important to empha-sise the longer Palestinian radical tradition. We have certainly witnessed in

recent years a form of analytic renewal or return5 to these earlier anti- apartheid framings, with a newer generation of Palestinian scholars, activists and allies re- emphasising the racial, apartheid and settler- colonial analyses of the 1960s

and 1970s.6

Strikingly, 2021 has been characterised as “the year of Israeli apartheid”7

due to a torrent of high- profile reports and interventions advancing the apart-heid critique. In this context, Palestinian scholars have made the crucial arguments that the more radical traditions of anti- apartheid politics beyond international law should also continue to guide Palestinian liberation efforts

and movement activism.8 But it is clear that international law’s definitions and prohibitions of apartheid, and the positions now increasingly being taken by major human rights organisations, have been a core driver of the broadening consensus on Israeli apartheid. Within the space of just over a year straddling either side of 2021, Israeli human rights organisations Yesh Din and B’Tselem and international organisations Human Rights Watch, Diakonia and Amnesty International all produced reports on Israel’s maintenance of an apartheid

regime or commission of apartheid crimes.9 The plo’s Negotiation Affairs

This breakthrough in 2021 did not emerge in a vacuum. Much as Human

This lineage includes the local and global organising towards the UN’s Durban anti- racism conference in 2001, the annual running of Israeli Apartheid Week across hundreds of cities and campuses since 2005, the ongoing campaign for boycott, divestment and sanctions – and the punishments endured by activ-

2 Palestinian Legal Activism and Anti- apartheid Analysis

In 1981, just two years after co- founding Al- Haq, Raja Shehadeh was writing about the “parallels” between the legal system underpinning Israeli colonisa-tion of the West Bank with that of apartheid South Africa and pointing to the “strong similarities which, all indications point, are only bound to increase

with time”.18 The core focus of Al- Haq and Shehadeh’s work through the 1980s was on questions of law and land. This required reckoning with the particular contours of Israel’s civil and military legal regimes. Shehadeh fixed his ana-lytic sights on the establishment of the Israeli civil administration in the West Bank in 1981, which formalised and “formulated the apartheid system in the

West Bank”.19 This was the legal- institutional consolidation of Zionism’s racial logic within the occupied territory: “a way of separating the civilian rule of Israeli Jews from that of non- Jews living in the same territory, making each group subject to different laws and different authorities that implement dif-

ferent laws, which is a form of apartheid”.20 For Shehadeh, Israeli apartheid in the West Bank was the process of these “different laws applicable to dif-ferent groups” producing “different and unequal distribution of resources and opportunities for development”. In 1992, in the maelstrom of the Madrid- Oslo conjuncture, Shehadeh stepped back to situate developments in the context of the UN’s 1973 Convention on the Suppression and Punishment of the Crime of Apartheid. He forewarned the plo that the way the negotiations were heading, they were going to end up blunting Palestinian self- determination and facili-tating Israeli colonialism. Unless the plo changed tack decisively “they would be contributing to the consolidation of the system of apartheid put in place by

successive Israeli governments over the past twenty- five years”.21 The warning



18 Raja Shehadeh, ‘The Legal System of the Israeli Settlements in the West Bank’, 27 icj

Review (1981) 59, pages 73– 74.

19 Raja Shehadeh, ‘Human Rights and the Israeli Occupation’, 8(1) cr: The New Centennial

was ignored, and as a result of the Oslo agreements “the apartheid structure …

became more entrenched”.22

Importantly, in this reading, Israel’s apartheid was always understood in the

context of Zionism’s expansionary “land acquisition policy” through which

“colonialism is to be formalized and made permanent”.23 In her compelling “global history” of Al- Haq, Lynn Welchman notes that the apartheid legal framework which is “now routinely invoked by al- Haq and other human rights NGOs” can be traced back to this period in the 1980s: “[i] t is easy to point to pre-

dictions of these frameworks in early al- Haq work”.24 Jonathan Kuttab, another co- founder of Al- Haq, appeared before the Foreign Affairs Committee of the US House of Representatives in 1985, for instance, and testified on the con-sequence of Israel’s colonisation, Judaisation and annexation of Palestinian land. He said that it:

[L]eads to systematically robbing Palestinians of their land and water

resources, placing such resources in the hands of Jewish settlers for

whom an entire infrastructure and separate regime is created. A classic

de facto apartheid system has thus been created, with two separate and

differing structures, courts, residences and rights – one for Palestinian

Arabs and another for Israeli Jews.25

In 1998, law – The Palestinian Society for the Protection of Human Rights and the Environment, published a 78- page study of the Oslo process enti-

tled “Apartheid, Bantustans, Cantonization: The abc of Oslo”.26 In 2001, they built on this with a further and longer report, “Israel’s Brand of Apartheid: The

Nakba Continues”,27 which analysed Israeli law and policy with reference to the Apartheid Convention and other international legal treaties, and situ-ated Israeli apartheid as very much part of a colonial project based on racial domination. After this, law referred repeatedly in its publications to Israel’s

“apartheid system” – its movement restrictions and permits which “resembles the system of ‘pass laws’ as used by the apartheid regime in South Africa” and

“cuts the West Bank into eight bantustans”.28 The organisation called on the international community to condemn racial segregation and apartheid, and

“to take effective measures to dismantle Israel’s apartheid system”.29

Other Palestinian rights organisations were also by now incorporating

the apartheid framework into their analysis and advocacy. The UN’s World Conference Against Racism in Durban in 2001 was a pivotal moment. Al- Haq prepared a legal position paper for the conference which linked apartheid to colonisation and self- determination, and concluded that under the terms of the Apartheid Convention, “Israel’s regime is, for all respects and purposes, an

apartheid regime”.30 The work of Palestinian legal activists informed the Cairo Declaration in advance of the conference, in which 65 Arab, Asian, African and international human rights organisations called for the abolition of Israel’s

“legal and institutionalised apartheid regime”.31 It similarly fed into the NGO Forum Declaration and Programme of Action adopted during the conference itself, which decided to formally “declare Israel as a racist, apartheid state” and call for an end to “the institutionalized racism and Apartheid regime in

Israel”.32 In doing so, the document characterised Israeli apartheid as a direct

obstruction to Palestinian collective rights to self- determination and return.33 The reactionary backlash – against the critiques of Israel by human rights NGOs and social movements, including the charge of apartheid – was such that it engulfed the entire conference.

Over the following twenty years, Palestinian legal research and advocacy

organisations like Badil and Al- Haq, and grass- roots organisations like Stop the Wall, continued to deepen and sharpen their apartheid analyses. Badil was at the vanguard of much of this work. In 2008, they published an important article

During this time, the two main legal organisations representing Palestinian rights on either side of the Green Line, Al- Haq and Adalah, were also core contributors to a major study commissioned in 2007 by South Africa’s

Human Sciences Research Council.36 I was working with Al- Haq at the time, where we had robust internal discussion about the implications of centering Al- Haq’s work more definitively around the analysis of Israeli apartheid. If the study would conclude that the Israeli occupation regime was part of a deeper apartheid regime, what would that mean for Al- Haq’s work that had drawn so extensively to date on the laws of war and occupation? How would anti- apartheid remedies relate to the Palestinian rights to self- determination and land restitution? Was there a risk that focusing on apartheid could in any way undermine the legal work against illegal settlement colonies in the West Bank?

The study was published in report and book form in 2009 and 2012 respec-

tively and found that Israel was in breach of the international legal prohibi-

tions on both apartheid and colonialism.37 While the findings emphasised that this was in addition to – and would not compromise or dilute – Israeli and international obligations under the laws of occupation, the thrust (and title) of the study made clear the need to go “Beyond Occupation”. The conversation among Palestine- oriented scholars, advocates and activists about how best to understand and mediate the various overlapping legal frameworks of occupa-tion, apartheid, colonialism and population transfer/ ethnic cleansing would

3 The Israeli Apartheid Regime

Israel defines itself in its basic (constitutional) laws as a “state of the Jewish people”, in which the right of self- determination is “unique to the Jewish

Racial Discrimination on Israel’s Seventeenth to Nineteenth Periodic Reports, 2019.

41 Lisa Hajjar, ‘The World that Al- Haq Made’, Jadaliyya, 17 June 2021. 114 Reynolds

people” and “Jewish settlement is a national value”.42 Its leaders state openly that “Israel is not a state of all its citizens … Israel is the nation- state of the

Jewish people and only them”.43 Across the territory of historic Palestine con-trolled by Israel, between the Jordan River and Mediterranean Sea, roughly half of the inhabitants do not identify as Jews and as such are not included in this articulation of constitutional belonging. As of 2021, there are approximately 6,8 million Jewish- Israelis living in this territory and 6,8 million Palestinians

(of whom 1,6 million are citizens of Israel).44 Israel’s constitutional, civil and military law and practice across Israel and the occupied Palestinian territory systematically discriminates against Palestinians as a racialised group, for the purpose of maintaining domination over them in favour of Jewish- Israelis. In this sense, it is an apartheid regime. But it is also intrinsically linked to ongo-ing Zionist settler- colonisation – and concomitant dispossession and dis-placement of Palestinians. It gives certain legal privileges to non- Israeli Jews elsewhere, over and above native Palestinians. In these senses, it is a colonial- apartheid regime.

Israel’s legal- constitutional apparatus, and in particular its citizenship,

nationality and immigration laws, defines Jewishness primarily on matrilineal blood lines. Jewish identity is codified in Israel’s Law of Return as based on descent (“for the purposes of this Law, ‘Jew’ means a person who was born of a Jewish mother…”) or conversion according to certain criteria. The law grants settlement rights in Israel (and, since 1967, the rest of occupied Palestine) to any Jewish person born anywhere in the world who meets that definition of a Jew, and then extends those rights further to anyone with at least one Jewish

grandparent (and their spouse).45 When originally introducing the Law of Return in 1950, Israel’s founding prime minister David Ben- Gurion emphasised its centrality to the very character and constitution of the State, asserting that the Jewish right of settlement “existed before the state did, and it is that which built the state”:



42 Israel, Basic Law: Israel – The Nation- State of the Jewish People (19 July 2018). See also

Israel, Basic Law: Freedom of Occupation (9 March 1994); Israel, Basic Law: Human

Dignity and Liberty (17 March 1992); Israel, Basic Law: Knesset (12 February 1958).

43 ‘Benjamin Netanyahu Says Israel is “Not a State of All its Citizens”’, The Guardian, 10

March 2019.

44 See ‘Mapping Israeli Occupation’, Aljazeera, 18 May 2021, collating population statistics

from the Palestinian Central Bureau of Statistics, the Israeli Central Bureau of Statistics,

the Israeli Ministry of Interior, and the Jerusalem Institute for Policy Research.

45 Israel, Law of Return (5 July 1950). This law was amended in 1970 to include definition

of “Jew”. According to the amendment “‘Jew’ means a person who was born of a Jewish

mother or has become converted to Judaism and who is not a member of another religion”. Apartheid and International Law in Palestine 115

The Law of Return is not one of these immigration laws which determine

under which conditions the country will accept certain kinds of immi-

grants. It is the unchanging law of Jewish history, reflecting the princi-

ple whereby the State of Israel was established. It is the historic right of

any Jew anywhere to come and settle in Israel for whatever reason. The

Nationality Law complements the Law of Return, determining that by vir-

tue of the fact that he has immigrated to Israel a Jew becomes a national

of his country, needing no further verification or condition other than his

desire to settle and live here. The Law of Return and the Nationality Law

constitute the charter of rights guaranteed to every Jew in Israel by virtue

of the fact that he is Jewish.46

To complement the Law of Return, the 1952 Nationality Law grants immediate automatic citizenship to Jews who migrate, while explicitly excluding those who were residents and citizens of Palestine before the creation of the State of Israel if they were not “in Israel, or in an area which became Israeli territory after the establishment of the State, from the day of the establishment of the

State [May 1948] to the day of the coming into force of this Law [April 1952]”.47 Thus, Palestinian refugees – including those born in Palestine who were forced out of their homes and into exile in 1948 – are barred from entering the territory, let alone returning to their homes and accessing even second- class citizenship or residency rights. Others with no prior connection to Palestine/ Israel (and no equivalent requirement of continuous residence from 1948– 1952), meanwhile, are entitled to citizenship on the basis of a particular construction of Jewish nationality. Anyone who is Jewish by descent can migrate as a colonist to any part of historic Palestine and will be granted automatic citizenship by Israel and the full benefits of Jewish nationality within its hierarchical constitutional system. This includes access to land, housing and social services on a level that native non- Jews, even those with citizenship, do not enjoy. The constitution of Israel as “a state for the Jews of the world”, as John Quigley put it in one of the earlier international legal analyses of Israeli apartheid, meant “less than

full status for others”.48 It is also central to understanding how and why the

46 David Ben- Gurion, Sitting 160 of the Knesset, 3 July 1950 (18 Tammuz 5710).

47 Israel, Nationality Law (14 July 1953), Article 3. It is crucial to note in this context that more

than 85% of the 900,000 Palestinians “who at the start of 1948 lived in the territory that

came to be Israel were gone by the end of that year, having becoming refugees in nearby

states”. John Quigley, ‘Apartheid Outside Africa: The Case of Israel’, 2 Indiana International

and Comparative Law Review (1991) 221, page 225.

48 John Quigley, ‘Apartheid Outside Africa: The Case of Israel’, 2 Indiana International and

Comparative Law Review (1991) 221, pages 224– 225. Given that the “state’s self- definition as 116 Reynolds

Israeli apartheid regime is also colonial, and not just internally discriminatory between two existing groups of citizens. As Darryl Li explains:

Although Zionism inscribes Jewishness in terms of nationality [le’om], it

is more usefully thought of as a racial category. It is primarily based on

bloodlines but in contrast to jus sanguinis principles that shape many

nationality regimes around the world, the Law of Return disclaims any

need to trace an individual’s connection to the territory through a family

member.49

Israeli law is indeed peculiar in how it distinguishes between nationality [le’om] and citizenship [ezrahūt]. For purposes of law as well as policy, no “Israeli nation” exists. Israeli Supreme Court jurisprudence confirms that Israel is defined as the State not of the “Israeli nation” but of the “Jewish nation” [le’om yahūdi]. When individuals have petitioned the Israeli Supreme Court seeking to register their nationality as “Israeli” as opposed to “Jewish”, the Court has held that “there is no Israeli nation separate from the Jewish nation”, with the Jewish nation “composed not only of those residing in Israel but also of

Diaspora Jewry”.50 The Supreme Court has made it clear that to recognise a common Israeli nationality would be to “negate the very foundation upon

which the State of Israel was formed”.51 Non- Jews who hold Israeli citizenship remain subordinated by virtue of the fact that they are not Jewish nationals. This racial privileging of Jewishness filters through the legal system and func-tions as an equivalent of whiteness in European settler- colonies historically. Its dynamics are deeply entangled with property and racial capitalism, and

prompt analyses of “whiteness as property in Israel”52 and “Jewishness as prop-

erty under Israeli law”.53



a state of a single racial group impliedly excludes others” Quigley concluded that Israel’s

nationality and citizenship laws amount to apartheid legislation calculated to prevent a

racial group from participation in the political, social, economic and cultural life of the

country, in breach of the Apartheid Convention.

49 Darryl Li, ‘On Law and Racial Capitalism in Palestine’, Law and Political Economy Project,

15 June 2021.

50 ca 630/ 70, George Rafael Tamarin v State of Israel (1972).

51 Ibid. The Court again rejected the existence of any Israeli nationality in ca 8573/ 08, Uzi

Ornan et al. v Ministry of Interior et al. (2 October 2013).

52 Noura Erakat, ‘Whiteness as Property in Israel: Revival, Rehabilitation, and Removal’, 31

Harvard Journal on Racial Ethnic Justice (2015) 69.

53 Rabea Eghbaria, ‘Jewishness as Property under Israeli Law’, Law and Political Economy

Project, 9 July 2021.

Apartheid and International Law in Palestine 117

While Palestinians holding Israeli citizenship make up approximately

22 percent of the State’s population and are entitled to vote, they are hugely restricted in critical areas such as land use and access to natural resources and key services. They are excluded by planning laws and institutions, and sys-tematically discriminated against at municipal and national levels in the eco-nomic, social, and cultural spheres. Jewish nationals, whose exclusive interests are served by parastatal institutions such as the Jewish Agency and the Jewish National Fund, are privy to exclusive access to most of the State’s territory and can claim extraterritorial rights and privileges in areas controlled by Israel. Notably, for instance, the 1960 Basic Law: Israel Lands provides that ownership of real property (“land, houses, buildings and anything permanently fixed to land”) held by the State of Israel, the Development Authority and the Jewish National Fund “shall not be transferred either by sale or any other manner”,

but is to be held in perpetuity for the benefit of the Jewish people.54 The World Zionist Organization and the Jewish National Fund are given an explicitly “cor-nerstone” role in Israel’s land policy, under which “93% of the land in Israel is in the public domain; that is, either property of the state, the Jewish National Fund or the Development Authority” and thus cannot be leased by non- Jews

(including non- Jewish citizens of Israel).55

This illustrates the foundation provided by the concept of Jewish national-

ity for an institutional regime that affords preferential legal status and mate-rial benefits to Jews over non- Jews. This is entrenched throughout Israel’s constitutional system. The 1958 Basic Law: The Knesset, for instance, makes the political participation of Palestinian citizens in the Israeli legislative sys-tem conditional on their acceptance of Israel as a Jewish State, and effectively bars them from challenging the laws that racially subordinate them. Most recently and perhaps most egregiously, the 2018 Basic Law: The Nation- State of the Jewish People consolidates the project of “constitutionally enshrining

policies that amount to apartheid”.56 Here, according to Hassan Jabareen and Suhad Bishara:

[A]partheid is evident in the fact that there is discrimination in citizen-

ship rights, cultural and language rights, the rights to land and housing,

and religious rights in every area of historical Palestine where both Jews



54 Israel, Basic Law: Israel Lands (25 July 1960), Article 1; see also Keren Kayemeth LeIsrael –

Jewish National Fund webpage ‘Jewish People Land’.

55 Israel Land Authority website.

56 Hassan Jabareen and Suhad Bishara, ‘The Jewish Nation- State Law’, 48(2) Journal of

Palestine Studies (2019) 43, page 43.

118 Reynolds

and Palestinians live … The law specifies that the two groups, Jews and

Arabs, living in areas subject to Israeli control are not constitutionally

equal.57

This is an apartheid constitutional regime which, as Jabareen and Bishara remind us, is part of a colonial constitutional regime:

The colonial nature of the regime is evident in the imposition of a Jewish-

Israeli constitutional identity on all Palestinians, in the severing of their

relationship to their homeland, and in the consolidation of Jewish ethnic

supremacy and domination.58

A total of fifteen petitions were filed challenging the legitimacy of the Nation- State Law. The Israeli Supreme Court allocated just one day for a single hearing on all fifteen petitions – including those setting out in detail the “discrimina-tory and racist nature of this law, a law which clearly shows the Israeli regime as a colonial one with distinct characteristics of apartheid” – before dismissing

the petitions in July 2021.59 At the same time, when Palestinian members of the Israeli parliament drafted a proposed Basic Law: State of all its Citizens, the Knesset Presidium refused to allow submission of the bill or even a discussion

of equal rights for all citizens, and the Supreme Court upheld this refusal.60

The apartheid nature of Israel’s legal regime is further visibly evidenced in

the separate legal systems in place for Jewish- Israeli settlers (civil law) and Palestinians (military law) in the occupied territory, and the racialised extra-territorial application of Israeli civil law to settlements and settlers from 1967

onwards, through a blend of personal and territorial jurisdiction grounds.61 The overarching structure of Israeli apartheid in the occupied territory mani-fests through specific “acts of apartheid” that feed into an institutionalised sys-tem of domination. Such acts range from the violent (torture and ill- treatment, extra- judicial killing, persecution of dissent) to the legal- administrative



57 Ibid., page 53.

58 Ibid., page 52.

Court Upholds the Racist and Discriminatory Jewish Nation- State Law, 8 July 2021.

Apartheid and International Law in Palestine 119

aspects (categorisation of different population groups, permit systems, appro-priation of land for exclusive Jewish use, carving up of territory into segregated reserves). The law, in this sense, is both instrumental to, and constitutive of, apartheid. Numerous authorities and extensive legal studies over recent years have outlined the reality of Israeli apartheid under international law and docu-

mented the “legal architecture”62 and the acts of apartheid perpetrated against Palestinians in granular detail.

In 2007, as UN Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in

the Palestinian Territories Occupied by Israel since 1967, John Dugard raised questions as to “the legal consequences of a regime of prolonged occupation

with features of colonialism and apartheid”.63 This was taken up directly in the aforementioned project initiated by the Human Sciences Research Council of South Africa, which was explicitly undertaken with the aim of studying and answering Dugard’s questions. The 300- page study set out detailed documen-tation and analysis of Israeli law and practice in Palestinian territory and con-cluded that there exists an institutionalised and oppressive system of Israeli domination and oppression over Palestinians as a group – that is, a system of

apartheid – which Israel and all States have legal obligations towards ending.64 Even with the territorial mandate of this study being confined to the 1967 occu-pation, it contained extensive analysis and critique of Israeli colonialism, and was unambiguous in pointing to the structural links between apartheid and settler- colonialism.

Discrimination, 2 April 2021.

63 unhrc, Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the

from 2008– 2014, noting in his 2010 report that the nature of the Israeli occupation as

of Israel’s Practices in the Occupied Palestinian Territories under International Law (Human

In 2011, the Russell Tribunal on Palestine went further, applying its inquiry

into Israeli apartheid beyond the 1967 territory. It found at the conclusion of its Cape Town session that “Israel subjects the Palestinian people” as a whole “to an institutionalised regime of domination amounting to apartheid as defined under international law”. While Israel’s “discriminatory regime mani-fests in varying intensity and forms against different categories of Palestinians depending on their location”, the Tribunal concluded that “Israel’s rule over the Palestinian people, wherever they reside, collectively amounts to a sin-

gle integrated regime of apartheid”.65 In 2017, the UN Economic and Social Commission for Western Asia report similarly examined “the treatment by Israel of the Palestinian people as a whole, considering the distinct circum-stances of geographic and juridical fragmentation of the Palestinian people

as a condition imposed by Israel”.66 The report concluded that “Israel has established an apartheid regime that dominates the Palestinian people as a whole”, and found that “the strategic fragmentation of the Palestinian people

is the principal method by which Israel imposes an apartheid regime”.67 As such, findings by both the Russell Tribunal and the UN Economic and Social Commission for Western Asia relate to the territory of historic Palestine in its entirety and refuse the logic of partition, as well as including the Palestinian refugees as subject to the apartheid regime. Under the Apartheid Convention, any measures “designed to divide the population along racial lines by the creation of separate reserves and ghettos for the members of a racial group” amount to an act of apartheid. Hence, as John Quigley notes, “[i] f forcing a group into a designated area within a country constitutes apartheid, forcing

the group out entirely would seem to be hyper- apartheid”.68

Apartheid and International Law in Palestine 121

Following the tendencies established over recent years, the 2021 Human

Rights Watch report was then simultaneously significant and superfluous in likewise detailing the nature of Israeli apartheid throughout all of the territory, from the river to the sea, in great depth. Human Rights Watch did also include the Palestinian refugees as affected by Israel’s intention to maintain domina-tion over Palestinians, though did not incorporate the question of refugees and return into its apartheid analysis as fully or coherently as it should have. The report was also somewhat uneven in the application of its analysis across the Green Line. Rania Muhareb has pointed out the oddity that when addressing the fragmentation of Palestinians, Human Rights Watch “only discusses ‘the separation policy’ within the occupied Palestinian territory, failing to mention the artificial division of Palestinians along the Green Line and the denial of the

right of return of Palestinian refugees and exiles”.69 The Human Rights Watch report does reinforce the logic of partition in some senses, finding that 1948

Palestinians face a type of discrimination which is “less severe”70 than 1967 Palestinians are subjected to, and that some of the core elements of systematic oppression and inhumane acts of apartheid “are only committed in the occu-

pied Palestinian territory”.71 The section of the report dedicated to document-ing Israel’s inhumane acts of apartheid explicitly differentiates “inhumane acts in the opt” from what it refers to as “other abuses” that are carried out “sepa-rately” by Israel against Palestinians inside the 1948 territory, even where it is the same essential policy perpetrated by the same state against the same racial

group.72 Land expropriation in the West Bank is presented by Human Rights

Watch as an inhumane act of apartheid,73 whereas land expropriation inside Israel (“the massive confiscation of land from Palestinian citizens” – and the ongoing denial of restitution claims) is characterised not as an act of apartheid

but an “other abuse” of Palestinian rights.74 In implementing apartheid laws throughout historic Palestine, however, in practice the Israeli State is guided more by the logic of ‘Greater Israel’ than of partition, and the reality as we must understand it and analyse it is effectively a one- State reality. Ultimately,

the land grabbing, home demolitions, evictions and prison conditions that Palestinians are subjected to are inhumane acts of apartheid whether in the Naqab or Ni’lin, Nazareth or Nablus.



4 Confronting Israeli Apartheid under International Law

Apartheid as imposed in South Africa was a system of white domination structured on three pillars: racial categorisation and segregation, territorial fragmentation, and political repression. It was an institutionalised system in the sense that it was created by law and enforced by legal institutions. From the 1960s onwards, with the “non- aligned” States having assumed a majority at the UN in the wake of the waves of decolonisation across Africa and Asia, concerns over the systemic malice of the apartheid system in southern Africa were addressed in treaties of international human rights and humanitarian law, in the adoption of a specific international convention to criminalise apart-heid, and in the procedures and resolutions of the UN General Assembly and

Security Council.75 As such, the definitions, prohibitions and criminalisations of apartheid that now exist across a number of branches of international law (human rights law, international criminal law, international humanitarian law, and public international law generally) are derived from the southern African experience but have a broader universal application.

In 1965, the same year as Fayez Sayegh published his canonical text theo-

rising race and racism in the context of Israeli colonialism in Palestine, the UN’s first global human rights treaty – the International Convention for the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination – was adopted. Article 3 laid down an obligation for the signatories to oppose and eliminate apart-heid: “States Parties particularly condemn racial segregation and apartheid and undertake to prevent, prohibit and eradicate all practices of this nature in territories under their jurisdiction”. As such, “governmental policies based on racial superiority or hatred, such as policies of apartheid, segregation or separation” were condemned and proscribed by the Convention as a systemic form of human rights abuse.



75 See further John Dugard and John Reynolds, ‘Apartheid, International Law and the

Occupied Palestinian Territory’, 24 European Journal of International Law (2013) 867. The

non- aligned States are those (mostly Global South) States who were/ are not formally

aligned with either of the Cold War era/ legacy power blocs. Building on the 1955 Afro-

Asian conference in Bandung, the Non- Aligned Movement was formally established at a

summit in Yugoslavia in 1961, and currently includes 120 Member States.

Apartheid and International Law in Palestine 123

When it comes to the crime of apartheid, the 1968 Convention on the Non-

Applicability of Statutory Limitations to War Crimes and Crimes against Humanity included among crimes against humanity, “inhuman acts result-

ing from the policy of apartheid”.76 Following on from this, the Apartheid Convention was adopted in 1973 to enable “more effective measures at the inter-national and national levels with a view to the suppression and punishment of

the crime of apartheid”, allowing for universal jurisdiction to be exercised.77 The categorisation of apartheid as a crime against humanity was retained in

the 1998 Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court.78 Within the rubric of the laws of war, apartheid is also framed as a crime amounting to a grave breach of the Geneva Conventions when practised in the context of an armed

conflict, under Additional Protocol i to the four Geneva Conventions.79

Apartheid is also an internationally wrongful act under public international

law, held by the International Court of Justice to constitute a “flagrant violation

of the purposes and principles of the [UN] Charter”.80 From the 1960s, the UN General Assembly condemned apartheid as an inherent violation of the right to self- determination, and consistently associated the rights of those living under such a regime of racial domination with the rights of peoples subjugated by colonialism or foreign occupation. In 1970, the General Assembly affirmed “the legitimacy of the struggle of peoples under colonial and alien domination rec-ognized as being entitled to the right of self- determination to restore to them that right by any means at their disposal”, and condemned “those Governments that deny the right to self- determination of peoples recognized as being enti-

tled to it, especially of the peoples of southern Africa and Palestine”.81 Such coupling of the Palestinian and South African causes was commonplace in

General Assembly resolutions during the 1970s.82 The UN Security Council oscillated and equivocated more in its approach to apartheid through the 1960s and 1970s, and its role was diluted by the frequent abstentions and vetoes



76 This followed references to apartheid as a crime against humanity in various instru-

ments of soft law from the mid- 1960s onwards. See for example unga Resolution 2202

(xxi) (16 December 1966), para 1; Proclamation of Teheran, Final Act of the International

Conference on Human Rights, a/ conf.32/ 41, 13 May 1968, para 7.

77 Apartheid Convention, preamble and Article 4.

78 Rome Statute, Article 7(1)(j).

79 Additional Protocol i, Article 85(4)(c).

80 icj, Legal Consequences for States of the Continued Presence of South Africa in Namibia

(South West Africa) Notwithstanding Security Council Resolution 276 (1970) (Advisory

Opinion) icj Reports 16 (1971) para 131.

81 unga Resolution 2649 (xxv) (30 November 1970).

82 See for example unga Resolution 3070 (xxviii) (30 November 1973). 124 Reynolds

cast by Britain, France and the US to shield the apartheid regime.83 By 1977, the Security Council’s language had eventually begun to sharpen, now “strongly condemning” South Africa’s racist regime and “demanding” the abandonment

of apartheid,84 before deciding under Chapter vii of the UN Charter to impose

a mandatory arms embargo on South Africa.85 This decision was followed up

and recalled by subsequent resolutions in the years that followed,86 and when the South African government declared a state of emergency in large parts of the country in 1985, the Security Council called for the adoption of sectoral

economic boycotts and the suspension of sports and cultural relations.87 The Security Council’s engagement (which continued until the termination of the

arms embargo with the formal dismantling of the apartheid system in 199488) demonstrates an international legal norm that apartheid is considered a threat to international peace and security, and that the violence and repression nec-essary to sustain it are illegitimate.

Apartheid is listed alongside genocide and other crimes against humanity

as an example of a breach of an international obligation consisting of a “com-posite wrongful act” under Article 15 of the International Law Commission’s

Articles on States Responsibility for Internationally Wrongful Acts.89 The prohibition of apartheid is also considered a norm of jus cogens giving rise to obligations erga omnes. The International Law Commission views the pro-hibition of apartheid as a peremptory norm of general international law and emphasises that the practice of apartheid amounts to “a serious breach on a widespread scale of an international obligation of essential importance for

safeguarding the human being”.90 The Commission notes the “widespread agreement” shared by States as to the peremptory character of the prohibition

(eds), The Challenge of Human Rights: Past, Present and Future (Edward Elgar 2012).

Apartheid and International Law in Palestine 125

on apartheid, and that apartheid is “prohibited in widely ratified international

treaties and conventions admitting of no exception”.91

The most powerful implication of the growing consensus that Israel is main-

taining an apartheid regime is the connections and intersections it provides between popular resistance, social movements, global solidarity and political action. Ultimately it will be mass mobilisation, civil disobedience and general strikes that will bring Israeli apartheid to an end, more than legal advocacy or remedies themselves. But the legal work that has been and is being done offers important outlets for critiques of Israeli apartheid to be elaborated and substantiated, and certainly feeds into broader consensus, campaigning and

political strategy.92

At the level of international human rights law, the claim of Israeli apartheid

as a breach of the icerd treaty has arisen on two parallel registers before the UN Committee on the Elimination of Racial Discrimination: the Committee’s own periodic reviews of Israel, and an inter- State dispute filed by Palestine against Israel before the Committee in 2018. As early as its 1987 review of Israel, the Committee criticised the Israeli settlement policy in the occupied territory and expressed a particular concern that “people had been settled there on a

racial basis”.93 It also highlighted “the problem of racial discrimination and the

increasing trend towards racist extremism in Israel”.94 In terms of structural racial discrimination in a broad sense, the Committee honed in on the ques-tion of “why Israel did not permit the Palestinian Arabs who had been driven from their lands to come back and obtain the same treatment as Jewish peo-

ple in respect of the recovery of their land”.95 And so while at this point the Committee was not explicitly invoking the specific prohibition of apartheid in its critiques of Israel, it was identifying core features of Israel’s racial structures and presages the legal analyses of Israeli apartheid that would follow. When it came to Article 3 and apartheid specifically, the Committee at this point also flagged Israel’s problematic affinity with the archetypal racial regime of the time. The Committee “regretted the attitude of Israel towards the apart-heid regime of South Africa” and its “full partnership”’ and “collaboration with



91 Ibid., page 112.

92 See further John Reynolds, ‘Anti- Colonial Legalities: Paradigms, Tactics & Strategy’, 18

Palestine Yearbook of International Law (2015) 8.

95 Ibid., para 593.

South Africa in every field, including the economic, cultural, arms and, in par-

ticular, the nuclear”. It lamented that “Israel was South Africa’s closest ally”.96

In subsequent periodic reviews through the 1990s, the Committee on the

Elimination of Racial Discrimination continued to highlight and condemn Israel’s institutional discrimination against Palestinians in the areas of return

and citizenship,97 education, labour and public services,98 as well as structural

“ethnic inequalities” particularly in the area of housing and land rights.99 In 2007, the Committee again expressed its concern about structural systematic racial discrimination against Palestinians across the entire societal spectrum of citizenship laws, public services, land access and allocation, rights to work, health, education, family life, childcare, freedom of movement, access to water, cultural rights, religious rights, racial profiling, lack of effective remedies against racial discrimination and violence, and the implementation of sepa-

rate and discriminatory legal regimes in the occupied territory.100 This effec-tively described the material outcomes of Israel’s constitutional composition as essentially an apartheid system, though having alluded to so many elements of systemic discrimination, when it came to the constitutional claims of the State itself the Committee arrived at the somewhat naive and contradictory recommendation that “the State party ensure that the definition of Israel as a Jewish nation State does not result in any systemic distinction, exclusion, restriction or preference based on race, colour, descent, or national or ethnic

origin in the enjoyment of human rights”.101 The Committee did at the same time make references in the context of Israel’s regime to Article 3 of icerd which prohibits segregation and apartheid – and raised specific warnings

about segregation while still not (yet) naming apartheid.102

A breakthrough of sorts came in 2012, when the Committee again criticised

Israel under Article 3 but this time referred to “policies and practices of racial segregation and apartheid” and directed Israel to “eradicate any such policies

of article 3 of the Convention”.103 The Committee condemned the general segregation of Jewish and non- Jewish communities under Israeli jurisdiction, and declared itself “particularly appalled at the hermetic character of the sep-

aration” between Jewish and Palestinian populations.104 Though this was a significant development as the first time that the Committee had explicitly invoked the prohibition of apartheid against Israel’s regime, it kept its word-ing sufficiently ambiguous for mainstream human rights scholars like Patrick Thornberry to assert that the Committee had not specifically “individuated” apartheid as a distinct violation committed by Israel. Instead, the Committee bracketed Israel’s regime as an overall violation of Article 3, which could be read as signifying a regime of apartheid, or segregation, or a composite of both. Thornberry, a former Committee member himself, noted that:

[T]he Committee has made an unusually high number of references to

Article 3 in concluding observations on the territories occupied by Israel,

employing the language of grave concern or deep concern and, more

unusually, as a violation, though without individuating the elements of

the article.105

In their 2019 shadow report submission to the Committee, Al- Haq and a col-lective of Palestinian rights organisations noted the “cumulative recognition of Israeli apartheid” as a continuing trend over recent years, pointing to the Committee’s own findings in 2017 and 2012, as well as the 2017 UN escwa

report.106 In its concluding observations after this 2019 review, however, the Committee did not fully and unreservedly continue its previous trajectory of taking a progressively stronger position on Israeli apartheid than it had in the previous review. It again invoked its General Recommendation 19 from 1995 “concerning the prevention, prohibition and eradication of all policies

and practices of racial segregation and apartheid”, and urged Israel to eradicate any violations of Article 3 of icerd against Palestinians on both sides of the

Line.108 On the other hand, in the context of this racial regime, the Committee referred to the obligation to “eradicate segregation between Jewish and non- Jewish communities and any such policies or practices which severely and dis-proportionately affect the Palestinian population”, without explicitly including

apartheid in that part of its formulation.109 This ambiguity may have been a conscious or unconscious defence mechanism after witnessing the reaction-ary backlash against the escwa report in 2017. It may also have been due to a desire to avoid pre- empting the outcome of the inter- State dispute initiated by Palestine against Israel in 2018, which is the second register on which Israeli apartheid has arisen before the Committee.

This complaint against Israel, submitted by the Palestinian authorities to the

Committee on the Elimination of Racial Discrimination in April 2018, was one of the first ever inter- State dispute communications under the UN human rights treaty system. It argues that Israel has established a legal regime of apartheid in the Palestinian territory in breach of Article 3 of icerd, as well as systemic racial discrimination in violation of Article 5. Israel responded with arguments that the complaint should be dismissed from the outset on grounds that the Committee had no jurisdiction to adjudicate a complaint brought against Israel by an entity that Israel did not recognise, and/ or that the complaint itself was inadmissible in an international forum as there are adequate legal rem-edies available in the Israeli judicial system which had not been exhausted. In December 2019, the Committee decided (by a majority of 13- 5) that it did indeed have jurisdiction to proceed with adjudicating the complaint. In April



107 UN Committee on the Elimination of Racial Discrimination, Concluding Observations

on the Combined Seventeenth to Nineteenth Reports of Israel, cerd/ c/ isr/ co/ 17– 19, 12

December 2019, para 23.

108 Adalah, For First Time, UN Body Criticizes Israel’s Policies of Racial Segregation against

Palestinians in Israel and OPT – as a Single Entity, 19 December 2019.

109 UN Committee on the Elimination of Racial Discrimination, Concluding Observations

on the Combined Seventeenth to Nineteenth Reports of Israel, cerd/ c/ isr/ co/ 17– 19, 12

December 2019, para 23. Emphasis added.

Apartheid and International Law in Palestine 129

2021, the Committee ruled that the complaint is admissible on the basis that there was enough evidence of “generalized policy and practice” by Israel to implicate it in substantive questions to answer under icerd. This meant that Palestine did not need to show that specific domestic remedies in the Israeli legal system had been exhausted. As indicative of Israel’s generalised policy and practice, the Committee drew again on its own prior language about “the continuing segregation between Jewish and non- Jewish communities” and the evidence of systemic discrimination against Palestinians “as regards their civil status, legal protection, access to social and economic benefits, or right to

land and property”.110 With the complaint deemed admissible, under icerd’s inter- State dispute procedures the establishment of an ad hoc Conciliation Commission is mandatory (in contrast to inter- State communications under some other human rights treaties where it is optional). Israel declined to par-ticipate in consultations over the membership of the Conciliation Commission and so it was left to the Committee to elect the five- person commission from its own membership. In December 2021, the chair of the Committee announced the appointment of this ad hoc Conciliation Commission, which will now seek

to work towards the ultimate aim of ensuring compliance with icerd.111

When it comes to the crime of apartheid under international criminal law,

the International Criminal Court is the primary institutional arena, and any potential prosecution there would again be breaking new ground: there has never been a prosecution of the crime of apartheid in any court to date (though notably, in November 2021, the first indictment of the crime against human-ity of apartheid in South Africa’s history was served on Christiaan Rorich and

Tlhomedi Mfalapitsa in the Gauteng Division of High Court in Johannesburg112). After Palestine joined the icc in 2015, a collective of Palestinian rights organi-sations began to file submissions to the Prosecutor detailing crimes committed by high- level Israeli civilian and military officials. The first three submissions related to crimes perpetrated in the wars and siege on Gaza. The fourth,

against Humanity’, Daily Maverick, 24 November 2021. 130 Reynolds

submitted in 2017, covered war crimes and crimes against humanity in the

West Bank – including the crime of apartheid.113 Following the icc Pre- Trial Chamber’s February 2021 decision that the icc has jurisdiction to move ahead to investigate and prosecute international crimes committed in Palestine, the organisations reiterated the charge that Israel’s systemic segregation and sub-jugation of the Palestinians constitutes “an institutionalised regime of racial domination and oppression, and amount to the crime of apartheid; it is imper-ative that the Prosecutor include acts of apartheid in the scope of her investi-

gation”.114 This intervention by Palestinian legal advocates is a vanguard claim of institutionalised oppression as an international crime beyond what most mainstream legal institutions have been willing to engage with to date. It is also the legal claim that most directly feeds into the mass popular mobilisation

of Palestinian and global social movements over the last twenty years.115 It is quite possible, of course, that the Prosecutor will choose to ignore apartheid entirely and focus the investigation on more discrete war crimes, irrespective of Palestinian interventions. For Israeli officials to be indicted for apartheid, the prosecution needs to show an intention to maintain systemic racial oppres-sion. There is a perception that this element of intent makes it more difficult to prove than some other categories of international crimes. But the intentional nature of the regime is what underpins the significance of the crime of apart-heid, and the concerted design and maintenance of Israel’s oppressive regime is well- documented. As a structural crime, it demands prosecution of its polit-ical architects at the highest level.

Finally, we turn to public international law and State responsibility for the

internationally wrongful act of maintaining an apartheid regime. Whereas international law has legitimised and immunised established settler- colonial sovereignties, its prohibition of an apartheid regime requires the application

of State responsibility norms.116 The existence of a regime of apartheid has pro-found implications under public international law. The State of Israel is subject to duties of cessation and reparation: it is obliged to dismantle the apartheid



113 Al- Haq, Palestinian Human Rights Organisations Submit Evidence to the ICC Prosecutor on

Crimes Committed in West Bank, 20 September 2017.

114 Al- Haq, Landmark Decision of the Pre- Trial Chamber of the International Criminal Court

Welcomed by Human Rights Organizations, 6 February 2021.

115 See further Noura Erakat and John Reynolds, ‘We Charge Apartheid? Palestine and the

International Criminal Court’, Third World Approaches to International Law Review –

twailr: Reflections 33 (April 2021).

116 John Reynolds, ‘Challenging the Nakba through International Law’, The Nakba Files, 27

July 2016.

Apartheid and International Law in Palestine 131

regime and make reparations. Third States have duties not to recognise, aid or assist the apartheid regime, and a duty to cooperate to bring the apartheid situation to an end. This obliges all States to restrict trade and to divest from complicit institutions and investments, as well as to impose arms embargoes and targeted sanctions.



5 Conclusion

Among the core international institutional mechanisms for monitoring and documenting apartheid and assessing Third State responsibility were the UN Centre Against Apartheid and the Special Committee against Apartheid. Both were dissolved in 1995, while the treaty- monitoring body for the Apartheid Convention was suspended. In September 2021, to coincide with the UN General Assembly’s high- level summit to mark the 20th anniversary of the Durban Declaration and Programme of Action against racism, another series of petitions against Israeli apartheid were published. Parallel interventions from

prominent intellectuals and public figures across both Global South117 and

Global North118 – again citing the B’Tselem and Human Rights Watch reports –

endorsed the campaign originating from Palestinian civil society119 which calls on the UN General Assembly to mandate an investigation of Israeli apartheid and to re- activate the Special Committee Against Apartheid and the Center on Apartheid to address Israel’s regime over the Palestinians. In March 2022, in his final report to the UN as Special Rapporteur on human rights in occu-pied Palestine, Michael Lynk similarly endorsed the position of Palestinian civil society. The report concluded that “apartheid is being practised by Israel in the occupied Palestinian territory and beyond” and echoed the calls for

re- establishment of the UN Special Committee Against Apartheid.120 Unlike the UN Economic and Social Commission for Western Asia study five years earlier, this time the UN Secretariat did not move to disassociate itself, under



117 ‘The Global South Stands United Against Apartheid’, Progressive International, 14

September 2021.

118 ‘#UNInvestigateApartheid: A Call from Intellectuals and Personalities in the West’, 22

September 2021.

119 Al- Haq et al., Global Response to Israeli Apartheid: A Call to the UNGA from Palestinian and

International Civil Society Organizations, 22 September 2020; Palestinian bds National

Committee, As UN Meets, Urge Investigation of Israeli Apartheid, 22 September 2020.

120 unhrc, Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the Palestinian

Territories Occupied Since 1967, Michael Lynk, a/ hrc/ 49/ 87, 21 March 2022, paras 61– 62. 132 Reynolds

external pressure, from the findings of the report. As the Unity Intifada con-tinues to evolve and global solidarity continues to grow, all States and interna-tional institutions will ultimately be forced to reckon with reality: apartheid in Palestine is unacceptable and untenable.





chapter 6


The Occupation– Colonialism Continuum: Impact

on Transitional Justice in Palestine/ Israel

Rimona Afana



Though belligerent occupations are, under international law, presumed to be extraordinary, temporary, and conservationist, Israel’s occupation of Palestinians appears normalised, permanent/ indefinite, and invasive/ inter-ventionist. Given its role in blocking resistance and normalising subjugation, the occupation functions as a tool of and smokescreen for colonialism. While diverging from “classic” settler colonialism in logic, in implementation the Zionist project features practices emblematic of colonial enterprises else-where. To delineate the specificity of transitional justice in the (com)promised lands, the continuities between different layers of harm were mapped. As evi-denced by my theoretical and empirical findings, these harms include settler colonialism and its offshoots: ethnic cleansing, military occupation, annex-ation, apartheid, the conflict’s protracted and deliberate non- resolution, third party interventionism, and a pervasive culture of intransigence.

To track the colonialism– occupation continuum, this chapter will first dis-

cuss the shifting interpretations of the Palestine/ Israel impasse, showing why settler colonialism and apartheid have eclipsed the occupation trope, in schol-arship and activism. Second, it will show that the occupation of Palestinians, because of its functional tie to a colonial project, diverges from conventional occupations and should thus be treated as unlawful. Third, though positioning settler colonialism as normalised State crime, the core process and structure fuelling the impasse, to me the ambiguous legal status of colonialism and its antagonising effect make the settler colonialism paradigm a less than ideal replacement for the occupation framework. Finally, drawing on fieldwork findings, a range of perspectives are illustrated on the colonialism– occupation continuum among founders, directors of, and activists in leading justice and reconciliation organisations in Palestine/ Israel. Our dialogues evidenced the handicap of civil society to meaningfully combat colonial criminality while stuck in the misleading occupation paradigm. Given the failure of the occupa-tion framework to capture the pervasiveness and chronicity of harm, a chasm between the 1948 and 1967 frameworks emerged in civic praxis.



© Rimona Afana, 2023 | DOI:10.1163/9789004503939

134 Afana

1 Backdrop: Shifting Interpretations of the Impasse

Over the past decade settler colonialism and apartheid have become the lead-ing interpretative frameworks for engaging critically with Palestine/ Israel. These paradigms have eclipsed the misleading trope depicting the impasse as rooted in the clash between two national movements and exacerbated by the occupation. Though for decades, critical scholars have treated Palestine/ Israel

as a colonial problem,1 only recently have we seen a boom globally in research and activism within the settler colonial framework. This reflects Palestine’s

“settler colonial turn” and “global turn”.2 These also echo “fourth generation

transitional justice”: the interrogation of peripheries.3 The limitations of tra-ditional transitional justice discourse are increasingly recognised, new stud-ies exploring colonial harms, structural violence, socioeconomic rights, or

development.4

1 Edward Wadie Said, ‘Zionism From the Standpoint of its Victims’, 1 Social Text (1979) 7;

Yeshayahu Leibowitz, Judaism, Human Values and the Jewish State (Harvard University

(Verso 2003); Lorenzo Veracini, Israel and Settler Society (Pluto Press 2006); Fayez Sayegh,

Khanyisela Moyo, Postcolonial Transitional Justice: Zimbabwe and Beyond (Routledge 2019). The Occupation-Colonialism Continuum 135

Notwithstanding growing interest in the impact of colonial legacies on tran-

sitional justice theory and praxis, research in this area is nascent, predomi-nantly focused on settler societies like Australia, New Zealand, the United States, and Canada. This drive towards expansive analytical frameworks was absent in early transitional justice research on Palestine/ Israel: studies only

assessed specific mechanisms and single thematic areas.5 Most did not even bring up colonialism, while others superficially touched on it, only as a facet of divergent narratives. Transitional justice research on Palestine/ Israel still pre-dominantly embraces political– legal or psychosocial approaches. Both seem reductionist given their poor engagement with the core source of harm here, settler colonialism. Also, these analyses do not track how the need for justice and for reconciliation often pull in opposite directions.

One way of looking at the roots of the impasse is seeing settler colonialism

as normalised State crime, examining the structural, intergenerational vio-lence it produces. In criminology, State crime designates an (in)action which breaches a State’s own criminal law or its international law obligations. Critical indigenous criminology highlights that colonialism and State crime remain interwoven: since some liberal democracies are rooted in State crime, an under-standing of colonial legacies should inform our analysis of crimes, State crimes,



5 Stanley Cohen, ‘Justice in Transition? Prospects for a Palestinian– Israeli Truth

Commission’, 25(194/ 195) Middle East Report (1995) 2; Aeyal Gross, ‘The Constitution,

Reconciliation, and Transitional Justice: Lessons from South Africa and Israel’, 40 Stanford

Journal of International Law (2004) 47; Yoav Peled and Nadim N Rouhana, ‘Transitional

Justice and the Right of Return of the Palestinian Refugees’, 5(2) Theoretical Inquiries in Law

(2004) 317; Ariel Meyerstein, ‘Transitional Justice and Post- conflict Israel/ Palestine: Assessing

the Applicability of the Truth Commission Paradigm’, 38(2) Case Western Reserve Journal of

International Law (2007) 281; Zinaida Miller, ‘Settling with History: A Hybrid Commission

of Inquiry for Israel/ Palestine’, 20 Harvard Human Rights Journal (2007) 293; Ron Dudai,

‘A Model for Dealing With the Past in the Israeli– Palestinian Context’, 1 International

Journal of Transitional Justice (2007) 249; Jeremie Bracka, ‘Somewhere Between Myopia

and Utopia: Conceptualizing an Israeli– Palestinian Truth and Reconciliation Commission’,

Minerva Center for Human Rights Working Paper 2011; Leila Hilal, ‘Transitional Justice

Reparations for Mass Violence’, Ethics and International Affairs (2015). 136 Afana

and injustice broadly.6 This reflects the growing recognition in transitional justice theory and praxis that “mature” democracies are not necessarily end points in the transitional journey; liberal democracies themselves are rife with unaddressed past harms. The International State Crime Initiative embraces a wide approach to State crime, contrasting strictly legal approaches to crime, which can be narrow, incomplete, reflecting the hegemonic, colonial nature of international law, or out of touch with current forms of criminality. “The law is

ill- equipped to respond to these diffuse, complex, subtle processes.”7

The limited role of law vis- à- vis colonial criminality informed my research:

while discussing applicable legal frameworks, I sought to move beyond law to map crime. This broad view on crime draws on different disciplines (inter-national law, critical legal theory, criminology, settler colonial and postcolo-nial studies, revisionist history, peace psychology) and expands the analysis thematically, spatially, and chronologically, to understand the continuities between different layers of harm and thus, the contours of repair. Tracking these was needed to contextualise the justice- reconciliation nexus. This nexus, encompassing the synergies, tensions, and trade- offs between justice and reconciliation, is to me the fundamental dilemma of transitional justice in Palestine/ Israel. My research treats settler colonialism as the core structure and process shaping the justice– reconciliation nexus, with the occupation as an offshoot of the colonial matrix. This conceptualisation foregrounds the multifaceted, intersectional, intergenerational nature of harm.



2 Occupation/ Colonialism: The Legalities of Illegality

Though both Palestinian communities and their lands remain occupied, pri-marily a people are subjected to the military might of another people. The opt designation foregrounds a space being occupied, obscuring its people. People belong to lands, yet lands do not belong to people: we all are transient beings on earth. “Israel’s occupation of Palestinians” instead of “Occupied Palestinian Territory/ Territories” thus better reflects my understanding of the impasse. While typically in belligerent occupations both lands and people are occu-pied, here the occupation is woven in a colonial- like enterprise which started



6 Christopher Cunneen, ‘State Crime, the Colonial Question and Indigenous Peoples’ in Alette

Smeulers and Roelof Haveman (eds), Supranational Criminology: Towards a Criminology of

International Crimes (Intersentia Press 2008).

7 Penny Green and Tony Ward, ‘Civil Society, Resistance and State Crime’ in Elizabeth Stanley

and Jude McCulloch (eds), State Crime and Resistance (Routledge 2013) 28. The Occupation-Colonialism Continuum 137

in the late 19th century, claimed as a return to the ancient homeland. The Jewish spiritual bond to the land is invoked as a justification for the crimes committed on and for that land. Whether one finds this bond legitimate or not, Jewish “return” remains tied to Palestinian displacement and dispossession. Seeing Israel as occupying a people, yet not necessarily the land, recognises the infringement on Palestinians’ rights while not erasing the Jewish bond to the land. This distinction between occupying lands and occupying people shapes my sense of transitional justice needs, and it also came up amid talks with Palestinians and Israelis during my fieldwork.

Israel’s occupation of Palestinians is often regarded as the longest mili-

tary occupation of our times. As an ongoing source of harm (which requires repair within the transitional journey), the occupation shapes the justice– reconciliation nexus. The occupation militarises the power asymmetry; it normalises brutality by abstracting it, feeding Israel’s culture of violence and denial; it obstructs Palestinians’ lives through multiple layers of arbitrariness; it erects physical and psychological barriers in the name of “military necessity”, institutionalising humiliation and despondency for Palestinians, which sus-tains trauma and enmity. As a smokescreen for Israel’s colonial- like structures, the occupation proactively (not inadvertently) disables the occupied. The fixa-tion with the occupation over the past decades in diplomacy, scholarship and activism has obscured the underlying structure: colonialism.

The deficiency of the occupation framework stems from its being routinely

violated by Israel and its being incomplete and misleading when depicting the brutalities punctuating Palestinians’ lives. Since occupations are toler-

ated under international humanitarian law,8 this induces an aura of lawful-ness. Yet, as the occupation of Palestinians is woven in a colonial matrix, its boundaries remain blurred. Legally, military occupations are presumed to be extra- ordinary: a territory coming under the authority of a hostile army is not ordinary, but it becomes (or is invoked as) necessary due to specific cir-cumstances on the ground. Occupations are mandated to be temporary and conservationist. The occupation of Palestinians is however not rooted in excep-tional military necessities. Instead, it is meant to preserve a socio- geographic design with salient colonial features. Settler colonialism, due to its intrinsic

“logic of elimination”,9 shapes the occupation. As avowed by Ze’ev Jabotinsky, founder of revisionist Zionism, no colonisation occurs with the consent of



8 See Hague Regulations, Fourth Geneva Convention, and Additional Protocol i.

9 Patrick Wolfe, ‘Settler Colonialism and the Elimination of the Native’, 8(4) Journal of Genocide

Research (2006) 387, page 388.

138 Afana

the indigenous.10 To extrapolate, since no people are willingly dispossessed– displaced– discriminated and the natural reaction to subjugation is resistance, this resistance will be resisted by colonisers, to preserve colonial hierarchies. To me, the occupation of Palestinians embodies this resistance to resistance.

While this functional tie between occupation and colonialism is attested

by current realities in Palestine/ Israel, events over the past decades point to a similar conclusion. To historians like llan Pappé and Tom Segev, the 1967 War served as a pretext to Judaize the remaining Palestinian territories (those not incorporated in 1948 in the State of Israel) and thwart Palestinian self-

determination,11 points substantiated also by several activists interviewed for my study. Architects and urban planners have likewise engaged with this colonialism– occupation continuum. Eyal Weizman notes, “[c] haos has its peculiar structural advantages. It supports one of Israel’s foremost strategies of obfuscation: the promotion of complexity — geographical, legal or lin-

guistic”.12 In spite of this apparent complexity sustaining the occupation, “the conflict over Palestine has been a relatively straightforward process of coloni-

sation, dispossession, resistance and suppression”.13 While the occupation was initially a “temporary aberration”, occupied territories have become “an inte-

gral part of the Israeli regime”.14 Though the overlapping strata of domination may appear autonomous “it is the perceived separation between these layers

that makes the politics of vertical apartheid so effective and resilient”.15

This colonialism– occupation continuum surfaces in how space affects

power and vice versa. “On the one hand, what you are (ontologically) and where you are (by Zionist placement and displacement) are fixed poles, between which Zionist- only institutions are reproduced. On the other hand,

Zionist Space, aiming to be pure, has permanently loose ‘borders’”.16 Indeed, “obfuscation and the blurring of boundaries is the defining feature of the

11 Ilan Pappé, ‘Decolonizing Israel’ (The Israel Lobby and American Policy’ Conference,

Fulfilled’, The Electronic Intifada, 6 June 2017; Tom Segev, 1967: Israel, the War, and the Year

that Transformed the Middle East (Metropolitan Books 2005).

The Occupation-Colonialism Continuum 139

Israeli occupation”.17 The colonised– occupied space emerges as “elastic geog-raphies”, a “‘structured chaos’, in which the — often deliberate — selective absence of government intervention promotes an unregulated process of vio-

lent dispossession”.18 Erasure, in its linearity of intent yet evershifting forms, persists: “there is an underlying and uninterrupted continuity of intent that recurring and sustained Zionist attempts to distinguish between pre- and

post- 1967 Israeli circumstances are unable to disguise”.19 Yet, “the occupation’s very success (in terms of unassailable control) renders the project’s success (in

terms of settler integration/ indigenisation) impossible”.20 It is the additional layer of domination presented by the occupation which exposes the colonial foundation: as Lorenzo Veracini notes, Israeli leaders failed to realise that “the systemic ‘matrix of control’, and the consequent separation they were enforc-ing, would inevitably produce precisely the colonised Palestinian subjectivity

that heralds the strategic failure of a settler colonial project”.21

While researching how these intersecting harms impact transitional justice

my aim was not to demonstrate Zionism’s colonial essence, but to document its colonial- like manifestations. Displacement, dispossession, land appropri-ations, unlawful exploitation of natural resources, racism, and impeding the self- determination of the occupied — widely documented practices against Palestinians — are in line with settler– extractive colonial enterprises else-where. Though the impasse is now routinely framed as a colonial problem, settler colonialism here is both typical and extraordinary. To Derek Penslar, Zionism exhibits concurrently colonial, anti- colonial, and post- colonial fac-

ets.22 Israel’s hybridity is attested by Ella Shohat: though most Nation States could be considered anomalous, “Israel is perhaps more anomalous than oth-ers. It is a mixed formation, on the one hand it represents a nationalist proj-ect — and thus analogous to Third World and minority struggles — but from

the Palestinian point of view, it is also a colonial settler project”.23

17 Orna Ben- Naftali, Aeyal Gross and Keren Michaeli, ‘Illegal Occupation: Framing the

Occupied Palestinian Territory’, 23(3) Berkeley Journal of International Law (2005) 551,

page 609.

18 Eyal Weizman, Hollow Land: Israel’s Architecture of Occupation (Verso 2012) 5.

19 Lorenzo Veracini, ‘The Other Shift: Settler Colonialism, Israel, and the Occupation’, 42(2)

22 Derek J Penslar, ‘Zionism, Colonialism and Postcolonialism’, 20(2– 3) Journal of Israeli
