140 Afana

The exceptional features of the Zionist project are also outlined by Moshe

Machover: “its anachronism — its extreme lateness in colonial history”, also the land’s geographic and socio- economic characteristics did not suit a typical

colonial project.24 The land was however picked for ideological reasons; these conditions “made the indigenous Palestinian people much more resistant to pulverisation — which had been the general rule in ‘classical’ exclusionary

colonisation”.25 To Machover, the logic of immigration was also exceptional, settlers lacking a mother country but “seeking a surrogate mother — an impe-

rial power that would dominate the region and promote the Zionist project”,26 namely Britain. As Nadim Rouhana and Areej Sabbagh- Khoury attest, Zionism is concurrently a settler colonial project with no motherland and a national

movement.27 These nuances also surfaced during my fieldwork: nearly half of my interviewees depicted the impasse as unequivocally colonial. Others, while recognising the effects of Zionism as consistent with those of other colonial projects, disputed the applicability of settler colonialism given the peculiarity or exculpatory rationale here: the Jewish spiritual (and arguably genetic) tie to the land. This paints Jewish people as both indigenous and non- indigenous, making Zionism both a national liberation movement and a settler colonial project.

The overlapping layers of violence and fragmentation surface in the

Occupying Power’s persistent violations of its legal duties: sovereignty was declared over part of the territory by annexation; insidious demographic engineering continues unabated via forcible transfer and expansion of illegal settlements; destruction and seizure of property, and undue exploitation of natural resources; discrimination and collective punishment; extrajudicial kill-ings, maiming, torture; obstruction of self- determination by moving the occu-pation from temporariness to permanence or open- endedness. Non- derogable rights such as the right to life, the prohibition of torture, the right to a fair trial, the prohibition of collective punishment, prohibition of forcible transfer, or the freedom of thought and conscience remain routinely violated. Yet, the occupation’s most widespread breaches have been vis- à- vis derogable rights, derogations invoked as serving security needs. This has normalised the occu-pation’s abnormality.



24 Moshe Machover, ‘The Decolonisation of Palestine’, The Weekly Worker, 23 June 2016.

25 Ibid.

26 Ibid.

27 Nadim N Rouhana and Areej Sabbagh- Khoury, ‘Memory and the Return of History in a

Settler- Colonial Context: The Case of the Palestinians in Israel’ in Nadim N Rouhana (ed),

Israel and its Palestinian Citizens: Ethnic Privileges in the Jewish State (cup 2017) 397. The Occupation-Colonialism Continuum 141

These pervasive breaches of core principles guiding the law on occupations

have led legal scholars to discuss the “criminality of the occupation”,28 “the

matrix of an illegal occupation”,29 while as early as 1977 the United Nations

expressed concern over the illegality of the Israeli occupation.30 As noted by Aeyal Gross, this body of law needs to shift from a “merely factual” to a nor-mative and functional approach, premised on the illegality of an occupation

which violates the basic norms of the law on occupations.31 An occupation that violates the following basic, interrelated principles is considered illegal: “(a) sovereignty and title to an occupied territory are not vested in the Occupying Power; (b) the Occupying Power is entrusted with the management of public order and civil life in the territory under control; (c) occupation is temporary

and may be neither permanent nor indefinite”.32

Violations aside, the law on occupations cannot adequately capture the

specificity of the transformative occupation33 and multilateral occupation34 in Iraq and Afghanistan, or the prolonged occupation meeting colonisation and apartheid in Palestine/ Israel. Circumstances unforeseen when humani-tarian rules were codified and others deliberately omitted (given the colonial background of international law) fall outside the scope of the current frame-work. Significantly, it does not cover occupations enmeshed in colonial enter-prises. Arai- Takahashi notes that the exclusion of colonialism makes the law on occupations incomplete and biased; colonial occupation remains tied to the civilising mission of colonial powers: “because the law of occupation was developed chiefly as a social construct among European powers entitled to sovereignty, it trivialised the fate of non- Western peoples divested of sover-

eignty”.35 Eyal Benvenisti draws attention also to a pattern of denial occupiers

engage in around the applicability of this body of law, because they seek to evade legal responsibilities and/ or intend to permanently control the territory

occupied.36 Both aspects seem to have motivated Israel’s engagement with humanitarian law.

Another difficulty with the laws of occupation concerns enforcement: the

Hague Regulations and Geneva Conventions do not include review, report-

ing and accountability mechanisms.37 Though Article 1 common to the four Geneva Conventions mandates the High Contracting Parties “to respect and to ensure respect for the present Convention in all circumstances”, Third Parties have persistently failed to fulfil their positive and negative obligations towards

Israel’s violations of the Conventions.38 The United States in particular, through its decades- long financial, military, and diplomatic support to Israel, sustains the occupation’s criminality. The paralysis of the United Nations vis- à- vis Israel’s breaches has also been denounced by the scholars and activists working with the Russell Tribunal on Palestine: “The unlawful nature of the UN omis-

sions is acute due to their exceptional gravity under international law”.39 The prohibitions of colonialism and apartheid, peremptory norms of international law, come with additional expectations from Third Parties. Attesting Israel’s systematic colonial and apartheid practices, the Human Sciences Research

Council of South Africa40 discusses Third Party obligations based on the ilc

Draft Articles on State Responsibility.41 To former UN Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the Palestinian Territories Occupied since 1967, Michael Lynk, “accountability has been applied by the international com-munity in a selective and partisan fashion to many serious issues, reflecting a

dispiriting mixture of design and indifference, collusion and apathy”,42 noting

2(2) Yearbook of the International Law Commission (2001).

42 unhrc, Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the Palestinian

Territories Occupied Since 1967, Michael Lynk, A/ 74/ 507, 8 November 2019, para 30. The Occupation-Colonialism Continuum 143

the multiple factors which make Israel’s crimes unique given the impunity they

have been met with.43 As most of my interviewees also indicated, addressing the complicity of Third Parties (governments, inter- governmental bodies, and corporations) will need to be integral to the transitional justice process.

While the length and gravity of the occupation here are unprecedented in

modern times, Adam Roberts discusses Palestine/ Israel alongside other cases of prolonged occupation after the Second World War, such as the Allied occu-pations of Germany and Japan, South Africa’s occupation of Namibia, the presence of Turkish forces in Northern Cyprus, of Moroccan forces in Western

Sahara, or Vietnamese forces in Kampuchea.44 As Roberts documents, norms become difficult to define and apply when occupations are not provisional and conservationist but transformational, and when the occupation extends during peacetime. He views a risk in not adjusting the law on occupation to the particularities arising from prolonged occupation, in that it may be used to leave a society disenfranchised: the population being unable to exercise rights of their sovereign State but also not entitled to citizenship of the occupying country. That is precisely what Palestinians are subjected to: “Israel enjoys both the powers of an occupant and a sovereign in the opt, while Palestinians enjoy

neither the rights of an occupied people nor the rights of citizenship”.45

To Richard Falk, the cumulative record of Israeli illegality, carrying both gov-

ernmental and individual responsibility, constitutes an abuse of its custodial

status, independent of the Palestinian claim to self- determination.46 To miti-gate the risks of prolonged occupations, which evade the scope of the Hague Regulations and Geneva Conventions, Falk suggested in 1989, an international convention on the effects of prolonged military occupation in deliberately

impairing the rights of the resident population.47 This convention would set up an automatic mechanism to challenge the occupation after a given period, so that the rights of the occupied are restored and their collective resistance to occupation translates into self- determination. In 2014, Falk recommended the Human Rights Council to “appoint an expert group to propose a special proto-col to the Fourth Geneva Convention with the specific purpose of proposing a

43 Ibid., para 77.

44 Adam Roberts, ‘Prolonged Military Occupation: The Israeli- Occupied Territories Since

1967’, 84(1) American Journal of International Law (1990) 44, pages 47– 52.

45 Orna Ben- Naftali, Aeyal Gross and Keren Michaeli, ‘Illegal Occupation: Framing the

Occupied Palestinian Territory’, 23(3) Berkeley Journal of International Law (2005) 551,

page 611.

46 Richard Falk, ‘Some Legal Reflections on Prolonged Israeli Occupation of Gaza and the

West Bank’, 2(1) Journal of Refugee Studies (1989) 40, page 43.

47 Ibid., pages 45– 47.

144 Afana

legal regime for any occupation that lasts for more than five years”.48 Roberts also considers a prolonged occupation one “that lasts more than 5 years and extends into a period when hostilities are sharply reduced — i.e., a period at

least approximating peacetime”.49

As both Falk and Roberts hold, it is desirable in prolonged occupations

to observe human rights standards which set the bar higher, in addition to humanitarian law. Yet, this may not expand protection but undermine it. As Aeyal Gross observed “the introduction of a rights analysis into the context of occupation abstracts and extrapolates from the context of occupation and puts all involved persons — the citizens of the Occupying State and the peo-

ple living under occupation — on a supposedly equal plane”.50 In its advisory opinion on the legal consequences of the Wall, the International Court of Justice also affirmed the applicability of human rights instruments, besides the

Hague Regulations, the Fourth Geneva Convention and Additional Protocol i.51 Although the icj warned that “the wall and its associated régime create a ‘fait accompli’ on the ground that could well become permanent … tantamount to

de facto annexation”,52 the court did not opine on the interface between pro-longed occupation and its colonial and apartheid features.

During his term as UN Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights

in the Palestinian Territories Occupied since 1967, Richard Falk recommended the UN General Assembly to request the International Court of Justice to issue an advisory opinion on the legal status of the prolonged occupation, aggra-vated by transfers of population by the occupant within the occupied terri-tory and by the discriminatory laws and practices in place against Palestinians, “and further assess allegations that the prolonged occupation possesses legally unacceptable characteristics of ‘colonialism’, ‘apartheid’ and ‘ethnic cleans-

ing’”.53 Prior UN Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the

48 unhrc, Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the

Palestinian Territories Occupied Since 1967, Makarim Wibisono, a/ hrc/ 25/ 67, 13 January

2014, para 81(c).

49 Adam Roberts, ‘Prolonged Military Occupation: The Israeli- Occupied Territories Since

1967’, 84(1) American Journal of International Law (1990) 44, page 47.

50 Aeyal Gross, ‘Human Proportions: Are Human Rights the Emperor’s New Clothes of the

International Law of Occupation?’, 18(1) European Journal of International Law (2007) 1,

pages 4– 5.

51 icj, Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory

(Advisory Opinion) icj Reports 136 (2004).

52 Ibid., para 121.

53 unhrc, Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the

Palestinian Territories Occupied Since 1967, Makarim Wibinoso, a/ hrc/ 25/ 67, 13 January

2014, para 81(b).

The Occupation-Colonialism Continuum 145

Palestinian Territories Occupied since 1967, John Dugard also notes that while occupations are tolerated in international law, colonialism and apartheid are

not.54 This view is increasingly recognised civically too. For instance, badil Resource Center for Palestinian Residency and Refugee Rights attests that fol-lowing the 1967 War, “Israel has developed a legal, political and military regime over the Palestinian people that combines occupation, apartheid and colo-

nization”.55 This juxtaposition of the three regimes was also substantiated by most human rights practitioners I interviewed.

Reviewing the specificity of the occupation here, the core disparities between

the law on occupations and the occupation of Palestinians can be observed on three levels. First, military occupations are presumed to be extraordinary, tem-porary and conservationist; Israel’s occupation of Palestinians appears per-manent/ open- ended, and invasive/ interventionist. Second, occupations are bound by a body of law; here, the systematic transgression of legal norms by the Occupying Power for over five decades creates an additional layer of harm for the occupied, due to the intergenerational, chronic, self- replicating nature of criminality. Third, though occupations are permitted, settler colonialism, annexation, apartheid, and acts amounting to ethnic cleansing are unlawful yet ongoing. In Palestine/ Israel, as the occupation intersects these overlapping illegalities, it appears difficult to deal with from a transitional justice perspec-tive. The “occupation” thus evokes a sliver of the problem. These are just some aspects questioning the relevance of the laws on occupation, given their defi-cient implementation combined with their inability to meaningfully capture the specificity of harm in Palestine/ Israel.



3 Colonialism: A Fuzzy Framework?

While lacking a precise legal definition, colonialism is outlined in the UN Declaration on the Granting of Independence to Colonial Countries and

Peoples.56 The Declaration on Colonialism upholds the right of all peoples

to self- determination, considered a jus cogens, erga omnes norm.57 Though

54 John Dugard, ‘Apartheid and Occupation Under International Law’ (Hisham B. Sharabi

Memorial Lecture, Palestine Center, Washington DC, 2009).

55 badil Resource Center for Palestinian Residency and Refugee Rights, Survey of Palestinian

Refugees and Internally Displaced Persons 2013– 2015, vol viii, 2015, page xxxiv.

56 unga Resolution 1514 (14 December 1960).

57 Matthew Saul, ‘The Normative Status of Self- Determination in International Law: A

Formula for Uncertainty in the Scope and Content of the Right?’, 11(4) Human Rights Law

Review (2011) 609; Virginia Tilley (ed), Beyond Occupation: Apartheid, Colonialism and 146 Afana

non- binding, the Declaration is a norm in customary international law.58 The Declaration on Colonialism, the United Nations General Assembly Resolution

3103,59 and the Fourth Geneva Convention all address contexts of alien subju-gation and domination, segregation, and discrimination. Additional Protocol i also identifies “armed conflicts in which peoples are fighting against colonial domination and alien occupation and against racist régimes in the exercise of

their right of self- determination”.60

Since Palestine/ Israel is not treated as a colonial problem at the United

Nations, colonial crimes remain abstracted in human rights talks. In 1961, a UN Special Committee on Decolonization was set up. The Palestinian terri-tory is not listed among non- self- governing territories under colonial rule and in recent reports of the UN Special Rapporteur on the Rights of Indigenous

Peoples,61 Palestinians are not once mentioned though the Rapporteur’s mandate claims to include indigenous peoples globally, who “experience the consequences of historical colonisation and invasion of their territories, and face discrimination because of their distinct cultures, identities and ways

of life”.62 The Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples notes the ties

between colonisation and dispossession.63 Though non- binding and lacking clarity in its definition of indigeneity, on self- determination and just use of land and resources, it stipulates a series of individual and collective rights which, in the context of Israel’s occupation of Palestinians, remain systemati-cally violated.

Two comprehensive studies64 concluded, based on the Declaration on

Colonialism, the Fourth Geneva Convention and international law, that Israel’s

International Law in the Occupied Palestinian Territories (Pluto Press 2012); Marc Weller,

Escaping the Self- Determination Trap (Martinus Nijhoff 2008).

58 Christos Theodoropoulos, Colonialism and General International Law: The Contemporary

Theory of National Sovereignty and Self- Determination (New Horizon 1988).

59 unga Resolution 3103 (12 December 1973).

60 Protocol Additional to the Geneva Conventions of 12 August 1949, and Relating to the

Protection of Victims of International Armed Conflicts (8 June 1977) Article 1(4).

61 unhrc, Report of the Special Rapporteur of the Human Rights Council on the Rights of

Indigenous Peoples, Victoria Tauli- Corpuz, a/ 73/ 176, 17 July 2018; unhrc, Report of the

Special Rapporteur on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples, Victoria Tauli- Corpuz, a/ hrc/ 30/

41, 6 August 2015.

62 unhrc, UN Special Rapporteur on Indigenous Peoples: Overview, 2020.

63 unga Resolution 61/ 295 (13 September 2007).

64 Human Sciences Research Council of South Africa, Occupation, Colonialism, Apartheid?

A Re- assessment of Israel’s Practices in the Occupied Palestinian Territories Under

International Law, 2009, pages 120– 151; Virginia Tilley (ed), Beyond Occupation: Apartheid,

Colonialism and International Law in the Occupied Palestinian Territories (Pluto Press

2012) pages 79– 106.

The Occupation-Colonialism Continuum 147

military occupation exhibits the character of a colonial regime. These stud-ies document how Israel has violated the territorial integrity of the occupied territory; deprived the occupied people of the capacity for self- governance; integrated the economy of the occupied territory into that of the occupant; deprived the occupied people of permanent sovereignty over their natural resources; and suppressed the culture of the occupied population. Though Israel’s settler– extractive colonial structures are evident, legally the colonial-ism framework is problematic. Because colonialism is not criminalised, little

accountability is attached to this framework.65 Also, the prohibition of colo-nialism might only apply to Israel’s actions in the Palestinian territory occu-pied since 1967 and not to Israel “proper” (lands and resources grabbed in 1948). Since anti- colonial liberation movements kicked off in the 1950s and the Declaration on Colonialism was only adopted in 1960, the prohibition would not apply retroactively to Israel’s establishment.

As specified during a conference at the Birzeit Institute of Law, “the domi-

nant legal opinion is that the legal framework of colonialism is not applicable within the borders of existing states, even where founded through aggression, colonization, ethnic cleansing or genocide, such as the United States, Australia

and Israel”.66 Indeed, colonialism is often treated as obsolete and anachronis-tic. Given the limited scope and deficient implementation of the occupation and colonialism frameworks, scholars and practitioners recognise the apart-heid framework as the strategic path for countering crime under international

law.67 Significantly, Human Rights Watch, arguably the leading international human rights organisation, which historically has only discussed violations within the occupation framework and avoided the colonialism or apartheid frameworks, now sees Israeli policies and practices amounting to apartheid

and persecution.68

Another aspect surfacing from my findings is the antagonising potential of

discussing the impasse in terms of settler colonialism. While the occupation framework is now seen as incomplete and misleading, this does not turn the



65 Nadia Hijab and Ingrid Jaradat Gassner, ‘Talking Palestine: What Frame of Analysis?

Which Goals and Messages?’, Al Shabaka, 12 April 2017.

66 Birzeit University Institute of Law, Advocating for Palestinian Rights in Conformity with

International Law: Guidelines (‘Options and Strategies for the Palestinian People in

International Law’ Conference, Birzeit, 2013) 6.

67 John Reynolds, ‘Challenging the Nakba Through International Law?’, The Nakba Files,

27 July 2016; Nadia Hijab and Ingrid Jaradat Gassner, ‘Talking Palestine: What Frame of

Analysis? Which Goals and Messages?’, Al Shabaka, 12 April 2017.

68 Human Rights Watch, A Threshold Crossed: Israeli Authorities and the Crimes of Apartheid

and Persecution, 27 April 2021.

148 Afana

settler colonialism paradigm into an ideal replacement. My research docu-mented its analytical value, its capacity to map in a nuanced and historically accurate manner the roots of injustice and discord, both significant for transi-tional justice. Yet, its theoretically critical and emancipatory nature contrasts its diffuse value practically. During fieldwork, practitioners distinguished between the dry analytical approach targeting theory- building (typical of aca-demic work) and the fluid problem- solving one (specific to civic psychosocial activism). Some Israeli Jewish interviewees, while recognising the regime’s colonial facets, felt that insisting on settler colonialism may impede justice and reconciliation.

This theory/ praxis dichotomy (intellectually dissecting the impasse versus

practically transcending it) is not as clear- cut. “There is nothing so practical as

a good theory”,69 believed pioneer of action research Kurt Lewin. Theories are meant to distil social processes and thus ideally to optimise them. Words (re) structure realities: as shown in my research on knowledge (re)production in and on Palestine/ Israel, words can precipitate emancipation or can entrench

subjugation.70 Boxing and labelling realities is reductionist and can alienate stakeholders who do not resonate with a certain framing. Yet, boxes and labels are needed to counter the epistemic erasures pervasive here. Parrhesia (can-did speech) is key to research and activism on Palestine/ Israel — a site featur-ing the colonisation of space locally and the colonisation of minds globally. Knowledge (re)production can be emancipatory if premised on intellectual rigor, ethical clarity, and risk- taking.

Part of this distinction between pertinence and usefulness, when framing

the impasse as settler colonial, has to do with reception: the right terminology can create the wrong effects. For Palestinians, internalising the colonialism framework might deepen a sense of entitlement, which can block the critical and conciliatory mindset needed in the transitional journey. The settler colo-nialism framework can also antagonise Israeli society, one already dominated by denial and intransigence. Aside from the affective component, terminology has material facets, it drives political, legal, economic, social consequences. Recognising a conduct as colonial drives the imperative of decolonisation. Yet, looking at other “post”- colonial societies, decolonisation seldom is a smooth, non- violent process. As standards of justice are uneasy to agree upon and apply, decolonisation remains partial and can replicate injustice.



69 Kurt Lewin, Field Theory in Social Science: Selected Theoretical Papers (Harper and Row

1951) 169.

70 Rimona Afana, Words as Swords: Resisting Epistemic Violence on Israel/ Palestine (‘European

International Studies Association’ Conference, Barcelona, 2017).

The Occupation-Colonialism Continuum 149

Worth noting is also that the power asymmetry and deep- seated antago-

nism common in colonial societies lead to essentialist conceptions of identity. Palestine’s “settler colonial turn”, appears to me, often marked by reduction-ism and militancy, inimical to critical reflection and praxis. After for several years insisting on settler colonialism as the pertinent lens for delineating the justice– reconciliation nexus in Palestine/ Israel, I concluded my transi-tional justice research in 2017 with a sense of ambivalence. The fuzzy legal status of colonialism, the partial applicability of colonialism to this site and its antagonising effect can deepen the tensions and tradeoffs between justice and reconciliation. The settler colonialism framework thus frustrates certain transitional justice goals. These aspects motivated me to explore other analyt-ical lenses relevant to the justice– reconciliation nexus, since my interest as a peace researcher and activist is not to simply designate a process as illegal or immoral, but to understand how antagonism and crime can be transcended so that fewer lives get broken.

Inspired by the literature on hybridity,71 my recent research has challenged

the unnuanced decolonisation trope previously embraced.72 Building on

hybridity, I added insights from vulnerability theory,73 jurisprudence which foregrounds vulnerability as universal and constant, an alternative to formal equality and to identity politics. Vulnerability characterises the universal human condition: embodied (we depend on a fragile material case, the body) and embedded (we are never autonomous but depend on others in complex ways). The opposite of vulnerability, resilience — the capacity to withstand harm, becomes the responsibility of law and of institutions. Vulnerability the-ory makes me imagine Israelis and Palestinians as a continuum of both vulner-

ability and resilience within the transitional journey.74 The conundrum here is that settler colonialism seems incompatible with vulnerability theory: how

71 Homi K Bhabha, The Location of Culture (Routledge 1994); Amar Acheraïou, Questioning

Hybridity, Postcolonialism and Globalization (Palgrave Macmillan 2011); Pnina Werbner

and Tariq Modood (eds), Debating Cultural Hybridity: Multicultural Identities and the

Politics of Anti- Racism (Zed Books 2015).

72 Rimona Afana, Israel/ Palestine and the Settler Colonial Paradigm: The Thorny Practicalities

of a Good Theory (‘University of Warwick Critical Legal’ Conference, Coventry, 2017);

Rimona Afana, Settler Colonialism and Hybridity: Transcending the Native/ Settler Binary in

Israel/ Palestine (‘Communication, Identity, Context’ Conference, Craiova, 2017).

73 Martha Albertson Fineman, ‘The Vulnerable Subject: Anchoring Equality in the Human

Condition’, 20(1) Yale Journal of Law and Feminism (2008) 1; Martha Albertson Fineman,

‘The Vulnerable Subject and the Responsive State’, 60(2) Emory Law Journal (2010) 251.

74 Rimona Afana, ‘Settler Colonial Binaries Unsettled: Exploring the Justice– Reconciliation

Nexus in Israel/ Palestine Through a Vulnerability Lens’ (Emory University School of Law,

Vulnerability and the Human Condition Initiative Visiting Scholars Talks, Atlanta, 2018). 150 Afana

does universal vulnerability apply to a settler colonial site where the focus is disproportionately on the vulnerability of the colonised? To me, the uni-versality of vulnerability unsettles the settler/ native binary common in this paradigm. Should we then consider the vulnerability of both oppressor and oppressed, or does placing them on an equal analytical plane only replicate the power differential?

When applied to settler colonial spaces and temporalities, vulnerability the-

ory illumes interdependence and challenges simplistic hierarchies of victim-hood. While Zionism itself is hybrid (blending features of both settler colonial and national emancipation movements), the lives of Israelis and Palestinians remain bound by the hybridity produced by cultural commonalities, demo-graphic dynamics, economic ties, and by the centrality of the impasse to each

side’s collective identity. To me, this “relentless reciprocity”75 in settler colonial sites makes the need to harmonise justice with reconciliation inevitable. One segment of that journey is transcending what Homi Bhabha calls “primordial

polarities”.76 The application here of vulnerability theory unsettles standard accounts of indigeneity and decolonisation. A fluid approach to identity, rights, responsibilities may thus be needed to transcend the impasse — not concen-trating on the exclusive vulnerability of the oppressed (and essentialised) native, but mapping gradients and interdependencies around vulnerabilities.



4 Occupation– Colonialism: Views from the Field

This section collates excerpts from my interviews, complementing the theory earlier discussed. My research on the justice– reconciliation nexus included the intersection between occupation and colonialism within my contextual-isation of this nexus (alongside its conceptualisation and operationalisation, in a structure akin to three concentric circles). During fieldwork in fall 2016, I spoke with Israelis and Palestinians engaged with justice and reconciliation issues: founders, directors of, and activists with: Al-Haq, badil, the bds move-ment, B’Tselem, Gisha, Zochrot, Machsom Watch, Rabbis for Human Rights, Al Quds University Human Rights Clinic, the Parents Circle, Roots, Two Nations One Homeland, Alternative Action, and other voices in what I identified as an epistemic community shaping the meaning of justice and reconciliation. As transitional justice studies on Palestine/ Israel are mostly theoretical and

75 Jean- Paul Sartre, ‘Introduction’ in Albert Memmi, The Colonizer and the Colonized

(Earthscan Publications 2003) 24.

76 Homi K Bhabha, The Location of Culture (Routledge 1994) 5.

The Occupation-Colonialism Continuum 151

reliant on secondary data, my empirical findings contribute a grassroots perspective.

The spatial and social fragmentation at the heart of the impasse seemed

mirrored by activism seeking to treat the impasse: civil society operates as a non- contiguous, heterogeneous space. To me, this transitional activism amid a non- transitional site evidences disparities between justice (materialised in truth- seeking and accountability efforts, engaged with the political and legal spheres, so vertically: people versus State and reconciliation (mainstream-ing interpersonal engagement, often within a psycho- social framework, so horizontally, people- to- people). My interviews evidenced the failure of the occupation lingo to capture everyday colonial realities, some contributors depicting the occupation as a smokescreen foror extension of colonialism. Practitioners’ insights also unveiled the handicap of civil society to meaning-fully combat colonial criminality while stuck in the misleading occupation paradigm. A tension emerged in civic praxis between the ’67 and ’48 frame-works: while most groups work within the 1967 paradigm (occupation), many activists recognised the need to shift to 1948 (colonialism) for a substantive engagement with injustice and discord. This is a recognition of how significant the ties between colonialism and occupation are for transitional justice here. These interview excerpts show how contributors see the ties between the colo-nial matrix and the occupation.

Professor Mazin Qumsiyeh, Director of the Palestine Museum of Natural

History and former Chairman of the Palestinian Centre for Rapprochement between People:

One needs to make a diagnosis: why did I mention the origin of the prob-

lem in the 19th century? Because if you need to make a diagnosis, the

doctor first asks a bit about the history: when did the first symptoms

manifest? The first symptoms and the history of the patient allow me to

make the right diagnosis, which helps me later not confuse a symptom

with the underlying aetiology. If you confuse a symptom with the under-

lying aetiology, you are a poor doctor. […] Identifying the cause, that is

colonialism, is critical and colonialism entails racism and all the issues

we talked about earlier. Once you make the right diagnosis, then you

are not confusing symptoms, like the occupation, like violence, the wall,

like colonies here — settlements (this is a colony right here, Jabal Abu

Ghneim in Arabic, Har Homa in Hebrew), with the underlying aetiology.

[…] When you deal with just one of the symptoms, you’re liable to leave

the disease uncured and you’re liable to get other symptoms. 152 Afana

Ingrid Jaradat, Al- Shabaka Policy Advisor, former Director and co- founder of badil, and founding member of the Palestinian Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions campaign:

You start with a colonial project, but the institutionalisation of that

here is apartheid. […] This framework of occupation, the Fourth Geneva

Convention, Hague Regulations, international humanitarian law is not a

framework suitable for justice. It’s the laws of war, who would think it’s

the basis for justice? But it’s the framework that became prominent in the

legal advocacy work and human rights, simply because it fits the political

approach we’ve had. It seemed if we advocate on this basis, we have a bet-

ter chance of getting support and making a difference. Nowadays there is

more recognition, especially among the human rights organisations with

more capacity and experience, like Al- Haq, that we need to go beyond

this framework. […] We need to package these individual human rights

violations into something bigger that stands for the serious violations

and crimes against mankind — colonialism, apartheid and other crimes

against humanity, and demand commitments from Third States on this

basis. Otherwise, we can talk human rights forever. […]

The problem is that the little human rights organisations don’t have

yet the capacity. International humanitarian law became prominent

starting with the First Intifada: everyone was training Palestinian civil

society on ihl. So, the smaller organisations do this kind of copy/ paste

of what they hear, they do not look into issues by themselves and develop

their own ideas. We repeat that talk as if the Fourth Geneva Convention

was the only framework we had. […] But then Palestinian organisations

are also afraid to talk colonialism or apartheid because their donors do

not like it or because they don’t really understand it themselves, and it

feels like sloganizing. To be brave, you need to study. People do not read,

unfortunately. It has to do with capacity and knowledge. Of course, there

is pressure; but you can resist pressure if you are convinced. But if you

don’t even know what it is about, how would you defend it?

Shawan Jabarin, General Director of Al- Haq:

Here you see a colonial nature, occupation nature, and apartheid nature

in one — in practices, policies, in all aspects of everyday life. […] At Al-

Haq we studied this prolonged occupation and in an academic paper,

together with other organisations, we now call it an occupation with the

nature of colonialism and apartheid. […] When you start with justice, The Occupation-Colonialism Continuum 153

you put a prevention wall before the next crimes. Here you cannot ignore

the crimes that took place. Without treating what happened before in a

just way, you will not prevent crimes in the future. We look at justice as

prevention; we are not dealing with justice as a technical, philosophical

thing. If you set a lesson that you can commit crimes and nothing will

happen to you, this is a very bad lesson in history. That is what I mean

when I say justice first, justice second, justice last. Without justice, no

way for reconciliation or transition.

Dr Munir Nuseibah, Director of the Al Quds University Human Rights Clinic and Director of the Community Action Center:

In international humanitarian law you have the principle that forced

displacement and transferring your own population in occupied terri-

tory are illegal, you also have the prohibition on discrimination. If you

combine different principles, you get enough to condemn as illegal the

colonial practices of Israel in the opt. The answer to illegality can come

in two forms: State responsibility (stopping the action, guarantees of

non- repetition, reparations including restitution, compensation, and

satisfaction which means apology) and individual responsibility. These

issues find expressions of illegality in international law: settlements, dis-

placement, torture, illegal imprisonment. […] We cannot do transitional

justice and keep the status quo at the same time. The peace process never

used transitional justice, only used realistic politics. What bothers me is

this: attempts to force transitional justice into the realistic framework.

I would argue we need to choose either/ or. Transitional justice is linked

to justice, justice is linked to international law and if we go down that

route, we need to introduce equality, reparations, and so on. If we want

to think of transitional justice, we need to be unrealistic; that’s my choice

in what I write and what I do.

Eitan Diamond, Manager/ Senior Legal Expert of Diakonia ihl Centre, for-merly Executive Director of Gisha — Legal Center for Freedom of Movement, and legal adviser for the icrc:

I share the view that framing it in the language of occupation obscures

the full scope and the core of the problem. To the law of occupation

terminology many Israelis would respond with anger; they see it as an

unacceptable critique. Yet, this language of occupation serves the State: it

gives a legal, political, ethical justification for maintaining a regime that 154 Afana

distinguishes between different populations on ethnic grounds. […] The

architects of this believe if you do it for long enough, then the violation

is no longer an issue. […] The international community predominantly

follows this narrative of the law of occupation and the two- state solu-

tion; alternative models are a threat to the accepted solution. But gradu-

ally circumstances on the ground dictate rethinking. The colonisation of

Palestine has been successful at setting realities on the ground. Like other

colonial enterprises in the New World, the idea that they would just leave

seems less and less viable. There has been no justice for the indigenous,

yet Palestinians are still here and alive. […].

Given the occupation, even without addressing the broader injustices

before the occupation, this human rights work has been about trying

to maximise justice within an unjust framework. There’s constant frus-

tration about the framework, about our limits to challenge the broader

problem. [...] For quite few of us and it is the case with me, even the way

the core problem is articulated is not enough. Israeli human rights organ-

isations identify the fundamental source of injustice and what needs

to be solved for reconciliation as the 1967 occupation. They don’t gen-

erally speak about other injustices and sources of tension, for instance

’48 is rarely addressed in this language of human rights. For some of us

that is a real concern. […] Some of the human rights NGOs now try to

shift emphasis, try tackle what they see as the core problem rather than

operating within the framework, they try to upset this framework and

pressure Israeli authorities using Western power agendas. The appeal

of international law for activists here is that it’s not the same as going

to Israeli courts and pleading their justice, but you bring in a normative

framework created outside and mobilise actors committed to those stan-

dards to put pressure on Israel.

Dr. Zahira Kamal, former Minister of Women’s Affairs of Palestine; founding and board member in several women’s rights NGOs:

Any occupation lasts for a certain time, not forever. Israel considers this

is their own land, although signing the Oslo. By 1995 Israel should have

withdrawn from all Palestinian lands and by 1999 they should have with-

drawn and negotiated a final status. But we find that after twenty years

Israel has not withdrawn, it confiscates more lands, tearing into pieces

Palestinian lands, dividing the country into small pieces through cutting

roads, like Bantustans in South Africa.

The Occupation-Colonialism Continuum 155

Osama Al Risheq, Legal Supervisor, Al Quds University Human Rights Clinic:

It’s an underestimation when you say what’s happening now is only

an occupation. […] That’s why you may find a huge gap between inter-

national humanitarian law and the status in Palestine. International

humanitarian law has been made to organise international conflicts

and occupation, but it was not at all in their mind that an occupation

could extend for 50 years. For example, you do not see much protection

of the right to education in international humanitarian law; you do not

see a clear and strong protection for many human rights of the people

under occupation. […] That is why we believe the word "occupation” is an

understatement. We believe it’s colonialism implementing an apartheid

system under the name of occupation.

Meissa Irshaid, Attorney formerly with the Public Committee Against Torture in Israel and with Gisha:

“Military occupation” is too naive and does not reflect the reality of the

Israeli control over the Palestinians’ bodies and minds in the broader

concept than the military frame. For example, Palestinians like me, hav-

ing Israeli citizenship, are not under direct military occupation. I can live

in any Arab city within Israel for my whole life and never see a check-

point or soldier! Still, I live under dominant control over my identity, life,

education, economic system: a colonialism maintained by the State civil

system. So, the symbol of militarism is not seen or felt in my daily life.

While if we take the West Bank, soldiers do apply the so- called “Israeli

law” on Palestinians (military courts, checkpoints), but not because the

military rule as a strong power with different agenda than the State poli-

cies. […] Even if the Israeli military control over the West Bank and Gaza

ends now, the colonialism does not stop by this act. Military occupation

isn’t comprehensive. Settlers also: another perspective why it is not a mil-

itary occupation by definition.

Doubi Schwartz, regional Program Officer of the Alliance for Middle East Peace; former co- Director of Parents Circle– Families Forum:

I find it difficult to call it occupation. It was an interim stage, it was sup-

posed to be something temporary, you are right. We had this illusion we

are a democratic State: Jews have many democratic privileges, even those

we are losing slowly. But how can you be democratic when millions live 156 Afana

without basic human and civil rights? I would take it to a different per-

spective: you need to understand what the army and the occupying force

mean in Israeli society. I was working for many years on different iden-

tity issues in informal education in Israel. The identity of secular Israelis

stands on two feet: the Holocaust and the army. People define themselves

using these two variables. The army is something sacred: this is why it’s

so difficult to talk about the occupation. You cannot talk badly about the

army, you’re treated as a traitor; how dare you talk about the army? People

in peace NGOs, our kids are all going to the army. […] Many Israelis don’t

see it as an occupation and it’s not just psychological: most Israelis came

here (or were born) after ’67. For them this is reality, they do not know

what was here before. The ‘Occupation’ to them became like ‘Peace’. […]

If you listen today to people on the left, by occupation they mean con-

trolling the life of people, they do not talk about the land. People under-

stand it better: you cannot deny you control them. For them occupying

the land is a taboo, they won’t listen when you talk about occupation.

Meron Rapoport, co- founder of Two States One Homeland:

I do not have an answer to what is the purpose of this almost fifty- year

occupation. I certainly agree with you and this is strangely enough a dis-

cussion I have with my friends on the left, the more radical left. For me

’67 is no accident. Many times it’s described especially by the classical

left (the Zionist left, the Labour Party) as if Israel was going in a cer-

tain way and then there was an accident: Israel was de- routed by this

occupation and all we have to do is move back this train on its tracks.

This is a total misconception of reality, of the very nature of Zionism.

[…] I remember that moment, for me as a leftist born and raised in Tel

Aviv, believing in the two- state solution, believing if we fix ’67 everything

will come back to normal. Understanding it’s all about ’48 was not easy

for me. […] But the fact that it goes on for fifty years is no accident; it

has to do with the deeper feeling that we Jews own all the land. […] My

movement does not dispute this feeling; that you feel part of it is alright.

I am not going to lecture it’s not like this, I’m just saying you need to

share it. We try to decolonise the practice of Jewish settlement, to make

Israeli Jews part of this land, to nativise those coming from Europe, from

Yemen, from Morocco. It won’t work if we see only ’67 and only the occu-

pation as a problem.

A 29- year- old Palestinian male who preferred to remain anonymous:

The Occupation-Colonialism Continuum 157

It’s just an excuse for both governments and extremist parties to keep the

conflict going, to get what they want from powerful countries they wish

to associate to as big daddies. They minimise the problem as mere occu-

pation as represented by checkpoints; they know they can remove these

at any point and everything that is the actual occupation which rep-

resents segregation — those would stay. It’s not difficult, but they make it

seem impossible, so both sides can keep blackmailing the world for secu-

rity and protection. That’s whom this narrative serves: the organisations

interested in violence and both governments. If it were not identified as

occupation, as it’s more than that — it’s segregation and oppression, then

you wouldn’t find all these organisations and businesses, and interna-

tional interests. It seems the international community is not so moved by

segregation as it is by what “occupation” sounds like. The problem is not

military occupation: there is no opposing side from Palestinians for us to

really call it a military conflict. […] As long as it keeps being defined as

traditional occupation, we are just delusional, in denial, and we keep try-

ing to fix semi- or sub- problems, like talking about “equality” or “justice”.



5 Conclusion

Seeing Israel’s occupation as an offshoot of the settler– extractive colonial matrix changes how we discuss criminality against Palestinians. While the occupation paradigm has monopolised academic, civic and diplomatic dis-course for decades, a rigorous engagement with the impasse requires treat-ing the illness (colonialism) and not persist in symptomatic relief (ending the occupation). Treating the occupation as a form of resistance to (or suppression of) the colonised people’s resistance to colonialism offers new ways of concep-tualising transitional justice and conflict resolution in Palestine/ Israel. While removing the occupation is more feasible logistically and politically than addressing colonialism, for any realistic prospects for nonviolence and equity, at least some ramifications of colonialism will need to be also addressed. A truth commission, apologies, prosecutions, restitution, and reparations are needed to repair colonial legacies and thus prevent further intergenerational trauma.

If the past is any indication of the future when it comes to Israel’s willingness

to take responsibility and the international community’s capacity to demand accountability, the prospects for transitional justice are bleak. Decolonisation is more aspirational than a process we have witnessed successfully unfold in other colonised societies. Both the extra- ordinary nature of Israel’s occupation 158 Afana

and its intricate ties to the colonial matrix evidence the inadequacy of inter-national law to prevent, halt and repair contemporary forms of criminality. The deficiency of international humanitarian law and criminal law, and the paralysis of international fora meant to deliver justice are shaped by the geo-politics of victimhood and accountability. The colonial nature of international law and the inconsistent ways in which it is applied leave illusory solutions for the weak and indefinite loopholes for the powerful. The brutality and impunity of Israel’s occupationare thus part of a wider discussion about the changing nature of crime, the limitations and fragility of international law, and the geo-political agendas shaping repair.





chapter 7


Israel’s Military Justice System as an Annexationist

Tool

Sahar Francis



Israel’s military justice system that came into effect in the wake of the Israeli occupation in 1967, as well as its various amendments and modifications over the subsequent decades, is the result of long- term Israeli strategic planning that was initiated even as far back as 1963. This included a legal scheme to take military control not only of Palestinian lands, but also of its civilian residents and every aspect of their daily lives to serve the Israeli interest in maintaining the occupation indefinitely. It is through military control that Israel has pro-gressed the occupation from a temporary situation into a long- lasting reality, ending with the actual de facto and de jure annexation of the land.

Since 1967, Israel in its capacity as Occupying Power, has issued almost

2,000 military orders governing all aspects of Palestinian political, civilian, economic, cultural, and social life. Through these military orders, Israel has outlawed activities that are considered fundamental human rights and form the cornerstones of democracy, such as freedom of expression and opinion,

political participation, and the formation of political parties.1 Military orders have been issued on, State property, absentee property, closed military areas,

land confiscation, urban planning, and taxation.2 Crucially, these military orders have paved the way for two internal systems of control; the judicial system, including the military courts, military objection committees, military



1 See Israeli Military Order No 101 (1967), Order to Prevent Incitement and Hostile Propaganda

(1967); Defence (Emergency) Regulations (1945), Section G, Article 84 and 85. The Israeli

occupation declared all Palestinian political parties at that time as illegal organisations.

2 Israeli Military Order No 59, Related to State Property (31 July 1967); Israeli Military Order No

291, Related to Land Registration and Water Planning (19 December 1968); Israeli Military

Order No 58, Related to Absentees’ Property (Private Property) (23 July 1967); Israeli Military

Order No 25, Related to Purchase of Immovable Property (18 June 1967); Israeli Military Order

No 28, Related to Income Tax and Property Tax (22 June 1967).



© Sahar Francis, 2023 | DOI:10.1163/978900450393

160 Francis

judges and prosecutors, and the civil administration system created in 1981.3 The focus of this chapter will be on the former.

In particular, this chapter will examine how Israel created a military judi-

cial justice system through a series of restrictive military orders.4 The problem with the Israeli military judicial system goes beyond its continuous violations of the guarantees to a fair trial. The military judicial system raises serious questions as to the legality of the occupation given its prolonged, and now entrenched, exercise of jurisdiction over Palestinian civilians before Israeli military courts. In 1990, after some 20 years of occupation, Palestinian legal

scholar Raja Shehadeh,5 proposed that the real intention behind Israel’s sys-tem of military control over the opt, is to impose irreversible changes on the geography and demography of the occupied territory, with one aim: to uproot

the Palestinian people and annex the land.6 Today, some 30 years after the so- called Oslo peace agreement, Israel still refuses to withdraw its troops from the occupied territory, the military governor acts as de facto sovereign, and the military courts have become a much more sophisticated system.



1 Establishment, Scope and Jurisdiction of the Military Courts

On 7 June 1967, in the immediate wake of the occupation, the Israeli military commander of the West Bank area issued three military proclamations. The first proclamation concerned the takeover of administration by the Israeli

Occupying Forces (security and public order) of the occupied territory,7 and

the second concerned administrative and judicial procedures. Section 2 of the second proclamation stated that:

Forces of the area.8

Further, Section 3 stated that:

Every governmental, legislative, appointive and administrative power

in respect of the region or its inhabitants shall henceforth be vested in

me (The West Bank Area Commander) alone and shall only be exercised

by me or by persons appointed by me for that purpose or acting on my

The third proclamation, Concerning Security Provisions (West Bank) of 1967,10 introduced the security provisions Order No 3 (which became Israeli Military

Order No 378).11 Order No 3 granted the military commander the authority to establish military courts in the opt. The military courts were granted lib-eral jurisdiction to hear any violation under the order or any other military order, proclamation or statement issued by the military commander, or any

other crime that the commander decides on.12 Following this, the military

commander established five military courts (currently there are two)13 and appointed military judges. Initially, the military courts were presided over

In practice, this resulted in the prosecution of thousands of Palestinians in a military court system that failed to provide professional, specialised judges. This practice continued for 37 years until the amendment of 2004 that required all members of the judicial panel to possess

legal and judicial competencies.16

Article 19 of the Order Concerning Security Provisions (West Bank) (No 3) of

1967,17 prevented the appeal of military court rulings, a practice that was only

amended in the 1980s when a military Court of Appeals was established.18 Military Order No 3 also established the judicial procedures of the military court, as well as the arrest and search procedures. Moreover, the military order defined crimes that fell within the scope of the courts and granted the military commander the authority to issue administrative detention orders. Most nota-bly, Article 35 of Order No 3 required the court to follow and give precedence to the Geneva Conventions stating that a:

[M] ilitary court and its administration shall follow the provisions of

the Geneva Convention of 12 August 1949 relating to the Protection of

Civilians in War insofar as concerns judicial proceedings, and where

this Order conflicts with the said Convention, the provisions of the

Convention shall prevail.19

Israel soon backtracked and removed this article in an amendment,20 under-scoring its intention towards the mistreatment and maladministration of the occupied territory and people. At the same time, the military orders granted the military commander and military judges a wide scope of author-ity enabling them to act in any way they deemed fit. For example, Articles 10 and 11 of Military Order No 3 concerning security provisions, allow the court to

In practice, the military courts have relied on unfamiliar procedural rules

and legal precedents borrowed from Israeli law, which most Palestinian law-yers who study in Palestinian universities are unfamiliar with. Hebrew is the main language in the court, there are no professional translators, instead poor incomprehensible translations from Hebrew to Arabic are done by soldiers

serving their military service.22 The language barrier seriously affects due pro-cess and is part of the coercive conditions that force plea bargain deals in the military courts.

1.1 The Scope of the Military Courts Notably, the Israeli military courts have breached international humanitarian law both in terms of their scope and legal procedures. In terms of scope, the military courts have extended their jurisdiction over civilians for minor civil law offenses. Palestinian detainees include those who have participated in active conflict or resisted the occupation, in addition to thousands of civilian Palestinian labour workers who have entered Israel without security permits, as well as civilian Palestinians summoned by the Israeli forces for minor traf-

fic violations or for building without permits from the Civil Administration.23 Many jurists argue that prosecuting civilians before military courts fails to uphold the guarantees of a right to a fair trial, in violation of their right to an

independent and impartial tribunal.24

21 Article 10, Israeli Military Order No 3, Concerning Security Provisions (1967), “Military

courts follow in evidentiary procedures, the procedures followed in the military courts of

soldiers. Under the condition that the military court is allowed to decide not to follow the

procedures if it thinks justices deems this, and for special reasons that is written by the

court”.

22 Article 116, Israeli Military Order No 1651 (2009). See also Lisa Hajjar, Courting Conflict –

The military commander has further expanded the jurisdiction of the mili-

tary courts beyond the borders of the opt, granting the military courts extra-territorial jurisdiction to prosecute anyone who has committed an act outside the region which would, “constitute an offence had it been committed within the region and the act harmed or was designed to harm the security of the

Region or public order therein”.25

Military orders grant the military courts the authority to try any person

who commits an offense in violation of the military orders, or any other law, regardless of the person’s citizenship status – whether an Israeli or a foreigner. However even when Israeli settlers kill Palestinians in the opt, they have not been prosecuted by the military courts. They are instead, prosecuted before

the Israeli civilian courts, outside the occupied territory.26 The practice clearly offends against Article 42 of the Hague Regulations, which establishes, that “the occupation extends only to the territory where such authority has been

established and can be exercised”.27 Accordingly, Sharon Weill persuasively argues, that the occupation possesses no legal jurisdiction outside of the State’s region; otherwise, the extraterritorial legislative authority becomes, “a tool for

domination and not necessarily for protection”.28

Rarely is an Israeli settler prosecuted for an offense before the military courts

in the occupied territory. One exceptional case, in 2006, was that of an Israeli

settler who was ordered under house arrest by the military commander.29 At

(fair trial rights under armed conflict). Amnesty International calls for the prosecution of

crimes related to human rights violations and crimes included in international law before

civil courts and not in military courts.

the time, judge Netanel Benisho, Vice President of the Military Appeals Court, concluded that Israeli settlers should be classed as local residents for the pur-poses of the Fourth Geneva Convention, and the military commander must therefore, protect their interests when deciding on their liberty. Judge Benisho further considered that the court’s territorial jurisdiction is applicable equally to all residents and visitors of the occupied territory without discrimination

based on nationality, religion, or place of residence.30 The reasoning is in stark violation of Article 49 of the Fourth Geneva Convention, prohibiting the Occupying Power from transferring parts of its own civilian population into

the occupied territory.31

1.2 Impartiality, Legality and the Rule against Retroactive Effect The military retains significant influence over military court judges, which seriously compromises their independence. Although, Article 8 of Military Order 1651 provides that “there is no authority over one authorized to adjudi-cate, apart from the authority of the law and security legislation”, in practice,

this has not been achieved.32 For example, the military commander appoints judges based on the recommendations of the Judicial Selection Committee comprising of seven members. The Committee is chaired by the President of the Courts- Martial Appeals Court and includes one civil representative of the Israel Bar Association, to be selected by the National Council of the Bar

Association.33 The majority of the Judicial Selection Committee are mem-bers of the military and subject to its authority; administratively, both the jsc and the military prosecutors, are subject to the authority of the Office of the Military Advocate General. Inevitably, therefore, most military court judges are military officers who climbed the ladder of the military ranks to become judges. Some of those judges having previously served as military prosecutors

before the military courts.34

That some of the judges are settlers is also a matter of serious concern for

the independence and impartiality of the military judicial system, especially

Similar, to the British Security Regulations of 1945, the crimes provided

for under military orders are drafted in a very general manner. In the opt for example, an offense against public order is prohibited under Military Order 1651 stating:

A person will not commit an act or omission which entail harm, damage,

disturbance or danger to the security of the region or the security of the

idf, or to the operation, use or security of one of the following: boat, air-

plane, port, platform, dock, harbor, airport.36

Accordingly, the military courts may liberally interpret what activities entail or pose a security threat, justifying the arrest of hundreds of Palestinian detainees.

International humanitarian law provides a number of critical procedural

safeguards protecting the occupied population against violations of due pro-cess and fair procedure by the military courts of the Occupying Power. The Geneva Conventions incorporate the principle of legality and protect civilians and prisoners of war against prosecution for acts that were not considered a

crime at the time they were committed.37 The principle of legality is further

reflected in international human rights treaties, applicable to the opt.38 The general principle provides that, “only the law can define a crime and prescribe a penalty and the principle that criminal law must not be extensively construed

to an accused’s detriment”.39 Further, the prohibition against retroactive appli-cation of criminal laws means that no one “may be prosecuted for an act or omission that did not constitute a criminal offence in national or international

law when it was committed”.40

Israel’s Military Justice System as an Annexationist Tool 167

One case illustrating how the military courts have breached the principle

of legality and the rule against retroactive effect, is the Case of the Palestinian

Legislative Council from 2006.41 In June 2006, Israeli forces arrested 48 mem-bers of the “Change and Reform” bloc of the plc who were members of par-liament and ministers in the Palestinian government. The plc members were charged with membership of and working for an illegal organisation, occupy-ing a position in an illegal organisation, as well as representing an illegal organ-isation through their membership in the plc. Key international law principles, such as sovereign immunity from prosecution, were rejected outright by the military court. At the time of their arrest, the “Change and Reform” bloc had not been declared by the Israeli military commander as an illegal organisa-tion. It was not for nearly another year in March 2007, that the “Change and Reform” bloc was outlawed in the opt under military order. Nevertheless, the parliamentarians were all retroactively sentenced for membership in an illegal organisation. Clearly, the main aim behind the arrest and prosecution of the 48 parliamentarians, was to paralyse the plc and impede Palestinian political discourse against the occupation.

In 2009, the Israeli military commander issued the Order Regarding

Security Provisions.42 This order, also known as the Criminal Code of the Military Courts, codified dozens of previous military orders on topics such as

criminal procedures and judges’ appointments.43 The codification addressed some key criticisms aimed at the military courts’ operating procedures by local

and international human rights organisations, Third States and UN bodies.44

court of Yehuda. The Military Court of Appeals decided to unite all the appeals of the

military prosecutor against the decision to release the plc members on bail by the first

military court. The case was published under the name Military Court of Appeals, Military

Prosecutor v Bassem Za’arur, Appeal No 3249/ 06, 12 September 2006.

For example, Israel raised the majority age of Palestinian children from 16 to

18 years.45 The following section will address some of these procedures and amendments, highlighting in particular, Israel’s failure to uphold its respon-sibility to ensure a fair trial to every Palestinian detainee arrested and prose-cuted before the military court system.



2 How Israel uses the Military Courts against Palestinian Civilians

Article 66 of the Fourth Geneva Convention, permits the Occupying Power to prosecute those accused of breach of penal provisions before “properly consti-tuted, non- political military courts, on condition that the said courts sit in the

occupied country”.46 However, while ihl grants the Occupying Power compe-tence to establish military courts and pass military laws, it also imposes strin-gent conditions to regulate them. Article 64 of the Fourth Geneva Convention only narrowly permits the Occupying Power to repeal or suspend the penal laws of the occupied territory on condition, “they constitute a threat to its security or an obstacle to the application of the present Convention”. The pri-mary consideration in replacing penal legislation is to safeguard the interests of the people of the occupied territory, or for the legitimate security interests of the Occupying Power.

Therefore, one must examine the jurisdiction of the military courts in the

opt over all aspects of life, including land confiscation, the construction of the Wall, settlements, checkpoints, and bypass roads, and question whether this

for Children International, No Way to Treat a Child: Palestinian Children in the Military

Detention System, April 2016; unicef, Children in Military Detention Observations and

Recommendations Bulletin No 2, February 2015; Delegation of British Lawyers, Children in

Military Custody, UK Foreign and Commonwealth Office, June 2012.

were considered adults between the ages of 16– 18. The UN Committee on the Rights of

Israel’s Military Justice System as an Annexationist Tool 169

exceeds Israel’s competence under ihl. Further, one must question whether the extension of jurisdiction beyond what has been provided for under the Geneva Conventions amounts to an unlawful extension of the Occupying Power’s sovereignty over the occupied territory, in breach of jus cogens norms

on the non- acquisition of territory through use of force.47

Even after the Oslo Accords, Israel continued to grant authority to the mil-

itary courts to prosecute any Palestinian citizen residing in Area A (per the Oslo Accords classification) who committed an offense that harmed or meant

to harm the security of the region.48 Further, the military courts retained the authority to prosecute any Palestinian residing in Area A who committed a criminal offense according to the Jordanian Criminal Code No 16, the gen-

eral law of the occupied territory applicable prior to 1967.49 This effectively means that the military commander has superseded the competence of the Palestinian domestic courts, a decades- long overreaching of jurisdiction that goes far beyond the competence of the military commander to maintain the status quo under Articles 43 of the Hague Regulations, and subject to the humanitarian and security considerations of Articles 64 of the Fourth Geneva Convention.

Worryingly, minor offences such as traffic violations and entry into Israel

without a work permit, constitute more than half of the cases prosecuted

before the military courts (Ofer and Salem court) in recent years.50 Out of 10,273 charge sheets filed before the courts in 2016, some 6,201 were traffic violations, while 1,092 were cases of entry into Israel without a work permit;

together accounting for 71.24 percent of the charge sheets.51 Security and public



47 For more detailed analysis of the legality of the occupation based on these three con-

ditions see: Orna Ben- Naftali, Aeyal Gross and Keren Michaeli, ‘Illegal Occupation: The

Framing of the Occupied Palestinian Territory’, 23(3) Berkeley Journal of International Law

(2005) 557.

48 Israeli Military Proclamation No 7, Concerning the Application of the Interim Agreement

(West Bank) (1995). Article 6(a) confirms that the military commander continues his

authority and responsibility in relation to legislation, judiciary and administration in

all areas outlined in the article, including security and public order of any area under

Israeli security. This may even include the area under the jurisdiction of the Palestinian

Authority.

(December 2009), Articles 10(E) and 10(F). In 1995, the Jordanian Criminal Code was

(Amendment No 6) (Judea and Samaria) (1995).

50 Military Advocate General, The Annual Activity Report of the Military Court, 2016 and 2017.

Report, 2017.

170 Francis

disorder offences constituted 25.52 percent of the overall charge sheets, that is 2,613 charges altogether. In 2017 alone, there were 10,454 charge sheets filed in the criminal courts, of which 5,857 were for traffic violations and 1,064 were for entry into Israel without a work permit; amounting to 66.26 percent of the

overall cases.52 In addition, there were 2,072 security offenses and 1,020 charge sheets for public disorder offenses; this in total accumulated to 29.69 percent of the total cases in 2017.

Consequently, most cases prosecuted before the Occupying Power’s mili-

tary courts in recent years are not relevant to the security of the Israeli forces or public order. Military courts have not only become a tool of oppression, but also a tool of economic exploitation of the Palestinian people. In 2016, the military courts collected 21,967,710 nis in fines, and in 2017, they collected 20,590,090 nis in fines. These fines were imposed in addition to other forms of punishment decided by the courts. Needless to say that these sums of money are not invested for the benefit of the protected Palestinian population in the occupied territory, but rather support the military judicial system and settle-

ment construction.53

2.1 The Use of Torture and Other Coercive Practices This section examines the coercive conditions that Israel, the Occupying Power imposes on Palestinian detainees, so that they are left with no other choice but to plead guilty and agree a plea deal to guarantee a shorter sentence. The plea bargain also shortens the length of the trial, that could last up to two years, and guarantees a shorter sentence. More than 90 percent of the cases prosecuted

before the military courts end in a plea bargain with the military prosecutors.54

Critically, Article 15 of the UN Convention Against Torture, which is binding

on Israel as a jus cogens norm, provides that a statement made as a result of torture is inadmissible. Despite this, the Israeli Supreme Court has approved the use of special interrogation methods in so- called “ticking bomb” sce-narios, including practices amounting to torture, inhumane and degrading



52 Ibid.

53 Amira Hass, ‘This is how the Palestinians Finance the Occupation: Millions in Fines in the

Military Courts’ Haaretz, 8 March 2013. Haas, an Israeli journalist writing for Haaretz, pub-

lished an article concerning the fines paid by Palestinian prisoners in the military court

system. She confirmed that she received an answer from cogat to her question: that the

money is deposited to the general account of the civil administration office and is used

for the sake of the area, including road structures, electricity, and other environmental

issues and development of infrastructures.

54 Lisa Hajjar claims it is over 97 percent. Lisa Hajjar, Courting Conflict – The Israeli Military

Court System in the West Bank and Gaza (University of California Press 2005) 219. Israel’s Military Justice System as an Annexationist Tool 171

treatment.55 In many cases, there is evidence of torture and mistreatment by Israeli authorities starting from the moment of arrest and continuing

throughout the interrogation stages, before the filing an indictment in court.56 Conditions imposed on the detainee during interrogation are inter alia severe physical and psychological torture including the detainees total isolation from the outside world, the prevention of the detainee from meeting with his law-

yer for weeks,57 as well as in many cases, the arrest of family members and

friends.58 All of these conditions aim to further psychologically pressure the detainee in order to force a confession.

2.2 Prosecuting Minors

The military commander of the occupied territory has made numerous amend-ments to the Order Regarding Security Provisions (Judea and Samaria), No 378 of 1970, and later to the Military Order 1651. An examination of all amendments to the military orders over the decades, is beyond the scope of this chapter. Notwithstanding, it must be noted that occasionally an amendment is adopted which supports the best interest of Palestinian detainees, usually as a result of continuous pressure from human rights organisations and international bod-ies, such as the increase in the age of majority.

Until 2011, Israeli military orders defined a Palestinian child as a person

under the age of 16. After this, following the adoption of Amendment 10, Article 136 of Military Order 1651 was changed to set the legal age of an adult to 18. However, in practice, the age of the detainee is decided on the filing date of the indictment, not the date of the committed offense. Therefore, the amend-ment to the legal age did not result in a fundamental change to the judicial procedures of prosecuting children. The military courts do not abide by the international principle that detention of children should only be the last resort



55 Much has been written and published about the hcj 94/ 5100, Public Committee Against

Torture in Israel v The State of Israel (6 September 1999) decision which laid the founda-

tion of the “necessity defense”. In subsequent rulings, the Supreme Court expanded the

necessity defence, in the case of Firas Tbeish for example. For more details, see Addameer,

Annual Violations Report, 2018. See also Chapter 11, Kiswanson and Chapter 12, Darcy.

56 Addameer prisoner support and human rights association issues annual reports on viola-

tions that Palestinian prisoners face under detention, that explains in detail all forms of

torture and ill treatment.
