Addameer, Annual Violations Report, 2019, page 24. 172 Francis

of any court- ordered punishment, instead the military courts adopt detention

as a first priority.59

2.3 Mass Prosecution of Minor Offenses Carrying out arrests strictly in accordance with the provisions of the law is a fundamental principle of a fair trial, and the guarantee against arbitrary detention is non- derogable, and therefore cannot be infringed upon without serious and legitimate reasons. In this vein, Article 42 of the Fourth Geneva Convention states that “the internment or placing in assigned residence of protected persons may be ordered only if the security of the Detaining Power makes it absolutely necessary”. Further, Article 78 of the Convention reiterates the absolute necessity of “imperative reasons of security” to justify detention. The International Criminal Tribunal for the Former Yugoslavia has interpreted

absolute necessity to include acts of the gravity of sabotage or espionage.60

As previously mentioned, the Israeli military courts prosecute Palestinians

not only for offenses like stone throwing or participation in demonstrations, but also for political affiliation, membership of student movements, and writ-ing or sharing posts on social media platforms under the pretext of incite-ment. Tens of thousands of Palestinian detainees incarcerated throughout the decades of Israel’s occupation have not committed serious offenses yet have been subjected to harsh punishments. For example, 16- year- old Palestinian girl, Ahed Al- Tamimi, was sentenced to eight months detention in a high secu-rity trial held in a closed military court for slapping an Israeli soldier during a

military raid on her family home.61

2.4 Presumption of Innocence The presumption of innocence, whereby an accused is presumed innocent until proven guilty, is a fundamental principle of criminal justice. This entails that the accused’s right to liberty is protected until all legal proceedings take

place and a verdict is reached.62 The accused remains free if acquitted, but if found guilty, is sentenced in accordance with the gravity of the offense. This principle is rarely upheld in Israeli military courts. In reality, there is a pre-sumption that persons accused of affiliation with or membership of any illegal organisation, including outlawed political parties and student groups, pose



59 Defence for Children International- Palestine, No Way to Treat a Child, 2016.

60 icrc, Customary International Humanitarian Law, Volume i: Rules (cup 2009) 304– 305.

61 Jaclynn Ashly, ‘Ahed Tamimi Gets Eight Months in Prison after Plea Deal’, Aljazeera, 22

March 2018.

62 Lawyers Watch Canada, Pre- Trial Release and the Right to be Presumed Innocent, 2013, 11. Israel’s Military Justice System as an Annexationist Tool 173

a real threat. The accused is generally interned until all legal procedures are finalised, regardless of the nature of the acts a person carries out through his

membership of the organisation.63 The burden of proof falls on the detainees, which have to show that they are no longer active members of the organisation.

2.5 Administrative Detention and Right to Information Administrative detention is a procedure whereby a person is detained without charge or a trial based on secret evidence. In the opt, the military commander

is authorised,64 to order the arrest and administrative detention of any per-son for a period of six months who is considered a risk to the security of the area or the State of Israel. Originally based on Article 111 of the British Defence

(Emergency) Regulations of 1945,65 an administrative order can be renewed based on the same allegations indefinitely. Although Article 78 of the Fourth Geneva Convention permits the use of administrative detention, this is limited to specific circumstances, “for imperative reasons of security”.

In practice, Israel carries out the arbitrary arrest and detention of thousands

of Palestinian detainees, based on secret files containing information that nei-ther detainee nor his lawyer is given access to, thus leaving them completely unaware of the nature of the allegations against him. Both the detainee and lawyer need to know the substance of the allegations in order to build a defence. Meanwhile, orders for administrative detention are routinely renewed on the same allegations no matter how much time passes. Often the administrative detention lasts more than a year, and in some cases, up to two years or more.

Notably, Article 9(2) of the International Covenant on Civil and Political

Rights, guarantees the right to be informed of the reasons for arrest or detention. Further, the UN Human Rights Committee outlines the right to be informed of the reasons for arrest, regarding the use of preventive

detention for reasons of public security.66 The UN Committee Against

63 Military Court of Appeals, 3249/ 06, Military Prosecutor v Basem Ahmad Musa Za’arur and

Others (2006). This ruling was on the arrest of members of the Palestinian Legislative

Council in June 2006. The Court of First Instance released them, deeming their activity to

pose no threat. However, the Military Court of Appeals ruled in favor of their detention

until the end of the criminal proceedings.

(December 2009). And inside Israel the Emergency Powers Law (Detention) (1979)

months.

174 Francis

Torture,67 has condemned Israel’s practice of administrative detention for indefinite periods of time. Such long detention based on secret information without clear legal procedures of defence, can amount to psychological tor-

ture.68 Despite international condemnation,69 Israel continues the practice targeting strategic sectors of the Palestinian population, including political leaders, students, social activists, human rights defenders, journalists, and

even children.70



3 Conclusion

Since 1967, Israel has used the military courts as a primary tool in oppress-

ing and controlling the Palestinian population in the opt.71 The longstanding criminalisation of all Palestinian political parties until this day, despite the Oslo Accords, as well the criminalisation of hundreds of civil organisations, Islamic charity organisations (Zakat committees), student movements, media outlets, and even sports clubs under the pretext of supporting terrorism is indicative of a broader policy of repression. By targeting the entirety of Palestinian society and criminalising any political or civic activity challenging the Israeli occu-

pying authorities,72 Israel maintains its effective control and indefinite legal jurisdiction over the Palestinian people, enabling its settlement and de facto

89(886) International Review of the Red Cross (2007).

annexation of Palestinian lands without resistance.73 As each amendment confers more and more power to the military commander while eroding the human and humanitarian rights of the Palestinian people, there is no doubt, that Israel’s military justice system is a cultivating tool designed to entrench its annexationist settler colonial regime.

chapter 8


Adaptation under Occupation: Climate Change

Vulnerability

Suha Jarrar †



Within the global general climate change framework, there is growing evi-dence and acknowledgement of the complex relationship between climate- related threats and armed conflict, often intersecting with socio- political and economic determinants. There is also increased realisation that armed conflict, including situations of occupation, contribute to, and may result in, substantial environmental harm, furthering the already prevalent marginali-sation of communities who are primarily dependent on natural wealth and resources, particularly indigenous communities in regions marred by impe-

rial and colonial powers.1 Simultaneously, the impact of climate change on the deterioration of natural resources, especially when it leads to the deteriora-tion of people’s livelihoods, has been examined as a major catalyst for conflict situations worldwide. Importantly, climate change is increasingly recognised

today as a concrete “threat multiplier” to international peace and security.2

Within the context of Israel’s prolonged occupation of Palestinian territory,

Israel has caused extensive environmental degradation, further deepening and exacerbating environmental vulnerabilities amongst Palestinians, in particular



1 “Indigenous peoples are among the first to face the direct consequences of climate change,

owing to their dependence upon, and close relationship with the environment and its

resources. Climate change exacerbates the difficulties already faced by vulnerable indig-

enous communities, including political and economic marginalization, loss of land and

resources, human rights violations, discrimination and unemployment”, United Nations

Permanent Forum on Indigenous Issues, Climate Change and Indigenous Peoples.

2 Numerous studies have emerged in recent years highlighting the relationship between

indigenous rights and environmental rights and the disproportionate impacts of environ-

mental damage and climate change on indigenous peoples and communities who are least

prepared and least able to adapt to the adverse impacts of climate change. See, ‘Climate

Change Recognized as “Threat Multiplier”, UN Security Council Debates its Impact on Peace’,

UN News, 25 January 2019. See also, ‘ICRC to UN Security Council: Double Impact of Climate

Change and Armed Conflict Harms People’s Ability to Cope’, icrc, 25 January 2019.



© Suha Jarrar, 2023 | DOI:10.1163/9789004503939_

Adaptation under Occupation: Climate Change Vulnerability 177

pastoral, herding, and other farming communities. This chapter will examine the applicability of community- based climate change adaptation measures in Masafer Yatta, in the South Hebron Hills region. It is one of the most cli-matically vulnerable areas of the Occupied Palestinian Territory, alongside the Jordan Valley and the Gaza Strip. Taking Masafer Yatta as a primary case study, this chapter will examine its adaptive capacities, the adverse impact of Israel’s occupation on Palestinian environment and resources, and the applicable law.

This chapter adopts an environmental justice approach,3 that draws atten-

tion to the linkages between climate change adaptation, the ramifications of prolonged occupation, and geopolitical injustices in the opt. This chapter will examine the applicability of international law, including international envi-ronmental law to the context in the South Hebron Hills and the Palestinian territory at large. It will conclude that Palestinians residing in climatically vul-nerable areas are precluded from applying basic community- based and small- scale climate change adaptation options and enjoying their inalienable right to exercise sovereignty over their natural resources.



1 Masafer Yatta – the South Hebron Hills4

Masafer Yatta is located in the southern hills of the Hebron Governorate, in the south of the occupied West Bank, and to the north of Al- Naqab Desert, located across the 1949 Armistice Agreement Line. The entire Masafer Yatta region is classified as Area C, which falls under Israel’s full civil and military control.

Masafer Yatta is home to approximately 1,700 Palestinians,5 living in small,

remote communities, most of whom are Palestinian Bedouin,6 who have tradi-tionally and historically, since before the Nakba in 1948, relied on pastoral and



3 Al- Haq, An Environmental Approach: The Protection of Natural Resources in the Occupied

Palestinian Territory, 2017.

and villages. See B’Tselem, The South Hebron Hills, 1 January 2013.

6 Al- Haq, Plight of Palestinian Bedouin Depicts Impacts of Illegal Israeli Occupation and Practices

in Palestinian Territory, 17 December 2014.

178 Jarrar

agricultural practices, namely livestock breeding and livestock production, as their main sources of livelihood and traditions of life.

To the east of Masafer Yatta is a large area of land covering 35,000 dunums,7

declared by the Israeli occupying authorities in 1970 as a closed military area

and active firing zone (the so- called “Firing Zone 918”).8 Consequently, the entirety of the Masafer Yatta region is either surrounded by Israeli settlers or by active Israeli military presence, putting the area and its residents’ lives at great risk. Its location also affects the Palestinian residents’ everyday livelihood and subsistence due to their inability to access vast areas of their lands and natural resources.

Since its declaration as a closed military area and active firing zone,

Palestinians have been systematically denied access to their land and the natu-ral resources therein. The firing zone encompasses lands on which 12 out of the 16 Palestinian communities of Masafer Yatta reside, comprising some 1,500 res-

idents.9 The Israeli occupying authorities have used the firing zone as a pretext,

to carry out systematic forcible transfer measures;10 the military commander has issued military orders designating the area a no- go zone requiring waves of

forced evictions of Palestinian residents.11 Most notably, in 1999, Israel expelled over 700 Palestinians who had resided in “Firing Zone 918” since before the Nakba in 1948.

Following the mass- displacement of Palestinians in the aftermath of the

1967 war, Palestinians in Masafer Yatta challenged military orders for their

expulsion from the firing zone before Israel’s High Court of Justice.12 On 4 May 2022, the hcj rejected their appeal against the eviction order holding that the Israeli military forces could forcibly transfer the residents and use the area for

its military training.13 The ruling has been condemned by UN experts who have

stated that their transfer is “all the more disconcerting, as it is done to allow Israeli military trainings in the area” and questioned how that can be given

priority.14 They have concluded that “Israel has shown no ‘imperative mili-tary necessity’ to vacate the area” and that therefore their displacement may

amount to a war crime.15



2 Climate Change Adaptation Planning in the Occupied Palestinian

Territory

The Convention on Climate Change aims to stabilise “greenhouse gas con-centrations in the atmosphere at a level that would prevent dangerous

anthropogenic interference with the climate system”.16 In 2016, following its

accession to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change,17 the Environment Quality Authority of the State of Palestine released its

National Adaptation Plan to Climate Change.18 Since then, the Convention on Climate Change has become binding on the State of Palestine in the opt. Importantly, Israel has extraterritorial duties and obligations towards the opt,

since its ratification of the Convention on Climate Change on 4 June 1996.19

The process of developing country- driven National Adaptation Plans was

established under the Cancun Adaptation Framework in 2010.20 The process is intended to enable States to identify climate change adaptation needs and develop adaptation strategies in accordance with those needs. In its National Adaptation Plan, the State of Palestine presented a vulnerability assessment for the opt, building on the guidelines provided by the Convention on Climate Change for least developed countries, as well as the Climate Change Adaptation Strategy and Program of Action for the Palestinian Government developed in

2011.21 The National Adaptation Plan identifies sectors highly vulnerable to



14 Ibid.

15 Ibid.

16 UN Framework Convention on Climate Change, Article 2.

17 UN Framework Convention on Climate Change, 21 March 1994.

18 State of Palestine, EQA, National Adaptation Plan (NAP) to Climate Change, 2016, page 15.

19 Marc Limon, ‘Human Rights Obligations and Accountability in the Face of Climate

Change’, Georgia Journal of International and Comparative Law (2010) 559.

20 UN Climate Change, Report of the Conference of the Parties on its Sixteenth Session, held in

Cancun from 29 November to 10 December 2010, fccc/ cp/ 2010/ 7/ Add.1, 15 March 2011.

21 undp- papp, Climate Change Adaptation Strategy and Programme of Action for the

Palestinian Authority, 9 June 2011.

180 Jarrar

climate change and proposes adaptation options for each sector in the West Bank and the Gaza Strip.

According to the analysis presented by the National Adaptation Plan, much

uncertainty surrounds projected impacts of climate change.22 That said, the National Adaptation Plan for the State of Palestine concludes that “[i] n gen-eral, the contention is that the overall water situation will deteriorate, with more potential for drought and floods, increased evaporation, [and] reduced

river flow”.23 It further concludes that, despite the uncertainties surrounding future climate change projections, there is “unanimity that temperatures will

increase, although there is some disagreement by how much”.24 In addition, Palestine’s National Adaptation Plan notes that, “most analyses suggest future decreases in rainfall, although the amount of decreases is somewhat uncer-

tain”.25 In light of such uncertainties, a precautionary approach,26 must be adopted, by prioritising the need to increase the adaptive capacity in partic-ularly vulnerable areas in the opt, even if the extent of the harm of long- term climate trends remains unclear. The National Adaptation Plan ranks an array of sectors as “highly vulnerable” to climate change across the opt, including, inter alia, agriculture, coastal and marine, energy, health, food, and water.

Sensitivity to climate change is relevant to the degree to which a region or

in this case, a community, is affected by climate- related stimuli such as climate variability and frequency and intensity of extreme weather events and disas-ters. Adaptive capacity is the degree to which a community has the capacity to cope with or adjust to the consequences of climate change. Vulnerability is therefore portrayed as the degree to which a community is unable to cope with or is susceptible to the consequences of climate change. An analysis of the interrelations between vulnerability, sensitivity, and adaptive capacity is key to conducting a comprehensive vulnerability assessment, a tool used to

22 Mitigation of climate change refers to human interventions to reduce greenhouse gas

emissions. In climate policy, mitigation measures refer to a variety of practices that

contribute to the mitigation process. The first UN Framework Convention on Climate

Change, mitigation objective was introduced under the Article 2 of the Convention was

implemented under the 1997 Kyoto Protocol, which established commitments to miti-

gate global warming by reducing greenhouse concentrations in the atmosphere through

reducing anthropogenic (human- made) greenhouse gases.

23 State of Palestine, EQA, National Adaptation Plan (NAP) to Climate Change, 2016, page 15.

24 Ibid.

25 Ibid.

26 In the context of climate change, a precautionary approach refers to considering the pre-

cautionary principle in decision making. The precautionary principle implies the need

to take precautionary actions to prevent adverse, and possibly catastrophic impacts of

climate change, even in the presence of scientific uncertainty.

Adaptation under Occupation: Climate Change Vulnerability 181

identify and prioritize climate change vulnerabilities and adaptation options for a system or a region.

2.1 The Dual Strike

During interviews conducted by the author in Masafer Yatta and surround-ing villages in the South Hebron Hills, Palestinian communities historically dependent on pastoralism and other agricultural activity recalled experienc-ing recent signs of climatic changes, including increases in annual tempera-tures, rainfall fluctuation, more frequent extreme weathers and temperatures, and notably intensified summer droughts leading to the observation of signs

of desertification.27 Combined with discriminatory Israeli measures imposed on Palestinians in the area, such changes adversely impact pastoral and other herding communities and agricultural activities in most parts of the South

Hebron hills, particularly in Masafer Yatta.28

According to community members, including heads of village councils

and agricultural communities in the area, Masafer Yatta, was once the entire region’s breadbasket. Most of the dairy products and seasonal crops were gen-erated, produced, and sold in the market – especially prior to the late 1970s. Today, the environmental crisis in the South Hebron Hills, attributable primar-ily to Israel’s prolonged occupation, has dramatically changed the demography and agricultural nature of the area, which once defined economic and social activity in the area. The greatest challenge facing communities’ ability to imple-ment climate change adaptation options and to cope with climate change are at large caused by Israel’s discriminatory measures, such as the targeting and exploitation of natural resources that are highly vulnerable to climate change. This is evidenced by Israel’s control over water and land resources, and Israeli- imposed restrictions on Palestinians’ access to cultivable lands and grazing areas, destruction of property, including homes, animal sheds, and water infra-

structure, as well as the confiscation of land and water infrastructure.29

Notably, Article 49 of the Fourth Geneva Convention prohibits “[i] ndividual

or mass forcible transfers, as well as deportations of protected persons from

27 Drought and desertification are related phenomena; drought can be a natural phenome-

non exacerbated by human activity such as exploitation of water resources, land degra-

dation, and negative agricultural practices. In extreme cases, drought can be one of the

factors contributing to the desertification of land, turning land that was once fertile into

a desert- like land. While most individuals interviewed throughout the research process

mentioned observing such changes, all of them attributed their magnified impacts to the

Israeli occupying authorities’ practices in the region.

28 Field information obtained by Al- Haq, 2018.

29 See also B’Tselem, The South Hebron Hills, 1 January 2013. 182 Jarrar

occupied territory to the territory of the Occupying Power or to that of any

other country, occupied or not […] regardless of their motive”.30 At the same time, international human rights law prohibits arbitrary displacement, includ-ing displacement “based on policies of apartheid, ‘ethnic cleansing’ or similar practices aimed at/ or resulting in altering the ethnic, religious or racial compo-

sition of the affected population”.31

2.2 Climate Change and Geopolitical Vulnerabilities Across the opt, a number of climate change trends are expected to intensify and exacerbate in the coming years. These include uneven rainfall distribution, a decrease in available natural grazing areas and vegetation cover, increased trends of droughts, and increased intensity and frequency of extreme weather

events and temperatures.32 Palestine’s National Adaptation Plan takes into account vulnerabilities resulting from existing political inequalities caused by Israel’s prolonged occupation and the occupying authorities’ policies and practices. Two sectors identified as highly vulnerable to climate change, are the

water and agriculture sectors, including livestock production.33 Israel’s policies and practices have thus contributed to the creation of a climate- vulnerable population.

Considering the interrelations between climate change sensitivity and

adaptive capacity, it can be concluded that restrictions on climate change adaptation in the opt are not only limited to restricting the implementation of adaptation options, but also involve practices contributing to decreased resil-ience and adaptive capacity amongst the protected Palestinian population, thereby increasing their sensitivity and vulnerability to climate change.

For indigenous peoples around the world, resilience to climate change is

rooted in traditional knowledge.34 Indigenous communities’ capacity to adapt

Adaptation under Occupation: Climate Change Vulnerability 183

to environmental change is based on a deep understanding of the land and tra-ditional agricultural practices. As climate change increasingly impacts indig-enous communities, responses seek to increase their resilience, reduce their vulnerability, and enable them to cope with inevitable existing and predicted changes. In a statement to the Conference of the Parties to the Convention on Climate Change, the International Indigenous Peoples Forum on Climate Change affirmed the importance of recognising traditional knowledge and practices as integral components of climate change adaptation and mitiga-

tion efforts.35 Indigenous Palestinians, residing in the South Hebron Hills, have extensive knowledge of the territory and its suitable agricultural and live-stock production, which has constituted their main source of livelihood for generations.

Women have traditionally been the providers as livestock caregivers in

Masafer Yatta, and have taken up the role of providing food for their fami-

lies, the staple traditional food including meat and dairy products.36 As such, women carry most of the traditional indigenous knowledge affiliated with livestock production, and are therefore the key observers of the diminishing production rate and its consequences, compounded by the Israeli occupation and climate change effects.

2.3 Gender- Differentiated Vulnerability to Climate Change In adopting a gender- differentiated analysis on climate change vulnerability in the opt, it is important to note that gender analysis is not merely about

women.37 Rather it includes analysis of the designated roles and responsibil-ities, the intersectionality of relations between women and men, and other social determinants, including class and location. Vulnerability, therefore, is shaped by social determinants and hence varies across social positions and locations.



35 In a recent statement to the Conference of the Parties to the UN Framework Convention

on Climate Change, the International Indigenous Peoples’ Forum on Climate Change reit-

erated “the need for recognition of our traditional knowledge, which we have sustainably

used and practiced for generations; and the need to integrate such knowledge in global,

national and sub- national efforts. This knowledge is our vital contribution to climate

change adaptation and mitigation”.

36 Field information obtained by Al- Haq, 2018.

37 Gender analysis is the analysis of the different roles women and men play in order to

understand the relationship between the resources to which women have access, their

assigned roles within their communities, and accordingly, their specific needs as a socie-

tal group. See Food and Agricultural Organization of the UN, Invisible Guardians: Women

Manage Livestock Diversity, 2012.

184 Jarrar

In every household visited, the author found that women were responsible

for the majority of the tasks associated with livestock production, including all areas of animal husbandry, from day- to- day care, to the raising of livestock and the production of dairy products for both commercial and domestic use. Accordingly, Palestinian women play a key role in managing livestock produc-tion, and therefore in attaining and sustaining food security.

Due to its dire effect on the water and agriculture sectors, climate change

has, and will continue to have, a direct and ominous impact on all aspects of food security, including food availability, accessibility, and stability. Within the threat of food insecurity as a result of the dual strike of climate change and Israel’s prolonged occupation of the opt, the risk facing Palestinian women’s livelihoods is magnified, rendering them overburdened and more vulnera-ble to climate change. This vulnerability is largely due to the risks resulting from unavailability of traditional food sources and sources of income, as well as water resources for domestic use in the context of both politically and

environmentally- induced scarcity.38



3 Frustration of Adaptability Actions

Israel’s prolonged occupation has hindered Palestinians’ ability to develop and implement the adequate adaptation strategies and tools necessary to positively cope with climate change. Palestine’s National Adaptation Plan has identified adaptation options necessary for coping with the risks projected for the agri-

culture sector.39 To overcome vulnerabilities related to irrigation, crop produc-tion, and loss of vegetation cover, access to and self- determination over water and land resources are key. Similarly, reducing vulnerabilities associated with livestock production and soil erosion requires access to rangeland and water resources to sustain livestock production, as well as control over building and construction in order to implement options needed to prevent soil erosion. However Palestinian communities are systematically denied these adaptation options as a result of Israeli administrative restrictions.

This section will provide examples of the ways in which Israel thwarts

Palestinian adaptability. It will focus on the adverse measures of discrimina-tory water allocation, loss of vegetation cover, restrictions on construction, and settler violence.

38 undp, Impact Gender: Food and Nutritional Security, Climate Change Adaptation. See also

UN Climate Change, Introduction to Gender and Climate Change.

39 State of Palestine, EQA, National Adaptation Plan (NAP) to Climate Change, 2016, page 15. Adaptation under Occupation: Climate Change Vulnerability 185

3.1 Discriminatory Water Allocation In order to sustain its illegal settlement enterprise in the West Bank, includ-ing the settlements’ agricultural activity, Israel has systematically controlled Palestinian water supply, limiting access to water for Palestinians. Today, nearly 113,000 Palestinians in the West Bank, some 50,000 of whom reside in Area C, are not connected to the water network, while approximately 600,000

Israeli settlers,40 illegally residing in the West Bank, are fully connected to water networks. The colonial settlers consume somewhere between three to

eight times,41 more water than the entire Palestinian population of the West

Bank, who make up some 2.9 million Palestinians.42

Discriminatory water allocation and supply to Palestinian communities

in Masafer Yatta and throughout Area C of the West Bank by Israel’s national water company, Mekorot, has resulted in insufficient amounts of water avail-able to Palestinians, inadequate quality, and physically inaccessible and unaf-fordable water, in violation of Palestinians’ right to an adequate standard of

living, including adequate water and sanitation.43 In Masafer Yatta, Israeli set-tlers are exclusively connected to the water network through pipelines laid out by Mekorot, often passing near or underneath Palestinian communities to sup-

ply water for illegal settlements and outposts.44 Meanwhile, most Palestinian villages in Masafer Yatta are not connected to the water grid. Palestinians are therefore forced to purchase water tankers at elevated prices. Water prices vary depending on the transportation distance from the water source to the com-munity. The more remote and less accessible the community is, the higher the price of water becomes.

Due to Israel’s systematic denial of access to water, most Palestinians in the

South Hebron Hills, including in Masafer Yatta, reported an inability to sus-tain livestock flocks that they had inherited and raised for generations because



40 Updated statistics obtained by Al- Haq in preparation of a revised edition of Al- Haq,

Water for One People Only: Discriminatory Access and ‘Water Apartheid’ in the Occupied

Palestinian Territory, 2013.

41 The water consumption in the opt rates vary depending on location. Information col-

lected by Al- Haq in 2018 indicates that the average water consumption by Palestinians in

2018 is estimated between 73 and 83 litres per capita per day (lpcd).

of the insufficient water supply to meet their basic domestic consumption

needs.45 Communities are therefore no longer able to afford to provide drink-ing water for their flocks, particularly during the summer months, forcing

them to abandon their traditions of life and undermining their livelihoods.46

3.2 Loss of Vegetation Cover

While climate change poses a threat to livestock production,47 it has not yet prompted the disappearance of the majority of fertile grazing areas in the South Hebron Hills area, including Masafer Yatta. Notwithstanding, due to restrictions placed by settlers and the idf on Palestinians’ access to available grazing lands, Palestinians have lost most of the vegetation cover in the limited areas of land they are able to access. While the decrease in precipitation has partly contributed to the decline in vegetation cover, the main source of stress causing the degradation of grazing lands remains political and is a direct result

of the occupation.48

Palestinians in Masafer Yatta identified the inability to access grazing areas

as the primary obstacle to adapting to changes in the vegetation cover of acces-sible lands, attributable to the overgrazing of the limited area of rangelands available to Palestinians. Restrictions on access are largely due to Israel’s dec-larations of various areas of the land as so- called nature reserves, closed mil-itary areas, and firing zones. Palestinians in the area have stated that military trainings are more frequent during the spring and productive grazing seasons. During Israeli military trainings, vast areas of agricultural lands are closed off entirely to Palestinian access and use, while nearby areas that are not closed off become extremely dangerous for Palestinians to reach, due to the possi-

bility of being injured by live bullets or grenades used during the trainings.49 Notably, restrictions associated with military trainings are not only limited to military training periods. The explosive remnants left behind by Israeli forces



45 Al- Haq Affidavit No 2013/ 8785, given by Khaled Hamad Nawaj’a, 59, resident of Sysiya

(2013).

46 Al- Haq Affidavit No 7081/ 2012, given by Jamil Ahmad Awad, 55, resident of Sadat Al- Tha’la

(2012).

47 Climate change is a global threat to livestock production as it impacts water availability,

biodiversity, animal reproduction, heat stress, and the transmission of livestock diseases.

For the opt, livestock production is identified as one of the most vulnerable sections to

the impacts of climate changes, coupled with the impacts of the Israeli occupation.

48 See also, Conflict and Environment Observatory, Country Brief: Occupied Palestinian

Territories, 26 March 2018.

49 Al- Haq interview in Al- Twaneh village in the South Hebron Hills, 2018. (Name redacted). Adaptation under Occupation: Climate Change Vulnerability 187

on Palestinian lands has made it generally dangerous for Palestinians to even

attempt to reach the grazing areas.50

The degradation of grazing lands available to Palestinians is partly due

to stress induced by the overgrazing of small areas of land available for

Palestinians.51 Palestinians are left with no choice but to engage in negative coping actions, such as overgrazing. When the grazing area is too limited in a region where the primary source of income is livestock production, the land is not given sufficient periods of time to recover. Overgrazing degrades the land, exhausts its vegetation cover, and reduces green cover biomass and grazing capacity, further enhancing desertification, and making the land more liable to erosion. The growth of vegetation cover that is native to the region is key to

avoiding soil runoff and to building soil stability.52

The exhaustion of the vegetation cover has also caused a sharp increase

in the prices of fodder and other types of animal feed, which has rendered Palestinian communities unable to sustainably maintain their livestock flocks, forcing many communities to abandon their traditions of life, which would otherwise have ensured their long- term self- sufficiency, food security, and community- managed adaptation to climate change.

The natural community- led response to the decrease in available rangelands

should be to expand the grazing areas and diversify grazing spatial patterns to avoid over- exhaustion of the vegetation cover in one region, along with cre-ating a balance between the grazing practice and fodder- feeding that would

ensure environmental and economic sustainability.53 Yet, for Palestinians in the South Hebron Hills, community- based responses, practiced formerly for generations, are not achievable due to Israeli- imposed restrictions on access to grazing areas resulting from the declaration of lands as military and firing zones, settler violence and harassment against Palestinian herders.

Overgrazing is a form of negative coping practice to climatic threat, which

coupled with politically induced environmental degradation, will reduce resil-ience, while fostering and increasing vulnerability. Such methods are referred

flock in Jinba, in southern Masafer Yatta, who were injured following the explosion of

188 Jarrar

to as ‘maladaptive actions’ or ‘mal- adaptation’.54 In the context of the opt, mal- adaptation may be caused by the implementation of large- scale projects that result in more environmental harm than benefit, further increasing the occupied Palestinian populations vulnerability to climate change. In this case, mal- adaptation could refer to practices that Palestinians are forced to adopt due to restrictions arising from the Israeli occupation, lack of political will to change the status quo, and the absence of genuine choice.

For example, the systematic and deliberate obstruction of the develop-

ment of livestock production in the South Hebron Hills has long been driven by Israeli State policy. A major obstacle in the development of livestock pro-duction, as repeatedly raised by the Masafer Yatta community, is Israel’s Plant

Protection (Damage by Goats) Law of 1950.55 This legislation, prohibited the breeding of black goats which are well adapted to the Mediterranean climate, on grounds that their feeding habits are allegedly harmful to Mediterranean lands. Although the law was introduced in the 1950s, Israeli authorities began to strictly enforce it in Masafer Yatta in the late 1970s, coinciding with the dec-

laration of vast areas of Palestinian land as “Firing Zone 918”.56 Palestinians interviewed have observed that the law has been disproportionately enforced against Palestinian herders, while Israeli settlers often get away with keeping and breeding their black goats, suggesting that the protection of Mediterranean lands is only a pretext.

In 2017, the Israeli Parliament adopted a bill proposed by a Palestinian

Member, Jamal Zahalka, to repeal the aforementioned piece of legislation. In his proposal to the Knesset, Zahalka argued:

The Plant Protection Act of 1950 … claims that the breeding of goats

should be restricted because they are harmful to Mediterranean lands.

We believe that the law is a racist law that was legislated for political

reasons and aims to eliminate one of the main sources of livelihood for



54 Maladaptive actions, also known as maladaptation, refer to actions that may cause unin-

tended increase in climate risks and vulnerability to climate change. In the case of the

opt, Palestinians’ engagement in maladaptive actions are largely caused by restrictions

imposed by the Israeli occupation. See also Inter- governmental Panel on Climate Change,

a large section of the Bedouin Arabs [of the Palestinian people] in the

Naqab.57

The second and third reading of the proposed bill, were finally approved by the General Assembly of the Knesset on 8 May 2018, not in response to Zahalka’s concerns, but on the grounds that new evidence reveals the black goats’ graz-

ing of undergrowth may help in preventing wild fires.58

3.3 Restrictions on Construction and the Constant Threat of Demolitions

As the Occupying Power, Israel bears responsibilities for the rights and well-

being of the protected Palestinian population.59 Contrary to its legal obliga-tions, Israel continues its effort to drive Palestinians from their most fertile lands. In 2013, an Independent UN Fact- finding Mission on the Implications of Israeli Settlements concluded that “[t] he denial of water is used to trigger displacement, particularly in areas slated for settlement expansion, especially since these [Palestinian] communities are mostly farmers and herders who

depend on water for their livelihoods”.60

In Masafer Yatta, as is the case in the rest of Area C, the Israeli Civil

Administration has long engaged in the practice of denying Palestinians per-mits for the construction or rehabilitation of any type of structures, including

water infrastructure, animal sheds, public clay ovens (taboun),61 and road con-struction. Consequently, any structure built by Palestinians without a permit

from the Israeli occupying authorities, may be at risk of demolition.62

In Masafer Yatta, soil erosion could be prevented by the construction of sim-

ple stone terraces (also known as retaining walls), an agricultural method prac-ticed by the Palestinian people on their ancestral lands for generations. Stone terraces are also used as a form of small- scale water harvesting tool to be used



57 ‘Final ratification: Abolition of the Law Restricting the Herding of Black Goat Herds,’

[Arabic] Knesset, 8 May 2018. See also, Majd Kayya, ‘The Day We Were Eaten Like Black

58 ‘Black Goat’ to Thrive Again in Israel’, Arutz Sheva, 23 November 2017.

for rain- fed agriculture, on which the Palestinian people have long relied on as a source of livelihood and a means of subsistence. Water harvesting is one of the oldest and most used adaptation tools by Palestinians to combat water scarcity. However, according to residents interviewed in Masafer Yatta, “[e] ven the stone terraces have been demolished […] Only stone terraces constructed before 1948 are allowed to stay and they need to be reconstructed […] But if we

try to renovate them, they would immediately be demolished”.63

Restrictions on building obstructs adaptation to extreme weather events in

relation to livestock production, including the implementation of small- scale adaptation measures to heat waves and extremely cold weathers. Palestinian construction is necessary to strengthen the structure of animal sheds, includ-ing adding material to shield from hail, rainfall, winds and sun exposure. Like all other structures, animal sheds are at risk of demolition and stop- work orders, and remain under constant threat of destruction by the Israeli occupy-

ing authorities in all areas of the South Hebron hills and Masafer Yatta.64

3.4 Settler Violence

For Israeli settlers residing in illegal Israeli settlements surrounding the South Hebron Hills, larger areas of grazing land with sufficient vegetation cover are available for small- scale Israeli farmers who, with the protection of the Israeli armed forces, have been able to illegally settle on the land for years. As such, Israeli settlers are able to enjoy access to lands that have been sustainably grazed by the indigenous Palestinian population for generations.

Coercion created by the Israeli occupation is also exacerbated by the vio-

lence and harassment to which Palestinian farmers and herders are exposed to at the hands of Israeli settlers. One resident has described how three masked settlers attacked him as he was herding sheep, and beat him on his head with a

rock.65 Another, farmer was thrown to the ground by a settler from Mitzpe Yair, who had stopped his car to throw rocks at sheep, while a female settler filmed

the incident from their car.66 These acts of intimidation and physical violence

are carried out with impunity.67

Adaptation under Occupation: Climate Change Vulnerability 191

4 Protection of Palestinian Resources and Environment in

International Law

Natural resources and the environment are protected under international law. Article 55 of the Hague Regulations provides that the Occupying Power:

[S] hall be regarded only as administrator and usufructuary of public

buildings, real estate, forests, and agricultural estates belonging to the

hostile State, and situated in the occupied country. It must safeguard

the capital of these properties, and administer them in accordance with

the rules of usufruct.68

According to the rules of usufruct, Israel, may only use the natural resources within the opt to the extent that it does not exploit these resources in a way

that would undermine their capital, or lead to their depletion.69 In addition, the destruction of “real or personal property belonging individually or col-

lectively to private persons, or to the State, or to other public authorities”,70 including natural resources, further violates Articles 53 of the Fourth Geneva Convention, which explicitly prohibits such destruction, unless rendered abso-lutely necessary for military operations. Such destruction amounts to a war

crime and grave breach of the Fourth Geneva Convention under Article 147.71

The occupied population’s public property, including its natural resources,

must not be administered or utilised for the sole benefit and economic gain of the Occupying Power’s own population, and must be used for the benefit of the occupied population. Acts exceeding the rules of usufruct may constitute

the crime of pillage,72 under the Rome Statute of the International Criminal

Court.73 See further Chapter 13, Power, on the crime of pillage.

The protection of natural resources and the natural environment in occu-

pied territory are provided for under Articles 35(3) and 55 of Additional



68 Hague Regulations, Article 55.

69 For more detailed analysis, refer to Al- Haq, Annexing Energy – Exploiting and Preventing

the Development of Oil and Gas in the Occupied Palestinian Territory, 5 December 2015,

pages 94– 95.

70 Fourth Geneva Convention, Article 53.

71 Rome Statute, Article 8(2)(a)(iv).

72 Fourth Geneva Convention, Article 33; Hague Regulations, Articles 28, 47. Pillage, as a

prohibited act, is also reflected in customary international humanitarian law. See icrc,

Customary International Humanitarian Law, Volume i: Rules (cup 2010) Rule 49.

73 Rome Statute, Article 8(2)(b)(xvi).

192 Jarrar

Protocol i to the Geneva Conventions of 1977.74 Article 35(3) prohibits the Occupying Power, and other parties to conflict, from “employ[ing] methods or means of warfare which are intended, or may be expected, to cause wide-spread, long- term and severe damage to the natural environment”.

Additionally, the right to self- determination of the Palestinian people

encompasses the right to exercise permanent sovereignty over natural wealth and resources, a peremptory norm of international law giving rise to erga omnes obligations on all States. This right entitles the Palestinian people to “freely dispose of their natural wealth and resources”, including land and water,

and in no case may they be deprived of their own means of subsistence.75 The right of the Palestinian people to self- determination, including to perma-

nent sovereignty over natural wealth and resources,76 is widely recognized by the international community and has been reaffirmed in countless UN

General Assembly resolutions,77 UN Security Council resolutions,78 and by the

International Court of Justice.79

Israel’s continued deprivation of the right of the Palestinian people to

self- determination, through restrictions on Palestinians’ access to and use of their own land and natural resources amounts to a violation of Israel’s obliga-tions under international human rights law to respect, protect, and fulfil the rights of all Palestinians subject to their effective control. In addition, Israel has obligations to ensure Palestinians’ rights to adequate food, adequate hous-ing, water, the highest attainable standard of physical and mental health, and an adequate standard of living, amongst numerous other fundamental rights owed to the Palestinian people and necessary for their full development.

Finally, the UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples provides

explicit recognition of indigenous peoples’ right “to the lands, territories and resources which they have traditionally owned, occupied or otherwise used



74 The 1977 Additional Protocol i to the Geneva Conventions of 1949, which Israel, the

Occupying Power, has not ratified, is applicable to the opt, to the extent that it reflects

customary international humanitarian law.

75 iccpr, Article 1(2).

76 The right to permanent sovereignty over natural wealth and resources is constitutive

of customary international law. See icj, Armed Activities on the Territory of the Congo

(Democratic Republic of the Congo v Uganda) (Judgment) icj Reports 168 (2005), para 244.

77 See for example unga Resolution 25 (24 March 2014) and unga Resolution 58 (22

December 2003).

78 unsc Resolution 242 (22 November 1967).

79 icj, Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory

(Advisory Opinion) icj Reports 136 (2004) para 122.

Adaptation under Occupation: Climate Change Vulnerability 193

or acquired”,80 and enshrines the right of indigenous peoples, including the Palestinian people, “to own, use, develop and control the lands, territories and resources that they possess by reason of traditional ownership or other tradi-

tional occupation or use, as well as those which they have otherwise acquired”.81 The Declaration further provides that “[i] ndigenous peoples have the right to the conservation and protection of the environment and the productive capac-

ity of their lands or territories and resources”.82 While the declaration generally applies to indigenous persons in already colonised territory, the indigenous people of Palestine (that is, the entirety of the Palestinian people) represent a sui generis in that the colonisation is still active and ongoing. The examples provided in this chapter illustrate how Israel has systematically deprived the indigenous Palestinian people of their means of subsistence, including the right to protection of the environment and to adapt to climate change.

4.1 International Environmental Law: The Paris Agreement The protection of environment during occupation is enshrined in the Rio Declaration on Environment and Development. Although non- binding, it affirms in Principle 24 that:

[W] arfare is inherently destructive of sustainable development. States

shall therefore respect international law providing protection for the

environment in times of armed conflict and cooperate in its further

development, as necessary.83

A recent legal framework under which rights to climate change adapta-

tion are outlined is the Paris Agreement,84 reached in December 2015 at the 21st Conference of the Parties, referred to as cop 21, to the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change, to which both Palestine and Israel are States signatories. The Paris Agreement entered into force on 4 November 2016 and is universally legally binding to all signatory parties, with no reservations permit-

ted.85 Although the Paris Agreement makes no specific references to situations



80 Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples, adopted by through unga 61/ 295 (13

September 2007), Article 26(1).

81 Ibid., Article 26(2).

82 Ibid., Article 29(1).

83 Rio Declaration on Environment and Development, 13 June 1996, Principle 24.

84 COP21, Paris Agreement under the United Nations Framework on Climate Change, 4

November 2016.

85 Ibid., Article 27.

194 Jarrar

of armed conflict or belligerent occupation, it does include multiple references to States Parties’ responsibilities to reduce climate change vulnerability and strengthen resilience to the impacts of climate change in climatically vulner-

able areas,86 and as such, clearly applies to situations of armed conflict and occupation, including the opt.

In particular, Article 2(b) of the Paris Agreement calls for the enhancement

and implementation of the Convention on Climate Change, including the objective to strengthen global responses to climate change threats by “increas-ing the ability to adapt to the adverse impacts of climate change and to foster climate resilience and low greenhouse gas emissions development, in a man-

ner that does not threaten food production”.87 Article 7 requires, that States Parties’ establish a global goal aiming at reducing climate vulnerabilities of climatically vulnerable communities, as well as the necessity to consider indig-enous and traditional knowledge in efforts related to climate change adapta-

tion.88 The Agreement outlines that:

Parties should, when taking action to address climate change, respect,

promote and consider their respective obligations on human rights, the

right to health, the rights of indigenous peoples, local communities,

migrants, children, persons with disabilities and people in vulnerable sit-

uations and the right to development.

Additionally, the Paris Agreement contains several provisions that make ref-erence to climate vulnerabilities. These references can be interpreted to cover situations where persons are vulnerable because the effect of climate change may have a magnifying impact on other factors that could lead to climate- related migration, or in the context of Palestine, the indirect displacement of protected persons. References to vulnerability in the Paris Agreement include Article 7(1), which provides:

Parties hereby establish the global goal on adaptation of enhancing

adaptive capacity, strengthening resilience and reducing vulnerability to

climate change, with a view to contributing to sustainable development

and ensuring an adequate adaptation response.89



86 Ibid., Articles 2, 7, 9, 8, 10 and 11.

87 Ibid., Article 2(b).

88 Ibid., Article 7.

89 Ibid., Article 7(1).

Adaptation under Occupation: Climate Change Vulnerability 195

In addition, Article 7(9) of the Agreement stipulates that:

Each Party shall, as appropriate, engage in adaptation planning pro-

cesses and the implementation of actions, including the development

or enhancement of relevant plans, policies and/ or contributions, which

may include [...] (c) [t] he assessment of climate change impacts and vul-

nerability, with a view to formulating nationally determined prioritized

actions, taking into account vulnerable people, places and ecosystems.90

It is worth noting that amongst commentators there is wide scepticism around the effectiveness of international environmental law due to the absence of adequate enforcement mechanisms relevant to State Parties’ obligations, par-

ticularly obligations relevant to the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions.91 Consequently, international environmental law may seem inherently weak in general, but the Paris Agreement in particular is perceived to have “carried

this weakness further”,92 as it lacks compliance mechanisms.93 Nevertheless, the Paris agreement re- emphasises obligations that are binding under existing international law.



5 Conclusion

Communities in the opt, particularly in Area C of the West Bank, experience the magnified impacts of climate change due to Israel’s discriminatory policies and practices. While adapting to climate change, Palestinians living in envi-ronmentally and politically vulnerable areas, simultaneously endure coercive environments created by Israel to drive Palestinian displacement and forcible transfer. Future climate change projections for the opt entail an increase in annual temperatures and a decline in rainfall. Consequently, the urgent need for proper climate change adaptation will continue to rise.

Israel’s practices of displacement and dispossession of the occupied

Palestinian population is demonstrated by its practice of systematically target-ing the resources necessary to maintain the livelihood of Palestinians in gen-eral, and herding and farming communities, in particular. Exploitation of and



90 Ibid., Article 7(9).

91 See UN Environment Programme, Environmental Rule of Law: First Global Report, 2019.

92 See Richard Falk, ‘Voluntary International Law and the Paris Agreement’, Wordpress, 16

January 2016.

93 Ibid.

196 Jarrar

control over natural resources has been central to build, sustain and develop Israel’s illegal settlement enterprise.

Water and agriculture are two of the sectors with high vulnerability to cli-

mate change, and adaptation options associated with them require access to and the exercise of rights of self- determination. Israel’s prolonged occupation of the Palestinian territory therefore constitutes the major cause for the exac-erbation of climate change impacts on the opt, as well as the deterioration of the standard of living for Palestinian communities relying on land and natural resources for their subsistence. As such, adaptation to climate change will not be possible for Palestinians, without the genuine realisation of the collective right to self- determination and permanent sovereignty over natural resources.

As previously established, natural resources and the environment are pro-

tected under international law, including international humanitarian law, inter-national human rights law, and international environmental law. Accordingly, Israel, as Occupying Power, must uphold its responsibility by refraining from causing environmental damage to the environment and to natural resources. Further, Israel must act in accordance with its responsibilities under Article 7, Paris Agreement on Climate Change, which affirms States Parties’ responsibili-ties to reduce climate vulnerabilities of vulnerable populations, and to respect, promote and consider their human rights obligations when taking action to address climate change.

Finally, Third States must respect and ensure respect for the Geneva

Conventions, including negative obligations on Third States to “neither encour-age, nor aid or assist in violations of the Conventions by Parties to a conflict” and positive obligations, to do “everything reasonably in their power to prevent

and bring such violations to an end”.94

Israel’s Impunity from Peremptory Norms

John Dugard



The year 2001 was a momentous year for Palestine. It saw the intensification of the Second Intifada that had been ignited by Ariel Sharon’s visit to the Haram al- Sharif on 28 September 2000. It was also a momentous year for international law, as that year saw the adoption of the International Law Commission’s Draft Articles on Responsibility of States for Internationally Wrongful Acts, which marked the endorsement of the notion that international law distinguishes between ordinary breaches of international law and serious breaches of inter-national law that violate peremptory norms of general international law, also

known as norms of jus cogens.1 Of course, the notion of a set of higher norms or peremptory norms was not new. There was support for such norms in judicial decisions such as the International Court of Justice in the Barcelona

Traction case,2 and in the Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties.3 But it was the 2001 Draft Articles on States Responsibility by the International Law Commission that drew a sharp normative distinction between “ordinary” breaches and “serious” breaches of international law that affected the interna-tional community as a whole, and imposed obligations on all member States of this community to take concerted efforts to bring such breaches of interna-tional law to an end; to refuse to recognise as lawful the consequences of such

a breach of international law; and to deny aid or assistance to the wrongdoer.4 Since then, it has been accepted that international law comprises a two- tier system that distinguishes between the response required of States to ordinary international wrongs and wrongs characterised as peremptory norms.

The notion that international law comprises a two- tier system draws support

from the four 1949 Geneva Conventions on the laws of war, which are binding on all States in the world, including Israel. Article 1 of the Geneva Conventions

Yearbook of the International Law Commission (2001), page 20.

2 icj, Case Concerning the Barcelona Traction, Light, and Power Company, Limited (Judgement)

provides that States Parties “undertake to respect and to ensure respect” for the Conventions “in all circumstances”. According to the 2016 updated com-mentary on the First Geneva Convention, Article 1, that is common to all four Geneva Conventions, obliges States to abstain from encouraging, aiding or assisting violations of the conventions and to take proactive steps to bring the

violation of the conventions to an end.5 Significantly, the basic rules of inter-national humanitarian law are included among the list of peremptory norms

recognised by the International Law Commission.6 This means that grave

breaches of the Geneva Conventions7 enjoy a superior status that require all States to take steps to ensure that they are not violated.

Israel is a serial and serious violator of international law. It has been a

wrongdoing State since its inception, but its wrongdoing has intensified since the occupation of the Palestinian territory in 1967. It has violated the most important human rights treaties to which it is a party, notably the International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination, the International Covenants on Civil and Political Rights and Economic, Social and Cultural Rights, the Convention against Torture, and the Convention on the Rights of the Child. It has also violated the principal international human-itarian law treaty applicable to the situation of occupation, namely the Fourth Geneva Convention. Israel’s objection, that the Fourth Geneva Convention

does not apply in the opt,8 has been dismissed by both the bodies monitoring

it,9 the International Committee of the Red Cross and the International Court

of Justice.10 Israel has also violated the fundamental principles of customary international humanitarian law prohibiting the excessive and disproportion-

ate use of force and violence against civilians.11 Many of Israel’s violations of international law constitute violations of peremptory norms.

Commission of Inquiry Established Pursuant to Human Rights Council Resolution S- 21/ 1, a/ Israel’s Impunity from Peremptory Norms 201

This chapter will first examine Israel’s violation of peremptory norms in the

opt. It will then consider the manner in which the international community responded to apartheid South Africa’s violation of such norms and whether it is fair to compare Israel’s occupation of Palestine with apartheid South Africa. Thereafter it will address the failure of the international community, the United Nations, the European Union, and individual States to confront Israel and to hold it accountable for its wrongdoings. Finally, the question will be asked and answered whether this amounts to a grant of impunity for crimes that violate peremptory norms.



1 Israel’s Violation of Peremptory Norms

Peremptory norms have recently received the attention of the International Law Commission. In 2019, the ilc adopted a set of Draft Conclusions on the Peremptory Norms of General International Law (jus cogens) which have been

transmitted to States for comment and observation.12 Conclusion 2 adopts the definition of peremptory norms contained in Article 53 of the Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties as a norm of general international law which is “accepted and recognized by the international community of States as a whole as a norm from which no derogation is permitted and which can be modified only by a subsequent norm of general international law having

the same character”.13 Conclusion 3 adds that these norms “reflect and protect fundamental values of the international community” and are “hierarchically superior to other rules of international law and are universally applicable”. The annex attached to the ilc Draft Conclusions on Peremptory Norms provides a non- exhaustive list of peremptory norms that the ilc has “previously referred

to as having that status”.14 This list includes the prohibitions on aggression, genocide, crimes against humanity, the basic rules of international humanitar-ian law, racial discrimination and apartheid, slavery, torture, and the right of self- determination.



hrc/ 29/ crp.4, 24 June 2015; unhcr, Report of the Detailed Findings of the Independent

International Commission of Inquiry on the Protests in the Occupied Palestinian Territory,

a/ hrc/ 40/ crp.2, 18 March 2019.

12 ilc, ‘Report of the International Law Commission on the Work of its Seventy- First

Session’, 2(2) Yearbook of the International Law Commission (2019), Annex.

13 Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties, Article 53.

14 Ibid., Conclusion 23.

202 Dugard

Although there have been serious suggestions that Israel’s 2014 military

offensive “Operation Protective Edge” against the occupied Gaza Strip, which resulted in the killing of 1,492 civilians and the wounding of some 10,000,

might have constituted the crime of genocide,15 this crime will not be exam-ined in the context of Israel’s actions. Nor will the crime of slavery be consid-ered. There are no suggestions that Israel has committed this crime.

There is evidence that Israel has violated all the other peremptory norms

listed by the ilc. At this time, it is necessary to refrain from drawing absolute conclusions about Israel’s culpability. Although there is strong evidence that Israel has committed certain crimes that violate peremptory norms, this evi-dence might successfully be challenged by Israel, or some justifiable defence offered. So, at this stage it constitutes prima facie proof only. Nevertheless, sufficient evidence of these crimes has been provided to warrant a political response from States and the United Nations or to justify an investigation by the International Criminal Court or national courts charged with the task of making such determinations. What follows is simply an account of the accusa-tions that Israel has committed crimes involving the violation of peremptory norms and an overview of the evidence that supports these accusations.

1.1 The Crime of Aggression

The crime of aggression, defined in UN General Assembly resolution 3314 (xxix) of December 1974 and Article 8bis of the Rome Statute adopted by the 2010 Review Conference in Kampala, Uganda, includes “an annexation by the use of force of the territory of another State or part thereof”. It also includes the invasion or attack by the armed forces of a State of the territory of another State, the bombardment by the armed forces of a State against the territory of another State or the blockade of the ports or coasts of a State by the armed forces of another State.

Article 1(a) of UN General Assembly resolution 3314 (xxix) provides in

an “explanatory note” that the term “State” is used in this definition “without prejudice to questions of recognition or whether a State is a Member of the United Nations”. Although this explanatory note does not appear in Article 8bis of the Rome Statute, it is clearly intended to give effect to resolution 3314 (xxix) as it is expressly referred to in Article 8bis. The consequence of this explanatory note is that Palestine must be accepted as “another State” for the purpose of the definition of aggression. In any event, Palestine is today widely



15 Russell Tribunal on Palestine, Russel Tribunal on Palestine – Emergency Session on Gaza,

2014, pages 35– 37.

Israel’s Impunity from Peremptory Norms 203

accepted as a State, having been recognised by some 138 States, accepted by the

UN General Assembly as a “non- member observer State”,16 admitted to une-sco, the International Criminal Court, the Permanent Court of Arbitration, the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons, and has become a party to all the important human rights and humanitarian law conventions. Further support for the statehood of Palestine is to be found in the February 2021 decision of the icc Pre- Trial Chamber which held that Palestine, as a State Party to the Rome Statute, qualified as a State for the purposes of Article 12(2)

(a) of the Rome Statute.17

The first question to consider is whether Israel’s annexation of East

Jerusalem in its Basic Law of 198018 constitutes an act of aggression. There can be no question that this annexation was the consequence of the use of force on the part of Israel in the Six- Day War of 1967, and that force was used against

another State, be it Palestine or Jordan. John Quigley explains in Chapter 1, that Israel was first to resort to force. In any case, annexation of the territory of another State is clearly unlawful whether it occurs after a legal or illegal use of force. UN Security Council resolution 242 of 22 November 1967 drew no such distinction in emphasising “the inadmissibility of the acquisition of territory by war” and the UN General Assembly Declaration on Principles of International Law Concerning Friendly Relations and Co- operation among

States in Accordance with the Charter of the United Nations of 197019 likewise declares that “[n] o territorial acquisition resulting from the threat or use of force shall be recognized as legal”.

That the annexation of East Jerusalem was unlawful was confirmed by the

UN Security Council in resolutions 476 and 478 which declare the annexation of Jerusalem to be null and void, a finding that has been endorsed by the UN

General Assembly in several resolutions20 and affirmed by the International Court of Justice in its advisory opinion on the Legal Consequences of the

Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory.21 Such a finding of illegality underscores the conclusion that even if Israel acted in self- defence

in 1967, its annexation of East Jerusalem remains an unlawful annexation and hence an act of aggression.

Israel’s annexation of East Jerusalem in 1980 is a case of de jure annexation.

Its de facto annexation of large parts of Area C must also be considered as an act of aggression. The seizure of some ten per cent of Palestinian territory by the construction of the Wall in Palestinian territory is an unequivocal act of annexation distinguishable in name only from the formal annexation of East

Jerusalem.22 Further, Israel’s repeated invasions, bombardment, and blockade of the Gaza Strip all fall within the definition of aggression. These attacks have been launched without justification and conducted in a wholly disproportion-ate manner, as evidenced by several international independent fact- finding

missions.23

1.2 Crimes against Humanity Article 7 of the Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court, which aims to codify the customary law prohibition on crimes against humanity, provides that crimes against humanity means:

[A] ny of the following acts when committed as part of a widespread or

systematic attack directed against any civilian population, with knowl-

edge of the attack:

(a) Murder; […]

(d) Deportation or forcible transfer of population;

(e) Imprisonment or other severe deprivation of physical liberty in vio-

lation of fundamental rules of international law;

(f) Torture; […]

great suffering or serious injury to body or to mental or physical health.

Israel’s actions in the West Bank and Gaza Strip qualify as crimes within the meaning of the above definition. These include murder in the intentional kill-

ing of protesters along the Gaza Strip border in the Great March of Return,24

the foreseeable killing of civilians in “Operation Cast Lead” (2008– 2009),25

“Operation Protective Edge” (2014),26 and Operation “Guardian of the Walls” (2021). Further, there are the extra- judicial killing of suspected militants in tar-geted assassinations and the collateral killing of bystanders in such operations; the forcible transfer of the Palestinian population through the destruction of

homes, evictions, appropriation of land for settlements and other measures;27 the imprisonment of some 5,000 Palestinians each year for political crimes following trial before military courts that fail to comply with international fair-

trial standards;28 the arbitrary detention of thousands of administrative detain-ees without any trial whatsoever and the inhuman and degrading treatment of prisoners; the systematic torture of detainees; the application of a system of apartheid; and the persecution of the Palestinian people as an identifiable

These acts have been committed as part of a widespread and systematic

attack directed against a civilian population, as evidenced by the multiple

commission of acts in pursuance of Israel’s policies,30 and by the fact that civil-ians have suffered disproportionately from Israel’s actions. That Israel and its agents have had knowledge of the nature and consequences of their actions,

which they have committed with deliberate intent, is beyond doubt.31

Crimes against humanity have recently received the attention of the

International Law Commission. In 2019, the ilc adopted a set of draft articles on this subject that have been referred to States for comment and observa-

tion.32 Articles 3 and 4 declare that States undertake to “prevent and to punish crimes against humanity” and to employ legislative, administrative, and judi-cial means to achieve this goal, in cooperation with other States and interna-tional organisations.

1.3 The Basic Rules of Humanitarian Law The two most basic rules of international humanitarian law in an armed conflict are the principle of distinction, which requires parties to distinguish between military objectives and the civilian population and to refrain from indiscriminate attacks that fail to make such a distinction, and the principle of proportionality, which requires parties to a conflict to avoid or minimise col-lateral excessive damage to civilians. In its operations “Cast Lead”, “Protective Edge” and “Guardian of the Walls”, and in its response to the protests along the Gaza Strip border as part of the Great March of Return, Israel has flagrantly violated these two principles as shown by the disproportionate number of civilians killed and wounded in these military operations. Three fact- finding

missions established by the UN Human Rights Council vividly testify to these

The rules governing the occupation of territory acquired in an armed con-

flict also rank among the basic rules of humanitarian law. Israel has violated several of the most fundamental rules of the law of occupation. It has de jure annexed East Jerusalem and de facto annexed the area between the Wall built in the occupied Palestinian territory and the 1949 armistice border, the interna-tional community’s recognised delineation between Israel and Palestine, com-

prising some ten per cent of Palestinian territory.34 The former annexation has

been condemned by the Security Council,35 and the latter by the International

Court of Justice.36 Israel’s settlement enterprise involving the transfer of some 700,000 Israeli settlers into the West Bank and East Jerusalem violates not only

the Fourth Geneva Convention,37 and customary international law,38 but it

also violates the prohibition on colonisation.39 Finally, the prolonged nature of the occupation coupled with Israel’s declared intention of annexing more parts of the West Bank and its refusal to negotiate a withdrawal from occupied Palestine in good faith, provide evidence of violation of this peremptory norm.

In its 2004 advisory opinion on the Legal Consequences of the Construction

of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory, the International Court of Justice held, that Israel had violated certain obligations under international human-itarian law which constituted obligations erga omnes – that is, obligations

belonging to the category of peremptory norms.40

1.4 Torture

The torture of Palestinians suspected of engaging in resistance to the occupa-tion is an inherent part of the apparatus of occupation. Although the Supreme Court of Israel sought to restrain the use of torture of detainees during interro-

gation in 1999,41 this practice continues.42 Mental torture in the form of sleep deprivation, exposure to persistent noise, humiliation, and threats to harm

family members persist, as does physical torture.43 In 2016, the UN Committee against Torture documented its concerns regarding torture in its concluding

observations on Israel.44 And more recently, in October 2019, four UN Special Rapporteurs expressed their concern about reports of the brutal torture of a

Palestinian detainee.45

1.5 Racial Discrimination and Apartheid The ilc treats the prohibition on racial discrimination and apartheid as a sin-gle peremptory norm in its Draft Conclusions on Peremptory Norms. There is, however, a material difference. Racial discrimination is prohibited by both the International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination and customary international law, but it does not feature as a core crime in the Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court. Both per-secution and apartheid do, however, appear as aggravated species of racial dis-crimination in the definition of crimes against humanity. Only apartheid will be discussed here.

The crime of apartheid is defined in Article 7(2)(h) of the Rome Statute

as including inhumane acts similar to those listed in the definition of crimes against humanity (that is, murder, forcible transfer, arbitrary imprisonment, torture and persecution) committed in the context of an institutionalised regime of systematic oppression and domination by one racial group over any other racial group, with the intention of maintaining that regime.
