Israel’s Impunity from Peremptory Norms 209

Israel’s policies in the West Bank including East Jerusalem (and possibly the

Gaza Strip46) meet the requirements for the crime of apartheid.47 First, two different racial groups reside in the territory – some 2.7 million Palestinians and around 700,000 Jewish settlers. The different identities of these groups are determined by their ancestry, descent, language, religion, and national origin and not by their skin colour. Second, Israel has, as shown above, committed the inhumane acts listed in the definition of crimes against humanity. Third, the settler group, aided and abetted by the Israel Defence Forces, constitute the dominant racial group. The primary function of the Israeli civil and military authorities responsible for enforcing the occupation is to maintain a system of unequal and discriminatory separation, in which Jewish settlers are privileged above Palestinians. The inhumane acts described above, are committed as part of a systematic and institutionalised oppressive regime designed to entrench the domination of Jewish settlers. This regime, like the apartheid regime of South Africa, is founded on a discriminatory ideology that has its roots in the Israeli legal system, which entitles persons of Jewish race or descent to supe-

rior rights and privileges.48 The situation is well described by Israel’s leading human rights lawyer, Michael Sfard:

[I] t is hard to look at the way Israel controls the West Bank, with separate

legal and administrative systems governing Palestinians and Israelis, and

with all local resources used to benefit one group at the expense of the

other, without being reminded of a legal term from the not- so- distant

past. A person would have to be unconscious not to pick up the whiff of

apartheid everywhere there is a settlement. Israel has created not only

an occupation that has persisted for generations but also a regime where

one group oppresses and discriminates against the other for the sole pur-

pose of preserving its control and supremacy.49

Israel’s system of apartheid is today the subject of scrutiny by the International Criminal Court which is conducting an investigation into alleged crimes by

Israelis – including the crime of apartheid.50 The monitoring committee of the International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination has recently found that the inter- State complaint lodged by Palestine against Israel for violation of this convention on the basis of Israel’s racially discriminatory practices and policies, and its implementation of the

policy of apartheid, is admissible.51 This complaint will now be examined by a specially appointed ad hoc Conciliation Commission in terms of Article 12 of the convention.

1.6 Self- determination

It is today recognised by all nations that the Palestinian people enjoy the right of self- determination in terms of which a people have the right to determine their own destiny. It is not necessary to describe the full import of this right. Suffice it to say that independent statehood is the ultimate aspiration of a people in the pursuit of self- determination. The fulfilment of this right has been thwarted mainly, but not only, by Israel which has used its full might and political resources to prevent Palestinians from achieving this goal. Israel has orchestrated a campaign aimed at limiting the number of States that recognise Palestine as a State and blocked its admission to membership of the United Nations. In 2004, the International Court of Justice identified Israel’s construc-tion of a wall in the Palestinian territory, as the violation of an obligation erga

omnes, to respect the right of the Palestinian people to self- determination.52 The Pre- Trial Chamber of the International Criminal Court has also recognised

the right of the Palestinian people to self- determination.53 A fortiori the denial and obstruction of statehood must be seen as a gross violation of the right of self- determination.

2 The Response of the International Community of Nations to

Apartheid in South Africa

The apartheid regime of South Africa was responsible for the violation of sev-eral of the above peremptory norms. Its racist and discriminatory practices and policies violated the prohibitions on racial discrimination and apartheid, crimes against humanity, torture, and self- determination. These violations, however, took place when the notion of peremptory norms was still in its infancy and the establishment of an International Criminal Court with juris-diction to try such crimes was a pipe dream.

Consequently, South Africa’s conduct was repeatedly condemned as a

violation of the human rights provisions in the UN Charter and customary international law governing human rights, racial discrimination, torture, and

self- determination.54 As South Africa had taken care not to become a party to any human rights treaty, it was necessary to claim that these norms were recognised by States as part of international custom. Little attempt was made to categorise these actions as international crimes or violations of peremptory norms. Despite this fact, the response of the international community was comprehensive and severe.

The question of apartheid appeared regularly on the agenda of the UN

General Assembly from 1952 to 1994 and on that of the UN Security Council after 1960, following the killing of demonstrators at Sharpeville, when it rec-ognised that, if continued, apartheid “might endanger international peace and

security”.55 During this time numerous resolutions of condemnation of apart-heid were adopted and measures for bringing an end to the policy of apartheid

were imposed.56



54 See John Dugard, Max du Plessis, Tiyanjana Maluwa, and Dire Tladi, Dugard’s International

Law: A South African Perspective (Juta 2018) 706– 716.

55 unsc Resolution 134 (1 April 1960).

56 See for example unsc Resolution 134 (1 April 1960); unsc Resolution 181 (7 August

1963); unsc Resolution 182 (4 December 1963); unsc Resolution 191 (18 June 1964); unsc

1985); unsc Resolution 602 (25 November 1987). The UN General Assembly adopted res-

olutions condemning apartheid annually from 1952 to 1994. See John Dugard, Max du

Plessis, Tiyanjana Maluwa, and Dire Tladi, Dugard’s International Law: A South African

Perspective (Juta 2018) 706– 714.

212 Dugard

2.1 The Sanctions of Non- recognition and Non- rendering of Aid Article 41 of the ilc Draft Articles on States Responsibility requires States to refuse to recognise as lawful a situation created by a serious breach of a peremptory norm and to withhold aid or assistance from a State that seeks to

maintain such a situation. This provision, which gives effect to State practice57 and echoes the finding of the International Court of Justice in the 1971 opinion on the Legal Consequences for States of Continued Presence of South Africa in

Namibia (South West Africa)58 is of particular importance to the present analy-sis, as it prescribes the measures to be taken by States to resist the violation of peremptory norms. The manner in which the United Nations and the interna-tional community upheld these norms in respect of South Africa is of special relevance to the question of whether the international community is willing to apply the same standards to Israel.

2.1.1 Withholding of Aid and Assistance for the Maintenance of

Apartheid

The international community lost no opportunity to condemn apartheid, both multilaterally and unilaterally, in South Africa. It went well beyond the obliga-tion to withhold aid and assistance in its positive efforts to end apartheid. Both the UN General Assembly and UN Security Council took measures that sought to bring pressure on the South African apartheid regime to abandon apartheid.

The UN General Assembly called in 1962 on States to terminate diplomatic

relations with South Africa, to close their ports to all vessels flying the South African flag, to refuse landing and passage facilities to South African aircrafts,

and to boycott all South African goods.59 This recommendation was repeated every year and States were in addition requested to suspend cultural, educa-

tional, and sporting exchanges with South Africa.60 After an attempt to exclude South Africa from the United Nations had failed in the Security Council as a result of vetoes cast by France, the United Kingdom and the United States, the President of the UN General Assembly ruled that South Africa could no longer

participate in the UN General Assembly because of its racial policies.61

57 ilc, ‘Report of the International Law Commission on the Work of its Fifty- Third Session’,

2(2) Yearbook of the International Law Commission (2001), pages 114– 115.

58 icj, Legal Consequences for States of the Continued Presence of South Africa in Namibia

(South West Africa) Notwithstanding Security Council Resolution 276 (1970) (Advisory

Opinion) icj Reports 16 (1971), paras 123– 126, 133.

59 unga Resolution 1761 (xvii) (6 November 1962).

60 See for example unga Resolution 39/ 72 (13 December 1984).

61 See DJ van Vuuren, ‘South Africa’s Foreign Policy and International Practice – 1975’, 1 South

African Yearbook of International Law (1975) 162, 217– 222.

Israel’s Impunity from Peremptory Norms 213

The UN Security Council recommended in 1963 the imposition of an arms

embargo,62 and in 1977 it adopted a mandatory resolution under Chapter vii of

the UN Charter, directing States to impose an arms embargo on South Africa.63 In 1985, the UN Security Council recommended the suspension of new invest-ment and guaranteed export loans in South Africa, the prohibition of all sales of computer equipment that might be used by the army and the police, and restric-

tions on sporting and cultural relations.64 The Security Council also demanded

the release of political prisoners65 and the lifting of a state of emergency.66

Other organisations and States followed the example of the United Nations.

The Organization of African Unity, the Commonwealth, and the European Community imposed different sanctions on South Africa, and individual States imposed economic and diplomatic restrictions on South Africa, culmi-nating in the US Comprehensive Anti- Apartheid Act of 1986, which imposed limited economic sanctions on South Africa and suspended the landing rights

of South African Airways on US territory.67

2.1.2 Non- recognition

The United Nations consistently applied the principle of non- recognition to South Africa’s Bantustans designed to further the policy of apartheid by the creation of “independent” States for South Africa’s different tribal groups.

When the first Bantustan, Transkei, was granted “independence” in 1976, the

UN General Assembly condemned this conferment of so- called independence as “invalid” on the ground that it was designed to consolidate the “inhuman policies of apartheid”, and called upon States to deny any form of recogni-

tion to it.68 This call was endorsed by the UN Security Council.69 The United Nations responded in similar fashion to the conferment of “independence”

on Bophuthatswana,70 Venda71 and Ciskei.72 The UN General Assembly and

62 unsc Resolutions 181 (7 August 1963) and 182 (4 December 1963).

63 unsc Resolution 418 (4 November 1977).

64 unsc Resolution 569 (26 July 1985).

65 unsc Resolutions 181 (7 August 1963), 182 (4 December 1963), 191 (18 June 1964), 311 (4

February 1972), 417 (31 October 1977), 473 (13 June 1980), 560 (12 March 1985).

66 unsc Resolution 569 (12 March 1985).

67 See United States Comprehensive Anti- Apartheid Act of 1986 (1987) 26 International

Legal Materials 111.

68 unga Resolution 31/ 6A (26 October 1976).

69 unsc Resolutions 406 (25 May 1977) and 407 (25 May 1977).

70 unga Resolution 32/ 105N (14 December 1977).

71 unga Resolution 34/ 93G (12 December 1979); Security Council, Note by the President of

the Security Council, S/ 13549, 21 September 1979.

72 Security Council, Note by the President of the Security Council, S/ 14794, 15 December 1981. 214 Dugard

UN Security Council made it clear that the creation of Bantustan “States” vio-lated a number of peremptory norms such as systematic racial discrimination,

apartheid and self- determination.73

As a result of these calls for non- recognition, no State apart from South

Africa recognised any of the “independent” Bantustans as independent States. States applied this policy of non- recognition consistently and thoroughly throughout the apartheid era. Consequently, it was easy for these territories to be re- absorbed into South Africa after the abandonment of apartheid in 1994. This experience was rightly construed as a victory for the United Nations pol-icy of non- recognition.



3 Is It Fair to Use the South African Precedent as a Guide to the

Response of the International Community to Israel’s Violations of

Peremptory Norms?

Since the fall of apartheid in South Africa, accusations have been directed at several States that they apply apartheid. Israel is, however, the State which most frequently stands accused of applying apartheid in the opt. Israeli apartheid, particularly in the opt, has been carefully analysed and compared to South African apartheid and Israel is the only State to be accused of committing this

crime before the International Criminal Court,74 and the Committee on the

Elimination of Racial Discrimination.75 Consequently, it would be reason-able to expect that the United Nations, international organisations and States would respond in the same way that they responded to apartheid South Africa. Indeed, it might be expected that a more severe response would follow, given the number of peremptory norms violated, in addition to the prohibition on apartheid and the fact that, by any measurement, Israel’s crimes exceed those of apartheid South Africa.



73 See John Dugard, Recognition and the United Nations (Grotius Publications 1987) 98– 108;

unga Resolution 31/ 6A (1976); Security Council, Note by the President of the Security

Council, S/ 14794, 15 December 1981.

74 State of Palestine, Referral by the State of Palestine Pursuant to Articles 13(a) and 14 of the

Rome Statute, 15 May 2018.

75 UN Committee on the Elimination of Racial Discrimination, Decision on the Admissibility

of the Inter- State Communication Submitted by the State of Palestine Against Israel, cerd/

c/ 103/ r.2, 20 May 2021.

Israel’s Impunity from Peremptory Norms 215

Considerations of space do not permit a comprehensive comparison of

apartheid in South Africa and occupied Palestine.76 Suffice it to say, that while there are differences, the two systems share many common features. In some respects, apartheid in South Africa was worse but in many respects Israel’s conduct is more egregious. Israel’s system of apartheid that has accompa-nied its occupation, has seen more deaths and destruction. More Palestinians have been imprisoned for political crimes, more Palestinians have been held in administrative detention, more homes have been destroyed for punitive or administrative reasons, more children have been killed or injured, and more civilians have been killed in security operations. Torture has been used by both Israeli and South African security forces but the killing of political oppo-nents by targeted assassinations has been perfected by Israel. Both systems of apartheid have imposed heavy restrictions on freedom of movement, but South Africa never excluded blacks from public roads. South Africa reserved parks and beaches for exclusive white use but never built a wall to separate blacks and whites. South Africa built separate schools and hospitals for blacks, but Israel has not built any such amenities for Palestinians – leaving this to international donors. No black community in South Africa was subjected to a blockade and carefully calculated restrictions on foodstuffs. And so on. Of course, it is unfortunate for Israel that new technology and increased surveil-lance by fact- finding missions, television cameras and video recordings have recorded and uncovered Israel’s violations of international law. The world is a much smaller place than it was thirty years ago.

South Africa’s apartheid was deeply humiliating. The subjugation of blacks

to whites, and the exclusion of blacks from public life and public amenities, left psychological scars on millions. Palestinians are likewise humiliated but in a different manner. The denial of rights enjoyed for centuries by the Palestinian people by an Occupying Power bent on seizing Palestinian land and erasing the Palestinian presence from these lands has been, and continues to be, an immeasurably humiliating experience.



4 The Response of the International Community to Israel’s Crimes

States of the West have taken the lead in the construction of a world order in which respect for self- determination, human rights and humanitarian law

76 See John Dugard, Confronting Apartheid, A Personal History of South Africa, Namibia and

Palestine (Jacana 2018); Virginia Tilley (ed), Beyond Occupation, Apartheid, Colonialism,

and International Law in the Occupied Palestinian Territories (Pluto Press 2012). 216 Dugard

are an integral part. This means that they bear a heavy responsibility for the adoption of a system which recognises the hierarchy of peremptory norms and which gives effect to these values. It is therefore largely the response of these nations to Israel’s violation of peremptory norms that informs and deter-mines the commitment of the international community to the acceptance and enforcement of a two- tier system of international justice.

4.1 The United Nations

The United Nations, with 193 member States, is representative of the interna-tional community as a whole. This community voice is reflected in resolutions of the plenary body of the organisation, the General Assembly. General Assembly resolutions that assert the right of self- determination of the Palestinian peo-ple and condemn Israel’s violation of fundamental human rights constitut-ing peremptory norms give voice to the concerns of the world community. But these resolutions, like the resolutions adopted by the UN Human Rights Council, are mere recommendations. The real power of the United Nations is to be found in the UN Security Council, empowered to take legally binding decisions, and the Secretariat responsible for implementing decisions of the UN Security Council and recommendations of the UN General Assembly. It is these two bodies that have provided protection to Israel and ensure that it is not exposed to the kind of action that was taken against South Africa for apply-ing apartheid and violating human rights.

On the one hand, the record of the UN Security Council on Israel is not con-

sistently bad. Over the years there have been a number of notable resolutions that have shaped the legal framework of Israel’s occupation of the Palestinian territory. Here, one thinks in particular of UN Security Council resolution 242 of 1967, which provides for the withdrawal of Israeli forces from territories occupied in the Six- day War and affirms the inadmissibility of the acquisition of territory by war; UN Security Council resolution 478 of 1980, which con-demns Israel’s purported annexation of Jerusalem and calls on States not to recognise this act of annexation; and UN Security Council resolution 2334 of 2016, that reiterates that Israel’s establishment of settlements in the opt is a flagrant violation of international law and calls upon States to distinguish between the territory of Israel and the opt. These resolutions apply the princi-ple of non- recognition to any attempt on the part of Israel to acquire territory occupied in 1967, to Israel’s purported annexation of East Jerusalem and to settlements in the occupied Palestinian territory.

On the other hand, the UN Security Council has not demonstrated a deter-

mination to compel Israel to desist from its de jure or de facto acquisition of Palestinian territory or to respect the rules of human rights law or international Israel’s Impunity from Peremptory Norms 217

humanitarian law. It has obstructed Palestine’s right of self- determination by

rejecting its membership of the United Nations.77 The Security Council has failed to impose an arms embargo on Israel as it did in the case of South

Africa,78 or to recommend sanctions of the kind it recommended against

South Africa.79 The failure of the UN Security Council to condemn Israel’s violations of peremptory norms, is nowhere more apparent than in its abject silence on most of Israel’s attacks on the Gaza Strip, and its timid resolution on “Operation Cast Lead” which refused to condemn Israel’s invasion of the

territory.80 The absence of measures to punish Israel for its repeated violations of peremptory norms or to take measures to prevent such violations, can only be construed as rendering aid or assistance to Israel.

The response of the UN Security Council to the 2004 International Court

of Justice advisory opinion on the Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory and its response to the 1971 advi-sory opinion of the same court on the Legal Consequences for States of the

Continued Presence of South Africa in Namibia (South West Africa)81 illustrates

its double standard. The UN Security Council endorsed the latter opinion,82 and proceeded to use it as a guide to the resolution of the conflict in Namibia. In stark contrast, the UN Security Council has virtually ignored the 2004 opin-ion on the Wall. It did not endorse the opinion and has only briefly referred to it in passing in UN Security Council resolution 2334. Here it asserts the illegal-ity of settlements with no mention of the fact that the icj advisory opinion on the Wall held settlements to be unlawful. The preamble of UN Security Council resolution 2334 simply recalls that opinion in a paragraph reaffirming Israel’s obligation under the Fourth Geneva Convention. Nor has the Quartet done any

better. It too has only mentioned the opinion in passing.83 To make matters

worse, the UN Security Council has allowed the Quartet, the body charged with the task of furthering peace in the Middle East, to let it sink into oblivion.

It is common knowledge that the UN Secretariat has been infiltrated with

bureaucrats sympathetic to Israel. This became clear when I was Special Rapporteur on the Human Rights Situation in the Occupied Palestinian Territories from 2001 to 2008. Senior UN officials were open in their opposition

to the advisory proceedings before the International Court of Justice,84 and the Office of the UN Legal Adviser advised the UN Secretary- General that he was not legally required to support the opinion, as it was not a binding legal

opinion and was not addressed to the UN Secretariat85- an unconvincing deci-sion that took no account of the strong body of opinion that the UN Secretary-

General was bound to comply with advisory opinions of the icj.86

4.2 States

States are primarily responsible for the failure of the international community to take action of the kind contemplated by Article 41 of the ilc Draft Articles on States Responsibility in relation to Israel’s occupation of the Palestinian territory. That is, to cooperate to bring to an end any serious breach of an obli-gation arising under a peremptory norm, to refuse to recognise as lawful a sit-uation created by a serious breach of such an obligation, and not to render aid or assistance in maintaining such a situation.

In resolutions of the United Nations and other international organisations,

and in both multilateral and unilateral practice, States have not gone beyond calls for peace in the Middle East, an end to occupation and settlement con-struction, recognition of Palestine’s right of self- determination, approval of a two- State solution to the conflict and non- recognition of Israel’s claims to sov-ereignty over territories occupied in 1967. Resolutions and practice of this kind echo resolutions of the United Nations during the first decade of apartheid which called on South Africa to observe its obligations under the human rights

provisions of the Charter,87 and suggested strategies that might be adopted by

the apartheid regime to achieve reconciliation between the races.88 In 1962,

84 In a meeting with Kiernan Prendergast, Under Secretary- General for Political Affairs, on

29 October 2004, he informed me that “we do not think the icj Opinion was a good idea”.

85 See John Dugard, ‘Advisory Opinions and the Secretary- General with Special Reference to

the 2004 Advisory Opinion on the Wall’ in Laurence Boisson de Chazournes and Marcelo

Kohen (eds), International Law and the Quest fort its Implementation, Liber Amicorum Vera

Gowlland– Debbas (Brill Publishers 2010) 403, 408.

86 Ibid., pages 409– 412.

87 unga Resolution 917(x) (6 December 1955).

88 unga Official Records (9th Session 1954), UN Doc a/ 2719, 90– 92.

Israel’s Impunity from Peremptory Norms 219

the UN General Assembly changed its approach and began to request member States to take action against South Africa in order to compel it to abandon apartheid, including by terminating diplomatic relations with and boycotting

South African goods.89 In 1963 and 1964, the UN Security Council called on

States to cease supplying arms to South Africa,90 a call that was converted into

a mandatory arms embargo in 1977.91 In 1985, the UN Security Council called for the suspension of new investments in South Africa and the suspension of

guaranteed export loans.92 States have yet to endorse such action in the polit-ical organs of the United Nations or in their dealings with Israel. They have not taken meaningful action to compel Israel to desist from its violations of peremptory norms.

States have made attempts to withhold recognition of Israel’s annexation of

East Jerusalem and the legality of settlements and the Wall built in Palestinian territory. But these gestures of non- recognition have been both half- hearted and ineffective. The United Nations, and western States in particular, have made little attempt to bring Israel’s annexation of East Jerusalem to an end. The de facto acquiescence in Israel’s unlawful action on the part of western States is demonstrated by its contrasting vigorous response to Russia’s annex-ation of the Crimea. Despite that this annexation was preceded by a referen-dum in the Crimea in favour of annexation and was condemned only by the

UN General Assembly,93 the European Union, the United States, Canada, and Australia have imposed sanctions on Russian individuals and businesses. Even less attempt has been made to sanction Israel for its de facto annexation of some ten per cent of Palestinian land in the West Bank by the construction of the Wall and its unlawful construction of settlements. Futile efforts have been made to prohibit the import of goods from settlements but there has been no suggestion of economic sanctions against Israel, Israeli businesses, or Israeli businesspersons.

Western States have failed to take unilateral action to compel compli-

ance with peremptory norms on the part of Israel as many States did against

Community to Gross Violations of Human Rights’ in Irwin Cotler and F Pearl Eliadis

(eds), International Human Rights Law: Theory and Practice (Canadian Human Rights

apartheid South Africa. To cite but one example; in 1986 the United States adopted the Comprehensive Anti- Apartheid Act, which imposed economic

sanctions on South Africa.94 Nor have States prosecuted Israeli officials for grave breaches of the Geneva Conventions (which include many peremptory

norms) as they are required to do.95 On the contrary, some western States (such as the United Kingdom) have amended their laws to afford immunity from prosecution to such persons.

In summary, States, particularly western States, have rendered aid and assis-

tance to Israel in its violation of peremptory norms by continuing to supply arms to, and engage in trade with Israel. Moreover, even when they have not done this, they have acquiesced in Israel’s crimes in a way which can only be interpreted as complicity.



5 Conclusion

States do not acquiesce in Israel’s violation of peremptory norms for reasons of fact or law. They accept most of the facts upon which allegations of the violation of peremptory norms are based. They accept that Israel has unlaw-fully annexed East Jerusalem, that Israel has unlawfully seized ten per cent of the West Bank by constructing a wall within Palestinian territory, that there are some 700,000 illegal settlers in the West Bank and East Jerusalem, that Israel prevents Palestinians from exercising their right of self- determination, that hundreds of Palestinians are imprisoned without trial, that the security forces employ methods of torture and that thousands of Palestinian civilians have been killed or maimed in disproportionate military offensives that fail to distinguish between combatants and civilians. (They do, however, blindly deny the existence of apartheid in occupied Palestine despite all the evidence to support this). Similarly States accept that Israel has unlawfully violated peremptory norms. Attempts are made by States to justify Israel’s attacks on the Gaza Strip but, by and large, they acknowledge the disproportionality of such actions and the failure to distinguish between civilian and military tar-gets. No attempt is made to argue that the construction of the Wall and set-tlements is legal, or that preventive detention, demolition of homes or torture



94 ‘United States: Comprehensive Anti– Apartheid Act of 1986’, 26 International Legal

Materials (1987) 79.

95 In terms of the Fourth Geneva Convention, Articles 146 and 147 and Additional Protocol

i, Article 85.

Israel’s Impunity from Peremptory Norms 221

can be justified in law. Moreover, there is agreement that Palestinians are in law entitled to the right of self- determination.

The reasons States make no attempt to hold Israel responsible for its illegal

actions are uncomplicated.96 Holocaust guilt on the part of countries such as Germany, the Netherlands and France; fear of being labelled as anti- Semitic, an accusation that will destroy politicians and public figures; the power and bribes of pro- Israel lobbies; and the fear of offending the United States, with the economic consequences that this entails. These are the reasons for refusing to hold Israel accountable, but they are not good reasons. Clearly, they provide no justification for failing to comply with international law.

It is interesting to compare the way the international community’s response

to South Africa’s violations of human rights law positively strengthened the growth and development of international law with the negative impact of the international community’s response to Israel’s violation of peremptory norms.

New rules of treaty and customary law to promote human rights, racial

equality and self- determination evolved as a result of international oppo-sition to apartheid in South Africa. Treaties such as the Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination and the International Convention on the Suppression and Punishment of the Crime of Apartheid, were adopted to assist opposition to race discrimination in South Africa. The prohibition on interference in the domestic affairs of States enshrined in Article 2(7) of the UN Charter, was substantially weakened by a succession of resolutions condemning apartheid. The non- recognition of States created in violation of international law was given new meaning by the non- recognition of the Bantustan States. International humanitarian law was rewritten to con-fer prisoner- of- war status on combatants belonging to national liberation

movements.97 South Africa’s six appearances before the International Court of Justice over Namibia provided the court with the opportunity to formulate new rules on self- determination and human rights. Apartheid succeeded per-versely in injecting new notions of respect for human rights, racial equality, and self- determination into an international legal order that in 1945 had few developed rules on these subjects.

The international community’s response to Israel has the exact opposite

effect. It has raised questions about the commitment of States, particularly western States, to some of the most fundamental prohibitions of international law – on aggression, torture, race discrimination and apartheid, arbitrary

96 John Dugard, Confronting Apartheid, A Personal History of South Africa, Namibia and

Palestine (Jacana 2018) 261– 264.

97 See Additional Protocols i and ii.

222 Dugard

imprisonment, the harmful treatment of civilians in armed conflict and the suppression of self- determination. The failure of States to take measures, col-lectively or unilaterally, to hold Israel accountable for its violation of a host of peremptory norms has weakened respect for human rights and humanitarian law. It has brought international law into disrepute and given rise to cynicism about the respect States have for international law. At the same time, it has destroyed the notion of a two- tier system of international law that elevates certain norms reflecting the most fundamental values of contemporary civil-isation to a higher status, whose violation is to be enforced by the concerted action of nations. Western States have placed the determination to protect Israel at all costs above respect for international law.





chapter 10


Third State Responsibility versus Sanctions

in Regulating Trade with Illegal Settlements

at the EU

Manuel Devers and Tom Moerenhout



Sovereignty, territorial integrity, and the right to self- determination are the corner stones of modern public international law. Consequentially, belliger-ent occupation and annexation are among the gravest threats to international peace and stability. As such, illegal settlements in occupied territories are not only a thorn in one specific conflict but can also be a thorn in the perceived authority of public international law altogether. The practice of transferring an occupier’s civilian population into an occupied or annexed territory, in sup-port of its own national interests shows a clear and deliberate disregard for international law.

Third States’ muted reaction to settler transfer signals that the fundamental

territoriality principle of international law is not so fundamental after all. In other words, if State practice in international law is about the practices that follow a sense of international legal obligation, then State non- practice is a demonstration of the absence of a sense of international legal obligation, ren-dering the social justice aspect of international law powerless and leaving the discipline reduced to its roots of bilateralism. There is no greater symbol of the latter than Third States trading with an occupier’s illegal settlements in occu-pied or annexed territories.

This chapter will explain why ending trade with illegal settlements such as

those of Israel in the West Bank, including East Jerusalem and the occupied Syrian Golan, and those of Morocco in Western Sahara, is not a sanction under international law or European Union law. Part 1 will discuss how the practice of illegal settlements triggers Third States’ responsibilities. It will highlight that trading with illegal settlements violates the legal duties of non- recognition and non- assistance, which are self- executing duties and not sanctions. Part 2 will elaborate on how the prohibition on settlement trade is not a sanction, but a trade measure under EU law as well. It will also reference recent caselaw that confirms this position and thereby helps protect the integrity of inter-national law since States can use sanctions to pick and choose who to apply international law to, which ultimately undermines international law. Part 3



© Manuel Devers and Tom Moerenhout, 2023 |

224 Devers and Moerenhout

will present the implications of understanding settlement trade as a general measure, rather than, incorrectly, as a sanction. Part 4 will address EU law and practice in dealing with trade with settlements. It will set out the inconsistency in the practice of the European Commission and discuss legal trends towards prohibiting settlement trade as a trade measure. It will also highlight a recent European Citizens Initiative, in relation to which the European Commission recognised that stopping trade with illegal settlements as a general measure indeed constitutes a trade measure.



1 Settlements as Breaches of Jus Cogens Norms

The prohibition of trading with illegal settlements under international law is relatively straightforward and relies on two steps: first, the confirmation that settlements breach peremptory norms of international law, and second, the confirmation that the duties of non- recognition and non- assistance include a trade prohibition.

Based on Article 49 of the Fourth Geneva Convention and Article 8 of the

Rome Statute, illegal settlements can be defined as settlements within an occupied territory that civilians of the Occupying Power are transferred into, and whose presence in and transfer to the occupied territory is facilitated – directly or indirectly – by the Occupying Power. The settlements in the occu-pied Palestinian territory fulfil this definition and they breach at least three peremptory norms of international law: the right to self- determination and the prohibition on the acquisition of territory by force, fundamental norms of international humanitarian law, and the prohibition against apartheid.

Importantly, while individually, each of those three dimensions likely con-

firm the jus cogens violations of illegal settlements in occupied or annexed ter-ritories, the combined violations undisputedly pass the threshold of finding a

violation of peremptory norms of international law.1 In its advisory opinion on the Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory, the International Court of Justice only relied on the first two dimen-sions to determine the applicability of the duties of non- recognition and non- assistance in the case of the Wall, and it is ipso facto equally sufficient to

conclude it in case of settlements in occupied territories.2



1 Tom Moerenhout, ‘The consequence of the UN Resolution on Israeli Settlements for the

EU: Stop Trade with Settlements’, ejil:Talk!, 4 April 2017.

2 icj, Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory

(Advisory Opinion) icj Reports 136 (2004), paras 157.

Sanctions in Regulating Trade with Illegal Settlements 225

1.1 The Right to Self- determination and Prohibition on the Acquisition

of Territory by Force

Illegal settlements violate the right to self- determination and can constitute de facto acquisition of territory through the use of force. This view finds support in several UN Security Council resolutions on Israel’s occupation of Palestine that confirm the inadmissibility of settlements and the acquisition of terri-

tory by force.3 The peremptory characters of the right to self- determination and the prohibition on the acquisition of territory were affirmed by the International Law Commission when drafting the ilc Draft Articles on States

Responsibility4 and Draft Conclusions on Peremptory Norms of General

International Law (jus cogens).5 They emphasise the quintessence of the ter-ritoriality principle in contemporary international law. The war crimes listed in the Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court also includes grave breaches of the 1949 Geneva Conventions, including extensive destruction and appropriation of property that is not justified by military necessity and carried out unlawfully. The icc war crimes definition further criminalises the transfer of the Occupying Power’s civilian population into the occupied territory, the

destruction or seizure of enemy’s property, and pillage.6

1.2 Fundamental Norms of International Humanitarian Law Illegal settlements violate fundamental norms of international humanitarian law, which the ilc Draft Articles on States Responsibility consider as having jus cogens character. These fundamental norms of international humanitar-ian law include a prohibition of population transfer to occupied territories

under the Fourth Geneva Convention.7 In its advisory opinion on the Wall, the International Court of Justice confirmed that those fundamental humanitar-ian norms had an erga omnes character and were to “be observed by all States” because “they constitute intransgressible principles of international customary

law” and are “fundamental to the respect of humanity”.8 It appears to us that the main reason the icj did not explicitly call them jus cogens norms was for reasons of judicial economy, in other words they did not make determinations

3 See for example unsc Resolution 2334 (23 December 2016).

4 ilc, ‘Report of the International Law Commission on the Work of its Fifty- Third Session’, 2(2)

Yearbook of the International Law Commission (2001), page 248.

5 ilc, ‘Report of the International Law Commission on the Work of its Seventy- First Session’,

2(2) Yearbook of the International Law Commission (2019).

6 Rome Statute, Article 8.

7 Fourth Geneva Convention.

8 icj, Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory

(Advisory Opinion) icj Reports 136 (2004), paras 157.

226 Devers and Moerenhout

on matters that they were not asked to determine. Many legal scholars,9 how-

ever, as well as icj judges Bedjaoui,10 Weeramantry,11 and Koroma,12 have explicitly concluded that these fundamental humanitarian norms are either jus cogens in statu nascendi or jus cogens.

1.3 Prohibition of Apartheid

Illegal settlements can also involve the crime of apartheid. The ilc Draft

Articles on States Responsibility13 have characterised the prohibition of apart-heid as a key jus cogens norm. The crime of apartheid is also recognised as a crime against humanity in the Rome Statute of the International Criminal

Court.14 The prohibition of apartheid is encompassed in the International Convention on the Suppression and Punishment of the Crime of Apartheid of

1973 and Additional Protocol i of 1977.15

In the European Journal of International Law, John Dugard and John

Reynolds scrupulously set forward the argument and legal evidence that the situation in the West Bank, including Israel’s settlement enterprise, meet the definition of apartheid in the International Convention on the Suppression

and Punishment of the Crime of Apartheid.16 The UN Economic and Social Commission for Western Asia similarly confirmed in a report that Israeli set-

tlements constitute apartheid.17 This report was censored by Israel and the

US and led to the resignation of escwa’s head, Rima Khalaf.18 The existence

of apartheid has also been recognised by several rights groups, including

2 Trade and the Duties of Non- recognition and Non- assistance

When peremptory norms are breached, as is the case in relation to Israeli set-tlements in occupied Palestine, Third States have the responsibility not to recognise as lawful a situation created by it, nor to render aid or assistance

in maintaining that situation.20 These duties are commonly referred to as the duties of non- recognition and non- assistance. Trade with illegal settlements violates both duties.

2.1 Trade with Settlements as a Violation of the Duty of Non- recognition

The fundamental prohibition of the transfer of the Occupying Power’s own civilian population ipso facto implies an equally strong prohibition on the eco-

nomic activity of transferred civilians for the benefit of the Occupying Power.21

In the icj advisory opinion22 on South Africa’s continued presence in Namibia of 1971, the court recognised the duty of non- recognition and confirmed that States should abstain from economic or other relationships with the violating

State on behalf of or concerning the violation (in this case Namibia).23 When the Occupying Power regulates the export of products from its illegal settle-ments, it takes a claim on that territory. And since the import of products from illegal settlements by Third States is a legal act, such trade constitutes implicit recognition because it recognises the Occupying Power’s claim over the



19 Amnesty International, Israel’s Apartheid Against Palestinians: Cruel System of Domination

and Crime Against Humanity, February 2022. See further Chapter 5, Reynolds.

20 ilc, ‘Report of the International Law Commission on the Work of its Fifty- Third Session’,

2(2) Yearbook of the International Law Commission (2001), Article 41.

21 Tom Moerenhout, ‘The Obligation to Withhold from Trading in Order Not to Recognize

and Assist Settlements and their Economic Activity in Occupied Territories’, 3(2) Journal

of International Humanitarian Legal Studies (2012) 344.

22 Up until today, the icj has mentioned the duty of non- recognition only twice. Both times

were in the context of territorial occupation: once in the 1971 advisory opinion on The

Legal Consequences for States of the Continued presence of South Africa in Namibia and

once in the 2004 advisory opinion on the Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall

in the Occupied Palestinian Territory.

23 icj, Legal Consequences for States of the Continued Presence of South Africa in Namibia

(South West Africa) Notwithstanding Security Council Resolution 276 (1970) (Advisory

Opinion) icj Reports 16 (1971) para 124.

228 Devers and Moerenhout

exportation of illegal settlement goods and services from illegal settlements from an annexed or occupied territory.

2.2 Trade as a Violation of the Duty of Non- assistance The agreement establishing the World Trade Organization explicitly refers to the economic benefits of liberalised trade, stating that “raising standards of liv-ing, ensuring full employment and a large and steadily growing volume of real income and effective demand, and expanding the production of and trade in

goods and services”.24 Trading with illegal settlements gives those settlements economic support. This constitutes concrete help to the maintenance of the

unlawful situation.25 Both the UN Office of the High Commission for Human

Rights26 and the UN Human Rights Council27 have recognised that the encour-agement of economic activity in Israeli settlements has been a reason for set-tlement expansion. It was also implicitly recognised in UN Security Council resolution 465 from 1980, that called upon all States “not to provide Israel with any assistance to be used specifically in connection with settlements in the

occupied territories”.28



3 The Duties of Non- recognition and Non- assistance versus

Sanctions

The duties of non- recognition and non- assistance under international law are identified by their customary and self- executing characteristics. All (non- injured) States have the duty not to recognise and assist a violation of

a peremptory norm of international law by another State.29 Whereas these duties safeguard the interests of the international community, the duties of non- recognition and non- assistance do not require collective action or the involvement of any particular international body such as the United Nations



24 Marrakesh Agreement establishing the World Trade Organization, 15 April 1994.

25 Helmut Aust, Complicity and the Law of State Responsibility (cup 2011).

26 ohchr, Israeli Settlements in the Occupied Palestinian Territory including East Jerusalem

and the Occupied Syrian Golan, a/ 71/ 335, 24 August 2016.

27 unhrc, Report of the Independent International Fact- Finding Mission to Investigate the

Implications of the Israeli Settlements on the Civil, Political, Economic, Social and Cultural

Rights of the Palestinian People throughout the Occupied Palestinian Territory, including

East Jerusalem, a/ hrc/ 22/ 63, 7 February 2013.

28 unsc Resolution 465 (1 March 1980).

29 Ian Brownlie, Principles of Public International Law (oup 2003). Sanctions in Regulating Trade with Illegal Settlements 229

Security Council acting under Chapter vii. Instead, they are directly binding

to each individual State.30

Sanctions are different from the above-mentioned duties. Sanctions are

intended to support foreign policy interests and they are limited in duration and disappear once a conflict is resolved, political determination dwindles, or the timeframe in the decision on sanctions expires. The duties apply when-ever there is a violation of peremptory norms. Sanctions are a policy option for individual UN Member States or the UN Security Council. The duties are a legal obligation on the collection of States, regardless of the position of the UN Security Council. The logic is clear: if we have duties of non- recognition and non- assistance to protect the most fundamental norms of international law then we cannot condition their application on UN Security Council action. This is because the UN Security Council is governed by the short- term domes-tic and political interests of its Member States, with five of them holding a veto. This is radically opposite to the raison d’être of the duties of non- recognition and non- assistance. In the EU guidelines on the implementation and evalua-tion of restrictive measures (sanctions) in the framework of the EU Common Foreign and Security Policy, the EU recognises that sanctions are intended to “bring about a change in policy or activity by the target country, part of coun-try, government, entities or individuals, in line with the objectives set out in the

cfsp Council Decision”.31 This again, shows why sanctions are different from the duties with respect to illegal settlements. The objective is not a change of policy in one or the other country, the objective is to ensure that there will be no trade, ever, with any illegal settlements in any occupied territory.

Finally, and most importantly, sanctions constitute a positive obligation,

whereas the duties consist of negative obligations. The duties mandate States not to recognise via diplomatic relations, treaties, economic relations related to the violation, and so forth. The duties do not require positive action on

behalf of the States.32 In this regard, the duties can be considered as duties of



30 Dissenting Opinion of Judge Weeramantry in icj, Case Concerning East Timor (Portugal

v Australia) (Judgement) icj Reports 90 (1995); Martin Dawidowicz, ‘The Obligation of

Non- Recognition of an Unlawful Situation’ in James Crawford, Alain Pellet and Simon

Olleson (eds), The Law of International Responsibility (oup 2010) 677– 686.

31 General Secretariat of the Council, Guidelines on Implementation and Evaluation of

Restrictive Measures (Sanctions) in the Framework of the EU Common Foreign and Security

Policy, 5664/ 18, 4 May 2018, page 5.

32 Andrea Gattini, ‘A Return Ticket to ‘Communitarisme’, Please’ 13(5) The European Journal

of International Law (2002) 1191.

230 Devers and Moerenhout

active abstention.33 Not trading with illegal settlements is one such negative obligation. States should withhold from trading with settlements, as this type of trade should not have existed in the first place and represents, in a consis-tent reading of international public law, an error in international economic relations. The fact that there is trade is an issue of non- compliance.



4 Settlement Trade and EU Law

The European Union “is bound […], when exercising its powers, to observe

international law in its entirety”.34 In that context, there is little doubt that the EU must not recognise or assist situations arising from serious breaches of

international law.35 Besides, respecting international law and human rights,

including international humanitarian law,36 is also one of the EU’s core objec-tives on the international scene, pursuant to Articles 3(5) and 21 of the Treaty on the European Union. However, despite the EU’s stated commitments to international law, the approach of the European Commission toward settle-

ment trade remains incoherent and deceptive (see Section 4.1). Given the massive scale of the problem, the adoption of a binding legal act is the only way forward to ensure the effective enforcement of EU’s international obli-gations and objectives. Analysed through the lens of EU law, the regulation of settlement trade confirms that banning it is a trade measure in favour of occupied peoples and not an economic sanction against Occupying States

(Section 4.2).



33 Stefan Talmon, ‘The Duty Not to “Recognize as Lawful” a Situation Created by the Illegal

Use of Force or Other Serious Breaches of a Jus Cogens Obligation: An Obligation with-

out Real Substance?’ in Christian Tomuschat and Jean- Marc Thouvenin (eds), The

Fundamental Rules of the International Legal Order (Brill Publishers 2006) 99– 125.

34 ecj, Western Sahara Campaign UK, c- 266/ 16, 27 February 2018, para 47.

35 ecj, Organisation Juive Européenne, Vignoble Psagot Ltd v Ministre de l’Economie et des

Finances, c- 363/ 18, 12 November 2019, para 48. It refers explicitly to para 120 of the

2004 icj advisory opinion on the Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the

Occupied Palestinian Territory.

human rights and fundamental freedoms and the rule of law. This includes the goal of

4.1 The Deceptive Approach of the European Commission toward

Settlement Trade: The Examples of Occupied Palestine and

Before assessing the benefit of banning settlement trade in favour of the occu-pied population, this section will present case studies on the EU’s response to trade with occupied territories of Palestine and Western Sahara.

4.1.1 Data on Settlement Trade with the European Block Although the European Commission claims to have no “detailed statistics on

goods imported from Israeli settlements to the EU”,37 figures transmitted to the World Bank in 2012 indicate that exports of illegal settlement goods from

occupied Palestine to the EU had reached the value of 300 million US dollars.38 In 2015, the European External Action Service had also estimated that settle-

ment trade amounted to 300 million euros approximately.39 By comparison, in 2020, the amount of Palestinian exports under the 1997 EU- plo Association

Agreement40 did not exceed 26 million euros.41 Consequently, taking the num-bers of settlers illegally based in occupied Palestine, “in per capita terms, the

EU imports at least 100 times more per settler than per Palestinian”.42 Even

though there is not an official list of products on sale in the EU,43 it appears

that settlement goods exported to the EU ranges from industrial products44 to

4.1.2 The “Cosmetic” Measures of the European Commission on

Settlement Trade

As regards Western Sahara, EU political institutions never bothered to pre-serve appearances. In 1995, after the conclusion of the 1991 Settlement Plan and the cease- fire between Morocco and the polisario Front, the European Commission began registering three Moroccan authorised establishments in

Dakhla and El Aaiún, allowing them to export fishery products to the EU.58

Their number increases from time to time.59 Today, 172 Moroccan authorised

establishments are currently based in occupied Western Sahara.60 Besides, in 2016, the European Commission acknowledged that all agricultural and fishery products originating in occupied Western Sahara benefitted de facto from pref-erential tariff treatment under the 2000 EU- Morocco Association Agreement

since its entry into force.61 This practice was so entrenched that Eurostat

However, at a closer look, they fall severely short of meeting EU’s obligations of non- recognition and non- assistance. with Israel in 2004,77 requiring Israeli authorities to ensure that the place of provenance is indicated on the export documents of all goods placed under their control, whether they originate in Israel or in the illegal settlements. This ensures that European customs authorities can distinguish between settlement products and goods originating in Israel by using a “list of non- eligible loca-

tions” provided to them.78 In short, the European Commission has first had to recognise Israeli settlements under the 2004 technical arrangement to be able to exclude them from the scope of application of the Association Agreement,

even though it claims that the EU does not recognise Israeli settlements.79

The labelling of Israeli settlement products is flawed and limited. First,

except for cosmetics, labelling does not apply to non- food/ industrial goods.80 Second, labelling transfers the onus from the European Commission to the EU

Member States Market Surveillance Authorities,81 the latter being unable to

ensure effective implementation.82 Lastly, the labelling of settlement products only concerns their conformity with EU marketing standards but avoids the question of their legality. Respect for peremptory norms of international law is reduced to a matter of personal choice of EU consumers who may decide whether and to what extent the fundamental rights of a people living under foreign occupation deserve protection. That is clearly not how international law is intended to work.

for the purpose of EU law, their very utility could be questioned.83 In any event,

4.2 Prohibiting Settlement Trade under EU Law: A Trade Measure in

Favour of Occupied Peoples and Not an Economic Sanction against

Occupying States

The European Commission has declared several times that the EU does not

consider taking sanctions against Israel in relation to settlements85 because it considers that its policy is best served by “diplomacy” and “constant engage-

ment at all levels with Israeli authorities”.86 The EC even excluded “to propose

import controls on products emanating from the West Bank”.87 Is it correct, however, to place sanctions and import controls on the same footing? Would a ban of settlement products constitute a trade measure or an economic sanc-tion? In the first scenario, the Common Commercial Policy could constitute the adequate legal basis under Article 207 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union. Otherwise, the measure would have to be adopted under the framework of the Common Security Foreign Policy. In terms of decision- making process, the ccp and the cfsp present significant differ-ences. In the case of the ccp, the EC proposes the adoption of legal acts to the

Council of the EU and the European Parliament acting as co- legislators.88 In

the Council, the vote only requires a qualified majority of Member States.89 To the contrary, the adoption of cfsp measures is decided by the Council of

238 Devers and Moerenhout

the EU alone, on its own motion, through unanimity.90 If the cfsp decision bears a commercial component, the High Representative and the European Commission intervene after its adoption to jointly propose to the Council the adoption of the EU implementing regulation based on 215 tfeu. The European

Parliament is only informed of the adoption of the measure.91

From an international law perspective, the solution seems straightforward.

First, non- recognition and non- assistance differ from UN or any other sanc-

tions (see Section 2 above).92 Since international sanctions and countermea-

sures are implemented by the Council of the EU via cfsp measures,93 the obligation to not trade with settlements should – by subtraction – be enforced though the ccp. Second, the very subject matter of the obligation not to trade with settlements deriving from non- recognition and non- assistance is trade, making settlement trade a ccp issue per se. Of course, the legal order of the EU is autonomous from international law and has its own approach that must be taken as a starting point, but the content of that legal order does remain bound by international law, particularly by jus cogens which sits at the apex of the pyramid of legal hierarchy. In this regard, even though the type of interna-tional act or obligation to implement may play a role, the decisive elements to ascertain the legal basis of a given EU measure is the “aim and content of that

measure”94 and its “legal framework”.95 In the case of a measure prohibiting settlement trade, part of the challenge lies in the potential overlap between

the fields and objectives of the csfp and the ccp.96

91 tfeu, Article 215.

96 This question is all the more fundamental that Article 40 of the teu contains a mutual

non- affectation clause which is designed to ensure the implementation of the cfsp does

not impinge upon the application of the procedures and the extent of the powers of EU

institutions applicable in the fields of non- cfsp EU external policies and vice- versa. The

objective is to avoid for instance that EU Member States use cfsp as a legal basis to adopt Sanctions in Regulating Trade with Illegal Settlements 239

Regarding the ccp, it is settled caselaw that “an EU act falls within that pol-

icy if it relates specifically to such trade in that it is essentially intended to promote, facilitate or govern such trade and has direct and immediate effects

on it”.97 By comparison, following a reading of Article 23 of the Treaty of the European Union, in conjunction with Article 21 of the teu, csfp objectives

have a “wide scope”.98 They include for instance, “the preservation of peace

and the strengthening of international security”99 and the objectives of consol-idating and supporting “human rights and the principles of international law”,

which are amongst those “traditionally”100 assigned to the cfsp. Given the fun-damental character of norms giving rise to Third State responsibility, one may argue that not trading with settlements pursues a cfsp objective. However, objectives laid down in Article 21 of the teu are horizontal and transversal,

and apply to all EU policies,101 especially to the ccp.102 In fact, it is easy to find trade regulations based on Article 207 of the tfeu that nonetheless pursues

cfsp objectives.103

It also happens often that measures are based on the cfsp first, but that the

legal basis is changed to the ccp as the perception of the issue evolves.104 For instance, a cfsp measure targeting a specific country can be used as a direct

a trade measure disguised as a sanction to preclude the European Parliament for being

involved in the decision- making process. Serving as a bridge between cfsp objectives and

EU actions involving economic measures, Article 215 tfeu is the counterpart under the

tfeu of the mutual non- affectation clause contained in Article 40 teu. This repartition

of labour explains why pesc decisions with an economic component always come along

implementing regulations based on Article 215 tfeu.

240 Devers and Moerenhout

source of inspiration for a general measure under the ccp.105 As for the case-law, the ecj ruled in the past that domestic measures pursuing national foreign and security policies of EU Member States can fall within the scope of the ccp

since they affect the trade relations of the EU,106 which is an exclusive compe-

tence.107 Regarding the articulation between the ccp and the csfp as an EU common policy, the caselaw is still under construction. However, the court has already ruled that the test of the predominant legal basis applies to the rela-

tions between the csfp and the ccp.108 Accordingly, if an EU legal act mainly pursues ccp objectives but contains a csfp component, it can be based solely on Article 207, tfeu if the ccp objective is predominant whereas the cfsp

component is incidental.109 It follows that whether an EU measure regulating settlement trade must be based on the cfsp or the ccp is a question of propor-tion and modalities, rather than a matter of principle. In this respect, several elements indicate that the ccp would constitute the adequate legal basis.

First, even though the ccp is mainly aimed at fostering trade relations

with third countries, it can be used as a legal basis for measures restricting or

prohibiting international trade.110 Second, there already exists EU legislation

242 Devers and Moerenhout

way to other Member State initiatives to regulate trade with illegal settlements in Finland and Belgium. Finally, according to the caselaw of the ecj, on the one hand, occupied territories have a separate and distinct status from that

of Occupying States.116 And, on the other hand, occupied peoples are Third

Parties to EU- relations with occupiers.117

Due to this distinction, a measure prohibiting settlement trade from occu-

pied territories could not be constructed as targeting Occupying Powers, since the notion of “country” within the meaning of Article 215, tfeu – which is the legal basis to implement the economic part of cfsp decisions of the Council – cannot be interpreted, as a matter of EU law, as including the sovereign terri-tory of an Occupying State and the territory it occupies. In the same manner, a product originating in an occupied territory does not originate from the ter-ritory of the Occupying State. Therefore, a ban on settlement goods does not affect goods produced by the Occupying State in its own sovereign territory since settlement goods do not originate in its sovereign territory but in the ter-ritory that it occupies. Besides, if a ban on settlement products may have some adverse effects on occupiers as any other measures implementing EU’s obliga-

tions of non- recognition and non- assistance,118 the degree of inconveniency

In this sense, the EU has already adopted measures based on the ccp having the effect of prohi-bitions in trade towards certain cases of prolonged occupation. For instance, the European Council adopted in 2004 a regulation based on the ccp estab-

It is a fact that Moldova’s steel industry is concentrated in Transnistria,120 a region occupied by the Russian Federation. When asked about the deliber-ate refusal of the Moldovan authorities to issue export licenses for products from Transnistria to European importers, the Commission emphasised the

virtues of this regulation.121 Later, in 2008, the Council adopted on the basis of the ccp, a regulation providing for autonomous trade preferences for the Republic of Moldova, de facto restricting exports from Transnistria unless com-panies settled in the occupied region registered with the Chisinau Chamber of

Commerce.122 However, those measures were never perceived or described as sanctions against Russia for its occupation of Transnistria.

Amongst the practice of the EU, at the time of writing, the only counter-

example is Crimea. In 2014, the Council adopted the Decision 2014/ 386/

cfsp,129 implemented by Regulation (EU) No 692/ 2014,130 which bans imports originating in Crimea/ Sevastopol into the EU, except for goods checked by

the Ukraine authorities and granted a certificate of origin.131 However, it is not conclusive for several reasons. First, this measure has been adopted “in response to the illegal annexation of Crimea and Sevastopol” but does not apply to the other occupied Ukrainian territories. Therefore, it constitutes a political reaction designed to send a strong signal to the Russian annexation of Crimea and Sevastopol rather than an economic measure aiming at regulating

settlement trade – a feature that is typical of sanctions.132 Second, the Council decision to ban imports from Crimea and Sevastopol was made after several

sanctions against Russia targeting individuals/ entities133 and specific sectors

of the Russian economy.134 Considering this legal framework, the decision

can be seen as one measure in a package- deal of sanctions justifying a global approach to the legal basis as a matter of consistency and efficiency. To the contrary, it would have been incoherent to adopt targeted sanctions on Russia but to continue to accept EU imports from Crimea and Sevastopol at the same time. Therefore, the choice of the csfp appears to be circumstantial and corre-sponds to a specific moment in time. It does not constitute a precedent inval-idating the analysis that settlement trade can be regulated on the basis of the ccp. In any event, if the ban on imports from Crimea and Sevastopol qualifies as a sanction, it is because it singles out Russia as an Occupying State, amongst other occupiers. This could be considered as problematic, because using it as a sanction allows the EU to pick and choose when to withhold trade from ille-gal settlements, rather than having a general rule to prohibit it, regardless of whether the occupying force is an ally of the EU or not. A permanent and gen-eral regulation applicable to all situations of military occupation – current and future – regardless the occupier, would constitute a trade measure and not an economic sanction.

4.3 The European Commission’s Decision to Register the European

Citizens’ Initiative on Settlement Trade: The Way forward for the

Adoption of a Trade Measure

In July 2019, in order to challenge the EU’s inaction, a group of EU citizens submitted to the European Commission a European Citizens’ Initiative asking

for a ban on settlement goods based on the ccp.135 The European Citizens’ Initiative is a novelty introduced by the Lisbon Treaty in 2009. Following from Article 11(4), teu, this mechanism allows for EU Citizens to invite the European Commission, “to submit any appropriate proposal on matters where citizens consider that a legal act of the Union is required for the purpose of implementing the Treaties”. The European Citizens’ Initiative bears a funda-mental character under EU law. Whereas democracy is one of the founding



European Union 2014, L 229, page 13; Council Regulation (EU) No 833/ 2014 of 31 July 2014

Concerning Restrictive Measures In View of Russia’s Actions Destabilising the Situation in

Ukraine, Official Journal of the European Union, 2014, L 229, page 1.

135 The specific petition text was: “The Commission, as Guardian of the Treaties, has to

ensure consistency of Union’s policy and compliance with fundamental rights and inter-

national law in all areas of EU law, including ccp. It must propose legal acts to prevent EU

legal entities from both importing products originating in illegal settlements in occupied

territories and exporting to such territories, in order to preserve the integrity of the inter-

nal market and to not aid or assist the maintenance of such unlawful situations”.

Sanctions in Regulating Trade with Illegal Settlements 247

values of the EU,136 the eci “concern[s] the right of citizens to participate in

the democratic life of the [EU]”137 by conferring on them “a right comparable

to that held […] by the Parliament and the Council”138 to request the European Commission to submit any appropriate proposal for a legal act.

In practical terms, EU citizens can use the eci to suggest concrete legal

changes in any field where the European Commission has the power to pro-pose new legislation, for instance in areas including the environment, agri-culture, or international trade. The eci must be launched by at least seven

EU citizens from seven different Member States.139 Once the Initiative has been registered, it must gather the support of at least one million EU citizens

with a minimum threshold reached in a least seven EU countries.140 If so, the European Commission is obliged to consider in- depth the measure pro-posed by the eci. As in case of requests by the Parliament and the Council, the European Commission remains free to decide whether or not to propose a

new legal act.141 It must though justify its decision, which is subject to judicial

review.142

Regarding the registration of the eci, the European Commission “shall reg-

ister the initiative if none of the parts of the initiative manifestly falls outside the framework of the Commission’s powers to submit a proposal for a legal

act of the Union for the purpose of implementing the Treaties”.143 Here the

notion of “powers” is two- folded.144 On the one hand, the subject- matter of the measure proposed by the eci must fall within the competences of the EU. On the other hand, it must concern a domain where the European Commission

144 General Court of the European Union, Balázs- Árpád Izsák and Attila Dabis v Commission,

t- 529/ 13, 10 May 2016, para 59.

248 Devers and Moerenhout

holds the power of initiative to propose a legal act. At the stage of the reg-istration, the European Commission has no margin of appreciation at all. It

must register the Initiative “to the extent”145 that it does not manifestly fall outside its competences whereas it has no general and inherent power but

is only “entitled”146 by EU law to refuse registration. Besides, the question as to whether the measure proposed by the eci falls within the framework of the Commission’s powers, is “essentially a question of the interpretation and

application of the relevant provisions of the Treaties”.147 It means that it is not necessary to ascertain that proof has been provided of all the factual elements relied on by the organisers or that the reasoning behind the Initiative is ade-quate. The European Commission must confine itself to examining whether, “from an objective point of view”, the proposed measure by the eci “envisaged

in the abstract” could be adopted on the basis of the treaties.148 Therefore, in

a similar fashion to principles applicable to the choice of legal basis,149 the Commission must only take into consideration “the subject matter and objec-

tives of the proposed eci”150 to decide on its registration.

In the case of the eci proposing a ban on settlement goods, the European

Commission considered that the proposed measure was a sanction, even if it had previously argued that the draft Irish bill on the same subject was

impeding on its exclusive competence.151 Accordingly, since it has no compe-tence to propose the adoption of cfsp decisions to the Council of the EU, it found that the measure proposed by the eci fell outside the framework of its powers and refused to register it. In reaction, the organisers of the Initiative

Sanctions in Regulating Trade with Illegal Settlements 249

successfully challenged the refusal of the European Commission before the European Court of Justice. On 12 May 2021, the EU General Court found that the European Commission grossly failed its obligation to state reasons for its

refusal152 because it neither explained why the ccp could not be used as a legal

basis,153 nor why the proposed measure “had to be categorized necessarily and

solely”154 as a sanction.

After the ruling of the court, due to the obligation of EU institutions to

implement judgements155 with regards to the grounds of annulment,156 the European Commission could no longer escape considering the merits of the

ccp as a legal basis for banning settlement products. On 8 September 2021,157 as expected, it reassessed its refusal and reversed its initial decision. Referring to the above- mentioned caselaw of the ecj, the European Commission found that a measure requiring “import and export prohibitions or restrictions vis- à- vis territories that are occupied under international law” would have a “spe-

cific link with international trade”.158 Since the measure proposed by the eci was “intended to govern in a general manner EU international trade, without targeting any specific country or territory”, it “would fall within the scope

of Article 207, tfeu even if it pursues foreign policy objectives”.159 In other words, the European Commission confirmed that the measure proposed by the eci mainly pursues a ccp objective whereas its cfsp component remains incidental at best. In line with the distinction between Third State responsi-bility and sanctions under international law, prohibiting settlement trade is a trade measure under EU law and not a sanction.

250 Devers and Moerenhout

As emphasised by the ecj, “the particular added value of the eci mecha-

nism resides not in certainty of outcome, but in the possibilities and oppor-tunities that it creates for [EU] citizens to initiate debate on policy within the EU institutions without having to wait for the commencement of a legislative

procedure”.160 Future will tell if the eci on settlement trade receives support from at least millions of EU citizens across Europe. In any case, the registration of the Initiative is already an important achievement given the implications of the recognition of settlement trade as a trade issue.

By accepting to register the Initiative, the Commission has acknowledged

that it has the competence to act. This acknowledgement raises serious ques-tions about the EU’s lack of reaction. It means that for all these past years the Commission has limited itself to ineffective measures where it could have proposed that the Council of the EU and the Parliament adopt a meaning-ful regulation on settlement trade. In light of the EU’s obligations of non- recognition and non- assistance under international law, the powers of the European Commission over settlement trade even imply that it has the duty to act against settlement products.

Moreover, csfp measures require unanimity amongst EU Member States

within the Council. By comparison, ccp measures are adopted according to the “qualified majority” rule, meaning that 55 percent of EU countries must vote in favour – in other words 15 out of 27 – representing at least 65 percent

of the total EU population. Therefore, absence of unanimity161 on settlement trade is no longer a valid excuse for not acting.

As a last note, the European Parliament co- legislates with the Council on

matters relating to the ccp. As the expectations grow amongst the public opin-

ion,162 the acknowledgement of settlement trade as a trade issue will allow

para 70.

161 See by way of comparison, European Commission, Answer to Parliamentary Question

162 Advocate General Hogan, opinion delivered on 13 June 2019, Organisation Juive
