“[A] dherence to the requirements of international law is regarded by many — and not

just by a limited cadre of experts specialising in the field of international law and diplo-

macy — as playing a vital role in the maintenance of international peace and security and

as a harbinger of justice in an otherwise unjust world. This is perhaps especially true in

the context of the citizens of the Union who, even within the lifetime of some, witnessed

the destructive impact of brute force in an era where some countries had come to believe Sanctions in Regulating Trade with Illegal Settlements 251

for greater scrutiny by the European Parliament. By unlocking Article 207 as a legal basis, the eci on settlement trade has helped to pave the way for the adoption – better sooner than later hopefully – of a regulation which satisfies the EU’s obligations of non- recognition and non- assistance.



5 Conclusion

It is clear under international law that the act of trading with illegal settlements recognises and assists breaches of peremptory norms of international law. Withholding trade with illegal settlements is therefore a customary and self- executing obligation under the duties of non- recognition and non- assistance. These are vastly different from sanctions, which are intended to support for-eign policy interests and are limited in duration and disappear once a conflict is resolved, political determination dwindles, or the timeframe in the decision on sanctions expires. Rather, not trading with illegal settlements is a rule that follows from the quintessential objective of international public law to safe-guard international peace and stability.

In the EU too, as a result of judicial proceedings around a European

Citizens Initiative, the European Commission has now recognised that hav-ing a general rule to stop trade with illegal settlements once and for all, is not a sanction, but a trade measure. This law is needed to implement duties of non- recognition and non- assistance and given the exclusive competence of the European Commission over trade, it is the European Commission that must propose the law. However, where EU law and jurisprudence develops in the right direction, the politics of the European Commission are more incon-sistent than ever before, regardless of its explicit acknowledgment that it has the competence to enact trade measures that are intended to protect interna-tional law.

If the European Commission continues not to act and therefore violate its

obligations under international law, even if it has clarified that it has the man-date to do so, then Member States can, and should, act unilaterally. They can and should do this to comply with their own obligations of non- recognition and non- assistance under international law. Within EU law, Member States can do so by relying on the Common Rules on Imports, which permit Member



that international law was simply an empty promise to the oppressed and vulnerable of

the world and that it could be disregarded with impunity”.

States to implement import prohibitions for reasons of public policy and pub-

lic morality.163

chapter 11


Palestine, Israel, and the International

Criminal Court

Nada Kiswanson



The Prosecutor of the International Criminal Court announced the initiation

of a long- awaited investigation in the Situation in the State of Palestine1 on 3

March 2021.2 This announcement followed a decision by Pre- Trial Chamber i, finding that the icc has territorial jurisdiction over the Palestinian territory occupied in 1967, namely the West Bank, including East Jerusalem, and the

Gaza Strip.3 Because the icc can only exercise complementary jurisdiction, the opening of an investigation has put the spotlight on the potential for account-ability through domestic mechanisms. There are two Israeli domestic avenues theoretically available to Palestinians in the opt that seek to obtain justice or bring violations to an end: the Israeli military justice system and the Israeli High Court of Justice. This chapter will examine these mechanisms, unearth the level of impunity afforded Israelis that commit crimes against Palestinians, and highlight the merits of an icc investigation in the Situation in Palestine as the only available avenue for criminal accountability.

This chapter is divided into three distinct but connected parts. The first part

will present the developments in the Situation in Palestine at the icc. The sec-ond part will introduce the Rome Statute’s complementarity framework. It will then analyse the Israeli High Court of Justice’s approach to international law and discuss the structural, substantive, and procedural characteristics of the Israeli military justice system. The third and final part will present the icc as the only international judicial institution mandated to investigate, prosecute, and punish Israelis responsible for committing war crimes and crimes against

Investigation of the Situation in Palestine, 3 March 2021.

humanity on the Palestinian territory. It will also provide an overview of Rome Statute crimes relevant to Israel’s practices and policies in Palestine.



1 Developments in the Situation in Palestine at the icc

The International Criminal Court was not established to absolve States of their primary duty to investigate, prosecute, and punish individuals responsible for criminal conduct. The complementarity principle on which the Rome Statute rests, expects of States that they exercise primary jurisdiction over crimes com-mitted by their nationals, or over their territory. The court will only exercise jurisdiction when a State is unable or unwilling to do so, with the aim of end-

ing impunity.4 Crucially, the icc’s complementary jurisdiction is restricted to

Rome Statute crimes that are committed after 1 July 20025 on the territory of

a State Party,6 or of a State accepting the jurisdiction of the court by declara-

tion.7 The court may exercise jurisdiction following a referral by a State Party or

the UN Security Council, or upon the initiative of the icc Prosecutor.8

The State of Palestine submitted a declaration accepting the jurisdiction of

the icc pursuant to Article 12(3) of the Rome Statute on 1 January 2015 “for the purpose of identifying, prosecuting and judging authors and accomplices within the jurisdiction of the Court committed in the occupied Palestinian ter-

ritory, including East Jerusalem, since June 13, 2014”.9 The following day, the State of Palestine deposited an instrument of accession to the Rome Statute with the UN Secretary- General in his function as depository of multilateral

treaties.10 On 16 January 2015, the icc Prosecutor opened a preliminary exam-

ination into the Situation in Palestine11 and the Rome Statute entered into force



4 The preamble of the Rome Statute recalls that the icc is “determined to put an end to

impunity” and that “it is the duty of every State to exercise its criminal jurisdiction over

those responsible for international crimes”. It also emphasises that the icc “shall be com-

plementary to national jurisdictions”. See also Rome Statute, Articles 1 and 17.

to Articles 13(a) and 14 of the Rome Statute.13

The icc Prosecutor completed the preliminary examination after five

territorial jurisdiction does extend, as the Prosecutor had asserted, to the ter-ritories occupied by Israel since 1967, consisting of the West Bank, including

East Jerusalem, and the Gaza Strip.17 A month after the Chamber’s decision, the icc Prosecutor announced that she had opened an investigation into the

Situation in Palestine.18

The icc Prosecutor is under an obligation to notify all States Parties, as

well as other States that would normally exercise jurisdiction over the crimes

concerned, of the initiation of an investigation.19 Within one month of such notification, a State may inform the court that it is investigating the criminal

conduct in question and request the Prosecutor to defer to its investigation.20 Importantly, the Rome Statute only accommodates requests for deferral of investigations of persons, and not requests for deferral of the entire situa-

tion.21 There is no information suggesting that either the State of Palestine or the State of Israel have made deferral requests of persons under Article 18(2).

In the absence of Article 18 deferral requests and in light of the Prosecutor’s

conclusions and the Pre- Trial Chamber ruling, there are no substantive or pro-cedural reasons why the icc Prosecutor should not investigate the Situation in Palestine and pursue all persons criminally responsible for the commission of Rome Statute crimes in the opt. All relevant Rome Statute criteria that safeguard the sovereignty and primary jurisdiction of States (see for example Articles 12, 13, 17, 18, and 53) are satisfied.



2 Israel’s Unwillingness and Inability to Investigate and Prosecute

Israelis

Israel is not a party to the Rome Statute and the court cannot therefore investi-gate and prosecute crimes committed on Israel’s territory unless the UN Security Council uses its powers to refer the situation in Israel to the Prosecutor under Article 13(b). Individual Israelis can however be investigated, prosecuted, and

Palestine, Israel, and the International Criminal Court 257

punished at the icc for Rome Statute crimes that they commit on the territory of the State of Palestine, which is an icc State Party, based on the icc’s juris-

dictional rules described above.22 That the location of the commission of the crime dictates the scope of the icc’s jurisdiction, rather than the nationality

of the perpetrator, flows from the notion of State sovereignty.23 As Kai Ambos recalls, a “State has plenary jurisdiction over persons, property and conduct occurring on its territory, subject only to obligations or the limitations imposed by international law” and no such rule prohibits a State to extend its jurisdic-

tion to the icc.24 In other words, the icc is respecting a State’s sovereignty and international law when it exercises jurisdiction over nationals of non- States Parties for Rome Statute crimes committed on the territory of a State Party. It

is certainly not “overreaching its jurisdiction”.25

The reality is that by virtue of the occupation, Israel has access to the

territory of another sovereign State. Israeli civilians and armed forces are either intermittently or permanently present in the territory of the State of Palestine and Israel controls its indigenous Palestinian population. Moreover, as is alleged in part 3 of this chapter, Israelis commit crimes in the opt. These crimes are not sporadic and rare but widespread and systematic, and they stem from policies and plans put in place by the State of Israel. The State of Israel consequentially finds itself confronted with the prospect of its nationals being brought before the icc.

The Rome Statute does not generate specific legal obligations for non-

State Parties such as Israel.26 Nonetheless, because the State of Israel is a “State which has jurisdiction” within the meaning of Article 17, and a “State which, taking into account the information available, would normally exer-cise jurisdiction over the crimes concerned” for the purpose of Article 18(1) and (2), Israel has before it three options for dealing with the crimes the icc

Prosecutor alleges its nationals have committed: genuinely investigate, and where appropriate prosecute and punish Israelis for international crimes; accept the possibility of Israelis being brought before the icc; or obstruct the

course of justice.27

Article 17(1) of the Rome Statute sets out three situations that will render a

case inadmissible at the icc under Article 19. The first is where the case is being

genuinely investigated or prosecuted by a State that has jurisdiction over it.28 The second is where the case has been investigated by a State that has jurisdic-tion but it has decided not to prosecute, unless that decision resulted from an

inability or unwillingness to genuinely prosecute.29 The third situation arises

when a person has already been tried for the same conduct elsewhere.30 The ne bis in idem rule does not bar the icc from exercising jurisdiction if the pro-

ceedings were for the purpose of shielding the person.31 Or otherwise, "were not conducted independently and impartially in accordance with the norms of due process and conducted in a manner which, in the circumstances, was

inconsistent with the intent to bring a person to justice.32 Indicia that serve to assist the icc in determining whether a State is unwilling include institutional deficiencies, procedural irregularities, longstanding knowledge of crimes with-out action, number of investigations opened, the investigation of persons up the hierarchy, the evidence collected, and rapport between suspected persons

and the authorities when the authorities are not independent.33 The lack of

penal legislation and procedures can also indicate inability.34 The court will also consider whether there has been unjustified delay which in the circum-

stances is inconsistent with an intent to bring a person to justice.35

There are two Israeli domestic avenues that Palestinians in the opt can

attempt to access in order to seek remedy. These are the Israeli military justice system and Israel’s Supreme Court sitting as the Israeli High Court of Justice. The hcj only exercises a judicial review function. It cannot itself investigate, prosecute, or punish Israelis within the meaning of Articles 17 and 19 of the

Rome Statute.36 An assessment of the hcj’s practice is relevant only in that it gauges the robustness of the Israeli judiciary vis- à- vis the policies and conduct of the State of Israel, including its military arm in the opt, and the potential for Palestinians to broadly enjoy and exercise their fundamental rights to truth, justice, and reparation. Such an assessment can also shed light on the involve-ment of the government and judiciary in acts by Israeli citizens that could amount to Rome Statute crimes.

The next section will demonstrate that: (a) the Israeli hcj has relied on a

matrix of incoherent dissections and distortions of international law and the concepts of non- enforceability and non- justiciability, to refrain from restrain-ing Israel and Israelis, (b) there are valid reservations to be made as to the hcj’s independence and impartiality when called upon to order a remedy for harm that Israelis or the State of Israel cause Palestinians, (c) Israel’s Military Advocate General is not an independent authority and it cannot be expected to open and carry out investigations into allegations of misconduct by Israeli soldiers in accordance with international standards, (d) the Israeli military jus-tice system does not investigate or prosecute the Israelis most responsible for Rome Statute crimes, (e) the Israeli military justice system does not investigate all incidents where the use of force resulted in the deprivation of Palestinian life, (f) preliminary examinations and investigations by the military justice sys-tem span several years and are unduly delayed, (g) domestic investigations are inadequate, and (h) Israeli legislation does not prohibit the transfer of Israeli civilians into the occupied territory and the hcj effectively promotes it.

2.1 Israel’s Supreme Court

Israel’s Supreme Court is a court of appeal of decisions of lower courts and

also a High Court of Justice. The hcj is a court of first and last instance,37 com-petent to deal with “matters in which it sees a need to grant a remedy for the sake of justice and which are not within the jurisdiction of another court or

tribunal”.38 The hcj, which is made up of Israeli citizens and sits as the highest

court of the Occupying Power,39 applies Israeli law and international obliga-tions that it considers binding upon Israel.



36 See Israel, Basic Law: The Judiciary (8 March 1984), Article 15.

37 David Kretzmer and Yaël Ronen, The Occupation of Justice, The Supreme Court of Israel

and the Occupied Territories (oup 2021) 14– 15.

38 Israel, Basic Law: The Judiciary (8 March 1984), Article 15(c).

39 Noam Sohlberg, who serves as an Israeli Supreme Court judge, is a known settler. He resides

inside the opt. See for example Uri Misgav, ‘The Israeli Justice Who Violates International

Law Every Time He Comes Home’, Haaretz, 10 June 2016; ‘Beinisch: I Regret the Sohlberg 260 Kiswanson

Vast numbers of petitions have been brought before the hcj in an attempt to

have it judicially review the legality of Israel’s practices and policies under the

applicable international law over the course of the occupation.40 Palestinians are not permitted to participate in the election of the members of the Israeli legislature or executive and they have no influence over the enactment of laws that the Supreme Court interprets and rules upon.

Like other domestic courts, Israel’s Supreme Court is created by and forms

an integral part of the State in which it sits. And like other domestic courts, Israel’s Supreme Court derives legitimacy and authority from laws drafted and enacted by elected politicians. These laws reflect the interests, ambitions, and outlooks of these politicians and their constituents. In this case, these are

Israeli politicians and Israeli citizens.41

The hcj’s caselaw on some of the most charged issues that have been

brought before it demonstrates that the hcj is attentive to, and indeed influ-enced by, the Israeli environment in which it operates. Its decisions refer to its position in and connection with the State of Israel, as well as its concern for the

Israeli society that it serves.42

Justice, exercises judicial review over the legality of the discretion exercised by the mil-

itary commander. [...] In exercising this judicial review, we do not appoint ourselves as

experts in security matters. We do not replace the security considerations of the military

commander with our own security considerations. We do not adopt any position with

41 In the view of Martin Shapiro and Alec Stone Sweet, domestic courts are “first and fore-

and Judicialization (oup 2002) 3.

42 See for example hcj 390/ 79, Izat Muhamed Mustafa Dweikat v The Government of Israel

et al. (19 October 1979). The judgement reads: “with regards to the justiciability of this

matter, that the issue of the settlements was ‘disputed among the government of Israel

and other governments and that it may come up in the crucial international negotiations

in which the government of Israel is involved’. In the interim, the intensity of the contro-

versy has not subsided; it has rather intensified among the Israeli public internally, and

this time it was also reflected in the very resolution to establish a civilian community in

Elon Moreh, which was adopted by a majority of the government of Israel. This is thus

not, when sitting to pronounce judgement, involve our personal views as citizens of a

country. Yet, there is still grave concern that the court would appear to be abandoning

its proper place and descending into the arena of public debate and that its ruling will

be applauded by some of the public and utterly, vehemently rejected by others”. See also Palestine, Israel, and the International Criminal Court 261

Eyal Benvenisti has identified three main approaches to international law

that are relied upon by national courts to accommodate national interests. The first approach is to narrowly interpret domestic legislation that imports inter-national law to limit courts’ opportunities to intervene. The second is to inter-pret the law so as not to upset the government’s interests. The third approach is to avoid deciding on the legal issue by invoking various legal doctrines such

as non- enforceability and non- justiciability.43 Israel’s Supreme Court, sitting as the High Court of Justice, is no exception. As will become evident below, all three of Benvenisti’s identified approaches have been employed by Israel’s hcj when ruling on petitions related to Israel’s practices and policies in the opt. The Supreme Court’s reliance on these approaches, its composition and connection to Israel, its role and position in Israel, and the ability of Israel to amend the laws and procedures that empower and maintain it, all raise serious concerns about the hcj’s independence and impartiality when it deals with issues relating to Palestinians living under Israel’s occupation. The UN Special Committee to Investigate Israeli Practices Affecting the Human Rights of the Palestinian People and Other Arabs of the Occupied Territories expressed in 2016 its fear that the “separation of powers between the judiciary and execu-tive branches in Israel is increasingly narrowing, potentially affecting the inde-

pendence of the judiciary and the decisions of the courts in Israel”.44



Concurring Opinion of Judge Goldberg in the Bargil case. Judge Goldberg writes that

the “petitioners have the right to place a ‘legal mine’ on the Court’s threshold, but the

Court does not have to step on a mine that may destroy its foundations, which are the

public trust in it”. hcj 4481/ 91, Bargil et al. v Government of Israel et al. (25 August 1993),

Concurring Opinion by Eliezer Goldberg. See also hcj 5100/ 94, Public Committee against

Torture v Israel (6 September 1999). Here the hcj said, that, “[d] eciding these applica-

tions weighed heavy on this Court. True, from the legal perspective, the road before us is

smooth. We are, however, part of Israeli society. Its problems are known to us and we live

its history. We are not isolated in an ivory tower. We live the life of this country. We are

aware of the harsh reality of terrorism in which we are, at times, immersed. The appre-

hension that our decision will hamper the ability to properly deal with terrorists and ter-

rorism, disturbs us. We are, however, judges. We demand that others act according to the

law. This is equally the standard that we set for ourselves”.

43 Eyal Benvenisti, ‘Judicial Misgivings Regarding the Application of International Law: An

Analysis of Attitudes of National Courts’, 4(2) European Journal of International Law

(1993) 159, page 161.

44 unhrc, Report of the Special Committee to Investigate Israeli Practices Affecting the

Human Rights of the Palestinian People and Other Arabs of the Occupied Territories, a/ 71/

352, 23 August 2016, para 64.

2.1.1 Non- enforceability and Non- justiciability According to Israel’s High Court of Justice, international law must be incorpo-rated into Israeli domestic law to be enforceable. The only exception is custom-

ary international law, which it considers can be enforced on Israel regardless.45 There are two main consequences to this approach; first, it allows the hcj the discretion to decide what rules are part of customary international law. Based on its assessment, it can exclude from its judicial review international law rules and norms that are otherwise relevant and applicable to Israel’s occupation. Second, it permits Israel to evade its international legal obligations by failing to incorporate the corresponding international law.

The full significance of this interpretation has come to the fore in petitions

submitted to the hcj on the legality of Israeli settlements and the transfer of Palestinians under Articles 49(1) and 49(6) of the Fourth Geneva Convention. Israel has not incorporated the Fourth Geneva Convention into Israeli law and the hcj does not accept that these prohibitions are part of customary interna-tional law, and therefore the HCJ has concluded that they are not enforceable

on Israel.46 This “non- enforceability construct” does not give due regard to the applicability of the Fourth Geneva Convention to a situation of belligerent occupation, as set out in Article 2, and the duties that it places on Israel as a High Contracting Party and Occupying Power.

Non- justiciability is another method used by the hcj to constrain its own

powers to adjudicate. That doctrine was put to use in the Bargil v Government of Israel case, in which the petitioners challenged the legality of settlements. hcj judge Meir Shamgar held in the judgement that:

[T] his petition should be denied, for it is defective in that it relates to ques-

tions of policy within the jurisdiction of other branches of a democratic

45 David Kretzmer and Yaël Ronen, The Occupation of Justice, The Supreme Court of Israel

and the Occupied Territories (oup 2021) 61.

46 In regard to Article 49(1) of the Fourth Geneva Convention, see hcj 698/ 80, Qawasmi

v Minister of Defence (4 December 1980), hcj 785/ 87 ‘Abed Al- ‘Afou v IDF Commander

in the West Bank (10 April 1988), and hcj 606/ 78 Ayoub v Minister of Defence (15 March

1979) cited in David Kretzmer and Yaël Ronen, The Occupation of Justice, The Supreme

Court of Israel and the Occupied Territories (oup 2021) 68. In regard to Article 49(6) of

the Fourth Geneva Convention, see hcj 390/ 79, Izat Muhamed Mustafa Dweikat v State

of Israel et al. (19 October 1979). In the opinion of Chief Justice Landau, the issue of set-

tlements is also non- justiciable. He said, “[i] t is better if matters that by their very nature

pertain to the international political plane are dealt with only on that plane” and there-

fore, “albeit the petitioner complaint is generally justiciable in court since property rights

of the individual are involved, this particular aspect of the matter should be regarded as

non- justiciable”.

Palestine, Israel, and the International Criminal Court 263

Government, and it raises an issue whose political elements are domi-

nant and clearly overshadow all its legal fragments. The overriding nature

of the issue raised in the petition is blatantly political. The unsuitability

of the questions raised in the petition for a judicial determination by the

High Court of Justice derives in the present case from a combination of

three aspects that make the issue unjusticiable: intervention in questions

of policy that are in the jurisdiction of another branch of Government,

the absence of a concrete dispute and the predominantly political nature

of the issue.47

By invoking the doctrines of non- justiciability and non- enforceability, the hcj has relieved itself of the task of striking down the formally sanctioned Israeli policy to promote Israeli Jewish settlement in the West Bank. In the process, it has given Israeli governments carte blanche to pursue its settlement policy fear-lessly. The cost of the High Court of Justice’s approach is borne by Palestinians, who have no other avenue to legally challenge Israel’s colonisation of Palestine at the domestic level.

2.1.2 Government- Accommodating Interpretations National courts can also accommodate government- interests and their constituencies by decontextualising, narrowly interpreting, convoluting or

applying select laws.48 Israel’s High Court of Justice masterfully applies these techniques. By way of example, the hcj has decided in several cases that there is no justification for Palestinians building houses without securing a permit from Israeli authorities – even if the planning policy makes it impossible for them to obtain it. The court accepts that the planning system instituted by Israel is appropriate and therefore concerns itself only with the minutia of

its structures, regulations and “remedies”.49 In stark contrast, the UN refers to

Israel’s planning regime as “discriminatory and restrictive”.50 The High Court

47 hcj 4481/ 91, Bargil et al. v Government of Israel et al. (25 August 1993).

48 See Eyal Benvenisti, ‘Judicial Misgivings Regarding the Application of International

denied in Area C. unhrc, Report of the Independent International Fact- Finding Mission to 264 Kiswanson

of Justice’s review of Israel’s planning policy disregards entirely the inter-

national law prohibition against the destruction of Palestinian property.51 Consequently, the hcj recently rejected a petition challenging the destruc-tion of property in the Khan Al Ahmar community in the occupied West Bank, located about two kilometres from the Kfar Adumim settlement. Khan Al Ahmar is home to 32 families and has a school built by an Italian organisa-tion that serves 150 children. Most Palestinians in Khan Al Ahmar are refugees expelled in 1948 from territory that became part of the State of Israel. Israel plans to build settlements on Khan Al Ahmar lands, that will eventually split the West Bank in half. On 24 May 2018, the hcj ruled that there was no jus-tification for it to intervene in the decision to execute the demolition orders. The hcj found support for its interpretation in its own caselaw on the dem-

olition of settlement outposts.52 Judge Isaac Amit explained the following in the majority decision:

I am not oblivious to the fact that the aforementioned judgments con-

cerned Jewish settlement on land privately owned by Palestinians,

whereas the matter herein concerns Palestinian settlement on privately

owned Palestinian land (even if the land has allegedly been expropri-

ated). Nevertheless, a final and conclusive judgment remains a final and

conclusive judgment and the military commander has an obligation to

take reasonable, equitable action to have it enforced, whether the illegal

construction was carried out by Israeli or Palestinian residents of Judea

and Samaria.53

Palestine, Israel, and the International Criminal Court 265

Due to international pressure and political interventions, Israel is still to exe-cute many of the outstanding demolition orders. The situation in Khan Al Ahmar drew the attention of former icc Prosecutor, Fatou Bensouda. She said that the Prosecution continues to “keep a close eye on the developments on the ground”, and warned that it would “not hesitate to take any appropriate action” within the confines of the Prosecution’s mandate under the Rome

Statute while respecting the principle of complementarity.54

The hcj decision in the Public Committee against Torture v Israel case offers

another example of the HCJ’s government accomodating interpretations. The hcj ruled in that case that while torture and other forms of cruel and inhuman treatment during interrogation are prohibited under domestic law, and that the law is consistent with international treaties in this regard, the necessity defence is available to Israeli agents who apply “physical interroga-tion methods” during “ticking bomb” situations. In the same breath, the hcj noted that Israel’s Attorney General “can establish guidelines regarding cir-cumstances in which investigators shall not stand trial, if they claim to have

acted from ‘necessity’”.55 As will be discussed further in Part 3, the hcj was thus “prepared to presume […] that if a [General Security Service] investiga-tor – who applied physical interrogation methods for the purpose of saving human life – is criminally indicted, the ‘necessity defense’ is likely to be open

to him in the appropriate circumstances”.56 What constitutes “ticking- bomb” situations and “physical interrogation methods” is decided upon by the Israeli agents involved in the interrogation. The Attorney General’s interpretation of those elusive concepts becomes relevant only after the method has been

applied.57

Two refusals to open criminal investigations into alleged torture were

brought before the hcj in 2012 and 2017 respectively. In Abu Ghosh, the peti-tioner asserted that he had been tortured and that the necessity defence can-not be invoked for that. The hcj confirmed that the necessity defence cannot be invoked for the use of torture but held that the acts that constitute torture are context specific. The hcj paved the way towards a rejection of the petition by holding that the petitioner had not proved he had been tortured (the Israeli

Security Agency denied it had tortured him) and that what may be consid-ered torture by an international body may not be considered torture in another

In the Tbeish case the Israeli authorities admitted to employing special

interrogation methods, but the hcj did not consider they amounted to tor-ture and conveniently omitted to consider the prohibition against cruel and inhuman treatment. The hcj confirmed in this case that “resort to the neces-sity defence did not depend on the imminence of the danger materialising, (as implied in the very term ‘ticking bomb’), but on the immediacy of the need to obtain information that would frustrate a bomb, which may or may not exist,

from exploding at some time in the fairly near future”.59

The UN Committee against Torture disagrees with the hcj’s above-

mentioned findings and interpretations. It reaffirmed in its 2016 concluding observations on Israel the absolute and non- derogable nature of the prohibi-tion against torture and expressed concern about the necessity defence con-

tained in Israeli penal law that can be used in torture cases.60

2.2 The Israeli Military Justice System Not a single high- level Israeli civilian or member of the Israeli Defence Forces has been held responsible for committing crimes against Palestinians since the foundation of the State of Israel. Israel has also failed to make any con-certed effort to hold to account lower- level, or even mid- level, soldiers who commit crimes against Palestinians. In a damning report, the UN Independent International Commission of Inquiry on the Protests in the Occupied Palestinian Territory stated that:

[T] he Government of Israel has consistently failed to meaningfully

investigate and prosecute commanders and soldiers for crimes and

violations committed against Palestinians or to provide reparation to

victims in accordance with international norms. Scarce accountabil-

ity measures arising out of Operations Cast Lead and Protective Edge

and public comments by high- ranking public officials cast doubt over

the State’s willingness to scrutinize the actions of military and civilian

leadership who drafted, approved and supervised the implementation

58 hcj 1265/ 11, Public Committee against Torture v Attorney General (6 August 2012); see also

David Kretzmer and Yaël Ronen, The Occupation of Justice, The Supreme Court of Israel

and the Occupied Territories (oup 2021) 368.

59 Ibid., page 369.

60 UN Committee against Torture, Concluding Observations on the Fifth Periodic Report of

Israel, cat/ c/ isr/ co/ 5, 3 June 2016, para 14.

Palestine, Israel, and the International Criminal Court 267

of the rules of engagement governing the actions of Israeli forces at the

demonstrations.61

Data compiled by Yesh Din shows that 430 complaints were filed with the Military Advocate General Corps, the body in charge of investigating Israeli soldiers, during the period 2017– 2018, with many of them submitted by human

rights organisations and Israel Police.62 Of these, 168 concerned shooting inci-

dents of which 68 resulted in fatalities.63 By the end of 2018, a staggering 80 per-

cent of the complaints had been closed without an investigation.64 The 430 complaints mentioned do not include complaints of harm against Palestinians in the context of the Great March of Return.

In response to complaints concerning the Great March of Return, the UN

Commission on the 2018 protests revealed in 2019 that “criminal investigations were opened in only five cases, including the deaths of four children” and that its findings “suggest strongly that other killings and gunshot injuries appeared

factually similar and therefore also warrant criminal investigation”.65 As of 2021, only one indictment had been filed and inquiries into 95 fatalities were closed without an investigation while 140 complaints were still under review,

years after the incidents occurred.66

In relation to Israel’s 2014 offensive, “Operation Protective Edge”, Al Mezan,

a human rights organisation based in the Gaza Strip, filed 122 criminal com-plaints with the mag. As of 2020, a total of 72 were closed without criminal investigation and 18 criminal investigations were opened. In total, 15 of the 18 were subsequently closed and only three remained under investigation, nearly

eight years after the commission of the alleged crimes.67

In the rare instances where a lower- level soldier is prosecuted for harm

inflicted on Palestinians, they receive meagre punishments that are not

commensurate with the gravity of the crime committed. For example, Israeli soldier Elor Azaria was video recorded executing Abed al- Fattah al- Sharif, who

lay motionless and injured on the ground.68 He was convicted of manslaughter and sentenced to only sixteen months imprisonment despite the Israeli judge

stating that he had not expressed any remorse.69 Elor Azaria was released from

prison after nine months.70 In another case, an Israeli military court found two undisclosed Israeli soldiers guilty of using a nine- year old Palestinian boy as a human shield during the 2008– 2009 offensive on the Gaza Strip. The two soldiers were demoted to the rank of sergeant and received a suspended three- month sentence for separating the child from his mother and forcing him at

gunpoint to open bags suspected of containing booby- trapped bombs.71

The impunity afforded Israelis is the result of persistent design flaws in the

military justice system responsible for investigating and holding Israeli soldiers to account for wrongdoing. Commenting on recent reforms of the Israeli mil-itary justice system, the UN Independent Commission of Inquiry on the 2014 Gaza Conflict said it was “concerned about a number of procedural, structural and substantive shortcomings, which continue to compromise Israel’s ability

to adequately fulfil its duty to investigate”.72 Relatedly, the UN Human Rights Committee lamented in its 2022 observations on Israel the lack of updated information on investigations into human rights violations allegedly commit-ted during the “Operation Cast Lead” of 2008– 09, “Operation Pillar of Defence” of 2012, and “Operation Protective Edge” of 2014. It called on Israel “to impar-tially and thoroughly investigate all allegations of human rights violations

committed” during these military operations.73

Israel’s military justice system revolves around the Military Advocate

General, mag Corps, military courts, and the Military Police Criminal

Investigations Division.74 The military justice system is responsible for

investigating, prosecuting, and trying Israeli soldiers for criminal miscon-duct. Military justice systems are not ipso facto inappropriate. What matters is whether the procedures, structures, and laws that make up the system are compatible with international laws and standards. An investigation needs to be assessed against its effectiveness and to be effective, it must be adequate,

prompt, and thorough.75 It must also be carried out by independent author-

ities,76 in an impartial manner.77 These requirements apply to investigations

into criminal conduct during an armed conflict.78

2.2.1 Constricting Investigation Policies The principal problem marring the Israeli military justice is its constricting investigation policies, according to which investigations into the killing and

mistreatment of Palestinians are not the norm.79

Indeed, in August 2016, the mag Corps announced that it “continues to

examine and investigate claims regarding allegations of exceptional incidents” and that these allegations were referred on behalf of Palestinian residents of the Gaza Strip and allegations from the media or internal idf operational

reports.80 In respect of Palestinians killed in the West Bank, the latest policy mandates the opening of an investigation into “every case of the death of a Palestinian resident as a result of operations by the idf forces in the West Bank, except in ‘cases where it is clear that the operations during which the

Palestinian inhabitant was killed were of real combat nature’”.81

These Israeli policies are concerning for three main reasons. First, they do

not acknowledge that the State has a duty under international law to investi-gate every killing of a person caused by its use of force and that such investiga-tions are necessary to secure the rights and freedoms of those within a State’s

effective control.82 The ECtHR confirmed in Al- Skeini v The United Kingdom that “[a] rmed conflict and occupation do not discharge the State’s duty to

investigate and prosecute human rights abuses”.83 Conversely, Israel’s policy in the West Bank presumes that there was no wrongdoing because the Palestinian was killed in so- called operations of “real combat nature”. Accordingly, the UN Human Rights Committee underlined recently that Israel must ensure investi-gations into all incidents involving excessive use of force by the Israeli Security

Forces.84 Second, the Israeli military justice system appears to require individ-uals, organisations, or media to encourage it to act before it will do so. The duty to investigate must not be made dependant on the proactiveness of others. This was made clear by the European Court of Human Rights when it stated that:

[W] hatever mode is employed, the authorities must act of their own

motion once the matter has come to their attention. They cannot leave it

to the initiative of the next- of- kin either to lodge a formal complaint or

to take responsibility for the conduct of any investigative procedures.85

Finally, Israel’s policies suggest that Israeli authorities are predisposed to view the killing of Palestinians by Israelis in a vacuum. This predisposition, coupled with the impunity described in Part 2.2., point to a lack of pattern analysis otherwise necessary to uncover systemic unlawful conduct and establish the

involvement of higher- level persons, such as commanders.86



Laws of Armed Conflict According to International Law, Second Report, February 2013,

pages 320– 321.

lethal force by State authorities. The obligation to protect the right to life under this pro-

vision, read in conjunction with the State’s general duty under Article 1 of the Convention

to “secure to everyone within their jurisdiction the rights and freedoms defined in [the]

Convention”. ECtHR, Al- Skeini et al. v The United Kingdom, Judgement, 7 July 2011, para 163.

2.2.2 Multiple and Contradictory Roles of the Military Advocate General

The mag is a commander of the idf vested with powers to legislate – “as he

instructs the idf on what is permitted and what is not permitted in warfare”;87 to execute – as he “directs the military’s operative legal counselling ahead of

and during hostilities”;88 and to enforce – as he decides to launch an investiga-

tion.89 The mag Corps, which falls under the mag, also offers free of charge legal advice and representation to soldiers under investigation or standing

trial, in the military justice system.90

In 2007, the enforcement and advisory roles of the mag Corps were sep-

arated and assigned to different entities within the mag Corps. The Military Prosecution department of the mag is, according to the idf website, “tasked with overseeing all investigations and conducting all prosecutions of alleged misconduct by idf soldiers occurring in the context of operational activity, including alleged mistreatment of detainees, looting, abuse of authority, or the use of force in a manner not conforming with idf rules and regulations (which

incorporate the law of armed conflict)”.91

One of the main reasons for why the Israeli military justice system does not

meet international standards is the multiple and parallel powers of the mag. The international standard of independence demands that individuals poten-tially implicated in violations should not have any supervisory role, whether indirect or direct, over those who conduct the investigation. There must exist a “lack of hierarchal or institutional connection but also a practical indepen-

dence”.92 This ensures that “where formal independence has been established,

actual independence” exists.93 However, the 2007 restructuring of the mag

87 Eyal Benvenisti, The Duty of the State of Israel to Investigate Violations of the Law of Armed

Conflict, Expert Opinion submitted to the Public Commission to Examine the Maritime

Incident of 31 May 2010, Israel’s Mechanisms for Examining and Investigating Complaints

and Claims of Violations of the Laws of Armed Conflict According to International Law,

13 April 2011, page 24 cited in B’Tselem, The Occupation’s Fig Leaf: Israel’s Military Law

Enforcement System as a Whitewash Mechanism, May 2016, page 27.

88 Ibid.

Preliminary Objections, Merits, Reparations and Costs, Judgement, 16 February 2017;

ECtHR, Isayeva v Russia, Judgement, 24 February 2005, para 211. See also UN Principles

Executions, 24 May 1986.

93 unhrc, Report of the Special Rapporteur on Extrajudicial, Summary, or Arbitrary

Executions, Philip Alston, a/ hrc/ 8/ 3, 2 May 2008, para 36. 272 Kiswanson

Corps did not affect the mag’s powers or rectify the hierarchical relationship between the mag, mag Corps officers, Attorney- General, and the Chief of Staff. The mag’s opinion is binding upon the army, since “it determines, for

the idf and its agencies, the current legal position”.94 Moreover, “an officer in the mag Corps, with the exception of the Military Defender, is subordinate in carrying out his or her duties solely to the mag, and not to the military com-

mander to whom he provides legal service”.95 This is also true for the military

advocates in various jurisdiction districts who also answer solely to the mag.96 As such, the mag remains the gatekeeper in Israel’s military justice system – the critical lever in the mag Corps’ chain of command.

The mag’s contemporaneous powers have been heavily criticised by UN

experts. In 2010, a UN committee of independent experts recalled that the mag advised on the same strategy, objectives, and policy underlying Israel’s operation in the Gaza Strip, that the UN Fact- Finding Mission on the Gaza

Conflict condemned.97 Noting the 2007 restructuring of the mag Corps, the subsequent UN Independent Commission of Inquiry on the 2014 Gaza Conflict described the mag’s powers as a “central failing of the investigation system” and pointed to his regular participation in cabinet meetings around the 2014

hostilities.98

2.2.3 Insufficient Civilian Oversight As explained, the mag occupies a powerful role within the Israeli military justice system and can set the course for justice for Palestinians. It is there-fore imperative that authorities have oversight over the mag. Israel’s Public Commission to Examine the Maritime Incident of 31 May 2010, also referred to

as the Turkel Commission, presents Israel’s High Court of Justice and Attorney General as having oversight over the mag and as being in a position to correct

it.99 There are several problems with that portrayal; the hcj is an institution to which persons who believe themselves to have suffered harm may turn. They can only do so on their own initiative and with their own funding, based on information that is at the disposal of mag Corps and the military, but not the victim. Israel’s hcj does not possess proprio motu powers to judicially review

decisions by the mag.100 When, nonetheless, given the opportunity to inter-

vene, the hcj has been evasive.101 Adalah, a human rights organisation and legal centre in Israel, petitioned Israel’s Supreme Court in 2007 on the need to open criminal investigations into incidents relating to Israel’s operations

“Rainbow” and “Days of Penitence” in Rafah in the Gaza Strip in 2004.102 Four years after the petition was filed, the hcj issued a decision that highlighted its reluctance to intervene in decisions taken by the mag. It stated that:

[T] he principle of maximal restraint in judicial intervention in the deci-

sions of the executive authority in regard to investigation and criminal

indictment is deeply embedded in the judicial tradition of this court.

Like the attorney general, the prerogative given to the chief military

prosecutor on the question of whether to order the initiation of criminal

proceedings is very broad [...] In accordance with this view, intervention

in the professional decisions of the chief military prosecutor is rare, and

should occur only in very exceptional circumstances.103

The hcj thus concluded that criminal investigations are not always an appro-priate tool of review, before rejecting the petition on grounds that it had been

Israel’s Attorney General appears equally intent on not intervening in the

mag’s exercise of its powers. Eyal Benvenisti has noted that the Attorney General “eschews its duty” by fully and broadly delegating his powers to the

mag.106 In any case, the mag and Attorney General “routinely consult one another on policymaking as well as decisions in cases considered to be of

importance”.107 It is for this reason, inappropriate to place the Attorney General in a position of oversight in relation to the mag.

2.2.4 Undue Delay

The success of an investigation hinges on the integrity and evidentiary value of evidential material. Its integrity and value may be compromised if it is not collected and examined within a certain time. A “prompt response by the authorities in investigating a use of lethal force” is also, according to the ECtHR, “essential in maintaining public confidence in their adherence to the rule of law and in preventing any appearance of collusion in or tolerance of

unlawful acts”.108

That said, whether an investigation is prompt and reasonably expeditious

depends on the context. The ECtHR brought attention to this in Jelić v Croatia when it accepted that an “investigation could initially have encountered

“the authorities’ inactivity for long subsequent periods”.109 Despite “the undeniable complexity of the present case, which concerns not only the iso-lated event of the killing of the applicant’s husband, but also concerns the investigation and prosecution of those responsible for the killing of a number of other individuals”, and the number of war crimes cases prosecuted simul-taneously, the ECtHR held that “political and social stakes relied on by the Government cannot by themselves justify the manner in which the investi-gation was conducted where leads given to the prosecuting authorities con-cerning the identification of direct perpetrators have not been thoroughly

followed”.110

This chapter has provided many examples of the lack of Israeli investiga-

tions, let alone prompt investigations. It suffices to recall that incidents of alleged violations during “Operation Cast Lead” (2008– 2009), “Operation Protective Edge” (2014), and the Great March of Return (2018), have been

excessively sluggish. A fact acknowledged by Israel’s State Comptroller.111

2.2.5 Inadequate Investigations An investigation cannot be effective unless it is adequate. According to the ECtHR, “this means that it must be capable of leading to the establishment of the facts, a determination of whether the force used was or was not justi-fied in the circumstances and of identifying and – if appropriate – punishing

those responsible”.112 Similarly, the icrc has established that an investigation must be effective insofar as it should be capable of enabling a determination of whether there was a violation of international humanitarian law, of identifying the individual and systemic factors that caused or contributed to an incident,

and of laying the ground for any remedial action that may be required.113 Armed conflicts can pose challenges to an investigation, but under no circumstance

The jurisprudence of regional human rights courts and icrc guidance pro-

vide a treasure- trove of examples of what constitutes an adequate investiga-tion, including: an autopsy in the case of a dead body that “provides a complete and accurate record of injury and an objective analysis of the clinical find-

ings, including the cause of death”,115 the timely collection of statements from

all witnesses,116 the collection of other physical and documentary evidence

(for example photographs and bullet analysis),117 an obvious line of inquiry

and stringent scrutiny,118 access by the victim’s family to the investigation

The European Court of Human Rights observed in the Salman v Turkey case “that the

autopsy examination was of critical importance in determining the facts surrounding

Agit Salman’s death. The difficulties experienced by the Commission in establishing

any of those facts, elements of which were still disputed by the parties before the Court,

derives in a large part from the failings in the post- mortem medical examination. In par-

ticular, the lack of proper forensic photographs of the body and the lack of dissection and

histopathological analysis of the injuries and marks on the body obstructed the accu-

rate analysis of the dating and origin of those marks, which was crucial to establishing

whether Agit Salman’s death had been provoked by ill- treatment in the twenty- four hours

preceding his death”. ECtHR, Salman v Turkey, Judgement, 27 June 2000, para 106. See

also Principles on the Effective Prevention and Investigation of Extra- legal, Arbitrary and

Summary Executions, para 12.

As has been detailed above, relevant Israeli authorities rarely open criminal

information on the few investigations122 conducted, paint a troubling picture of inadequacy; numerous reports demonstrate that Israeli authorities do not

keep the victims and their families123 continuously and regularly informed about the progress and status of assessments/ examinations, investigations,

and further proceedings. Years may pass before such information is shared.124 It has also been documented that the complainants are not sufficiently informed of how the investigation was conducted and that generic reasons are given as

to why a decision not to open an investigation is taken.125 Vague, unreasoned



scrutiny must be applied by the relevant domestic authorities to the ensuing investiga-

opinion”.127 The State Comptroller noted that documents were missing from

the reports128 and that the fact- finding assessment mechanism did not carry

out its work in an orderly fashion and with prior preparation.129 The State Comptroller also found that “at the time of the completion of the interim draft, August 2015, the debriefing teams did not question civilians, and it is possible that they did not take advantage of a very extensive range of possible evidence

that would have assisted in processing of the debriefing”.130 B’Tselem noted the same deficiencies from the mag’s responses to its complaints; that decisions to investigate are based on “incomplete information and an extenuating read-ing of the circumstances of the incident”, that external evidence is rarely col-

lected, and that investigations rely on soldiers’ accounts of the incidents.131 For example, it appears that foreign correspondents that witnessed the egregious airstrike incident that killed four Palestinian children from the Bakr family as

they played football on the Gaza beach in July 2014 were not interviewed.132 The mag Corps decided to close the investigation into the killing of the chil-dren on 11 June 2015.

3 Rome Statute Crimes Relevant to Israel’s Occupation

The icc has jurisdiction over crimes against humanity, war crimes, genocide

and aggression.136 In her Article 19(3) request for a ruling on jurisdiction of 2020, the icc Prosecutor submitted to the Pre- Trial Chamber that a reasonable basis existed to believe that war crimes have been, or are being, committed in

the opt.137 Regarding the conduct of the idf, the icc Prosecutor mentioned in

particular war crimes committed during the 2014 hostilities in the Gaza Strip,138 including wilful killing and wilfully causing serious injury to body or health and intentionally directing an attack against objects or persons using the distinc-

tive emblems of the Geneva Convention.139 The icc Prosecutor’s request also

mentioned Israel’s transfer of Israeli civilians under Article 8(2)(b)(viii),140 and the shooting of Palestinian civilians as part of the Great March of Return demon-

arrested, detained and prosecuted in the Israeli military court system.145 The icc Prosecutor had also previously stated that her office had “received informa-tion regarding the purported establishment of an institutionalised regime of systematic discrimination that allegedly deprives Palestinians of a number of

their fundamental human rights”.146 The icc Prosecutor can, and should, also investigate crimes against humanity and other war crimes such as pillage.

Part 1 of this chapter has explained that the icc can exercise jurisdiction

over the territory of the State of Palestine under the Rome Statute, including over Israelis who commit crimes there, and the second part has underscored that violations of international law as well as acts that may constitute inter-national crimes committed in Palestine go unpunished, demonstrating that cases at the icc will be admissible. What sets the icc apart from other courts and human rights bodies is that the icc is the first and only independent international criminal court capable of investigating, prosecuting, and pun-ishing Israelis and Palestinians for Rome Statute crimes committed in the opt. Furthermore, the Rome Statute is the first treaty to comprehensively criminal-ise many of the grave acts that have come to characterise Israel’s occupation of Palestine and its subjugation of Palestinians.

Consider Israel’s transfer of around 700,000 Israeli civilians to the opt

including East Jerusalem147, now living in nearly 300 settlements that control

approximately 40 percent of the opt and more than 60 percent of Area C.148 Article 8(2)(b)(viii) of the Rome Statute lists the “transfer, directly or indirectly, by the Occupying Power of parts of its own civilian population into the terri-tory that it occupies”, as a serious violation of the laws and customs of war in an

international armed conflict and a war crime.149 This act was prosecuted in the Nuremberg trial of major war criminals as the “colonization by Germans”, with reference made to the related expulsion of the native populations of Poland

and the Soviet Union.150 The Nuremburg trial judgement remarked that Heinrich Himmler did not plan to “Germanize the East in the old sense, that is to teach the people there the German language and the German law, but to see

to it that only people of purely Germanic blood live in the East”.151 The transfer of the civilian population into the occupied territory by the Occupying Power was prohibited under Article 49(6) of the Fourth Geneva Convention about two years after the Nuremburg trial judgement was delivered. The inclusion of settler transfer in the Rome Statute was supported by most States during the drafting of the Rome Statute with only Israel requesting that it be excluded

from the statute.152 Israel went so far as to declare that it would have been in

favour of the Rome Statute had it not included the article on settler- transfer.153 The reference to “directly or indirectly” in Article 8(2)(b)(viii) and the corre-sponding elements of the crime affirm that the definition of this crime does not require that the Occupying Power forcibly transfers its civilian popula-tion into the occupied territory. Nor must the Occupying Power organise the

transfer.154

Israeli settlements continuously expand and connect to achieve the unin-

terrupted presence and movement of settlers in the West Bank and to link them to Israel. These settlements force Palestinians into increasingly smaller and fewer areas that are physically detached, and in so doing consolidate Israel’s control over the opt and frustrate the basic principle of temporariness

of occupation.155 That the State of Israel plays a leading role in the construc-tion of settlements and settler- transfer is not contentious. Israel recently justified Israeli Jewish settlement in the opt in a proceeding

before Israel’s Supreme Court concerning the Settlements Regularization Law for Judea and Samaria. During that proceeding, State representatives argued that Jewish settlement in the West Bank fulfils the values of Zionism, that the contested law is justified by its political, national and social purposes, that it is the “natural right” of Jewish Israelis to live in the opt, and that the military com-

mander is not subject to international law.158 The Settlements Regularization Law for Judea and Samaria allowed Israel to expropriate Palestinian private land and retroactively “legalise” settlements that had not received prior formal government approval.

the Rome Statute. Their transfer is caused by a myriad of measures: the revo-

The Rome Statute crimes of transfer and deportation apply irrespective of

whether persons move across State borders.166 Furthermore, the crime against humanity of forcible transfer has been interpreted by an icc Pre- Trial Chamber and Trial Chamber as an “open- conduct crime” that can be the result of mul-

tiple different conducts that amount to expulsion or other coercive acts.167 In Prosecutor v Bosco Ntaganda, the Trial Chamber further clarified that what needs to be demonstrated is “that there was a genuine lack of choice on the

part of the individuals transferred”.168

As to the requirement of lawful presence in the area from which the pop-

ulation is deported or transferred, this does not translate into a requirement of legal residence under domestic laws and procedures. As the Trial Chamber noted in the above-mentioned case, supported by the jurisprudence of the UN Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia and the commentary to Additional Protocol ii:

The requirement of ‘lawful presence’ does not mean that the victim must

have had legal residence in the area. Indeed, this protection extends to

individuals who, for whatever reason, have come to live in a community,

including internally displaced persons who have established temporary

homes after being uprooted from their original communities.169

The rationale is that “the prohibition against forcible transfer is intended to protect the right of individuals to remain in their homes or communities

unhindered”.170 Whether or not the local authorities recognise the local popu-lation’s presence is irrelevant. As is the question whether the affected persons are the formal owners of the homes that they occupy and are displaced from. Any other reading of the above decision would facilitate the displacement of persons, as opposed to protect them.



166 Articles 7(1)(d) and 8(2)(a)(vii) apply to the transfer of persons to “another location”

Elements of Crimes, Articles 7(1)(d), 8(2)(a)(vii), and 8(2)(b)(viii).

Palestine, Israel, and the International Criminal Court 285

Torture and other inhumane acts are also examples of Rome Statute crimes

alleged to be committed in Palestine against Palestinians. Such acts could fall within the meaning of Articles 7(1)(f) and (k) (torture and other inhuman acts as crimes against humanity), 7(1)(g) (rape or any other form of sexual violence as a crime against humanity), 8(2)(a)(ii) (torture and inhuman treatment as a war crime), Article 8(2)(a)(iii) (wilfully causing great suffering, or serious injury to body or health as a war crime), 8(2)(b)(xxi) (committing outrages upon personal dignity as a war crime), and 8(2)(b)(xxii) (torture or inhuman treatment as a war crime).

It is well- known that Israel resorts to torture and other inhuman treatment

during the interrogation and detention of Palestinians. Israel’s position during the first decades of its occupation that its agents did not use torture during

interrogation171 was dispelled by Israel’s Commission of Inquiry into Methods of Investigation of the General Security Service regarding Hostile Terrorist Activity in 1987 when it uncovered the use of "physical pressure” interrogation

techniques.172 The Israeli government responded to the report that:

[S] o long as these practices did not deviate from the guidelines that existed

in the service at the time of the interrogation (and generally speaking,

they did not deviate from the guidelines), the interrogator who employed

such measures can justly claim, on the basis of paragraph 24 (1) (a) of the

Penal Code, that he was obeying the orders of his superiors, and that these

orders were not clearly illegal, and that he had reason to believe that he

was acting in order to extract necessary information on the terrorist activ-

ity of an organization which the suspect was suspected of belonging to.173



171 Human Rights Watch, Torture and Ill- treatment, Israel’s Interrogation of Palestinians from

the Occupied Territories, 1994, pages 48– 49.

172 The Landau commission’s informal name refers to one of its members, namely the for-

mer President of Israel’s Supreme Court, Moshe Landau. The Landau commission uncov-

ered the use of torture by Israeli agents and found that perjury became an unchallenged

norm relied on by Israeli agents when called to testify before military courts. Rather than

restraining the use of torture and similar measures, the Landau commission proposed

that torture be legitimised. It said, that, “[measures] should principally take the form of

non- violent psychological pressure through a vigorous and extensive interrogation, with

the use of stratagems, including acts of deception. However, when these do not attain

their purpose, the exertion of a moderate measure of physical pressure cannot be avoided.

gss interrogators should be guided by setting clear boundaries in this matter, in order to

prevent the use of inordinate physical pressure arbitrarily administered by the interro-

gator”. Cited in Human Rights Watch, Torture and Ill- Treatment, Israel’s Interrogation of

Palestinians from the Occupied Territories, 1994, pages 50– 54.

173 Ibid., page 50.

286 Kiswanson

Torture techniques and other unlawful measures that Israeli agents are known to have used against Palestinian adults and minors in custody include forced body contortions (such as the “frog” and “banana” positions), sexual abuse, beatings, and sleep deprivation. These techniques are frequently applied

across Israeli prisons and detention facilities.174 The UN Committee against Torture noted in its most recent concluding observations on Israel, in 2016, that it remains concerned at allegations that Israeli Agency interrogators resort to methods that are contrary to the Convention against Torture, such as stress positions and sleep deprivation. It also underscored concerns about informa-tion received on the lack of accountability for torture and ill- treatment and stated that none of the hundreds of complaints submitted to Israel’s Inspector for Complaints against Israel Security Agency interrogators have resulted in

prosecution.175 The Public Committee against Torture in Israel reported in 2016, that out of 1,000 complaints examined between 2001 and March 2016, not a single criminal investigation into the alleged use of torture of Palestinians by

an interrogator of the Israeli Security Agency has been opened.176

It is also important to mention the crimes against humanity of perse-

cution and apartheid under Articles 7(1)(h) and (j) of the Rome Statute, both of which are relevant to Israel’s conduct in the opt. A detailed exam-ination of these crimes is not possible in this chapter due to space con-straints but advanced, careful and detailed analyses on these crimes

have been carried out by local human rights organisations Al- Haq,177 Al

Chapter 5 by John Reynolds and Chapter 9 by John Dugard, present a con-vincing web of practices and policies that deprive Palestinians of their funda-mental human rights and substantiate the existence of a sophisticated system of institutionalised racial discrimination. Lynk concluded that, “[w] ith the eyes of the interna-tional community wide open, Israel has imposed upon Palestine an apartheid


reality in a post- apartheid world”.184



4 Conclusion

It is astonishing that Israelis have not been held criminally responsible for the conduct detailed in this chapter, especially because the international commu-nity agrees that the conduct as such is heinous and must not go unpunished. The impunity afforded Israelis at the domestic level is, however, not unex-pected considering the deficiencies of the Israeli military justice system and



178 Al Mezan, The Gaza Bantustan, Israeli Apartheid in the Gaza Strip, November 2021. Al

Mezan focuses its report on apartheid “experienced by all Palestinian people, is specif-

ically visited upon the two million Palestinians living in the Gaza Strip”.

179 B’Tselem, A Regime of Jewish Supremacy from the Jordan River to the Mediterranean

Sea: This is Apartheid, 12 January 2021.

180 Yesh Din, The Israeli Occupation of the West Bank and the Crime of Apartheid: A Legal

Opinion, June 2020.

181 Human Rights Watch, A Threshold Crossed: Israeli Authorities and the Crime of Apartheid

and Persecution, 27 April 2021.

182 Amnesty International, Israel’s Apartheid Against Palestinians: Cruel System of Domination

and Crime Against Humanity, 1 February 2022.

183 International Human Rights Clinic, Harvard Law School and Addameer, Apartheid in

the Occupied West Bank: A Legal Analysis of Israel’s Actions, Joint Submission to the United

Nations Independent International Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Territory,

Including East Jerusalem, and Israel, 28 February 2022.

184 unhrc, Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the Palestinian

Territories Occupied Since 1967, Michael Lynk, a/ hrc/ 49/ 87, 21 March 2022. 288 Kiswanson

the Israeli Supreme Court’s view of justice, described at length herein. It is also not surprising considering the international community’s exceptionalistic response to Israel. Indeed, States have either frequently opted to observe in silent acquiescence the mistreatment of Palestinians or actively intervened to thwart efforts that seek to ensure that Palestinians enjoy their rights to truth,

justice, and reparation.185

Despite the brutality of Israel’s lengthy belligerent occupation, the violence

that permeated the establishment of the State of Israel in 1948,186 and the large number of active hostilities and uprisings over more than half a century, too little has been done to vindicate Palestinian victims and grant them remedy, while much has been done to shield Israeli perpetrators. There is no doubt that the icc’s investigation in the Situation in Palestine challenges this modus operandi. It also tests the international community’s commitment to the icc, the universality of rights and norms, and the icc’s fair application of the law in

accordance with international standards and human rights law”.187

At the risk of otherwise shaking the foundations of the international legal

order and international law and ignoring victims’ suffering, the international community must support the unparalleled opportunity that the icc offers to end impunity, irrespective of where it reigns and whoever it involves, including in Palestine and Israel.

chapter 12


Private Sector Responsibility for the Treatment

of Palestinian Prisoners and Detainees in Light

of the Law and Policy of the International

Criminal Court

Shane Darcy



1 Introduction*

In 2019, USA Today ran a story on the various scandals associated with the global

security firm G4S.1 The feature described how international investors, includ-ing the United Methodist Church and the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation, had divested from the company because of accusations that it was “complicit

in war crimes”.2 In particular, the company had equipped Israeli prisons and provided services at checkpoints in the Occupied Palestinian Territory where

the rights of Palestinians were infringed.3 With the increasing presence of pri-vate military and security companies in contemporary armed conflicts, it is not uncommon for the staff of such companies to be connected in some way with aspects of the capture, detention and possibly even the mistreatment of pris-

oners and detainees.4 Where abuses or human rights violations occur, not only might companies suffer reputational damage and lose investment or business opportunities, but the criminal responsibility of individual directors, officials or other employees, or of the company itself, may arise if the acts in question



1 Brett Murphy, Nick Penzenstadler and Gina Baron, ‘Show of Force: The Pulse Nightclub

Shooting and Other G4S Scandals’, USA Today, 1 November 2019.

2 Ibid.

3 Ibid.

4 See generally Lindsey Cameron and Vincent Chetail, Privatizing War: Private Military and

Security Companies under Public International Law (cup 2013); Simon Chesterman, From

Mercenaries to Market: The Rise and Regulation of Private Military Companies (oup 2009);

Emanuela- Chiara Gillard, ‘Business Goes to War: Private Military/ Security Companies and

International Humanitarian Law’, 88(863) International Review of the Red Cross (2006) 525.



© Shane Darcy, 2023 | DOI:10.1163/9789004503939_

290 Darcy

amount to crimes under applicable national or international law,5 and if the

nature of their involvement gives rise to criminal liability.6

While the concept of complicity in international crimes is frequently

invoked in the context of corporate accountability,7 there has been relatively limited application of international criminal law by judicial bodies to the activities of companies during armed conflict to date. Nonetheless, the princi-pal treaty of international criminal law, the Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court, can be brought to bear on the actions of individual company officers or employees, even though the treaty does not address the criminal lia-

bility of corporations themselves.8 Some precedents for such individual crimi-nal responsibility can be found in the caselaw of the post- World War ii period

and in a number of national jurisdictions.9 Moreover, well- substantiated find-ings of corporate complicity in international crimes may serve an important deterrent purpose, even in the absence of formal adjudication of corporate criminal conduct by a court or tribunal.

This chapter will explore the legal responsibility for private sector indi-

viduals and enterprises in the context of the reported violations of human rights committed against Palestinian prisoners and detainees. It does so in light of the law and policy of the International Criminal Court, given the ongoing investigation by the icc Prosecutor into the Situation in the State of Palestine. This chapter will begin by outlining which violations might amount to international crimes within the jurisdiction of the icc. It then turns to the involvement of private companies in such activities and assesses whether such involvement might give rise to criminal liability under the Rome Statute. This is done by examining applicable modes of criminal liability under the Statute,

Unarmed Iraqi Civilians’, The Guardian, 13 April 2015; Wolfgang Kaleck and Miriam Saage-

Maaβ, ‘Corporate Accountability for Human Rights Violations Amounting to International

Crimes’, 8 Journal of International Criminal Justice (2010) 699.

6 See generally Alan Reed and Michael Bohlander (eds), Participation in Crime: Domestic and

Comparative Perspectives (Routledge 2013).

7 See for example James G Stewart, Corporate War Crimes: Prosecuting the Pillage of Natural

Resources (Open Society Justice Initiative 2011); International Commission of Jurists, Report

of the Expert Legal Panel on Corporate Complicity in International Crimes, 2008; Danielle

Olson, ‘Corporate Complicity in Human Rights Violations Under International Criminal

Law’, 1(1) International Human Rights Law Journal (2015).

8 See generally William Schabas, ‘Enforcing International Humanitarian Law: Catching the

Accomplices’, 83 (842) International Review of the Red Cross (2001) 439.

9 See Anita Ramasastry ‘Corporate Complicity: From Nuremberg to Rangoon: An Examination

of Forced Labor Cases and Their impact on the Liability of Multinational Corporations’, 20

Berkeley Journal of International Law (2002) 91.

The Treatment of Palestinian Prisoners and Detainees 291

in particular the concept of aiding and abetting. In light of prosecutorial policy at the icc, which is said to focus on “those who bear the greatest responsibil-

ity” for the most serious crimes,10 it is asked whether corporate actors are to be considered the most appropriate targets in this particular context when turn-ing to international criminal justice mechanisms. Regardless, the invocation of international criminal law even without any formal denunciation may prove effective at prompting business enterprises to alter their complicit behavior.



2 Treatment of Palestinian Detainees and Prisoners

The United Nations and civil society reports have shown consistent evidence of human rights violations committed against Palestinians detained or impris-oned by Israel, and at times against individuals detained by the Palestinian Authority and Palestinian armed groups. Israel is bound by relevant rules of international humanitarian law, human rights law and international criminal law, both in the occupied West Bank, including East Jerusalem, and the Gaza

Strip and in Israel itself.11 In 2014, the UN Human Rights Committee expressed its concern at reports of Israel’s “use of torture and other ill- treatment in the State party’s detention facilities, including widespread, systematic and institu-

tionalized ill- treatment of Palestinian children”.12 The UN Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the Palestinian Territories Occupied Since 1967 has referred to “serious, ongoing concerns” raised by Israel’s policies and practices relating to prisoners and detainees. These include:

[D] etention without charge and other forms of arbitrary detention, such

as Israel’s misuse of administrative detention; torture and other forms of

ill, inhumane and humiliating treatment; coerced confessions; solitary

confinement, including of children; denial of equality of arms; denial of
