65 icc, Prosecutor v Jean- Pierre Bemba Gombo, Judgement Pursuant to Article 74 of the

Statute, icc- 01/ 05- 01/ 08, 21 March 2016, page 59, para 124.

66 Elements of Crimes, Article 8 (2)(b)(xvi), War Crime of Pillaging, Element 2.

67 James Stewart, Open Society Foundations, Corporate War Crimes: Prosecuting the Pillage

of Natural Resources, May 2019, page 20.

68 Plunder, pillage and spoliation are terms that can be used interchangeably.

69 Singapore Court of Appeal, NV De Bataafsche Petroleum Maatschappij and Others v The

War Damage Commission, Singapore Law Reports, 1956, page 65. Prosecuting Systematic Economic Exploitation 323

territories occupied by Japan, and the home economy.70 However the seizure of property and subsequent exploitation for commercial purposes is a private use of property. In the words of the icc in Prosecutor v Bemba, pillage is also “intended to include situations where the perpetrator did not intend to use the

pillaged items himself or herself”.71 For example, where property appropriated for public purposes such as (the war effort) might later be designated a private

or personal use (for trade).72

A footnote to element 2 explains, “as indicated by the use of the term “pri-

vate or personal use”, appropriations justified by military necessity cannot con-

stitute the crime of pillaging”.73 This “military necessity” exception appears to derive from Article 23(g) of the Hague Regulations, prohibiting parties to the conflict from seizing “the enemy’s property unless such […] seizure be imper-

atively demanded by the necessities of war”.74 However the immediate con-cern lies in the apparent breadth of “military necessity” as a sweeping concept. For example Donia et al. argue, that, “the very mention of military necessity in connection with pillage is arguably unfounded in that it introduces an extra element and permits an evaluation to be made, whereas pillage is prohibited

absolutely”.75

4.2.1 Prosecutor v Katanga

The icc’s first ruling on pillage in Prosecutor v Katanga, established that the pillage of a town or place constitutes a war crime committed when the per-petrator appropriates public or private property without the consent of the owner. Citing a number of icty rulings, Prosecutor v Naletilic and Martinovic, and Prosecutor v Simic, and the icrc Commentary to the Fourth Geneva Convention, the court considered obiter that pillage may include appropria-tion by self- interested combatants, alongside organised and systematic appro-priation of property in occupied territory, giving a considerable nod to the

Nuremberg standard.76 The court considered:

70 Ibid.

71 icc, Prosecutor v Jean- Pierre Bemba Gombo, Judgement pursuant to Article 74 of the

Statute, 21 March 2016, page 59, para 120.

72 Court of Cassation, Grilli v Administration of State Railways, 20 ilr 1961, 429, 430; Italian

Court of Cassation, Colorni v Ministry of War, Ann Dig 419 (No 139), 1950, page 421.

73 Elements of Crimes, Article 8 (2)(b)(xvi), War Crime of Pillaging, Element 2.

74 Hague Regulations, Article 23.

75 José Doria, Hans- Peter Gasser, M Cherif Bassiouni, The Legal Regime of the International

Criminal Court: Essays in Honour of Professor Igor Blishchenko (Martinus Nijhoff Publishers

2009) 529.

76 icc, Prosecutor v Germain Katanga, Judgement Pursuant to Article 74 of the Statute, icc-

01/ 04- 01/ 07, 7 March 2014, para 905.

324 Power

In the opinion of the Chamber, the pillaging of a town or place comprises

all forms of appropriation, public or private, including not only organised

and systematic appropriation, but also acts of appropriation committed

by combatants in their own interest.77

In making a determination on private or personal use, the icc concluded that Ngiti combatants in Bogoro had extensively pillaged roofing sheets from houses, house ware and livestock from the civilian population for essentially personal reasons. The combatants had pillaged for their own self- interest as a form of remuneration and had seized the spoils, using them as they pleased. Based on this evidence the chamber concluded that the acts were intentional

and motivated by private or personal gain.78 Notably, when considering military necessity the court referenced the icty ruling in Prosecutor v Hadžihasanović

and Kubura on whether the property had a “direct military use”.79 However the reference to “direct military use” was merely pinpointed in a footnote and the court did not offer any concrete test as to how it would determine “direct mil-itary use”, which would appear to be a narrower standard than military neces-sity. For example, in nv De Bataafsche Petroleum Maatschappij and Others, the Singapore Court of Appeal previously explained that:

It may be that certain types of raw material or semi- manufactured prod-

ucts, such as cloth for uniforms and leather for boots, which could pos-

sibly be made up into finished articles by army personnel without the

assistance of civilian technicians and outside plant can, without stretch-

ing the meaning of “munitions- de- guerre” unduly, be regarded as having

a sufficiently close connection with direct military use to bring them

within Article 53.80

However, in Hadžihasanović the Trial Chamber of the icty clarified that “household appliances, furniture and clothing do not fall into the category of

property having direct military use”.81 Although seized property not having a



77 Ibid.

78 Ibid., para 952.

79 Ibid., page 356, footnote 2252.

80 Singapore Court of Appeal, NV De Bataafsche Petroleum Maatschappij and Others v The

War Damage Commission, 22 Malayan Law Journal 155, 13 April 1956.

81 icty, The Prosecutor v Enver Hadžihasanović Amir Kubura, Judgement, it- 01- 47- t, 15

March 2006, para 1875.

Prosecuting Systematic Economic Exploitation 325

direct military use may amount to pillage, it then remains to be established whether the property appropriation was for “private or personal use”.

4.2.2 Prosecutor v Jean- Pierre Bemba Gombo In Prosecutor v Jean- Pierre Bemba Gombo, the icc made reference to the Special Court for Sierra Leone’s objection to the requirement that pillaged property be intended for “private or personal use” and noted that the qualifi-cation did not derive from treaty or customary law nor was it expressed in the

jurisprudence of international tribunals.82 Significantly, the court drew on the conjunction “or” in “private or personal use” which it found broadened the use of the pillaged property to include situations where property was not used by

the perpetrator themselves.83 This may include for example, private entities such as corporations. The distinction here is important and relates more to the separation of pillaged property from war booty, which may under certain

circumstances be lawful.84 While the appropriation of public revenues for the treasury of the Occupying Power might not be a private use of property, the conjunctive distinction drawn by the Court in Bemba means that it could potentially be considered “personal”.

The purposes of military necessity can only be invoked “if the laws of armed

conflict provide for it and only to the extent that these laws provide for it”.85 The court explains obiter by way of example, that circumstances pertaining to military necessity are documented in Article 51 of the Hague Regulations on contributions during occupation, and Article 52 and Article 53 of the Hague

Regulations on requisition.86 The identification of Articles 51– 53 are important given that they refer to the laws governing belligerent occupation, in particular the occupant’s administration of the economy of the occupied territory. In this respect, it is important to add, that Article 55 of the Hague Regulations does not explicitly provide for a military necessity exception, and therefore it does not apply the exception of military necessity to the appropriation of public immoveable property and its fruits, which is regulated under the more strin-gent regime of usufruct. Where the belligerent occupant exceeds “the extent

82 icc, Prosecutor v Jean- Pierre Bemba Gombo, Judgement Pursuant to Article 74 of the

Statute, icc- 01/ 05- 01/ 08, 21 March 2016, page 59, para 120.

83 Ibid.

84 For example, under customary international law, “parties to the conflict may seize military

equipment belonging to an adverse party as war booty”. See generally, icrc, Customary

International Humanitarian Law, Volume i: Rules (cup 2010) Rule 49.

85 icc, Prosecutor v Jean- Pierre Bemba Gombo, Judgement Pursuant to Article 74 of the

Statute, icc- 01/ 05- 01/ 08, 21 March 2016, page 61, para 123. Emphasis added.

86 Ibid., page 61, footnote 291.

326 Power

that the laws provide” for requisitions and contributions under Articles 51– 53, this may amount to pillage for the purposes of the Elements of Crimes. For the permanent appropriation of the cash assets and revenues of the occupied territory, not used for military operations, this may amount to an underlying breach of Article 53 of the Hague Regulations.

In Bemba, the Court determined that it would examine the nature, location

and purpose of the pillaged items, and the circumstances of their appropria-tion when considering whether they are appropriated for “private or personal use”. These criteria appear to be borrowed from Article 52(2) of Additional Protocol 1 on the principle of distinction, which assesses the nature, location,

and purpose of the items.87 This brings other problems relating to the specific nuances of the occupation articles, providing for “needs of the army” or “mili-

tary operations”.88 More interestingly, it also appears to denote private or per-sonal use, more generally as the inverse of the “military objectives” test. Using the “nature, location and purpose” considerations to determine “private or per-sonal use” mirrors to a certain extent the function tests employed by military

manuals, to determine the ownership of property (see Section 3.2).89 These are also reminiscent of the 1970’s State immunity cases, which used, for example, purpose, nature and subject matter jurisdiction tests. These tests allowed the property of States in certain contexts to be treated as private for the consider-

ation of State immunities from suit.90 For example, public monies in pension

funds may have a private use.91 Laudably, this key consideration appears to bridge the gap arising from element 2 on “private or personal use”, providing for a more nuanced evaluation pertaining to the nature, location and purpose of the property’s use.



87 Ibid., page 62, para 125. Although Additional Protocol i, has an additional criterion of ‘use’,

the Court did not include ‘use’ into the assessment.

88 See also, James Stewart, Open Society Foundations, Corporate War Crimes: Prosecuting the

Pillage of Natural Resources, page 20.

89 The United States Army Field Manual, The Law of Land Warfare, 18 July 1956, fm 27- 10,

para 394(b).

90 UK Court of Appeal, Trendex Trading Corp v Central Bank of Nigeria [1977]; UK House of

(1995).

91 Ernst Feilchenfeld, The International Economic Law of Belligerent Occupation (Carnegie

Endowment for International Peace 1942) 6, 52.

Prosecuting Systematic Economic Exploitation 327

To conclude, Bemba has clarified that military use refers only to the narrow

circumstances where the laws of armed conflict provide for it. The dolus spe-cialis does not exclude economic spoliation of occupied territory, as exploita-tion beyond the permitted public requisition, confiscation and seizure in the Hague Regulations may be considered personal or private for the purpose of the Elements of Crimes, where the property does not meet the military neces-sity exception and based on an evaluation of its nature, location or purpose. In this respect, when examining whether the use of appropriated revenues and fiscal leakages, the question pivots on whether the use under Article 53 was for military operations. In assessing the perpetrators personal use of the reve-nues, the Court can examine the nature, location, and purpose of the revenues use. One such purpose as previously articulated in the Nuremberg Tribunal, is where the occupier seizes everything of value in the occupied territory to subjugate the country and bring it under its control, to both enslave the occu-pied population and further enrich the Occupying Power. Such tests enable the

court to make these broader contextual examinations.92

4.3 Consent

Finally, the icc Elements of Crimes provide that for pillage, the appropriation

must take place without the owners consent.93 Lack of consent may be evi-dent from coercion or “can be inferred from the absence of the rightful owner

from the place from where property was taken”.94 Coercive acts may include, “withholding licenses, raw materials, the threat of uncertain drastic treatment in peace- treaty negotiations or other effective means of bending the will of the

owners”.95

Article 7 of the Fourth Geneva Convention states that “[n] o special agree-

ment shall adversely affect the situation of protected persons, as defined by the present Convention, nor restrict the rights which it confers upon them”. Similarly, Article 8 provides that “protected persons may in no circumstances



92 United States Military Tribunal, Trial of Carl Krauch and Twenty- Two Others (The I.G.

Farben Trial), 1947– 1948, 10 Law Reports of the Trials of War Criminals (1949), page 45.

93 Elements of Crimes, Article 8(2)(b)(xvi), War Crime of Pillaging, Element 3 requires that

“the appropriation was without the consent of the owner”.

94 icc, Prosecutor v Germain Katanga, Judgement Pursuant to Article 74 of the Statute, icc-

01/ 04- 01/ 07, 7 March 2014, para 954; and icc, Prosecutor v Germain Katanga, Judgement

on the Decision of Charges, icc- 01/ 04- 01/ 07- 717, 14 October 2008, para 337, where Pre-

Trial Chamber i held that looting was committed without the owners’ consent when civil-

ians were in hiding.

95 United States Military Tribunal, Trial of Krupp and Eleven Others, 1947– 1948, 10 Law

Reports of the Trials of War Criminals (1949), page 50.

328 Power

renounce in part or in entirety the rights secured to them by the present Convention”. While Article 47 provides that:

Protected persons who are in occupied territory shall not be deprived,

in any case or in any manner whatsoever, of the benefits of the present

Convention by any change introduced, as the result of the occupation of

a territory, into the institutions or government of the said territory, nor by

any agreement concluded between the authorities of the occupied terri-

tories and the Occupying Power, nor by any annexation by the latter of

the whole or part of the occupied territory.

This prohibition is articulated by the US Military Tribunal at Nuremberg in Krauch, which found that even where there is genuine consent by the repre-sentatives of the occupied population, this does not legitimize the spoliation of property, where it amounts to the disruption of the economy and the alien-ation of industry from its inherent purpose. Furthermore, consent obtained “by threats, intimidation, pressure, or by exploiting the position and power of the military occupant under circumstances indicating that the owner is being induced to part with his property against his will, it is clearly a violation of the

Hague Regulations”.96 Although one could argue that the Oslo Accords essen-tially codified the administrative control that Israel had previously practiced de facto over Palestinian customs, value added taxes, banking, postal service, elec-tricity, and water among others- it is clear that the overall administration has eroded the economic subsistence of the occupied territory with both the State of Israel, public and private commercial enterprises, and international finan-cial institutions profiting from the settlement economy and revenues, which should be directed to the benefit of the occupied population. It is therefore questionable whether the Palestinian Authority could legitimately consent to the denial of humanitarian guarantees protecting the occupied population.



5 Conclusion

Public moveable property title may pass to the belligerent subject to certain conditions, but the appropriation is limited to military purpose and certainly not for the economic aggrandisement of the occupier. While the Special Court



96 United States Military Tribunal, Trial of Carl Krauch and Twenty- Two Others (The I.G.

Farben Trial), 1947– 1948, 10 Law Reports of the Trials of War Criminals (1949), page 47. Prosecuting Systematic Economic Exploitation 329

for Sierra Leone had indicated that the door was closed on prosecuting sys-tematic economic exploitaiton at the icc, this chapter demonstrates that the icc is open to a more expansive interpretation of the Elements of Crimes that would include an analysis of the location, nature and purpose, to ascertain whether the perpetrators intended appropriation of property was for personal use. Broadly speaking, one such purpose might be the systematic economic exploitation of the occupied territory, a purpose that does not have a military objective and which is therefore personal in nature. Provided there is an under-lying international humanitarian law property violation, this opens the door to prosecuting individual Israeliparliamentarians or civil servants complicit in intentionally depriving the Palestinian people of their revenues and economic resources. That policy makers in the State of Israel could be held accountable for acts of systematic economic exploitation is now a real possibility following the Prosecutor’s opening of an investigation into the Situation in the State of Palestine at the icc in 2021.





chapter 14


Criminal Jurisdiction under Occupation: The Oslo

Accords and the icc

Halla Shoaibi and Asem Khalil



On 1 January 2015, the government of the State of Palestine lodged a decla-ration under Article 12(3) of the Rome Statute accepting the jurisdiction of the International Criminal Court over crimes committed on the occupied Palestinian territory since 13 June 2014. It also submitted documents of acces-

sion to the Rome Statute to the UN Secretary- General on 2 January 2015.1 Accordingly, the icc Office of the Prosecutor initiated a preliminary examina-tion into the Situation in Palestine, which resulted in the opening of an inves-

tigation.2 Several questions on the scope of the icc’s territorial jurisdiction under Article 12(2)(a) of the Rome Statute have arisen in the context of the opening of the investigation into the Situation in Palestine. This chapter will discuss the effects, if any, of the Oslo Accords on the icc’s jurisdiction in the Situation in Palestine.

This chapter will first elaborate on the concept of jurisdiction in interna-

tional law and how occupation affects the jurisdiction and sovereignty of States. Second, it will address the jurisdiction of the icc over the occupied Palestinian territory. Third, it will examine the Oslo Accords and explain why the agreements do not bar the icc from exercising jurisdiction. Specifically, this chapter will assert that the absence of enforcement jurisdiction does not negate prescriptive jurisdiction, and that a special agreement cannot be con-cluded in violation of the right to self- determination or in contradiction with the State of Palestine’s obligations under the Geneva Conventions.



1 State of Palestine, Declaration Accepting the Jurisdiction of the International Criminal Court, 31

December 2014.

2 icc Office of the Prosecutor, Statement of ICC Prosecutor, Fatou Bensouda, Respecting an

Investigation of the Situation in Palestine, 3 March 2021.



© Halla Shoaibi and Asem Khalil, 2023 | DOI:10.

Criminal Jurisdiction under Occupation 331

1 The Meaning of Jurisdiction in International Law

The term “jurisdiction” refers to the legitimate assertion of authority to affect

legal interests.3 National systems dictate how States exercise jurisdiction and it is generally accepted that a State’s jurisdiction is based on two pillars: territo-riality and nationality. While States may prescribe acts as criminal and adjudi-cate such crimes when committed on their territory, nationality is recognised

as a basis for jurisdiction over extraterritorial acts.4 There are generally three forms of jurisdiction: (i) jurisdiction to prescribe, meaning the ability to make its law applicable to persons and conduct, in other words the right to crimi-nalise conduct, (ii) jurisdiction to adjudicate, meaning the ability to subject natural and legal persons to legal process, (iii) jurisdiction to enforce, that is to induce or compel compliance with a determination reached through exec-

utive and/ or judicial actions.5 States maintain prescriptive jurisdiction even when they are unable to exercise it due to certain limitations derived from cus-tomary or treaty law, such as diplomatic or State immunity, or special agree-ments. The following section will propose that the International Criminal Court asserts territorial jurisdiction on the basis of a State’s prescriptive juris-diction. But first, it will explain the relevance of sovereignty in any assessment on the existence of jurisdiction during occupation.

1.1 Sovereignty as a Reliable Indicator of Jurisdiction during Belligerent

Occupation6

The State of Palestine acceded to the Rome Statute, making the Statute appli-cable in respect of Palestine’s territory. Like all States Parties to the Rome Statute, Palestine is permitted to delegate its jurisdiction to the icc so that it may investigate the situation on Palestine’s territory. Palestine, however, is not required to exercise effective control nor full sovereignty over its territory in

the U.S. Position’, 64(1) Law and Contemporary Problems (2001) 71.

4 James Crawford (ed), Brownlie’s Principles of Public International Law (oup 2019) 441– 445.

5 Restatement (Third) of the Foreign Relations Law of the United States § 401 Bases of

6 The first three paragraphs in this section are taken from first author’s unpublished the-

332 Shoaibi and Khalil

order to delegate its jurisdiction to the icc. The Rome Statute is clearly man-dated to prosecute crimes occurring in occupied territory. This is indicated, for example, in the case of population transfer in occupied territories under

Article 8(2)(b)(viii).7 Since jurisdiction is based on sovereignty rather than effective control in respect of situations of occupation, one must examine

whether States under occupation maintain sovereignty.8

The fact that Israel occupies the Palestinian territory and asserts effec-

tive control does not undermine Palestinian sovereignty over the same area. International scholars have argued that occupation is “the effective control of a power (be it one or more states or an international organisation, such as the United Nations) over a territory to which that power has no sovereign title,

without the violation of the sovereign of that territory”.9 The sovereignty of an occupied State is not terminated nor suspended, even if the act of occupa-

tion weakens and reduces the sovereign State’s control.10 As such, de jure sov-

ereignty remains vested with the occupied population,11 who have an inherent

vant to their entitlement to such sovereignty.13

The United Nations supports this view. UN General Assembly resolution 43/

177 of 25 December 1988 confirmed “the need to enable the Palestinian peo-

ple to exercise their sovereignty over their territory occupied since 1967”.14 Moreover, UN General Assembly resolution 61/ 19 of 2012 maintained that international recognition to the Palestinian State signifies international recog-nition of the territory belonging to it, within the 1967 borders, even if the exact

borders are not identified.15 In any case, there are no competing formal claims

of sovereignty over the Palestinian territory, except for over East Jerusalem.16

Sovereign title to a territory can be determined through recourse to two

methods. The first method is for a claimant State to show that it lawfully received the territory from a prior sovereign, whether from another State through a treaty of cession or by way of State succession, including decolo-

nisation.17 The second method is for the claimant State to demonstrate “con-tinuous and peaceful display of the functions of State within a given region

is a constituent element of territorial sovereignty”.18 Arguably, current- day Palestine is a successor State of either the Ottoman State or of Palestine under the British Mandate. This chapter will not be discussing this issue, as it is out-

side its scope and comprehensively argued by other writers.19 In any case, the

State of Palestine’s title to its territory (West Bank, including East Jerusalem, and the Gaza Strip) can be found in the display of its functions as a State within that region. As an example of this, the State of Palestine provides education and health for Palestinians in the West Bank and the Gaza Strip, maintains a functioning judicial system, and ratifies treaties in the name of the State.



2 The Source of the International Criminal Court’s Jurisdiction

According to Article 12 of the Rome Statute, the icc may exercise its jurisdic-tion if the crime took place on the territory of a State Party (territorial jurisdic-

tion) or the accused is a national of a State Party (personal jurisdiction).20 The icc’s territorial jurisdiction refers to the reach of the icc’s authority over the

territory of the State Party,21 and includes the land territory of the State Party

and its registered vessels and/ or aircrafts.22 That said, the Rome Statute does not determine the nature, scope, and source of a State Party’s jurisdiction nec-essary for it to delegate jurisdiction to the icc. The Rome Statute also does not provide that its authority is derived exclusively from the act of delegation nor that there must there be a symmetry between the jurisdiction of the icc and the domestic jurisdiction of a State Party.

A convincing argument can be made, however, that it is the delegation of

primary jurisdiction over a specific area that provides the legal basis for the icc’s jurisdiction when either the territorial State or the State of nationality



20 Rome Statute, Article 12(2). “In the case of article 13, paragraph (a) or (c), the Court may

exercise its jurisdiction if one or more of the following States are Parties to this Statute or

have accepted the jurisdiction of the Court in accordance with paragraph 3: (a) The State

on the territory of which the conduct in question occurred or, if the crime was committed

on board a vessel or aircraft, the State of registration of that vessel or aircraft; (b) The State

of which the person accused of the crime is a national”.

21 Michail Vagias, ‘The Territorial Jurisdiction of the International Criminal Court –

A Jurisdictional Rule of Reason for the icc?’, 59(1) Netherlands International Law Review

Prosecutor’s Application for a Warrant of Arrest, Article 58, icc- 01/ 04- 01/ 06, 24, 10

February 2006.

22 See Rome Statute, Article 12(2)(a); Michail Vagias, ‘The Territorial Jurisdiction of the

International Criminal Court – A Jurisdictional Rule of Reason for the icc?’, 59(1)

Netherlands International Law Review (2012) 43, page 45. Michail Vagias has argued that

the icc “shall have territorial jurisdiction when a crime is sufficiently closely connected

to the territory of a State Party. The meaning of ‘sufficiently close connection’ would need

to be specified on a case- by- case basis”.

Criminal Jurisdiction under Occupation 335

has consented to the Rome Statute.23 When a State Party possessing territorial jurisdiction delegates its jurisdiction to the icc, the court is handed the power

to act in cases where the State Party could have acted on the national level.24

Another argument can be made that States can delegate the exercise of its

jurisdiction to the icc regardless of how it chooses to exercise such jurisdic-tion on the national level. The test to determine the capacity of the entity in question to delegate jurisdiction, is whether such entity possesses, “an interna-tionally recognized legal authority, and not of the material ability of actually exercising jurisdiction over either the territory in question or over certain indi-

viduals within or outside that territory”.25 To delegate jurisdiction, it is nec-essary for the State to exercise prescriptive jurisdiction, not the jurisdiction

to enforce or to adjudicate.26 A State is able to delegate its prescriptive juris-diction “irrespective of how it chooses to prescribe such jurisdiction domesti-

cally”.27 Hence, accession to the Statute merely authorises the icc to exercise jurisdiction, and does not establish the title for icc jurisdiction.

2.1 The Nemo Dat Quod non Habet Maxim Nemo dat quod non habet is a principle derived from private property law, meaning “one cannot give what one does not have”. This notion gives prior-ity to the owner of the property or goods. For example, if goods were sold by a person who is not their owner and without the consent of the owner, the buyer neither acquires title to the goods (similar to that of the seller who did

Michael Newton’, 49 Vanderbilt Journal of Transnational Law (2016) 447. 336 Shoaibi and Khalil

not acquire title), nor authorisation.28 The nemo dat quod non habet maxim has been used to claim that an entity lacking enforcement and prescription jurisdiction cannot delegate jurisdiction to the icc, as one cannot give what one does not have in the first place. Such an argument presupposes an exact

symmetry between domestic criminal jurisdiction and that of the icc.29 It also presupposes that the icc can extend its jurisdiction only to situations where the entity in question has an identical jurisdiction to that of the icc. Such a presumption is erroneous.

Indeed, the Oslo Accords limited the Palestinian Authority’s ability to exer-

cise criminal jurisdiction over Israeli nationals, but they did not relinquish Palestine’s prescriptive jurisdiction nor have they impeded Palestine’s ability to accede to numerous multilateral treaties. On 10 March 2020, the icc Appeals Chamber authorised the investigation into the Situation in Afghanistan, even though Afghanistan had signed so- called Status of Forces Agreements with the United States, waiving Afghanistan’s criminal jurisdiction over US forces. In this situation, Afghanistan maintained its prescriptive jurisdiction and waived its enforcement jurisdiction in relation to American forces. The icc Appeals Chamber did not view these agreements as relevant to the issue of jurisdic-tion; a State waving or restricting its jurisdiction to adjudicate and enforce does not preclude the icc from extending its jurisdiction. Rather, the Rome Statute deals with limitations to prosecution arising from lack of enforcement jurisdiction under Part 9 of the Rome Statute (issues of cooperation and judi-cial assistance). The Appeals Chamber emphasised the distinction between issues pertaining to its jurisdiction under Part 2 of the Rome Statute, and issues pertaining to pre- existing treaty obligations under Part 9 of the Rome

Statute, which include safeguards with respect to pre- existing obligations.30 The court concluded that the Status of Forces Agreements are not to be dis-cussed as an issue affecting jurisdiction and confirmed the icc’s jurisdiction

in this situation.31

28 Louise Merrett, ‘The Importance of Delivery and Possession in the Passing of Title’, 67(2)

The Cambridge Law Journal (2008).

29 Carsten Stahn, ‘Response: The icc, Pre- Existing Jurisdictional Treaty Regimes, and the

Limits of the Nemo Dat Quod Non Habet Doctrine – A Reply to Michael Newton’, 49

Vanderbilt Journal of Transnational Law (2016) 448.

30 icc, Situation in The Islamic Republic of Afghanistan, Judgment on the ‘Appeal Against

the Decision on the Authorization of an Investigation into the Situation in the Islamic

Republic of Afghanistan’, icc- 02/ 17- 138, 5 March 2020, para 44. In fact, “[t] he raison d’être

for Article 98 [under part 9] is recognition that the Court has and is exercising jurisdiction

with respect to a particular person sought”. Rod Rastan, ‘Jurisdiction’ in Carsten Stahn

(ed), The Law and Practice of the International Criminal Court (oup 2015) 162.

31 Ibid.

Criminal Jurisdiction under Occupation 337

An assessment of the jurisdiction of the icc should primarily be guided

by the objectives of international criminal law, instead of being restricted to mechanical formulas originating in private property law such as the maxim

nemo dat quod non habet.32 International tribunals, especially the icc, aim to achieve justice, end impunity, and resolve to guarantee lasting respect for international justice.



3 The Oslo Accords and the icc33

In 1991, following the first Palestinian uprising (the Intifada), the Palestine Liberation Organization and the Israeli Government commenced peace nego-tiations. The two parties signed the Declaration of Principles on Interim Self-

Government in 199334 and the Israeli- Palestinian Interim Agreement in 1995.35 The plo recognised Israel’s right to exist, and, in return, the Government of Israel recognised the plo as the legitimate representative of the Palestinian

people.36 The agreement established the Palestinian Authority37 for a tran-sitional period that would eventually lead to a permanent resolution and a

viable Palestinian State based on the 1967 borders.38 As an initial step, Israel would transfer its authority in the West Bank and Gaza Strip to the Palestinian Authority in the following five spheres: education and culture, health, social

welfare, taxation, and tourism.39



32 Carsten Stahn, ‘Response: The icc, Pre- Existing Jurisdictional Treaty Regimes, and the

Limits of the Nemo Dat Quod Non Habet Doctrine – A Reply to Michael Newton’, 49

Vanderbilt Journal of Transnational Law (2016) 446.

33 The first four paragraphs in this section are heavily based on the first author’s unpub-

lished thesis; Halla Shoaibi, ‘Pregnant Women’s Access to Health Care in the Occupied

Palestinian Territory: Application Under the Rome Statute of the International Criminal

Court’ (sjd Thesis) (2015) 19– 21 [unpublished].

35 See Oslo ii Accord.

37 The Palestinian National Authority was established through the 1993 Declaration of

Principles to govern the Palestinian people in the West Bank and the Gaza Strip, for a

democratic principles, direct, free and general political elections. See Oslo I Accord.

The Oslo ii Accord divided the West Bank into Areas A, B, and C,40 with the

pa having complete autonomy over civil and security issues in Area A. In Area B, the pa would maintain civil control, while both the pa and the Government

of Israel would have security control.41 Area C, which includes Israeli settle-

ments, was temporarily designated under the full control of Israel,42 on the basis that the Israeli military would gradually withdraw and transfer all pow-

ers and responsibilities to the pa over a period of time.43 Area C constituted

72 percent of the land of the West Bank at the time of Oslo ii.44 It currently

constitutes around 61 percent of the occupied West Bank.45 The transfer of

Area C to the pa was never implemented.46

Annex iv of Oslo ii Accord, the “Protocol Concerning Legal Affairs”, regu-

lates the judicial and legal work of the pa and grants it criminal enforcement jurisdiction only over offenses committed by Palestinians and/ or non- Israelis

in the “Palestinian Territory”.47 The Protocol defines that territory as the Gaza

Strip and West Bank, except for Area C.48 Offenses committed therein, by

years beginning in 1994, which should have led to a permanent settlement between Israel

47 Oslo i Accord, Article 1.

48 Ibid., Article i, 1 (a). “For the purposes of this Annex, “Territory” means West Bank terri-

tory except for Area C which, except for the Settlements and the military locations, will

be gradually transferred to the Palestinian side in accordance with this Agreement, and

Gaza Strip territory except for the Settlements and the Military Installation Area.” The

Palestinian Authority has jurisdiction in Area C over Palestinians and non- Israelis if they

committed an offense against Palestinians that is not related to Israel’s security, “[i] n addi-

tion, the Council has criminal jurisdiction over Palestinians and their visitors who have

committed offenses against Palestinians or their visitors in the West Bank and the Gaza

Strip in areas outside the Territory, provided that the offense is not related to Israel’s secu-

rity interests”. In the case, the offense was committed against Israel or an Israeli, Israel has

jurisdiction over any crime committed against Israeli or an Israeli in the territory, “[w]

ithout prejudice to the criminal jurisdiction of the Council, and with due regard to the

principle that no person can be tried twice for the same offense, Israel has, in addition to Criminal Jurisdiction under Occupation 339

Israelis or against Israelis, were placed under the sole criminal enforcement jurisdiction of the Israeli justice system. As a result, Palestinian courts are not permitted under the Oslo Accords to enforce its laws on any Israeli citizen, even if the offense is committed against Palestinians or on the “Palestinian Territory”, as defined under the agreement. While Annex iv of the Oslo ii Accord entails a temporary and limited waiver to exert enforcement and adju-dicative jurisdiction, the Oslo Accords do not allude to a relinquishment of Palestinian prescriptive jurisdiction. Notably, limited Palestinian enforcement jurisdiction does not pose an obstacle for the State of Palestine to delegate to the icc the jurisdiction necessary for it to investigate and hold individuals to account, for their perpetration of Rome Statute crimes.

It is within a State’s power to relinquish (temporarily or permanently) its

enforcement and adjudication jurisdiction because jurisdiction stems from State sovereignty. States, including those occupied, retain the power to revoke and delegate authority. In such cases, because the prescriptive jurisdiction of the State remains intact, a State can agree in an international treaty to limit its own adjudication and enforcement jurisdiction. In other words, the plo agree-ing to limit the adjudication jurisdiction of the pa when it comes to Israeli cit-izens not only signals the maintenance of Palestine’s prescriptive jurisdiction, but confirms it. How can Palestine agree to limit its adjudication jurisdiction towards Israeli citizens through an international agreement, if it does not have and maintain the prescriptive jurisdiction to begin with? Moreover, the limita-tion of adjudication jurisdiction towards Israeli citizens does not impact the State of Palestine’s adjudication power towards Palestinian and foreign (non- Israeli) subjects within its territory.

Notably, the idea of an Occupying Power concluding a treaty with the

occupied State that restricts the occupied State from bringing cases and pros-ecuting the Occupying Power’s agents, military personnel and/ or citizens, is

not novel in the international legal realm.49 For example, the United States restricted Iraqi jurisdiction against its military personnel in Iraq, Japan, and



the above provisions of this Article, criminal jurisdiction in accordance with its domestic

laws over offenses committed in the Territory against Israel or an Israeli”.

49 Valentina Azarov and Chantal Melonie, ‘Disentangling the Knots: A Comment on Ambos’

“Palestine, ‘Non- Member Observer’ Status and ICC Jurisdiction”’, ejil: Talk!, 27 May 2014.

“It established the Palestinian Authority (pa) as an interim Palestinian local government,

and merely granted the pa limited capacities in specific domains of daily life. It is com-

mon practice for the foreign military government of an occupied territory to avail itself of

a form of local government by the inhabitants of the occupied territory”.

340 Shoaibi and Khalil

Afghanistan.50 Such limitations in treaties should be viewed as a consequence of occupation, and such consequences should not go beyond their original purpose. That is, to prohibit national occupied courts from prosecuting the

occupier’s agents, but not to prevent the delegation of jurisdiction.51 It cannot be imagined that the icc will not have jurisdiction over cases within a situa-tion of occupation due to a bilateral agreement usually forced upon the occu-pied State. This would be contrary to the icc’s purpose and aim.



4 The Oslo Accords’ Compliance with International Law

According to the Rome Statute, the icc must interpret and apply the appli-cable law, including “where appropriate, applicable treaties and the princi-ples and rules of international law, including the established principles of the

international law of armed conflict”.52 The application and interpretation of the applicable law “must be consistent with internationally recognized human

rights”.53

In accordance with international law, treaties are considered invalid if

they conflict with a peremptory norm. Therefore, the treaty shall be deemed invalid, or at least relevant parts of it must be considered unlawful, where any interpretation and/ or application of the Oslo Accords conflicts with a peremp-tory norm. The following section will posit that self- determination qualifies as a perempory norm and that – by extension – a restriction on Palestinian prescriptive jurisdiction infringes on that right.

Withdrawal of United States Forces from Iraq and the Organization of Their Activities

During Their Temporary Presence in Iraq, 17 November 2008; Treaty of Mutual

United States nationals, 23 June 1960.

to delegate its jurisdiction to the icc, “then the question whether and why Palestine

refrains from exercising its jurisdiction in a specific instance or category of cases, includ-

complementarity for the purpose of admissibility”; Yaël Ronen, ‘Israel, Palestine and

Criminal Jurisdiction under Occupation 341

4.1 Self- Determination

Article 53 of the Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties provides that:

A treaty is void if, at the time of its conclusion, it conflicts with a peremp-

tory norm of general international law. For the purposes of the present

Convention, a peremptory norm of general international law is a norm

accepted and recognized by the international community of States as a

whole as a norm from which no derogation is permitted and which can

be modified only by a subsequent norm of general international law hav-

ing the same character.54

In its judgement on the Questions Relating to the Obligation to Prosecute or Extradite, the International Court of Justice provided relevant jurisdictional underpinnings of the norms of jus cogens that fall within the premises of Article 53 of the Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties. It established the evidence needed to determine when a norm of general international law has been elevated to a peremptory norm (jus cogens). In that decision, the icj held that the prohibition of torture has become a peremptory norm of general

international law,55 and it grounded its findings on the widespread interna-tional instruments that prohibit torture as a substantiation of State practice

and opinio juris.56

In the case of self- determination, the unanimously adopted UN General

Assembly resolution 2625(xxv) of 1970 regarding the Declaration of Principles

of International Law Concerning Friendly Relations,57 emphasised the impor-tance of self- determination as a principle of international law and imposed a duty upon States to take the necessary measures to promote the realisation of the principle. In considering this principle, Judge Robinson, in his separate opinion in the advisory opinion on Legal Consequences of the Separation of the Chagos Archipelago from Mauritius in 1965, stressed that self- determination is a core principle of international law and cited the prominent international instruments of universal application that embody the peremptory nature of



54 Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties, Article 53.

55 icj, Questions relating to the Obligation to Prosecute or Extradite (Belgium v Senegal)

(Judgement) icj Reports 422 (2012), para 99.

56 Ibid. See also icj, Legal Consequences of the Separation of the Chagos Archipelago from

Mauritius in 1965 (Advisory Opinion) icj Reports 169 (2019), Separate Opinion of Judge

Robinson.

57 unga Resolution 2625 (24 October 1970); Christine D Gray, International Law and the Use

of Force (oup 2000) 13.

342 Shoaibi and Khalil

the right of self- determination.58 Judge Robinson underlined that norms that emanate from the UN Charter and constitute a purpose of the United Nations, such as self- determination, have earned the characterisation of peremptory

norms by virtue of their normative value.59 He further indicated that the rec-ognition and acceptance of that norm by the international community as a

whole,60 are fundamental features for the identification of norms as jus cogens as established by the icj, in its decision on Armed Activities on the Territory of

the Congo.61

The peremptory characteristic of self- determination was additionally

highlighted in the ILC’s fourth report on peremptory norms of general inter-national law, in which the Special Rapporteur, Dire Tladi, indicated that self-

determination is a peremptory norm.62 Similarly, in the commentary to Article 26 of the Responsibility of States for Internationally Wrongful Acts, the right to self- determination was recognised by the International Law Commission

as a “clearly accepted and recognised” peremptory norm.63 The caselaw and

other supporting materials above- mentioned,64 not only illustrate that self- determination is customary international law, but more significantly, that it warrants the characterisation of a peremptory norm.

59 icj, Legal Consequences of the Separation of the Chagos Archipelago from Mauritius in 1965

(Advisory Opinion) icj Reports 169 (2019), Separate Opinion of Judge Robinson.

60 unga Resolution 2625 (24 October 1970); Christine D Gray, International Law and the Use

of Force (oup 2000) 22.

of The Congo v Rwanda) icj Reports 6 (2006), para 64.

Session’, 2(2) Yearbook of the International Law Commission (2019).

63 ilc, ‘Report of the International Law Commission on the Work of its Fifty- Third Session’,

2(2) Yearbook of the International Law Commission (2001), page 85.

64 See also UN Human Rights Committee, General Comment 12: The Right to Self- determination

of Peoples (Article 1), 13 March 1984, para 1. It recognises that self- determination is partic-

ularly important as “its realization is an essential condition for the effective guarantee

and observance of individual human rights and for the promotion and strengthening of

those rights”. The right to self- determination is further reiterated by the icj in its East

Timor case, where it identifies the right to self- determination as an “essential principle of

contemporary international law”. icj, Case Concerning East Timor (Portugal v Australia)

(Judgement) icj Reports 90 (1995), para 29. See further unga Resolutions 67/ 19 (29

November 2012) and 66/ 146 (19 December 2011), that refer to the inalienable right of the

Palestinian people to self- determination.

Criminal Jurisdiction under Occupation 343

Limiting Palestinian perspective jurisdiction and its ability to delegate juris-

diction to the icc risks negating Palestinians’ right to self- determination. If Israeli crimes on Palestinian territory, such as the transfer of Israeli citizens into the occupied territory, are not delegated to the icc, then Palestinians are

precluded from exercising their right to self- determination.65 Although the Oslo Accords do not create a direct obligation to violate a peremptory norm, fulfilling the limitation on delegating jurisdiction over Israeli nationals to the icc would lead to a violation of the norm of the right to self- determination.

4.1.1 The Oslo Accords Shall Not Bar the icc from Exercising

Jurisdiction over Israeli Nationals

Pursuant to Article 21(3) of the Rome Statute, the icc must interpret and apply the applicable law, including the jus cogens norm of self- determination, con-

sistently with internationally recognised human rights.66 William Schabas suggests that “Article 21(3) does not, strictly speaking, provide a new source. Rather, it makes all the applicable law in article 21(3) subject to internationally

recognized human rights”.67 The icc has recalled in several cases that Article

21(3) is part of the process of interpreting the provisions of the Rome Statute.68 When interpreting whether the icc has jurisdiction, it is important that the court does not recognise a treaty that violates peremptory norms.

Therefore, an examination of the effect of the Oslo Accords on the icc’s

jurisdiction over the Palestinian territory must consider whether allowing the Oslo Accords to limit the icc from prosecuting Israeli nationals gives rise to a violation of the norm of the right to self- determination. Indeed, where a conflict exists between a jus cogens norm and another rule of international law, the Rome Statute provisions establishing the court’s jurisdiction and its parameters, must surely be “interpreted and applied” as to be consistent with

65 According to the authors, the continued existence of settlements in the West Bank is one

of the main reasons driving the occupation and hindering Palestinians from exercising

their right to self- determination.

66 Rome Statute, Article 21(3); icc, Prosecutor v Saif Al- Islam Gaddafi, Pre- Trial Chamber

i, Decision on the ‘Admissibility Challenge by Dr Saif Al- Islam Gadafi pursuant to

Articles 17(1)(c), 19 and 20(3) of the Rome Statute’, Judge Perrin de Brichambaut Separate

Concurring Opinion, para 112.

67 William Schabas, The International Criminal Court: A Commentary on the Rome Statute

(oup 2010) 385.

68 See for example icc, Prosecutor v Lubanga Dyilo, Pre- Trial Chamber i, Decision

Establishing General Principles Governing Applications to Restrict Disclosure pursuant

to Rule 81(2) and (4) of the Statute, icc- 01/ 04- 01/ 06- 108, 19 May 2006, para 7. See also

Rebecca Young, ‘‘Internationally Recognized Human Rights’ Before the International

Criminal Court’, 60(1), The International and Comparative Law Quarterly (2011) 189. 344 Shoaibi and Khalil

the peremptory norm.69 Hence, if the Rome Statute limits its own jurisdiction based on provisions in the Oslo Accords and its annexes, it will create a vio-lation of the right to self- determination of the Palestinian people. The effects of the Oslo Accords on the icc jurisdiction should be interpreted in a way to

respect Palestinian self- determination.70

Even if the interpretation of the rule in question does not give rise to a viola-

tion of a peremptory norm but the application of the rule somehow does, then

such application would be contrary to the relevant peremptory norm.71 Hence, resulting in the unlawfulness of the application of the said rule. The obligation of States to respect the right to self- determination may suspend another treaty obligation – in this case, the limitation on the adjudication jurisdiction over Israeli nationals.

4.2 The Oslo Accords Incompatibility with Palestine’s International

Obligations

As a High Contracting Party, Palestine has an obligation to prosecute grave breaches under the Geneva Conventions and customary international law

(such as settlements,72 extensive destruction and appropriation of property,73 and practices of apartheid, and other inhuman and degrading practices involv-

ing outrages upon personal dignity74). The Fourth Geneva Convention con-firms in Articles 7, 8, and 47, that special agreements between the Occupying Power and the occupied population cannot derogate from or deny protected

persons the safeguards of the convention.75 Palestine’s obligation as a High Contracting Party to prosecute grave breaches, including by delegating such



69 See ilc, ‘Report of the International Law Commission on the Work of its Seventy- First

Session’, 2(2) Yearbook of the International Law Commission (2019), pages 146, 198– 199.

70 This was applied for example was applied in ecj, Council of the European Union v Front

Populaire pour la Libération de la Saguia- el- hamra et du Rio de Oro (Front Polisario), c- 104/

16 P, 21 December 2016, paras 88– 114. The court maintained that interpreting the treaty

between the European Commission and Morocco (Liberalisation Agreement) should be

concluded in a way to respect the rule of self- determination which is a “legally enforce-

able right erga omnes and one of the essential principles of international law”. See also

ilc, ‘Report of the International Law Commission on the Work of its Fifty- Third Session’,

2(2) Yearbook of the International Law Commission (2001), page 198.

71 Ibid.

72 Additional Protocol ii, Article 85(4)(a). See also icrc, Customary International

Humanitarian Law Volume 1: Rules (cup 2009) Rule 158.

73 Fourth Geneva Convention, Article 147.

74 Additional Protocol ii, Article 85(4)(c).

75 Fourth Geneva Convention, Articles 7, 8 and 47.

Criminal Jurisdiction under Occupation 345

duty to an international tribunal, takes precedence over any conflicting obliga-tion that arises out of a bilateral treaty.

Furthermore, the icj, in its advisory opinion on Legal Consequences of the

Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory, held that Israel vio-lates the erga omnes obligation to respect the right of the Palestinian people to self- determination along with other obligations it has under international

humanitarian law.76 The icj further recalled the advisory opinion on the Legality of the Threat or Use of Nuclear Weapons, in which rules of humanitar-ian law applicable in armed conflict “incorporate obligations which are essen-

tially of an erga omnes character”.77 The icj identified these as obligations to be owed by “states towards the international community as a whole” and

“thus all states can be held to have a legal interest in their protection”.78 Hence, Palestine has an obligation to prosecute grave breaches, especially those of an erga omnes character.

Within the realm of human rights law, the ECtHR in Soering v The United

Kingdom maintained, that, “the object and purpose of the [European Convention on Human Rights] as an instrument for the protection of indi-vidual human beings require that its provisions be interpreted and applied

so as to make its safeguards practical and effective”.79 The ECtHR held that the extradition of the applicant to the United States to stand trial for capi-tal murder would violate Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights, even if such extradition was not explicitly referred to in the general

wording of Article 3.80 Similarly, the Fourth Geneva Convention, as an instru-ment to protect civilians in armed conflict and in times of occupation, should be interpreted so as to make its safeguards effective. The limitation on the State of Palestine from delegating jurisdiction to the icc through a bilateral treaty would violate the obligation of the State of Palestine to prosecute grave

(Advisory Opinion) icj Reports 136 (2004), para 155; Ali Reza Hojatzadeh and Hossein

American Journal of International Law (1991) 138.

346 Shoaibi and Khalil

breaches under the Geneva Conventions, rendering it ineffective and non- practical. Such application would defy the object and purpose of the Geneva

Conventions as well as the object and purpose of the Rome Statute as well.81

The Rome Statute of the icc lists grave breaches as a category of war crimes

under Article 8(2)(a). Grave breaches may overlap with other international

crimes like genocide, crimes against humanity, and torture.82 International crimes, such as those codified in the Rome Statute, are of such importance and concern to the international community, that they give rise to the need

for individual criminal responsibility to end impunity.83 Given the grave nature of Rome Statute crimes, an argument could be made that carrying out the agreement in the Oslo Accords, in a manner which limits the delega-tion of Palestinian jurisdiction to the icc over grave breaches of the Geneva

Conventions and the Rome Statute of the icc, is therefore antithetical.84



5 Conclusion

Amid a long- standing colonial regime, the Israeli government- imposed restric-tions on Palestinian jurisdiction, or exercise thereof, through military acts on the ground and bilateral agreements that arguably resulted in a violation of the Palestinian right to self- determination. This chapter has engaged with



81 Jure Vidmar makes a similar argument with to the prohibition against the extradition

of persons to States where torture is committed. “This means that international law

does not absolutely prohibit extradition to states where torture is commonly practiced

[if states make a promise or diplomatic assurances the person would not be tortured].

Consequently, concluding an extradition treaty with a notorious torture jurisdiction is

not illegal or automatically void under Article 53 of the vclt. It is rather that the prohibi-

tion of torture may suspend the treaty obligation to extradite (where assurances are not

given)”. Jure Vidmar, ‘Rethinking Jus Cogens after Germany v. Italy: Back to Article 53?’, 60

Netherlands International Law Review (2013), page 11.

82 Ward Ferdinandusse, ‘The Prosecution of Grave Breaches in National Courts’, 7(4) Journal

of International Criminal Justice, (2009) 723, pages 724– 725.

83 Dinah Shelton, ‘Normative Hierarchy in International Law’, 100(2) The American Journal

of International Law (2006) 291, page 318; Rome Statute, preamble.

84 Dinah Shelton, ‘Normative Hierarchy in International Law’, 100(2) The American Journal

of International Law (2006) 291, page 318. “It has been clear since the Nuremberg trials

that conforming to or carrying out domestic law is no excuse for breach of international

criminal law; it would seem plausible as well, if unlikely to arise in practice, that a defence

based on carrying out international legal obligations, such as those contained in a bilat-

eral treaty, would fail if those obligations contradict the requirements of criminal law. In

this respect, norms of criminal law would be given supremacy over other international

law in practice”.

Criminal Jurisdiction under Occupation 347

questions arising from the effect of the Oslo Accords on the jurisdiction of the icc – precisely whether the Oslo Accords bar the exercise of the icc’s juris-diction in Palestine. It has argued that Palestine’s agreement to limit its own adjudication and enforcement jurisdiction through a treaty does not negate its prescriptive jurisdiction. The inability of the Palestinian national judicial sys-tem to prosecute Israeli officials who commit international crimes, rather, pro-vides an additional reason for the icc to intervene based on complementarity. Furthermore, any interpretation or application of the Oslo Accords negating Palestinian prescriptive jurisdiction – that is, negating the Palestinian right to self- determination – is a violation of a peremptory norm. Given that Palestine is a High Contracting Party to the Fourth Geneva Convention, it has an obliga-tion to prosecute grave breaches or to delegate such duty to an international tribunal. That duty takes precedence over any conflicting obligation that arises out of a bilateral treaty such as the Oslo Accords.

Although the icc might render possible relief for some victims of Rome

Statute crimes committed on Palestinian territory, it should not be viewed as means for justice for the entire Palestinian people, nor as a means of libera-tion. Palestinian engagement with the icc should consider the risks that come with placing expectations of justice on an international system that was born

within the colonial context of international law.85 With that said, the icc may, and can, expose some crimes committed by Israeli officials, for example per-taining to the war on Gaza or concerning the colonial regime in Palestine, such as settlements and apartheid.

chapter 15


Domination, Transformation, and Annexation via

Utilities

Marya Farah and Maha Abdallah



Prior to the Nakba and the establishment of the State of Israel in 1948, Zionist leaders and businessmen understood the importance of controlling water resources and electricity networks in order to create a viable State. Underpinning these plans was the prerequisite dispossession of Palestinians. Israel’s prolonged military occupation of Palestinian territory, administered

through a system of institutionalised racial discrimination and apartheid,1 and the territorial takeover that it has achieved, is an extension of this ideology and strategy.

Over the course of its 55 years as Occupying Power, Israel has instituted

countless policies and practices that seek to control, regulate, and hinder nearly all aspects of Palestinian life, while denying the Palestinian people the right to self- determination and sovereignty over their natural resources. This is perhaps most palpable when considering basic necessities for human life, health and a functioning society such as access to water and electricity. Israel’s administration of the opt and provision of these utilities has enabled it to exploit Palestinian natural resources, hold the Palestinian economy as a

captive market, and establish perilous living conditions for Palestinians,2 to

benefit and expand its settlement enterprise.3 Central to this system are both

1 unhcr, Report of the Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the Palestinian

Domination, Transformation, and Annexation via Utilities 349

Israeli- State owned and transnational corporations that enable, facilitate and profit from this regime. The foundations of some of these companies are linked to Zionist ambitions that preceded the 1948 Nakba.

In this context, Palestinians are often forced to engage with the same

oppressive discriminatory system and the actors within it, including Israeli authorities, institutions, and businesses, that target, marginalise, and exploit them. This includes situations where Palestinian authorities consent to utility- related projects for settlements in order to ensure Palestinian access to water, electricity, telecommunications and infrastructure is fulfilled, even if partially.

Indeed, Palestinian access to such utilities is subordinate to that of the over

700,000 illegally transferred Israeli settlers in the opt,4 and exemplary of a wider regime of apartheid that Israel has established throughout the territory

under its control.5 Accordingly, utility networks in the opt not only serve and

prioritise settlers, but also benefit Israel’s broader population and economy.6 In doing so, Israel has laid the physical infrastructure for its proverbial “facts on the ground”, entrenching its colonisation and ongoing process of annexation of Palestinian land.

This chapter will explore how Israel has used its authority to administer

the provision of water, electricity, and telecommunications in the West Bank7 to undermine Palestinian life while ensuring that such networks support the viability and growth of settlements, their integration into Israeli territory, and Israel’s unlawful claim of sovereignty. This chapter will first review the obliga-tions of an Occupying Power under international humanitarian law and inter-national human rights law. It will also examine the role and responsibility of businesses in this context. Three case studies focusing on water, electricity and telecommunications are then presented, with the aim of underscoring Israel’s overt decades- long path towards annexation and the critical role played by

The severe context faced by Palestinians in the Gaza Strip in relation to access to utilities,

including due to Israel’s 15- year closure, repeated and deliberate military assaults on infra-

structure, and other policies, is therefore not addressed. The narrow focus is not intended

to add to the imposed political and geographic fragmentation of the Palestinian people, nor

suggest that these sectors are not equally targeted and manipulated in other areas under

Israel’s control.

business enterprises. This chapter is neither exhaustive of the countless Israeli policies and practices targeting each sector nor of other measures used to

transform the opt.8



1 Continuity of Life during Occupation

An Occupying Power’s obligations under international law, in connection with the provision of utilities, include those related to the lives and livelihoods of protected persons as well as the framework in which the territory should be

administered more broadly.9

International humanitarian law seeks to protect civilian populations during

armed conflict, including by prohibiting direct attacks against civilians and civilian objects and by seeking to “ensure adequate conditions of life for the

civilian population”.10 In situations of occupation, an Occupying Power is thus required to restore and ensure public order and safety, and to respect human

life.11 The Fourth Geneva Convention also requires that protected persons

be treated humanely and with respect for their person.12 Accordingly, “acts

impairing individual life or health” are prohibited.13 Additional Protocol i pro-vides further protections towards civilians and civilian objects, including those

necessary for survival.14

These protections are supplemented by international human rights law

which obliges States to promote, protect, and fulfil human rights in a non- discriminatory manner. Similar to international humanitarian law, interna-tional human rights law recognises the inherent dignity of persons, and the

rights to life and health, amongst others.15 Interlinked with these is the right



8 See for example Breaking the Silence, Highway to Annexation – Israeli Road and

Transportation Infrastructure Development in the West Bank, December 2020.

9 This framework includes the Hague Regulations, the Fourth Geneva Convention, certain

provisions of Additional Protocol i, customary international humanitarian law, and inter-

national human rights law.

10 ohchr, Protection of Economic, Social and Cultural Rights in Conflict, 2015, para 4.

11 Hague Regulations, Articles 43 and 46.

12 Fourth Geneva Convention, Article 27.

13 Ibid., icrc, Commentary: Fourth Geneva Convention Relative to the Protection of Civilian

Persons in Time of War (icrc 1958).

14 See Additional Protocol i, Articles 52 and 54. In particular, Article 54(2) refers to objects

including “drinking water installations and supplies”.

15 See for example iccpr, preamble and Articles 10 and 6; icescr, preamble and Article 12;

udhr, Article 3.

to water.16 The UN Human Rights Committee and the UN Committee on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights have both recognised access to water as

a key condition for the enjoyment of the right to life with dignity.17 The cescr has also affirmed access to safe and potable water and adequate sanitation as

an underlying determinant for the right to health.18 Moreover, water and elec-

tricity are requisites for the right to an adequate standard of living.19 The UN Human Rights Committee has asserted that, where necessary, States should take measures to ensure access to electricity, amongst other essential goods

and services, in order to protect the right to life.20

Central to the upcoming case studies, the UN Committee on Economic,

Social and Cultural Rights has outlined States’ minimum core obligations under the International Covenant on Economic, Social, and Cultural Rights, alongside the obligation of States to ensure the “progressive realization” of the

rights.21 In situations of occupation, measures taken by the Occupying Power

life and human dignity”. UN Committee on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights, General

352 Farah and Abdallah

towards progressive realisation – including by refraining from adopting “ret-

rogressive measures incompatible with core obligations”22 – are reinforced by other prohibitions under international law in regard to its administration of the territory. For example, and especially important in respect to the right to water and the right to adequate housing, the Occupying Power is prohibited from: pillaging; destroying the real or personal property of private persons, the State and other public authorities and social and cooperative organizations, unless required by military necessity; confiscating private property; and as aforementioned, attacking, destroying, removing, or rendering useless “objects indispensable to the survival of the civilian population such as [...] drinking

water installations and supplies and irrigation works”.23

In fulfilling State obligations, the UN Committee on Economic, Social and

Cultural Rights calls on States to take steps towards the realisation of rights,

“including through the adoption of legislative measures”.24 However, both the Hague Regulations and the Fourth Geneva Convention limit the legislative authority of the Occupying Power to cases of necessity (“unless absolutely pre-vented” from respecting “the laws in force in the country”) and where necessary

to fulfil obligations under the Fourth Geneva Convention.25 The commentary to the Fourth Geneva Convention, for example, affirms that certain legisla-tion may be promulgated if “required for the application of the Convention in accordance with the obligations imposed on it by the latter in a number of

spheres: child welfare, labour, food, hygiene and public health”.26 While this exception would include legislation on the provision of water and electricity, changes are not boundless. Regarding situations of occupation, Marco Sassóli

to enable the Occupying Power to fulfil its obligations under the present Convention,

to maintain the orderly government of the territory, and to ensure the security of the

Occupying Power, of the members and property of the occupying forces or administra-

26 International Committee of the Red Cross, Commentary: Fourth Geneva Convention

Relative to the Protection of Civilian Persons in Time of War (icrc 1958) 337. Domination, Transformation, and Annexation via Utilities 353

has underscored that the Occupying Power “is not the sovereign – it may intro-duce only as many changes as is absolutely necessary under its human rights obligations and must stay as close as possible to similar local standards and the

local cultural, legal, and economic traditions”.27

Sassóli further clarifies cases of prolonged occupation where legislation

may be necessary for the occupied territory to “evolve”, in contrast to situations

that are aimed at transformation.28 The former could, for example, include legislation related to utilities where advancements have occurred during the course of an occupation, such as internet provision, which the UN Committee on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights has affirmed States are obliged to pro-

vide access to.29 Utility provision and the development of infrastructure must, however, also be consistent with the role of the Occupying Power as adminis-trator or usufructuary of the public immoveable property, including water and land, and limitations on the use of natural resources by the Occupying Power

more broadly.30

Legislation may be considered beyond the authority of the Occupying Power

if it transforms the institutions or political structures present in the territory,

such as changing a “liberal into a communist economy”31 or alters the demog-

raphy.32 One expert meeting organised by the International Committee of

the Red Cross also emphasised “the need to differentiate between full- fledged transformative projects entailing disruptions of sovereignty and smoother changes aimed at getting the basic infrastructure of the occupied society to

work in accordance with the relevant norms of occupation law”.33

As will be illustrated by the following case studies, utility provision and

related infrastructure have been used as a central tool of transformation – obstructing the right to self- determination and permanent sovereignty over natural resources of the occupied population, and creating a coercive envi-ronment for Palestinians, while facilitating the unlawful transfer of Israeli set-tlers into the occupied territory. Indeed, as noted by the former UN Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the Palestinian Territories Occupied Since 1967, Michael Lynk, water, electricity, telecommunications and other utilities and infrastructure are indispensable to settlements. According to Lynk, the settlements themselves create “demographic facts on the ground to solidify a permanent presence, a consolidation of alien political control and

a claim of sovereignty”.34 These measures contravene international law, where the acquisition of territory through threat or use of force is unlawful under the UN Charter, while transfer is prohibited under the Fourth Geneva Convention,

and a war crime under the Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court.35 Underpinning this framework is the inviolability of the rights of protected persons and protections guaranteed under the Fourth Geneva Convention. Protected persons cannot renounce their rights, even when territory is annexed or where representatives of the occupied population enter into agreements

with the Occupying Power.36

While an Occupying Power has primary obligations towards the terri-

tory and protected persons therein, and Third States have exterritorial obli-gations, it is important to note that corporate and other private actors also

have responsibilities, as they may facilitate and benefit from the manner in

which a territory is administered.37 International humanitarian law is bind-ing upon all actors whose activities are closely linked to an armed conflict, including business enterprises, even if they do not take part in active hostili-

ties.38 Corporate and private actors are also expected to respect international human rights law, undertake enhanced human rights due diligence to ensure that adverse impacts are prevented, and to responsibly divest and disengage

as necessary.39



2 Water: Making Settlements Viable

Israel’s control and exploitation of Palestinian water resources in the West Bank, including East Jerusalem, have been well- documented and analysed, both in relation to the international law violations that have ensued as well as to how it has facilitated Israel’s settlement enterprise and annexation of terri-

tory.40 What has not been emphasised enough, however, is that the roadmap for annexation, centred on the water supplies in the region, was laid out well before 1967, and even prior to the establishment of Israel in 1948. At the Paris Peace Conference in 1919, following the end of World War i, Zionists acknowledged



37 The UN Independent International Fact- Finding Mission on Settlements noted, for

example, how businesses have “directly and indirectly, enabled, facilitated and profited

and underscored the importance of controlling water resources and accord-ingly proposed that the borders of Palestine include parts of Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, and Egypt, with special attention given to Mount Hermon, which

extends to the 1967- occupied Syrian Golan.41 The Zionist Organization stated:

Palestine must have its natural outlets to the seas and the control of its

rivers and their headwaters […] The economic life of Palestine, like that

of every other semi- arid country, depends on the available water supply.

It is, therefore, of vital importance not only to secure all water resources

already feeding the country, but also to be able to conserve and control

them at their sources.42

Control over water resources continued to be a primary aim for Zionists during

negotiations of mandate borders and throughout the mandate period,43 influ-

encing the proposed borders of partition under UN Resolution 181 of 1947.44

In parallel with these international efforts, Zionist organisations, such as

the Jewish National Fund, the Jewish Agency, and the Nir Company of the Histadrut Labor, moved to establish the Mekorot Water Company in 1937 in mandate Palestine. By 1943, Mekorot had prepared a detailed plan of how to

develop “all the water sources in the Land of Israel”,45 which Zionists defined to

include all of mandate Palestine.46 This vision was immediately demonstrated during the 1948 war when Mekorot was assigned a “national mission” to “lay a

water pipeline to the besieged capital” of Jerusalem.47 Following the end of the war, the company laid a second pipeline, further facilitating the annexation of

west Jerusalem.48

Domination, Transformation, and Annexation via Utilities 357

Issues concerning water continued to be paramount following the estab-

lishment of the State of Israel; control over water led to clashes between Israel, Jordan, and Syria, and is considered to be a driver for the 1967 war and ensuing

occupation.49

Even prior to the occupation, however, Israel was already using Palestinian

water supplies in the water- rich West Bank “by means of artesian wells drilled in Israel” constituting “approximately one- third of Israel’s annual water con-

sumption before 1967”.50 It is therefore unsurprising that immediately fol-lowing the start of the occupation, Israel issued a series of military orders

to control the abundant and key water resources in the opt.51 This included granting “full control over water resources” to the Israeli military commander (Military Order 92), prohibiting the establishment, ownership, or admin-istration of water infrastructure without an Israeli issued permit (Military Order 158), and invalidating “all prior settlement of disputes regarding water” (Military Order 291). Alongside these immediate legislative actions, through-out the course of the occupation, Israel has also used declarations of “closed military zones”, including along the bank of the Jordan River, and designated land as nature reserves or national parks, to further prohibit Palestinian access to water sources.

By 1980, Israel’s policies in the opt and the occupied Syrian Golan caused

the UN Security Council to pass resolution 465, calling for an investigation into “the reported serious depletion of natural resources, particularly the water

resources”.52 Unabated, Israel furthered its unlawful integration of the Palestinian water system into that of Israel and in 1982 granted Mekorot ownership of

Palestinian water infrastructure.53 In 1984, a UN team of experts warned that such full integration would “lead to the complete dependence of” Palestinians

on Israel “and will eventually make the separation of the two very costly and

ultimately annexing these territories”.55

These warnings and Israel’s clear intention to annex and colonise came

with continued impunity. In the following years, the Oslo Accords, including

its establishment of the Joint Water Committee,56 followed by the Wye River Memorandum, served to deepen Israel’s control over and exploitation of water

and other resources in the opt.57 Israel further reinforced this domination via the construction of the Wall, which UN experts affirmed would annex “some

of the most important water wells in the region”58 and “most of the western

aquifer system”.59
