confiscate, and destroy water infrastructure, and prevent the modernisation

3 Electricity: Stolen Autonomy, De- development and Subjugation

Similar to the takeover of water, Zionist ambitions in Palestine were central to the establishment and control of the electricity sector. Pinhas Rutenberg,

a Russian Zionist who co- founded the Jewish terrorist group, the Haganah,66 was granted two concessions by British Mandate authorities in 1921 that led to the establishment of the Palestine Electric Company in 1923 (becoming the

Israel Electric Corporation in 1961).67 While Rutenberg’s plans were financially

supported by Zionists in various countries,68 he was met with consistent objec-tions and boycott actions by the Palestinian Arab population. For example, in 1924, various Palestinian groups in Haifa requested that the mayor end plans for work with Rutenberg. In 1929, Palestinian traders called for a boycott of the company. Shortly thereafter, the Jaffa municipalities refused to purchase

electricity from the company, with the Acre municipality following in 1932.69

Concessions for the provision of electricity in Palestine, however, were not

only in the hands of Rutenberg. The concession for providing electricity in Jerusalem was granted by Ottoman authorities to M. Euripide Mavrommatis,

a Greek national, in 1914, and renewed in 1926 by British authorities.70 Having this concession separate from those granted to Rutenberg later preserved some Palestinian autonomy over electricity where part of the concession eventually



65 See Mekorot’s webpage on “International Operations”.

Post, 2018.

May 1924.

68 ‘American Organizations to Invest in Rutenberg Project’, Jewish Telegraphic Agency,

Telegraphic Agency, 2 January 1924.

Agency, 19 November 1929; ‘Jaffa Lights Streets with Gas; Carries out Boycott’, Jewish

Refuses’, Jewish Telegraphic Agency, 27 September 1932.

70 Permanent Court of International Justice, Case of the Readaptation of the Mavrommatis

For more information on the concession, including after 1948 see Michael Dumper,

The Politics of Jerusalem Since 1967 (Columbia University Press 1996) 153– 155. Domination, Transformation, and Annexation via Utilities 361

became the Arab- held, Jerusalem Electric Company.71 The company is now known as the Jerusalem District Electricity Company.

Following the start of the 1967 occupation, Israel moved to control and

dismantle the already existing electricity generating capacity and associated infrastructure, to ultimately make Palestinian village councils and districts

dependent on the Israeli electricity grid.72 These measures include Military Order 159, which transferred all issues related to electricity to the “Israeli

official- in- charge”,73 and Military Order 602, requiring a permit for any elec-

tricity work or generator.74

In October 1977, witnesses before a United States Senate subcommittee

meeting highlighted Israel’s control over electricity as a means to take over

territory.75 One witness, Ibrahim Dakkak, noted:

Another problem which we have faced also on the west bank was elec-

tricity and the promotion of electrical power. For instance, in Nablus,

the municipality has been working very hard to convince the authorities

that they should be allowed to import electrical generators to increase

the power used in that area. Until now the authorities have refused, and

suggested that it might be better and fit to connect the Nablus area with

the national grid in Israel and thus annex the area electrically.76

Corresponding policies related to Israeli settlements were also recognised by a report of a commission established by the UN Security Council in 1980, which noted “roads were being built and electricity and water systems installed in

In parallel, Israel has tried to exert full control and take over the Jerusalem

expanding settlements.79 Military Orders, however, were later issued allow-ing for the connection of a number of Israeli settlements in the West Bank to the Israeli electric supply grid, separating them from the Jerusalem Electric

Company, while advancing the de facto annexation of the areas.80 In 1988, the

company was forced to stop generating electricity.81 Although Israel has yet to fully succeed in integrating the company, it is unable to operate independent of the Israel Electric Corporation and Israeli authorities – including due to its location in annexed Jerusalem. The Jerusalem District Electricity Company mainly relies on Israel for its electric supply and has been reduced to being a

mere electricity distributor given Israel’s control over the sector.82 As of 2019,

More broadly, the Oslo Accords exacerbated Palestinian dependency on

and subordination to Israel for electricity and other infrastructure, giving

Israel ultimate authority for the approval of any such projects and activities.84 Prior warnings were quickly reiterated in a 1994 UN Conference on Trade and Development report that highlighted Israel’s measures aimed at controlling electric power, along with other socioeconomic sectors, and making them

dependent on Israel’s infrastructure and economy.85

Palestinians can, in theory, partially resolve the electricity crisis and their

dependency on Israel through solar energy.86 However, Israel has prohibited Palestinians from constructing any alternative energy infrastructure – espe-cially in Area C, and the Jordan Valley in particular, where such projects may

be viable.87 Often citing a lack of permits, Israel regularly demolishes and confiscates already constructed solar panel installations and associated equip-

ment,88 including those donated by the international community as humani-

tarian relief.89

4 Control and Domination via Telecommunications

Following the start of the occupation, Israel also moved to control the tele-communications sector. In August 1967, Israel issued Military Order 79, which forbade Palestinians from owning “any broadcasting equipment without per-

mission, or to broadcast on it”.101 This was amended to also include the term “communications receiver”, and later, to require licenses for using broadcast-ing or telecommunications equipment to be authorised by the Israeli Civil

Administration.102

In the decades after 1967, Israel controlled telecommunications in the opt

via these and other military orders,103 the Israeli Ministry of Communication and the then State- owned company Bezeq (which was privatised in 2005). Even though the Oslo ii Accords gave lip service to the Palestinian right to a “separate and independent” communications system, in reality, it guaran-teed Israeli control by requiring Israeli “confirmation” prior to “any digging or building regarding telecommunications and any installation of telecommuni-

cations equipment” in Area C.104 It also ensured the continued presence of Israeli companies in the West Bank, given that telecommunications services

to settlements and military outposts would continue under Israeli authority.105

As a result, telecommunications companies106 have established mobile

telephone infrastructure, including transmission stations and more than 600

Mehola Solar Field. See Who Profits, Greenwashing the Occupation: The Solar Energy

Industry and the Israeli Occupation, January 2017.

5 Conclusion

As illustrated in the chapter, in violation of its duties as Occupying Power, Israel has ensured that Palestinian access to necessities, such as water, electric-ity, and telecommunications, remains obstructed and a tool of domination and

persecution.129 Israel has implemented an array of policies and practices, rang-ing from military orders to a discriminatory permit system, that are intended to obstruct full Palestinian administration of and control over their land, resources and infrastructure. These policies, part of a continuum of Zionist plans predating 1948 and later entrenched by Israel’s prolonged military occu-pation, have facilitated the physical, economic, and demographic transforma-tion of the opt in a manner that wholly defies international law. This has not only posed a direct threat to the lives of Palestinians and undermined their livelihoods and rights but has also established the opt as a captive market for utilities and other services and goods.

At the same time, Israel has ensured the discriminatory provision of water,

electricity and telecommunications, alongside other services and infrastruc-ture, enables the establishment and growth of unlawful Israeli settlements and benefits its wider population and economy. The presence and growth of settlements and their integration via utility networks, roads, and other infra-structure into Israel unmistakeably demonstrate Israel’s intent to establish permanent control and ultimately become sovereign, while ensuring that the Palestinian right to self- determination remains obstructed.

To achieve this, Israel has sought the support and financial resources of

States and private actors, including Israeli and international “charitable” asso-ciations and business enterprises. For their part, businesses have largely sup-ported Israel’s policies through their activities and relationships and reaped the financial rewards of impunity. Indeed, many of the companies discussed in this chapter – and the sectors that they work in more broadly – have been the subject of UN and civil society reports and campaigns that have under-scored their enabling and profiting from grave violations of international law,



unhrc, Database of all Business Enterprises Involved in the Activities Detailed in

Paragraph 96 of the Independent International Fact- Finding Mission to Investigate the

Implications of the Israeli Settlements on the Civil, Political, Economic, Social and Cultural

Rights of the Palestinian People throughout the Occupied Palestinian Territory, including

East Jerusalem, a/ hrc/ 43/ 71, 12 February 2020.

129 Human Rights Watch, A Threshold Crossed – Israeli Authorities and the Crimes of Apartheid

and Persecution, 27 April 2021.

370 Farah and Abdallah

including internationally recognised crimes. However, these countless warn-ings and condemnations have gone unheeded.

Instead, the annexation of Palestinian territory and its transformation has

continued unabated, as has Israel’s broader settler- colonial project premised on the transfer of Palestinians, the exploitation of their land and resources, and the subjugation of those who remain. Within this unlawful and unilateral reality and vacuum of international action, challenges to the ever- deteriorating

status quo continue.130



130 See for example calls by UN Special Rapporteurs for the examination of the legality of

Israel’s prolonged military occupation. unhrc, Report of the Special Rapporteur on the

Situation of Human Rights in the Palestinian Territories Occupied Since 1967, Richard Falk,

Richard Falk, a/ hrc/ 25/ 67, 13 January 2014, paras 51 and 81.





chapter 16


Palestinian Refugees and International Law

Thomas Hammarberg



The United Nations decided in November 1947 to divide Palestine into an inde-pendent Arab State and an independent Jewish State, and to place Jerusalem

under international administration, after the end of the British Mandate.1 At the time, however, the overwhelming majority of the population was Palestinian and they possessed most of the lands. Only 5.8 percent of the cultivated land

was owned by the Jewish population in 1947.2 Palestinian leaders immediately rejected the plan that would give more than half of the Palestine territory to Jews. The then leader of the Jewish communities, David Ben- Gurion, was for other reasons dissatisfied with the UN partition plan but noted that the ques-

tion of precise borders was undetermined.3

There had been tensions between the two societies for several years, but

now relations escalated into armed conflict. Jewish militias were better pre-pared and linked to paramilitary groups, ready to use brutal methods. As the war escalated, the Palestinians began to flee in large numbers as refugees. In the middle of May, the State of Israel was formally declared. An attempted military intervention by some Arab States failed to put an end to unilateral Israeli actions. In the meantime, attacks against Palestinian cities and villages continued and the number of refugees increased further. At the end of the hostilities, the area under Israel’s control was larger than that allocated under the UN partition plan. Jordan took control over the West Bank and Egypt took control over the Gaza Strip.

The UN appointed Swedish Count Folke Bernadotte as mediator between

the parties. He outlined that steps had to be taken to ensure that Palestinian refugees could return to their homes. He was murdered by members of one of the Jewish terror groups in September 1948. A few months thereafter, the UN General Assembly agreed to resolve “that the refugees wishing to return to their homes and live at peace with their neighbours should be permitted to do so at the earliest practicable date, and that compensation should be paid for the property of those choosing not to return and for loss of or damage to

1 unsc Resolution 181 (ii) (29 November 1947).

2 Ilan Pappe, The Ethnic Cleansing of Palestine (One World 2006) 30.

3 Ibid., pages 33– 35.



© Thomas Hammarberg, 2023 | DOI:10.1163/978900

372 Hammarberg

property”, in accordance with international law.4 To this day, Palestinians have not been allowed to exercise their right of return or receive compensation.

The consequence of this failure is the subject of this chapter. This chap-

ter seeks to address the key aspects of this serious crisis. It will explain why a majority of Palestinians fled their homes, why they have not been able to return, what their current situation is, and how the international community has reacted to their displacement. It will also delve into the right of return under international law, the actors responsible for refugees, including UN agencies, and discuss what can be done to remedy the injustice caused.



1 The Nakba Reality

What happened in 1947– 49 is known among Palestinians as the Nakba, the catastrophe. From the outset, the international community failed to fully com-prehend the reasons as to why most of the Palestinian population, around

750,000 Palestinians,5 fled their homes. More is known today, largely owing to the research by independent historians, many of them Israeli, of documents that were kept secret until the 1980s. The documents that have now been accessed shed a stark light on the events surrounding the establishment of

the State of Israel and the Palestinian exodus.6 Importantly, these documents make clear that there had been no call from either Arab or Palestinian lead-ers for Palestinians to leave their homes, as argued by Israeli representatives. Rather, the reason for the Palestinian exodus was the orchestration of system-atic and brutal attacks, even executions of Palestinians by Zionist militias.

The massacre of the village of Deir Yassin on 9 April 1948 was known early

on but academics have, since 1980, been able to uncover some additional and important details. For example, two paramilitary Zionist groups, Irgun and Lehi, in cooperation with the Haganah (the military arm of the Jewish com-munity) played a significant role in the massacre, crushing whatever limited Palestinian resistance existed with extreme violence. More than 100 villag-ers were killed in the massacre, including women, children, and the elderly.



4 unga Resolution 194 (iii) (11 December 1948).

5 The exact number of original refugees has not been totally established. The number 750,000

Palestinian survivors were put on trucks, brought to West Jerusalem and

paraded through the streets.7

What happened in Deir Yassin was not unique. Several researchers have

documented a dizzying number of serious atrocities in villages and towns, including murders and mass expulsions. The destruction of entire Palestinian villages and towns was methodical and widespread. Ilan Pappé records the

destruction of 531 Palestinian villages,8 while Benny Morris lists 369 cases,9 and other researchers have concluded that the number of evacuated and destroyed

places is even higher.10

New information on war crimes committed in the 1940s was made public

over the course of 2021 by the Israeli newspaper Haaretz in cooperation with

the Akevot Institute.11 In one of the several incidents reported, the Irgun mili-tia executed between 80– 100 Palestinian villagers who waived a white flag

to show that they had no intention to resist.12 The Zionist militia made the Palestinian civilians dig their own grave before forcing them into it and shoot-

ing them dead.13

Palestinian villages were also emptied and destroyed during the 1967 war

that resulted in Israel’s occupation of the Palestinian territory. Aerial attacks, large scale detention of male civilians and repeated threats contributed to mass displacement during and after the days of the war. About 320,000 Palestinians

were forced to flee their homes in the Gaza Strip and West Bank.14

Researchers agree that Palestinians left their homes as a consequence of

violence, expulsion, intimidation and fear. Benny Morris confirmed that:

[T] he refugee problem was caused by attacks by Jewish forces on Arab

villages and towns and by the inhabitants’ fear of such attacks, com-

pounded by, and rumors of atrocities – and by the crucial Israeli Cabinet

decision in June 1948 to bar a refugee return.15

Internal documents, now being partly made public, leave no doubt about the fact that Jewish leaders, including Ben- Gurion and subsequent ministers, knew of the violence unleashed against Palestinians and that some played a

role in it.16 The methods used to cleanse Palestine were set out explicitly in

various plans.17 Some Israeli members of the cabinet expressed shock at what was happening, some demanded that those responsible be punished, and some requested a thorough investigation. But an investigation has never been

carried out.18

1.1 The Israeli Response to Palestinian Refugees and the Nakba While Palestinians have had to struggle for the Nakba not to be forgotten, Israelis have chosen to suppress it. Shlomi Ben- Ami, diplomat and former

Foreign Minister of Israel,19 explains:

The Israelis chose to repress the memory of their war against a dispos-

sessed, autochthonous Palestinian community claiming national rights,

and preferred the ethos of the struggle against foreign and supposedly

Palestinian Refugees and International Law 375

superior invading Arab armies. Israel as a society oppressed the memory

of its war against the local Palestinians because it could not really come to

terms with the fact that its finest Sabras, the heroes of its war for indepen-

dence and the role models of the new nation, expelled Arabs, committed

atrocities against them and dispossessed them. This was like admitting

that the noble Jewish dream of statehood was stained forever by a major

injustice committed against the Palestinians and that the Jewish state was

born in sin. When the war was over the Palestinian problem practically

disappeared from Israeli public discourse; it was conveniently defined as

one of “refugees” or “infiltrators”. There was no Israeli- Palestinian conflict,

hardly a Palestinian plight. This was submerged into one single issue: the

Arab- Israeli conflict.20

Zionist leaders had before the war discussed how to encourage the forcible transfer of Palestinians from Palestine. Ben- Gurion believed that Zionism would not succeed in establishing a homogeneous Jewish State and fulfilling its imperative of absorbing the expected influx of Jewish immigrants if the indigenous inhabitants were allowed to remain. In February 1948, he stated that “[t] he war will give us the land. The concepts of ‘ours´ and `not ours’ are

peace concepts only, and in war they lose their whole meaning”.21

The Israeli explanation as to why so many Palestinians left their homes was,

as mentioned above, that they had been ordered to do so by leaders in Arab countries. The leaders were said to have ordered the Palestinians to get out of the way, before their armies would invade the new Israeli State. Even Shimon

Peres repeated this version in a book in 1993.22 However, it has been clearly proven that no such communication took place and that the actual position

of the Arab countries was that the Palestinians should stay where they lived.23

While the UN demanded that the refugees should be allowed to return

home, the Israeli leaders decided that they would not accept the return of

Palestinian refugees. They even adopted a special law to that effect.24 Houses

and other properties owned by the refugees were confiscated and transferred to Israeli hands. The responsibility for the refugees was now, according to the

Israeli position, a matter fully for the Arab countries.25 Notably, on its admis-sion to the UN, Israeli representatives explained that the Palestinian refugee

problem “was not of its making”,26 and that its resolution depended on the establishment of peace and practicability. Israel’s “resolution” essentially pro-posed the resettlement of Palestinians refugees in countries other than Israel. Israel’s representative to the UN explained that:

A study of the economic, irrigation and other potentialities of the under-

populated and under- developed areas of the Arab States revealed greater

possibilities for a stable solution by the latter method than by resettle-

ment in Israel. Therefore, the Government of Israel contended that

resettlement in neighbouring areas should be considered as the main

principle of solution.27

There was no recognition that Palestinians are indigenous to Palestine and had been forcibly transferred. Furthermore, there was no acknowledgement that Palestinians are intrinsically connected to Palestine, as individuals and a collective, and to their homes, lands, and families therein. In the post- war situation there seemed to be little political space for the kind of self- criticism that had been previously voiced by Israeli ministers at a closed meeting in the

autumn of 1948 (as reported by the Akevot Institute).28

After the dispossession and displacement of Palestinians, several meth-

ods were deployed to erase the traces of Palestinian presence and history in what became the State of Israel. Across Palestine, the Jewish National Fund planted entire swathes – tens of thousands of dunums of forests, made up on

Recently, in 2011, the Israeli Parliament enacted the so- called Nakba Law that

authorises the Minister of Finance to reduce State funding or support to any institution that commemorates, as a day of mourning, the Israeli Independence

Day, or the day on which the State was established.30 Palestinians often com-memorate the Nakba on that day. A petition by school children and school alumni, requesting that Israel’s Supreme Court deem the law unconstitutional,

was rejected in 2012.31

Those responsible for the unlawful killing of Palestinian civilians, destruc-

tion of civilian property, and the forcible transfer of Palestinians during the war have not been held to account. Many Israelis involved have benefitted from this impunity, with some able to climb the hierarchical ladder to occupy top positions within the State of Israel. It must be noted that no statute of limitations applies to war crimes and crimes against humanity committed between 1947 and 1948. They still await investigation and prosecution. The UN Convention on the Non- Applicability of Statutory Limitations for War Crimes and Crimes against Humanity confirms that persons that perpetrate such crimes the must be brought to justice, however long after the crime in question

occurs.32



2 The Right to Return or to Receive Compensation

The right to return to one’s homeland is an established and recognised human right in international law. Article 13(2) of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights provides unequivocally that “[e] veryone has the right to leave any coun-try, including his own, and to return to his country”. Only one day after the UN General Assembly adopted the Universal Declaration on Human Rights, it



29 Nur Masalha, The Palestine Nakba: Decolonising History, Narrating the Subaltern,

Reclaiming Memory (Zed Books 2012) Chapters 2 and 3.

30 Israel, Budget Foundations Law (Amendment No 40) (2011).

31 Israel, Budget Foundations Law (Amendment No 40) (2011). See also Adalah and

Association for Civil Rights in Israel, Adalah and ACRI: Israeli High Court Ignored the

Chilling Effect Already Caused by the ‘Nakba Law’, 5 January 2012.

32 Convention on the Non- Applicability of Statutory Limitations to War Crimes and Crimes

Against Humanity, adopted in unga Resolution 2391 (xxiii) (26 November 1968). 378 Hammarberg

voted in favour of resolution 194 (iii), which declares that Palestinians should

be permitted to return to their homes or else receive compensation.33

International human rights instruments have since affirmed the existence

of the right of return. Notably, the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights provides that “[n] o one shall be arbitrarily deprived of the right to enter

his own country”.34 Specifically, the UN Human Rights Committee has clari-fied that the right to return also applies to disputed territories and territories which have undergone political change. It has stated:

The scope of ‘his own country’ is broader than the concept ‘country of

his nationality’. It is not limited to nationality in a formal sense, that is,

nationality acquired at birth or by conferral; it embraces, at the very least,

an individual who, because of his or her special ties to or claims in rela-

tion to a given country, cannot be considered to be a mere alien. This

would be the case, for example, of nationals of a country who have there

been stripped of their nationality in violation of international law, and

of individuals whose country of nationality has been incorporated in or

transferred to another national entity, whose nationality is being denied

them.35

The UN Human Rights Committee has further elaborated that the holders of the right of return are not only the original refugees and their families, but

also their descendants.36 Children born into a refugee family would accord-ingly not be deprived of the right to be treated themselves as refugees. They would thereby have the same right to live in the country their families once had to leave. Indeed, this is also the approach of the UN High Commissioner

for Refugees in relation to refugee situations in other parts of the world.37

Palestinians’ right to return to their homeland, whether inside Israel or in

the occupied Palestinian territory, finds further support in international cus-tomary law with the icrc confirming that persons displaced, voluntarily or

involuntarily, on account of a conflict, have a right to return.38 The right to

33 unga Resolution 194 (iii) (11 December 1948).

ccpr/ c/ 21/ Rev.1/ Add.9, 2 November 1999, para 20.

return has been mirrored in several UN resolutions on the Palestine issue over

the years. UN General Assembly resolutions 194 (iii) (see Section 2.1), 3236, and 52/ 59 are informative in this regard, with the latter two resolutions explic-

itly referring to the right of Palestinians to return to their homes.39

2.1 The Relevance and Implementation of UN General Assembly

Resolution 194 (iii)

One key source of discussions about the right of return for the Palestinians has

been UN General Assembly resolution 194 (iii) from December 1948.40 It states that the UN General Assembly:

Resolves that the refugees wishing to return to their homes and live at

peace with their neighbours should be permitted to do so at the earliest

practicable date, and that compensation should be paid for the property

of those choosing not to return and for the loss of or damage to property

which, under principles of international law or in equity, should be made

good by the Governments or authorities responsible.41

This resolution has been crucial in international discussions on the issue of Palestinian refugees as it clearly confirms the right of return. It also mentions compensation for the loss of property, as well as compensation for those who choose not to return. This last point has been referred to in international peace- making efforts, as a possible opening for a solution that would not end with a massive return of Palestinian refugees to the State of Israel. However, resolution 194 (iii) has not been accepted by any Israeli government.

For some time, there was resistance also from the Palestinian side to the

resolution. The Palestine Liberation Organization feared that the text of the resolution would be interpreted as an indirect recognition of the State of Israel

and thereby undermine the basic right of return for many of the refugees.42 It was only when the plo accepted the two- state solution in 1988 that it finally

accepted resolution 194 (iii).43 In doing so, the plo implicitly recognised that



39 unga Resolutions 3236 (22 November 1974) and 52/59 (10 December 1997).

40 unga Resolution 194 (iii) (11 December 1948).

41 Ibid.

42 Thomas Hammarberg, Justice Postponed, Analytical Report on Palestinian Refugees (The

Swedish Institute of International Affairs 2018) 53.

43 Jaber Suleiman describes the gradual recognition on Resolution 194 (iii) by the plo. Jaber

Suleiman, ‘The Palestine Liberation Organization: From Right of Return to Bantustan’ in

Naseer Aruri (ed), Palestinian Refugees: The Right of Return (Pluto 2001). 380 Hammarberg

a Palestinian refugee could “return” to a Palestinian State, instead of to their home within the State of Israel. However, a constructive discussion on the implementation of resolution 194 (iii) has not followed. As explained above, the Israeli government continues to refuse to acknowledge any responsibility for the Palestinian refugee problem.

As Israeli participation is understood as necessary for the implementation

of UN General Assembly resolution 194 (iii), there have not been many con-crete preparations for how to organise the return of Palestinians or the dis-bursement of compensation. Although the United Nations Compensation Commission has conducted some preparatory work on relevant documenta-

tion, to date, no comprehensive plan has been put in place.44 This is regretta-ble, as the return or compensation to millions of Palestinians is a mammoth task of great importance. The genuine implementation of the resolution would necessitate the establishment of a special agency within the UN to secure nec-essary funding and prepare solutions, which would be relevant even to indi-vidual cases. Of course, under international law, the party responsible for the return of Palestinians and compensation is principally the wrongdoing party –

namely Israel.45



3 International Meetings to Make Solutions Possible

A serious attempt at initiating a real discussion on ways to implement UN General Assembly resolution 194 (iii) was made at the Madrid Conference, convened in 1991 and co- sponsored by the United States and the former Soviet Union. However, the Refugee Working Group set up by the conference in that process did not get the Israeli delegation to take part in any meaning-ful exchanges. Israel’s delegation insisted that Israel had no responsibility for the refugee problem and would therefore abstain from any discussion on the

issue.46

The Refugee Working Group was chaired by Canadian diplomats who in the

end delivered a Vision Paper based on consultation among the other delegates

uted to the closure of the UN Compensation Commission.

“with options from which they can make a free and informative choice”.48 This would require more clarity on the concrete substance of the real options. One aspect mentioned, relates to the “absorptive capacity” in the future West Bank

and Gaza Strip.49 Parallel to such clarifications it would be necessary to know more about the individual situations and preferences of the refugees. In other words, the paper highlights the need to start preparing a serious implementa-

tion phase in the work for the rights of the refugees.50

The refugee issue was not dealt with in substance during the 1993 Oslo nego-

tiations or as part of the Camp David process in 2000. Soon after the talks in Camp David failed, a less official attempt to negotiate was organised in Taba,

Egypt, by Israeli and Palestinian officials.51 This appeared, at first, to be a promising meeting with concrete exchanges on substance. Even UN General Assembly resolution 194 (iii) came up in the discussion, although limited

to aspects relating to compensation for the refugees.52 However, these talks ended when Ariel Sharon became the Prime Minister of Israel.

More than a decade later, a peace- making attempt was made by the US

Secretary of State, John Kerry in 2013– 2014. This initiative also failed, but Kerry made an important speech which was interpreted to reflect the position of then President Obama. He said, among other things, the following on the par-ticular issue of Palestinian refugees:

The plight of many Palestinian refugees is heartbreaking, and all agree

their needs must be addressed. As part of a comprehensive resolution,

they must be provided with compensation, their suffering must be

acknowledged, and there will need to be options and assistance in finding

permanent homes. The international community can provide significant

4 UN, unrwa and Its Responsibility to Protect

The United Nations Relief and Works Agency is a special agency set up to address Palestinian refugees and their needs. Its mandate was from the begin-

ning focused on humanitarian assistance.54 Not all Palestinian refugees can benefit from the support of unrwa. Its area of operation is limited to Jordan, Syria, Lebanon, the West Bank and Gaza Strip. A Palestine refugee (this is the

term used by unrwa, rather than Palestinian refugee)55 was decided to be any person whose “normal place of residence was Palestine during the period of 1 June 1946 to 15 May 1948 and who lost both home and means of livelihood as a

result of the 1948 conflict”.56 Those who fled for a second time because of the 1967 hostilities have been listed by unrwa and are serviced like the 1948 refu-

gees within the unrwa geographic areas.57 Palestinians from the West Bank, who fled around 1948, were considered by the Jordanian government as inter-nally displaced and have received support by the authorities in Amman. The unrwa was mandated to assist them as well, on an exceptional basis.

Although the unrwa programme is focused on providing relief and human

development, it also has a protection mandate. Its role, in this respect, is dif-ferent from that of the UN High Commissioner for Refugees, which is the special authority for global implementation of international refugee law. The 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees provides that refugees

supported by other UN agencies are not covered by that UN treaty.58 In this



53 Congressional Record Volume 163, Number 3 (5 January 2017) H159.

54 Chapters 4 – 4.4 are largely based on facts presented in a book by Francesca Albenese and

Lex Takkenberg, Palestinian Refugees in International Law (oup 2020).

55 Reference to “Palestine” instead of “Palestinian” was to make clear that unrwa was man-

dated to support all refugees from Palestine, irrespective of their national/ ethnic origin.

The Agency has registered refugees from Palestine to some two dozen nationalities. The

overwhelming majority are, however, Palestinians.

56 unrwa, Frequently Asked Questions, Who Is UNRWA Mandated to Serve.

57 Ibid.

58 UN Convention Relating to the Status of Refugees, adopted in 1951. Article 1D states that

the convention does “not apply to persons who are at present receiving from organs or Palestinian Refugees and International Law 383

regard, “other UN agencies” includes the unrwa and therefore the conven-tion have been interpreted by some to mean that Palestinians registered with unrwa would have less internationally agreed rights than other refugees. This unfortunate perception is based either on a misunderstanding or a deliberate misrepresentation.

It is true that Article 1D of the Convention relating to the Status of Refugees

restricts its application to persons who are not receiving protection and assis-tance from other UN bodies. In fact, it was clear when this formulation was agreed that it did refer to unrwa. Palestinians can nevertheless fall within its scope, as is made clear by the second part of Article 1D. The second part of Article 1D provides that:

When such protection or assistance has ceased for any reason, without

the position of such persons being definitively settled in accordance with

the relevant resolutions adopted by the General Assembly of the United

Nations, these persons shall ipso facto be entitled to the benefits of this

Convention.59

The unhcr has clarified that the Convention relating to the Status of Refugees may apply to Palestinians in its official handbook:

With regard to refugees from Palestine, it will be noted that unrwa oper-

ates only in certain areas of the Middle East, and it is only there that its

protection or assistance are given. Thus, a refugee from Palestine who

finds himself outside that area does not enjoy the assistance mentioned

and may be considered for determination of his refugee status under the

criteria of the 1951 Convention. It should normally be sufficient to establish

that the circumstances which originally made him qualify for protection or

assistance from unrwa still persist and that he has neither ceased to be a

refugee under one of the cessation clauses nor is excluded from the applica-

tion of the Convention under one of the exclusion clauses.60

In conclusion, Palestinian refugees are entitled the same rights as other ref-ugees, irrespective of which UN body has the more direct relation to the



agencies of the United Nations other than the United Nations High Commissioner for

Refugees protection or assistance”.

59 UN Convention Relating to the Status of Refugees, Article 1D.

60 unrwa, Handbook and Guidelines on Procedures and Criteria for Determining Refugee

Status, December 2011.

384 Hammarberg

individual. The fact that Palestinian refugees outside the five unrwa serviced areas would have the same protection as all other refugees, speaks for such equality of treatment. Article 1D thereby has an inclusive dimension which is in line with the arguments voiced when the relevant documents were drafted

and adopted.61

4.1 The Upgrade of the unrwa Protection Programme Protection aspects for Palestinian refugees were originally to be covered by another body, the UN Conciliation Commission for Palestine, set up in December 1948 as per the decision of the General Assembly on resolution 194 (iii). The intention was that this commission would also take over tasks after

the murder of Count Bernadotte a couple of months earlier.62

The UN Conciliation Commission and unrwa worked in parallel when

unhcr was established and, at the time, it was felt that Palestinians already had at least as good legal protection as to what unhcr could offer. However, the UN Conciliation Commission was gradually undermined and then phased out, without unrwa’s mandate being clearly amended on the question of protection. This is one reason for the continued misunderstandings about the mandate of unrwa. Today, unrwa has activities on protection within its area of competence and it cooperates with unhcr to ensure “continuity of protec-

tion”.63 unhcr and unrwa have both on occasion sought to intervene with governments when Palestinian refugees’ lives and fundamental rights have

been violated, unfortunately, with limited results.64

Proposals have been made to merge unrwa’s mandate with the unhcr’s

programme. Considering the differences in expertise and working meth-ods, such a marriage would not be easy. However, it should be clarified that Palestinian refugees, whether connected to unrwa or not, should have the same protection support as all other refugees. That intention was clearly there from the beginning and should be honoured. In reality, it has been difficult for unrwa to fully integrate protection into its programme and a protection



61 Susan Akram, ‘Reinterpreting Palestinian Refugee Rights under International Law’ in

Naseer Aruri, Palestinian Refugees, The Right of Return (Pluto Press 2001).

62 For a thorough analysis of the common efforts to protect the rights of the Palestinian ref-

ugees, see Francesca Albenese and Lex Takkenberg, Palestinian Refugees in International

Law (oup 2020) I.4.

63 Guy S Goodwin and Jane McAdam, The Refugee in International Law (oup 2007) 189.

64 See Francesca Albenese and Lex Takkenberg, Palestinian Refugees in International Law

(Oxford University Press 2020).

Palestinian Refugees and International Law 385

gap remains. It is therefore imperative that UN Member States support unrwa and enable it to progress further on this aspect.

The fact that Palestinian refugees differ from most other refugees in that

they do not seek to settle in any other country – but continue to request the right to return – is no reason to deny them the benefit of international refugee rights. The recognition of protection rights for all Palestinian refugees is con-sistent with international human rights standards, which apply to all human beings. Further, the Convention relating to the Status of Refugees should be seen as applicable to Palestinian refugees even if the host country has not rati-

fied it – given its contemporary status as customary international law.65

4.2 Rights of Stateless Persons and Human Rights Standards Applied to

Palestinians

For Palestinian refugees who have no recognised citizenship, the two UN con-

ventions on statelessness are critically relevant.66 The UN Convention relating to the Status of Stateless Persons from 1954 contains the same formulation as the Convention relating to the Status of Refugees, that those who have pro-tection and assistance from a UN body other than unhcr are not covered. Accordingly, refugees serviced by unrwa cannot therefore benefit from the treaty. However, there are a significant number of stateless Palestinians living outside the unrwa serviced areas and in countries that are parties to the two conventions on statelessness.

The international community has so far failed to ensure that the problem

of statelessness among Palestinians is solved. Continued cooperation between unrwa and unhcr on this matter is important. Responsibility for stateless Palestinian refugees outside the unrwa domain will rest with the host coun-try. Clearly, Palestinian refugees have the same right as other refugees, to ben-efit from international human rights and international humanitarian law. For example, the rights of refugee children are protected by the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child. Even if unrwa is taking substantive responsibility for its implementation, other parts of the UN family, as well as host countries, will be expected to cooperate.

4.3 The unrwa Dilemma

The mandate of unrwa has been repeatedly renewed by the UN General Assembly. Exceptional services to refugees and people displaced by the

65 Lebanon, Syria and Jordan have not ratified the UN refugee treaties.

66 UN Convention Relating to the Status of Stateless Persons and the 1961 Convention on the

Reduction of Statelessness, adopted in 1954.

386 Hammarberg

Arab- Israeli conflict in 1967 and subsequent hostilities have been added to the programme. The mandate was extended in the autumn of 2020 for a further

three years until June 2023.67

Today, more than half a million girls and boys attend 700 unrwa schools,

with a personnel staff of 22,000.68 About 8.4 million patients visit the 143

health clinics annually and more than 3,000 are employed in this sector.69 About 900 staff members are running the relief and social services programme,

which includes providing cash in emergency situations.70 To date, more than

500,000 microloans have been awarded by the microfinance programme.71 It must be noted also, that as a UN agency, unrwa is unique in that its staff are overwhelmingly recruited from the people that it is created to support. There are around 30,000 Palestinian employed while less than 200 of the posts are

international.72

Unfortunately, funding has not kept pace with the increasing needs of ser-

vices for the growing number of refugees, resulting in a reduction of services. The average annual spending per refugee has decreased from 200 US dollars

per year in 1975, to 110 US dollars per year in 2017.73 This trend has continued. The very system of funding is problematic. The budget depends largely on vol-untary contributions from Member States and is not part of the UN assessed contribution system. Only the core international staff posts are financed from

the regular UN budget.74

The recent emergency situations in the Gaza Strip, the West Bank and Syria

have created further major capacity problems.75 The response to emergency appeals for assistance has not been sufficient. Over one million people in the West Bank and Gaza Strip have in recent times been impoverished by the con-flict, violence, and restrictions. The situation is similar for the refugees in war affected Syria. The need for food assistance, support for the reconstruction of destroyed homes, and even cash assistance, has become acute.



67 unrwa, UNRWA Requires US$ 149 Million in 2020 for Operations in Jordan, 5 February 2020.

68 ‘Palestinian Schools to Reopen – But UN Agency Warns Funding Will Run Out Within

Weeks’, Their World, 16 August 2018.

69 unrwa, What We Do, 15 March 2022.

70 unrwa, In Figures, 31 December 2020.

71 unrwa, What We Do, 15 March 2022.

72 unrwa, Working at UNRWA, 15 March 2022.

page 28.

74 This applies to some other UN humanitarian agencies as well, for instance unicef.

75 unrwa, Emergency Appeal for the Occupied Palestinian Territory, 15 March 2022. Palestinian Refugees and International Law 387

4.4 The Politics Undermining the unrwa Israeli governments have been critical of unrwa. The mere existence of this agency has been seen as an unwanted reminder of a problem created in connec-

tion with the birth of the State of Israel.76 There has also been criticism against the inclusion of descendants in the definition of a refugee. Furthermore, there has been a perception in Israel that the agency did not promote resettlement in the Arab host countries with sufficient determination – and thereby, at least indirectly, encourage - Palestinians to maintain their demand for a right to

return.77

Benjamin Netanyahu did earlier advise the US government to minimise its

funding of the agency and recommended that available resources instead be

transferred to the Jordanian government.78 Despite recommending the closure of unrwa, he has reportedly stressed that international aid be continued to

cover humanitarian needs in the West Bank and Gaza Strip.79 Key representa-tives of the Israeli Defence Forces are known to have expressed concern about the risk of new popular uprisings or other security problems in the occupied

territories if living conditions are not improved.80

The unrwa funding crisis worsened considerably during Donald Trump’s

presidency. The cuts were dramatic as the US had been the number one donor over the years. From having provided roughly 30 percent of the overall funding, it ended up paying nothing. Both Trump and Netanyahu declared that unrwa

should be dismantled and closed.81 They also pressured other governments to support this position. In response some other governments did in fact increase

their contributions.82 However, the financial crisis of unrwa is still acute.

In recent years unrwa has had to face serious disasters. The military oper-

ation in the Gaza Strip in 2014 and 2021 further burdened unrwa. Four years after the 2014 offensive against the Gaza Strip, the agency reported that thou-

sands of Palestinian refugee families remained displaced.83 More than 50,000



76 ‘UN agency for Palestinian Refugees Faces Funding Crisis’, Aljazeera, 30 November 2021.

77 Ibid.

78 Barak Ravid, ‘Netanyahu Wants to Transfer Part of unrwa Funding to Jordan’, Axios, 8

March 2018.

79 Ibid.

80 Thomas Hammarberg, Justice Postponed, Analytical Report on Palestinian Refugees (The

Swedish Institute of International Affairs 2018) 35.

81 Hady Amr, ‘In one move, Trump Eliminated US Funding for unrwa and the US Role as

Mideast Peacemaker’, Brookings, 7 September 2018.

82 Norway Ministry of Foreign Affairs, ‘Norway Increases Support for UNRWA’, 16

November 2021.

83 unrwa, Occupied Palestinian Territory Emergency Appeal, 2018. 388 Hammarberg

homes were still not repaired.84 Several of them were in ruins also when the 2021 attacks started. The continued closure has caused increased poverty and

added to the high rate of unemployment.85 More than one million Palestinian

refugees are dependent on unrwa for emergency food assistance.86

The war in Syria has re- victimised the Palestinian refugee community. In

spite of the continued war, unrwa has managed to provide life support to the

more than 400,000 remaining Palestinians registered as refugees.87 More than 50,000 Palestinian refugees in Syria have sought to escape the hostilities by fleeing to Lebanon or Jordan – where their acute situation has required mas-

sive humanitarian assistance in order to prevent further agony.88 Among the most vulnerable are the many families in which women are single parents. It falls largely on them to protect the children and ensure that they can access the schooling options available.

Despite all the problems, unrwa has run crucial programmes to care for the

refugees, many of them in camps. Today, unrwa is a lifeline for the majority in the suffering people in Gaza but important as well on the West Bank and for the refugees in Lebanon, Syria and Jordan. A premature closure of unrwa would be a serious and dangerous mistake. The programmes of the agency are essential for the more than five million humans who benefit from its services. A decision to close or scale down these programmes would, almost certainly, result in a serious political crisis and even violent unrest.



5 New Framework for Resolution of the Humanitarian Crisis

As a matter of immediate urgency, the financial crisis imposed on unrwa has to be resolved. The gaps in the current budgets must be filled. Steps should be taken to ensure a more stable and predictable system of funding its pro-grammes. unrwa should be kept going until a just and lasting solution to the demands and needs of the refugees is in place.

The situation for the refugees in the occupied Palestinian territory is very

precarious, particularly in the Gaza Strip. Real efforts must be made to resolve the urgent humanitarian needs without delay. It should be understood that the



84 Thomas Hammarberg, Justice Postponed, Analytical Report on Palestinian Refugees (The

Swedish Institute of International Affairs 2018) 36.

85 Ibid.

86 Ibid.

87 Ibid.

88 unrwa, Where We Work, 15 March 2022.

Palestinian Refugees and International Law 389

occupation in the West Bank and the blockade against the Gaza Strip are major root causes underpinning these human conditions. Palestinian refugees flee-ing from Syria to other countries have also suffered terribly. They should not be treated any less than other refugees from the war. They have the right to the same protection based on international standards of refugee rights, including the Convention relating to the Status of Refugees and its 1967 protocol.

There is a notion amongst officials in some of the host countries, but also

to some extent in the refugee communities, that when demanding the right to return home, they should avoid being integrated into the society where they now live. This notion has been particularly prominent in Lebanon and has complicated daily life for the Palestinians there. Owing to this, Palestinians do not have the same rights as other refugees to seek employment, higher educa-tion, or medical treatment. This is a violation of their right to the same oppor-tunities and services as other groups of refugees.

It is important to build upon the spirit and recommendations of the New York

Declaration for Refugees and Migrants, which was adopted in September 2016, by the total majority of 197 UN Member States. That declaration, reaffirms the right to leave ones country and to return to it, commits to promote human rights and the rule of law, and recognises that, “armed conflict, persecution and violence, including terrorism, are among the factors which give rise to large refugee movements” and that, “the root causes of such crisis situations

must be addressed”.89 It also reaffirms, that, “international refugee law, interna-tional human rights law and international humanitarian law provide the legal

framework to strengthen the protection of refugees”.90

This declaration and two detailed follow- up documents are highly relevant

to the efforts to establish just and durable solutions for the Palestinian refu-gees. The broad support by UN Member States for the New York Declaration ought to be seen as a call for genuine efforts to break the unfortunate deadlock. There is particularly, a need to ensure that new initiatives are based on interna-tional law and human rights.

5.1 Breaking the Political Deadlock The work by historians and other researchers has been important to establish the facts about what really happened in 1948 and 1967 to the Palestinian peo-ple. The documents they were able to study, analyse and make public, have become essential in establishing the truth. However, not all documents are

89 New York Declaration for Refugees and Migrants, September 2016, para 64, unga resolu-

tion 71/ 1 (16 September 2016).

90 Ibid., para 66.

390 Hammarberg

available; several Israeli documents remain classified, and Israel has destroyed

and looted the Palestinian archives and documentation centres.91 In any case, it is not likely that these “missing documents” would undermine the detailed and extensive picture that so far has been painted of the Nakba.

That picture provides that Palestinians are victims of the most serious

human rights violations, including the execution and rape of civilians. It is also clear that no real legal action has been taken against those responsible. Moreover, it has been convincingly shown that Palestinians fled their homes because of the violence that engulfed them and the region. Israel’s denial of the Nakba, as well as its role and the role of its founding fathers, in the delib-erate mass- displacement and dispossession of Palestinians evades the truth of the facts since uncovered.

With Israel “hardly [recognising] a Palestinian plight”,92 and the interna-

tional community’s lackluster efforts at implementing decisions taken to sup-port their rights, the Palestinians have been left to suffer in refugee camps or compelled to become nationals of other States. Their own leaders have also not been effective in presenting the Palestinian case.

Today, close to 75 years after the decisive 1948 developments and all the

changes which have taken place ever since, what can really be done for genuine justice? The passing of time means that none of the current leaders in Israel has any personal, individual, responsibility for what happened in 1948. Like their predecessors, they have stated that UN General Assembly resolution 194 (iii) is impossible to implement as it would allow several million Palestinians to move to Israel, including many who have never been in the country. A related argument is, of course, that Israel now is dramatically different from what it was when the refugees fled. Many of their original homes have been destroyed.

These arguments might be seen as more convincing with the passing

of time. However, it is clear that time does not heal this wound. It is crucial that the refugee tragedy be addressed, no matter the time- lapse. Ignoring the injustice would be a serious mistake; it will prolong the political tension it has caused and undermine any effort to establish peaceful relations.

Critically, UN General Assembly resolution 194 (iii) will not be imple-

mented without the decisive intervention of the international community. Notwithstanding, Israel’s position has consistently been one of total denial.



91 See Nur Masalha, The Palestine Nakba: Decolonising History, Narrating the Subaltern,

Reclaiming Memory (Zed Books 2012) Chapter 4.

92 Quote from Shlomo Ben- Ami text in this chapter.

Palestinian Refugees and International Law 391

International peace negotiations, when tried, have referred the refugee issue to a “final status” discussion, which has not taken place. Meanwhile, Israel’s pol-icy of entrenched control over the opt and its transfer of settlers into the West Bank have further limited the actual physical space available for Palestinians wishing to return to a liberated Palestinian State. The situation of Palestinian refugees from 1967 is linked to the unfolding occupation of the Palestinian ter-ritory, which saw many Palestinians being internally displaced. For example, Palestinians living in Sheikh Jarrah are refugees from 1948, who are now at risk

of being expelled once more.93

The UN should be asked to organise the necessary steps towards a fair solu-

tion for the refugees. The concrete options for them will have to be defined and their precise wishes solicited. Financial resources will be needed to cover compensation. However, the stalemate so far has prevented the collection of such necessary knowledge. While economic compensation will of course be important, the point is that the process gets started and and that there be a genuine examination of what is wanted and what is possible.

Except for the preliminary exchange in Taba twenty- two years ago, there has

been no real attempt in this direction. The argument that implementation of UN General Assembly resolution 194 (iii) would amount to suicide for Israel misses a crucial point. The mere discussion on what can be done to address the refugee problem is essential by itself.

An Israeli statement of regret would change a lot. My personal impression is

that what many of the refugees are seeking is a clear recognition that they, and their families, have been mistreated. If Israel offers a genuine acknowledge-ment of responsibility, takes ownership, and expresses remorse then meaning-ful solutions may be possible. But there are of course many obstacles, not least because of the current occupation of the Palestinian territory.

It is important that the UN plays a key role in this process. The UN would

depend heavily on support from the Member States in doing so. Actions on the refugee issue need to relate to other steps to ensure a two- state solution. The settlement activities on the West Bank will have to be stopped and the blockade of Gaza Strip must be lifted. At the same time, active and determined involvement by the international community is necessary.



93 unrwa, Statement of the Humanitarian Country Team on the Imminent Eviction of the

Salem Family in Sheikh Jarrah in East Jerusalem, 20 February 2022. 392 Hammarberg

6 Conclusion

That Palestinians have a right to return and compensation is supported by UN decisions and international law. Recognition of this right by the collective inter-national community has not been withdrawn and remains valid. Solutions for the Palestinian refugees have been proposed ever since 1948 but all efforts to protect their basic human rights have failed or been ignored. A major obstacle has been Israel’s Nakba denial and the role that it played in ridding historic Palestine of Palestinians. It is most unlikely that there will be any solution to this serious injustice until that denial is overcome.

This is exacerbated by Israel’s occupation and settlement policy in the West

Bank, which undercuts any potential for Palestinians to return to the opt or leave the camps in the opt to return to their homes in the State of Israel. It has become clear that no real progress will be made without the direct and deci-sive involvement by the international community. Almost 75 years have now passed since the first wave of dispossessions and displacement of Palestinians. Sadly, the passing of time has not made this injustice disappear. The Nakba never ended for the refugees as well as other Palestinians. Continued failure to address this gross violation will similarly undermine future peace efforts in the region. And the impotence of the international human rights system will be further exposed by its negative consequences.
